title: Creating an Emergency Board to Investigate a Dispute Between the Long Island Rail Road Company and Certain of Its Employees

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authority rests in a three-member board appointed by the President that only investigates and reports findings; it holds no binding decisional power over the parties, so no meaningful authority is pooled in a single unaccountable position.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The board is expressly directed to 'report its findings to the President within thirty days,' giving a named actor a defined obligation and a clear deadline, so responsibility for the instrument's function is traceable.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order addresses a single problem (one labor dispute) with a single remedy (an investigatory board under the Railway Labor Act); there are no distinct remedies or problem frames packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The statutory status-quo freeze ('no change, except by agreement, shall be made ... in the conditions out of which the dispute arose') states no enforcing actor or consequence for violation, so two officials could reasonably differ on how it is enforced; the vagueness is inherited from the statute and is not core to purpose, so PRESENT not CRITICAL.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The board is barred from any pecuniary interest in a railroad organization or carrier, and its fact-finding, time-limited design creates no incentive for any actor to undermine the stated purpose of resolving the dispute.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The authority is inherently time-bound: the board must report within thirty days and the status-quo freeze runs only thirty days past that report, so the instrument self-terminates rather than persisting until revoked.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order operates squarely within the Railway Labor Act's existing emergency-board machinery and removes no pre-existing judicial, legislative, or administrative check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The two disputing parties (the railroad and the labor organizations) each have standing and incentive to surface the other's breach of the status-quo freeze, so enforcement does not depend solely on self-reporting by one regulated party.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: This is a routine, self-limiting invocation of a standing statutory procedure for one dispute; it establishes no precedent or definitional framework usable in unrelated contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order builds on the National Mediation Board's judgment and creates a new presidential board without redirecting or countermanding any agency's statutory mandate; no lateral jurisdictional conflict is created.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The only carve-out is the statutory 'except by agreement' allowance permitting the parties to settle voluntarily, which advances rather than undermines the purpose; there is no discretionary waiver structure.

note: Standard Railway Labor Act emergency-board order; the only firing flag is Vague Enforcement on the statutory status-quo freeze, which names no enforcer.

title: Restoring Certain Land Reserved for Military Purposes of the United States to the Jurisdiciton of the Territory of Hawaii

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: This is a one-time, self-executing land restoration ('is hereby restored to the possession, use, and control of the Territory of Hawaii'); no ongoing decision authority is concentrated in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The action completes upon issuance with no implementation to fail; there is no failure chain requiring an accountable actor.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: A single land tract is restored under a single rationale (no longer needed for military purposes); nothing distinct is packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time land-status transfer creates no ongoing behavioral rule for anyone to comply with, so there is no enforcement mechanism to assess for vagueness.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory or compliance relationship is created by a completed land restoration, so there is no incentive structure for any actor class to assess.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing transfer grants no continuing authority, so there is nothing that could sunset or require reauthorization.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; it simply changes the jurisdictional status of a defined parcel.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no ongoing compliance to surface, so the concept of a third party with standing to detect violations does not map onto a completed land transfer.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: This narrow administrative land action has no systemic or precedential implications extending beyond the single tract described.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the transfer between the United States military reservation and the Territory of Hawaii is involved; no second agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: There is no rule of coverage from which anyone could be exempted; the order simply restores one parcel.

note: Pure one-time land-status restoration; per the mechanical test the compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11) and Flags 6/9 are NOT_APPLICABLE, while the decision-structure flags (1,2,7,10) and Flag 3 are cleanly ABSENT.

title: Terminating the War Food Administration and Transferring Its Functions to the Secretary of Agriculture

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: All functions, powers, records, property, personnel, and funds of the War Food Administrator are consolidated in a single official, the Secretary of Agriculture, who also receives broad power to redelegate and to confer further-delegation authority; authority pools upward into one office with no external check specified.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is clearly and singly assigned to the Secretary of Agriculture, who is a Senate-confirmed, named actor answerable for the transferred functions; there is no diffusion of blame.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order does one coherent thing (wind up a wartime agency and move its functions to an existing department under a single reorganization rationale); no unrelated remedies or frames are packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order is a reorganization transfer, not a conduct rule; the operative directives (functions transfer, existing regulations continue 'unless and until modified or revoked by the Secretary') are precise about who acts, so no enforcement ambiguity arises.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Continuing existing food-program regulations in force under a single accountable Secretary during wartime creates no incentive for any actor to undermine the stated purpose of orderly transfer.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The transferred functions fold into the permanent Department of Agriculture's standing administrative structure, which is maintained through ordinary supersession; the order itself terminates a temporary wartime body rather than creating open-ended crisis authority, so no natural end-state is left unaddressed.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order preserves existing 'orders, directives, rules, and regulations' in effect and removes no judicial, legislative, or administrative check that previously applied.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The transfer imposes no self-reporting compliance regime; existing food-administration rules continue under normal enforcement, so no gap in third-party detection is created by this order.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Absorbing a terminated wartime agency's functions into an existing department is a bounded reorganization; the amendment of 'all prior Executive orders in conflict ... to the extent of such conflict' is routine housekeeping and enables no identifiable precedent beyond the stated purpose.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The War Food Administration is being terminated by the same authority that created it (EO 9334), and its functions are consolidated into Agriculture rather than conflicting with another agency's continuing statutory mandate; no lateral jurisdictional conflict results.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order contains no waiver or carve-out structure exempting any class from coverage; existing regulations simply carry over unchanged.

note: Diff-aware: this terminates the WFA created by the available EO 9334 and consolidates its functions in the Secretary of Agriculture (Flag 1 PRESENT for the upward pooling), but the underlying regulatory relationships continue intact under a clearly accountable Senate-confirmed officer.

title: Amending Executive Order No. 11248, Placing Certain Positions in Levels IV and V of the Federal Executive Salary Schedule

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order merely re-labels one entry in a salary schedule (substituting the EPA Water Quality Office Commissioner for the former Federal Water Pollution Control Administration Commissioner); it concentrates no decision authority in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The action is a self-executing textual substitution in a pay schedule with nothing to implement or fail, so no accountability chain is implicated.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: A single, discrete amendment to one schedule item; there is nothing bundled against it.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Setting a position's pay-schedule level creates no behavioral rule or compliance obligation for anyone, so there is no enforcement mechanism to evaluate for vagueness.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A pay-schedule placement establishes no ongoing regulatory relationship in which any actor could be perversely incentivized.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The amended predecessor EO 11248 is part of a salary-schedule framework maintained through routine supersession and amendment (EO 11248 itself superseded EO 11244/11245), evidencing an ordinary-amendment maintenance pattern rather than frozen open-ended authority.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The substitution removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; it operates under the standing 5 U.S.C. 5317 authority.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance obligation exists to be surfaced by a third party; this is a definitional pay-level edit.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow administrative pay-schedule relabeling has no systemic or precedential consequences beyond the single position renamed.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The change reflects an already-completed reorganization (transfer of the water-pollution function into EPA); it redirects no agency's statutory mandate itself and creates no jurisdictional conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: There is no rule of coverage and thus no exemption structure; the order simply updates one schedule entry.

note: Diff-aware: predecessor EO 11248 (available) is a bare salary-schedule placement; this partial amendment touches only one item's label, so it is assessed independently and is structurally trivial.

title: Amendment of Executive Order No. 10501 of November 5, 1953, Relating to Safeguarding Official Information in the Interests of the Defense of the United States

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The new historical-research access provision vests in each agency head sole, largely unchecked discretion to grant outside access to classified information based on his own determinations that access is 'clearly consistent with the interests of national defense' and the person is 'trustworthy,' with no external review of those determinations.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each provision names the responsible actor (the agency head, who must 'take appropriate steps to assure that classified information is not published or otherwise compromised'), so responsibility for a bad access decision is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The three amendments (declassification reach, historical-research access, and transmission methods for confidential material) all fall under the single coherent frame of refining the EO 10501 classification system; they are severable technical edits, not distinct problems forced together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The historical-research access standard turns on undefined terms ('clearly consistent with the interests of national defense,' 'trustworthy,' 'appropriate steps'), which two agency heads could apply to reach materially different access decisions in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The amendments tighten and clarify handling of classified material; no design feature rewards an actor for behavior that would undermine the safeguarding purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This amends a standing classification framework (EO 10501) that is maintained through ordinary supersession and amendment; the underlying administrative function of information-security is permanent by nature, not an emergency grant needing a reauthorization trigger.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds discretionary access authority but removes no pre-existing external oversight mechanism that previously applied to classified information.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Whether a granted historical-research access improperly compromised classified information is knowable essentially only to the granting agency itself; no external actor is given standing, access, or incentive to surface an improper grant, so enforcement depends on agency self-policing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The amendments are bounded technical refinements to an existing classification regime; no identifiable precedent or definitional framework usable in unrelated contexts is created by the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Each agency head exercises the new discretion over his own agency's originated information; no other agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The historical-research provision is expressly framed as an 'exception to the standard for access,' placing an open-ended, single-actor-controlled carve-out from the access rules in each agency head's discretion with only vague qualifying criteria.

note: Diff-aware: this partial amendment adds a discretionary historical-research access exception to the EO 10501 classification framework; that single exception legitimately fires Flags 1, 4, 8, and 11 on distinct grounds (single-actor discretion, undefined standard, no third-party detection, and an exception from coverage).

title: Placing an Additional Position in Level V of the Federal Executive Salary Schedule

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order adds one position (GSA Commissioner, Property Management and Disposal Service) to a pay schedule; it concentrates no decision authority anywhere.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: A self-executing single-line addition to a salary schedule has no implementation that could fail and thus no accountability chain to break.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: One discrete addition under one authority; nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Assigning a position a pay level imposes no compliance obligation or enforcement mechanism whose clarity could be assessed.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory relationship arises from a pay-schedule placement, so there is no incentive structure to evaluate.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The amended EO 11248 salary-schedule framework is maintained through routine supersession and amendment, evidencing an ordinary-amendment maintenance pattern rather than open-ended frozen authority.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Nothing pre-existing in the oversight architecture is removed; the order acts under standing 5 U.S.C. 5317 authority.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no compliance behavior for any third party to surface; this is a definitional pay-level entry.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Adding one position to a salary schedule has no systemic or precedential effect beyond that position.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No agency's statutory mandate is redirected; the order only sets a pay level for one GSA office.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage rule exists and thus no exemption structure; the order simply adds one schedule item.

note: Partial amendment adding one item to the EO 11248 salary schedule (predecessor available); structurally trivial with only decision-structure and Flag 6/10 flags applicable, all ABSENT.

title: Blocking Government of Haiti Property and Prohibiting Transactions With Haiti

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Treasury is authorized to identify sanctioned Haitian nationals, promulgate rules, 'employ all powers granted to me by' IEEPA and the UN Participation Act, and redelegate freely, concentrating broad discretionary sanctions authority in one cabinet officer; this is a standing feature of IEEPA sanctions architecture rather than a single-official design collapse, so PRESENT not CRITICAL.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is clearly assigned: the Secretary of the Treasury (in consultation with the Secretary of State) administers the program, and all agencies are 'directed to take all appropriate measures within their authority,' so a named actor holds the obligation.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: All provisions (asset blocking, trade prohibitions, and identification of complicit nationals) serve the single stated frame of pressuring the de facto Haitian regime under UNSC Resolution 841; the remedies are mutually reinforcing rather than distinct problems forced together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Coverage of blocked persons turns on undefined thresholds like 'substantial financial or material contributions' and 'doing substantial business,' 'as identified by the Secretary of the Treasury,' which two officials could apply to reach different designation decisions in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The sanctions design aligns incentives with the stated purpose (blocking assets and cutting off supply pressures the regime); no design feature rewards behavior that undermines that purpose. The authority rests on the active, unexpired national emergency declared in EO 12775, not a lapsed statute.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The sanctions are emergency/crisis authority tied to conditions in Haiti but contain no sunset or reauthorization trigger in the order itself; they continue until affirmatively revoked, which is exactly the kind of emergency grant that should track a resolution condition (permanence carve-out does not apply).

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The blocking operates 'notwithstanding the existence of any rights or obligations conferred or imposed by any international agreement or any contract entered into or any license or permit granted before the effective date,' overriding pre-existing contractual and permit-based entitlements, and Sec. 8 states nothing 'shall create any right or benefit ... enforceable by any party,' foreclosing external challenge.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Detection of blocked property held by, or transactions benefiting, the de facto regime depends heavily on U.S. persons' own compliance and Treasury's identification; no external actor is given standing and incentive to surface concealed regime-linked assets, so enforcement leans on self-policing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order extends and layers onto the EO 12775/12779 sanctions framework, and the broad 'employ all powers granted to me by' IEEPA delegation plus vessel/aircraft entry restrictions establish reusable precedent for expanding blocking authority through subsequent orders in this and adjacent contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Treasury and State are assigned complementary lead/consultation roles and other agencies act 'within their authority'; the order coordinates rather than countermands any agency's statutory mandate, so no lateral conflict is created.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Broad licensing discretion runs throughout ('Except to the extent provided in regulations, orders, directives, or licenses which may hereafter be issued'), giving Treasury essentially unbounded, single-actor authority to license exceptions to the blocking with no defined criteria or external check on such grants.

note: Diff-aware/incorporation: this order supplements EO 12779 and partially revokes provisions of EO 12775/12779 (both available), building on their IEEPA sanctions architecture; it exhibits the characteristic sanctions pattern of broad Treasury discretion, licensing carve-outs, and oversight-foreclosing 'no rights' language.

title: Exemption of Edward B. Russ From Compulsory Retirement for Age

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order is a one-time exercise of a statutory presidential power to exempt one named employee from mandatory retirement for a fixed one-year period; no ongoing decision authority is pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The action is complete on issuance (a defined exemption ending February 29, 1940) with nothing to implement or fail, so no accountability chain is implicated.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: A single exemption for a single individual under a single statutory authority; nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time individual retirement exemption creates no ongoing behavioral rule or enforcement mechanism whose clarity could be assessed.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory or compliance relationship arises from a completed individual exemption, so there is no incentive structure to evaluate.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The exemption is expressly time-limited ('for a period of one year ending February 29, 1940'), so it self-terminates rather than persisting until revoked.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; it is a bounded statutory exemption.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no ongoing compliance behavior for any third party to surface; the action is a completed personnel exemption.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: An individual, time-limited retirement exemption has no systemic or precedential effect beyond the named person.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the Department of State employee's status is affected; no other agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Although the order grants an 'exemption,' it is a single completed individual action, not an exemption structure carving a class out of a coverage rule; there is no ongoing rule to which an exemptions architecture could attach.

note: Diff-aware: this further exempts the same individual previously exempted by the available EO 7791, but as a one-time, fixed-term personnel action it is structurally trivial; the compliance-regime flags are NOT_APPLICABLE and the decision-structure flags are ABSENT.

title: Amending Executive Order No. 11174, Establishing the Presidential Service Certificate and the Presidential Service Badge

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Award authority is distributed among the several service Secretaries (Army, Navy, Air Force, Transportation) for their respective personnel; no single official holds concentrated discretionary power over the certificate and badge.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each service Secretary is the named, responsible actor for awards to their own service members, so responsibility is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order coherently renames and reestablishes one recognition scheme (certificate and matching badge); the two are a single integrated program, not distinct problems bundled together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Eligibility criteria are concrete (assignment to the White House for at least one year subsequent to January 20, 1961), and award mechanics are defined, so two officials would apply them consistently.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A service-recognition award tied to a defined assignment period creates no incentive for any actor to undermine the program's purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural/administrative recognition framework (an award program) maintained through ordinary amendment, as evidenced by this very order amending predecessor EO 11174; it has no natural end-state requiring reauthorization pressure.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The amendment removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; it restructures an award program.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Awards flow through the service Secretaries on defined eligibility; there is no self-reporting compliance regime lacking third-party detection, so the flag does not fire.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A ceremonial award-program rename and restructuring has no identifiable systemic or precedential consequences beyond the recognition itself.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order allocates award authority cleanly among the service Secretaries within their respective services with no overlap; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order defines who is eligible for an award but contains no exemption or waiver structure carving anyone out of a coverage rule.

note: Partial amendment renaming/reestablishing the Presidential Service Certificate and Badge (predecessor EO 11174 not needed for comparison as the amended paragraphs are self-contained); architecture is clean across all flags. Signature date shown as May 1, 1968 per the Federal Register series for EO 11407.

title: Administration of Foreign Assistance and Related Functions and Arms Export Controls

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order distributes delegated statutory functions across multiple officials (Secretary of State, Secretary of Defense, AID Administrator, and others), frequently requiring consultation among them; authority is dispersed rather than pooled in one office.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each delegated function is assigned to a specifically named officer, and reserved presidential functions remain with the President, so responsibility for each function is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order is a single coherent delegation-updating instrument amending EO 12163 to reflect current appropriations statutes; the many sub-amendments all serve the one frame of allocating foreign-assistance functions.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The amendments are precise cross-referenced delegations (specific statutory sections to specific officers, many 'in consultation with' a named counterpart); the mechanics are defined, not ambiguous.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Reallocating delegated statutory functions among officials creates no design feature rewarding conduct that would undermine the administration of foreign assistance.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This amends a standing foreign-assistance delegation framework (EO 12163) that is maintained through ordinary supersession and amendment; the underlying administrative function is permanent by nature and updated routinely rather than left as frozen open-ended crisis authority.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order reallocates internal delegations and removes no pre-existing external oversight; several functions are made subject to added consultation requirements, which adds rather than removes checks.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is an internal allocation of delegated statutory authority among executive officers, not a compliance regime dependent on self-reporting by a regulated party, so no third-party detection gap arises.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The amendments track current appropriations statutes with specific citations; they are bounded delegation updates with no identifiable precedent or definitional expansion usable in unrelated contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order delegates presidential statutory functions to agencies and coordinates them through consultation requirements rather than countermanding any agency's own statutory mandate, so no lateral jurisdictional conflict is created.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order allocates functions and carries reserved-function exclusions but contains no discretionary waiver structure exempting a class from an ongoing coverage rule.

note: Diff-aware: this is a detailed partial amendment of the standing EO 12163 foreign-assistance delegation framework (predecessor available); despite its length it is architecturally clean, dispersing functions across named officers with consultation requirements.

title: Exemption of J. Edgar Hoover from Compulsory Retirement for Age

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order is a one-time exercise of a statutory presidential power exempting one named official from mandatory retirement; it pools no ongoing decision authority in any position, and it expressly preserves the Civil Service Commission's delegated authority under EO 10530/10682.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The action is complete on issuance and expressly leaves intact the Commission's authority; there is no implementation chain that could fail without an accountable actor.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: A single exemption for a single individual under a single statutory authority; nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time individual retirement exemption creates no ongoing behavioral rule or enforcement mechanism whose clarity could be assessed.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory or compliance relationship arises from a completed individual exemption, so there is no incentive structure to evaluate.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Unlike a fixed-term exemption, this one exempts Hoover 'for an indefinite period of time' with no review, renewal, or expiration trigger; the exemption is designed to be permanent by default, which is a real absence-of-sunset gap for what is otherwise an exception to a standing age rule.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order expressly disclaims any limit on the Civil Service Commission's delegated authority ('nothing contained in this Order shall be deemed to limit or restrict ... the exercise of such authority by the Civil Service Commission'), so it removes no pre-existing oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no ongoing compliance behavior for any third party to surface; the action is a completed personnel exemption.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: An individual retirement exemption has no systemic or precedential effect on governance architecture beyond the named person's tenure.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the FBI Director's status is affected and the Civil Service Commission's authority is expressly preserved; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Although the order grants an 'exemption,' it is a single completed individual action, not an exemption structure carving a class out of an ongoing coverage rule.

note: Individual retirement exemption; the referenced EO 10530/10682 are cited only to preserve the Commission's delegated authority, not amended. Flag 6 fires (PRESENT) because the exemption is 'indefinite' with no expiration, unlike the fixed-term individual exemptions in this batch.

title: Amending the Foreign Service Regulations of the United States

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order amends a fee tariff by setting fixed dollar amounts for specified consular services; it concentrates no discretionary decision authority in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The fees are self-applying schedule entries administered under existing Foreign Service Regulations; there is no implementation that could fail without an accountable actor.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The several fee amendments all fall under the single frame of updating the consular fee tariff; they are severable schedule edits, not distinct problems forced together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Because the amendments set precise fixed dollar amounts (e.g., '$10.00' for a Chinese certificate, 'No fee' for limited entry certificates), two officials would apply them identically; the standard is fully determinate.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Fixed fee schedules for defined services create no incentive for any actor to undermine the purpose of the fee regulation.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This amends a standing procedural framework (the Foreign Service fee tariff) maintained through ordinary amendment, as this very order demonstrates; the administrative function is permanent by nature, not open-ended crisis authority.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The amendment removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; it revises fee amounts under existing regulatory authority.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Fee collection for services rendered is inherently observable at the point of service and does not depend on regulated-party self-reporting, so there is no third-party detection gap.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Adjusting specific consular fee amounts has no identifiable systemic or precedential consequences beyond the fees themselves.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the State Department's own fee tariff is amended; no other agency's statutory mandate is implicated or redirected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order contains bounded, defined fee exceptions (e.g., 'No fee' for documents authorized to be furnished without charge), but these are narrow, specific rules rather than a discretionary carve-out structure that swallows the fee rule.

note: Ongoing fee-administration relationship exists (fees charged to applicants), so the compliance-regime flags are applicable and scored ABSENT rather than NOT_APPLICABLE; architecture is clean across all eleven.

title: Delegation of Functions Relating to the Implementation of the Inter-American Convention on Letters Rogatory and Additional Protocol

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order designates the Department of Justice as the treaty Central Authority to transmit and process letters rogatory; this is a ministerial designation implementing a ratified treaty, not a pooling of discretionary decision authority over eligibility, enforcement, or interpretation.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Department of Justice is the single named, responsible actor for performing the Convention's central-authority functions, so responsibility is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order does one thing (designate a central authority and allow related additional designations) under one frame; nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: DOJ is directed to proceed 'in conformity with applicable provisions of the Convention and Additional Protocol,' which supply the operative standards; the delegation itself introduces no ambiguous enforcement trigger or consequence.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Designating a treaty central authority creates no design feature rewarding conduct that would undermine the treaty's implementation.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The designation implements a ratified, standing treaty obligation that is evergreen by nature (the central-authority function persists as long as the treaty is in force); no reauthorization-pressure end-state applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; it establishes a treaty-implementing designation under existing authority.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The letters-rogatory process is inherently bilateral and party-driven (foreign and domestic litigants and courts initiate and observe the transmittals), so surfacing of non-performance does not depend on regulated-party self-reporting.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A ministerial designation to give effect to a specific ratified treaty has no identifiable precedent or definitional consequence usable in unrelated contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: DOJ is designated for a function the treaty requires be assigned to a central authority; the order does not redirect or countermand any other agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order contains no exemption or waiver structure; it is a straightforward designation implementing treaty obligations.

note: Ministerial treaty-implementation designation; an ongoing processing relationship exists (so compliance-regime flags are applicable), but all are cleanly ABSENT because the treaty supplies the operative standards.

title: Ethics Commitments by Executive Branch Appointees

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The President alone may waive any pledge restriction whenever he 'certifies in writing that it is in the public interest,' concentrating unbounded waiver authority in one office; this coexists with a distributed enforcement structure, so PRESENT rather than CRITICAL.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is clearly assigned throughout: agency heads administer the pledges, the Director of the Office of Government Ethics develops forms and guidance, and the Attorney General investigates and litigates breaches, so named actors hold both obligation and capacity to correct failures.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order addresses a single coherent frame (post-employment ethics commitments by appointees) with mutually related provisions (pledges, definitions, waivers, administration, enforcement); no distinct unrelated problem is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Enforcement is precisely defined: debarment proceedings 'after notice and hearing,' civil actions in U.S. District Court, defined relief, and procedures conforming to existing 5 C.F.R. 2637.212, with the Attorney General directed to publish a 'Statement of Covered Activities'; two officials would apply these consistently.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The pledge design aligns appointee incentives with the anti-revolving-door purpose (breach triggers debarment and disgorgement via a constructive trust); no feature rewards conduct undermining the purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is a standing ethics-administration framework, evergreen by the nature of the anti-corruption mandate and maintained through ordinary supersession (Sec. 6 addresses interaction with prior/future orders); no emergency or temporary-program end-state applies that reauthorization pressure should track.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds oversight and enforcement machinery (agency procedures, OGE role, AG enforcement, published waiver certifications) rather than removing any pre-existing external check; per the direction rule, new machinery does not fire this flag.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Detection of a former appointee's prohibited lobbying or foreign representation depends largely on the Attorney General 'receiving information regarding the possible breach'; the order creates no external actor with standing, access, and incentive to systematically surface violations, so enforcement leans on incidental disclosure.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order is bounded to executive-branch appointees' post-employment conduct and creates no authority claim, definition, or precedent identifiable from the text as extending its reach into unrelated contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Roles are allocated cleanly (agency heads, OGE, White House Counsel for EOP, Attorney General) without redirecting or countermanding any agency's existing statutory mandate; the design coordinates rather than conflicts.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The Sec. 3 waiver lets the President exempt any person from any restriction on a bare 'public interest' certification with no defined limiting criteria, and Sec. 2(c) enumerates broad categories of communication excluded from 'lobby'; the single-actor, criteria-free waiver is an exemption structure concentrated in one actor. Publication in the Federal Register mitigates but does not bound the discretion, so PRESENT not CRITICAL.

note: Structurally well-built ethics order with clear enforcement and accountability; the firing flags are the criteria-free presidential waiver (Flags 1 and 11) and reliance on incidental breach reporting (Flag 8).

title: Directing the Civil Service Commission to Resume Operations Under the Civil Service Rules, and Authorizing the Adoption of Special Regulations During the Transitional Period

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Substantial discretionary authority over the transition is pooled in the Civil Service Commission, which may establish boards, issue transitional regulations, extend war-service appointments 'in its discretion,' except positions from the competitive service, and confer competitive status; this is broad single-body discretion, though bounded by the Veterans' Preference Act and reporting duties, so PRESENT not CRITICAL.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Commission is the single named, responsible actor for the transition and must submit recommendations to the President by defined dates (e.g., 'no later than July 1, 1946'), so responsibility is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: All provisions serve one coherent frame (transitioning federal hiring from wartime to normal competitive procedures while implementing veterans' preference); they are integrated steps of a single reconversion, not distinct unrelated problems forced together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Key operative directives use undefined temporal standards ('as rapidly as its resources will permit,' 'as soon as practicable') and discretionary extension authority without metric or deadline, so two administrators could reasonably differ on compliance timing.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order's design (mandatory competitive examination, ordered veterans' preference, and removal remedy where the selection order 'has not been followed') aligns incentives with restoring merit-based competition; no feature rewards conduct undermining that purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The transitional authority is self-limiting with concrete dates (revision recommendations 'before July 1, 1946'; Schedule A exceptions expiring 'after December 31, 1946' unless included); the order builds toward restoring the permanent Civil Service Rules rather than granting open-ended authority, and it revokes the prior wartime orders.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order restores operation of the standing Civil Service Rules and Regulations and revokes the wartime orders that had suspended normal competition; it thus reinstates rather than removes pre-existing oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Compliance with the mandatory selection order in temporary appointments depends on an affected person 'establish[ing] before the Commission' that the order was not followed, and on agency self-reporting via records the Commission requires; a non-selected veteran must detect and prove the violation, and no external actor with independent access is given incentive to surface breaches systematically.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order manages a bounded, dated reconversion of federal hiring; its provisions are tied to the postwar transition and establish no authority claim or definition identifiable from the text as reusable in unrelated contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Commission acts 'after consulting the departments and agencies concerned' and the boards it may establish are staffed by those agencies' own officers under Commission supervision; the order coordinates with agencies rather than countermanding any agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Section 7 lets the Commission, on agency request, except positions or classes of positions from the competitive service and place them under Schedule A whenever it 'determines ... in the public interest,' a broad, single-body discretionary carve-out from competitive coverage with only a public-interest standard, though time-bounded to December 31, 1946, so PRESENT not CRITICAL.

note: Diff-aware: revokes the available EO 9063 (plus EOs 9378/8514, which are not in referenced-predecessors, so that portion of the comparison could not be made) to restore normal competitive hiring; broad but dated Commission discretion drives Flags 1, 4, 8, and 11.

title: Administration of the Agricultural Trade Development and Assistance Act of 1954

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Act's functions are dispersed across many officials (Secretaries of Agriculture, State, Treasury, Defense, the FOA Director, the Budget Director, and ODM Director), with State given a foreign-policy overlay and reports to Congress reserved to the President; authority is distributed, not pooled.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each delegated function is assigned to a specifically named officer or agency, so responsibility for each is clearly located; the President retains the reporting-to-Congress function.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order is a single coherent delegation instrument allocating the various functions of one statute (the Agricultural Trade Development and Assistance Act) among agencies; the provisions all serve that one frame.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The delegations are precise, tying named officers to specific lettered subsections of section 104 and other identified statutory functions; the mechanics are defined rather than ambiguous.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Allocating statutory functions among agencies for administering the food-aid program creates no design feature rewarding conduct that would undermine the Act's purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is a standing administrative delegation implementing an ongoing statute, maintained through ordinary supersession; the underlying program-administration function is permanent by nature rather than an emergency grant needing a reauthorization trigger.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order preserves congressional oversight by expressly reserving the section 108 reporting-to-Congress function to the President and adds inter-agency coordination via the incorporated EO 10476 Part III; it removes no pre-existing external check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is an internal allocation of statutory administration among executive agencies with reserved congressional reporting, not a compliance regime dependent on a regulated party's self-reporting, so no third-party detection gap arises.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order allocates administration of one identified statute; its delegations are bounded to that Act and establish no authority claim or definition identifiable from the text as reusable in unrelated contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order carefully partitions functions among agencies and subordinates them to State's foreign-policy responsibilities and the incorporated EO 10476 Part III coordination procedures (which include a defined referral mechanism to reconcile inter-agency disputes), so it structures coordination rather than creating mandate conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order contains no exemption or waiver structure carving a class out of coverage; it is a functional delegation with a statutory waiver of one appropriations-section applicability assigned to a named officer.

note: Incorporation-by-reference: Sec. 3(c) extends Part III of EO 10476 (available) to these functions; that Part supplies a defined coordination-and-referral mechanism (Secs. 301-302), which reinforces the ABSENT finding on Flag 10 rather than firing it.

title: Amending the Selective Service Regulations

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authority remains distributed across the existing Selective Service structure (local boards, appeal boards, State Directors, the national Director, and the Army Surgeon General for medical lists); the amendments adjust procedures without pooling decision authority into a single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each amended procedure names the responsible actor (the local board mails questionnaires, the medical advisor makes findings, the State Director forwards records), so responsibility for each step is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: All amendments fall under the single coherent frame of updating the Selective Service classification and processing regulations; they are severable regulatory edits to one scheme, not distinct unrelated problems forced together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The amended rules set concrete triggers and criteria (e.g., ordered mailing 'in strict accordance with the dates of birth ... in chronological order,' defined 'child'/'physician' terms, and a Surgeon-General-prescribed disqualifying-conditions list applied by a medical advisor); two officials would apply them consistently.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The procedural amendments (deferment definitions, medical-interview screening, quota ordering) do not create design features rewarding conduct that would undermine the classification system's purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This amends a standing procedural framework (the Selective Service Regulations under a continuing statute) maintained through repeated ordinary amendment, as the long list of amending predecessor orders shows; the administrative function is permanent by nature rather than open-ended crisis authority.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The amendments preserve the existing multi-level review architecture, including appeal to appeal boards and reopening on request of State or national Directors; no pre-existing oversight or appeal mechanism is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The classification process gives affected registrants and dependents standing to appeal (Part 1626 appeal rights are preserved and amended), and multi-level board and State Director review provides external checks on individual determinations, so enforcement does not rest on regulated-party self-reporting.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The amendments are bounded procedural adjustments to an existing regulatory chapter; no authority claim, definition, or precedent identifiable from the text extends into unrelated contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The only cross-agency touchpoint is the Army Surgeon General prescribing the medical-disqualification list, a defined supporting role within the existing scheme; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The amendments refine deferment/classification categories (e.g., Class III-A parenthood) with defined criteria rather than creating a broad discretionary waiver structure that swallows coverage; classification categories are bounded and rule-based.

note: Partial amendment of the standing Selective Service Regulations; the many cited predecessor orders (EO 10001 available, others such as 10008/10202 not needed since amended sections are self-contained), and the architecture is clean and rule-based throughout.

title: Authorizing the Secretary of War to Take Possession of and Operate the Properties of the Cleveland Electric Illuminating Company, Located in and Around Cleveland, Ohio

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order vests in a single official, the Secretary of War, sole authority to seize and 'operate such properties in such manner as he deems necessary,' and to determine when possession is no longer necessary; substantial discretionary control over a private company is pooled in one officer with no external check specified, though tied to an active wartime statute, so PRESENT not CRITICAL.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of War is the single named, responsible actor for the seizure and operation, so responsibility is clearly located even though his discretion is broad.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order does one thing (authorize seizure and operation of one company's properties for war production) under one frame; nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The operative standard is entirely open-ended: the Secretary may operate the properties 'in such manner as he deems necessary for the successful prosecution of the War' and himself determines when possession is 'no longer necessary,' a self-judged standard two officials could apply very differently.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The seizure is a one-time wartime takeover to maintain production; the design does not reward any actor for behavior undermining that purpose. Authority rests on the active, unexpired Selective Training and Service Act of 1940 wartime power, not a lapsed statute, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Despite being an emergency/wartime seizure, the order contains an explicit termination trigger tied to the emergency's resolution: 'Possession ... shall be terminated within sixty days after the Secretary of War determines that possession is no longer necessary,' so the natural end-state is addressed.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes no pre-existing external oversight mechanism; it exercises a specific statutory seizure power under Section 9 of the Selective Training and Service Act.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is a direct government takeover and operation of a specific company, not a compliance regime imposing conduct rules on a regulated party whose violations a third party would surface, so the concept does not map.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order exercises and reinforces the executive's asserted authority to seize and operate private industrial property by fiat under wartime statute; that authority claim establishes precedent usable to justify further seizures of private enterprises, an effect identifiable from the broad seizure-and-operate power in the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the Secretary of War is directed to act on one company's properties; no other agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded, so no lateral jurisdictional conflict is created.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order imposes no coverage rule from which any class could be exempted; it is a single-target seizure authorization, so an exemptions architecture does not apply.

note: Wartime plant-seizure order with broad self-judged operating discretion (Flags 1, 4, 9); Flag 6 is ABSENT because of the explicit 60-day termination trigger, and the Zombie Trap does not fire since the underlying wartime statute was active and unexpired.

title: Inspection of Income, Excess-Profits, Estate, and Gift Tax Returns by the Committee on Internal Security, House of Representatives

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order authorizes a congressional committee to inspect specified tax returns for a defined purpose; it grants no discretionary decision authority to a single executive official and channels access through existing Treasury rules.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Access is tied to a named body (the House Committee on Internal Security), a defined statutory purpose, and compliance with Treasury Decisions 6132 and 6133, so responsibility for proper handling is located within an existing rule framework.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order does one thing (open specified returns to one committee for its authorized investigations) under one frame; nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Access is bounded by defined parameters (returns for 1950-1969, during the Ninety-first Congress, for the committee's authorized investigations) and made subject to 'the rules and regulations prescribed by the Secretary of the Treasury in Treasury Decisions 6132 and 6133,' supplying a determinate standard.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Granting a committee broad access to citizens' confidential tax returns for 'investigations authorized by clause 11 of Rule XI' creates an incentive to use tax data for purposes beyond legitimate oversight; the design gives the accessing body reason to expand inquiry using sensitive information it would not otherwise obtain, cutting against the confidentiality baseline of the tax system.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The authority is inherently time-bound, limited to inspection 'during the Ninety-first Congress' and to returns for defined years, so it lapses with that Congress rather than persisting indefinitely.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order operates within and expressly conditions access on existing Treasury inspection rules (TD 6132/6133); it removes no pre-existing oversight or safeguard, so the flag does not fire on direction.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The taxpayers whose confidential returns are opened have no notice of, standing over, or means to detect misuse of the access, and no external actor is given incentive to surface improper handling of the inspected returns; oversight of the committee's use depends on the committee and Treasury themselves.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Opening confidential tax returns to a congressional investigative committee normalizes and establishes precedent for using citizens' tax data as a tool of political investigation, an effect reaching beyond the stated inspection purpose and identifiable from the breadth of the access granted (all returns 1950-1969).

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order coordinates Treasury (which controls the inspection rules) with a House committee's access; it does not redirect or countermand any agency's statutory mandate, so no lateral conflict is created.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order defines a scope of accessible returns rather than creating a discretionary waiver or carve-out structure; there is no exemption architecture that could swallow a coverage rule.

note: Tax-return-inspection order creating an ongoing access relationship, so compliance-regime flags apply; the confidentiality-eroding access to citizens' returns drives the Flag 5, 8, and 9 findings even though the access is time-bounded to one Congress.

title: Waiver of the Time Limitations in Civil Service Rule IX To Permit the Reinstatement of Mrs. Martha Overstreet to a Clerical Position in the Post Office at Douglas, Georgia

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order is a one-time statutory waiver permitting the reinstatement of one named individual; it pools no ongoing decision authority in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The action is complete on issuance (a single permitted reinstatement) with nothing to implement or fail, so no accountability chain is implicated.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: A single waiver for a single individual under a single statutory authority; nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time individual reinstatement waiver creates no ongoing behavioral rule or enforcement mechanism whose clarity could be assessed.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory or compliance relationship arises from a completed individual waiver, so there is no incentive structure to evaluate.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing waiver grants no continuing authority, so there is nothing that could sunset or require reauthorization.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; it is a bounded statutory waiver of a rule's time limitation for one reinstatement.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no ongoing compliance behavior for any third party to surface; the action is a completed individual personnel waiver.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: An individual reinstatement waiver has no systemic or precedential effect on governance architecture beyond the named person.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only one post-office position is affected; no other agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Although the order 'waives' a time limitation, it is a single completed individual action, not an exemption structure carving a class out of an ongoing coverage rule.

note: Pure one-time individual reinstatement waiver; per the mechanical test the compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11) and Flags 6/9 are NOT_APPLICABLE, and the decision-structure flags are cleanly ABSENT.

title: Prohibiting New Investment in Burma

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authority to issue rules, licenses, and to 'employ all powers granted to me' under IEEPA is pooled in the Secretary of the Treasury (Sec. 6), who may redelegate at will, with the Secretary of State holding certification functions (Sec. 5); the President's own determination that Burma poses an 'unusual and extraordinary threat' is self-made. No external check is created within the order.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Sec. 7 states the order creates 'no right or benefit... enforceable by any party,' and if the sanctions regime fails to achieve its purpose no named actor bears responsibility beyond the implementing Treasury Secretary who also administers it. Failure-chain responsibility is diffuse.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order addresses a single problem frame (new investment in Burma tied to repression of the democratic opposition) with one coherent remedy; no distinct unrelated remedies or problem frames are packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Core prohibitions on 'new investment' and 'economic development of resources' are defined with detail (Sec. 4), but the anti-evasion clause in Sec. 2(b) reaching any transaction with 'the purpose of evading or avoiding' the prohibitions gives implementing officials broad, standardless discretion over what counts as evasion.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The prohibition structure does not reward the regulated party for non-compliance or reward the agency for failure; the incentive it creates (avoid prohibited investment) aligns with the stated purpose. The order invokes an active, unexpired national-emergency declaration and IEEPA, not a lapsed statute.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The prohibition is grounded in a declared national emergency, exactly the kind of crisis-response authority that should have a natural end tied to the emergency's resolution, yet the order sets no expiration or reauthorization trigger of its own (relying on the separate NEA annual-continuation mechanism is not built into the text).

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order does not remove any pre-existing oversight mechanism; the 'no rights created' clause (Sec. 7) limits private enforcement but is a standard non-justiciability clause that does not strip a check that previously operated over this subject.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement depends on Treasury detecting prohibited investments and facilitation by U.S. persons; no external actor with standing and incentive to surface violations (e.g., a private right of action) exists, and Sec. 7 forecloses it, leaving detection to the agency alone.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order's self-certification of an 'unusual and extraordinary threat' and use of IEEPA to bar an entire class of investment establishes precedent for economy-wide sanctions imposed by executive determination, a framework readily transferable to other countries and contexts beyond Burma.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order allocates functions between Treasury and State along their conventional sanctions roles and directs all agencies to assist; it does not countermand or redirect another agency's existing statutory mandate in a conflicting way.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Sec. 1 and Sec. 2 permit exceptions 'to the extent provided in regulations, orders, directives, or licenses,' and Sec. 3 exempts contracts for goods/services/technology with carve-outs; the licensing authority vested in Treasury to grant exceptions is broad and discretionary, though bounded by the defined scope of 'new investment.'

note: An IEEPA sanctions order invoking an active national emergency; broad delegated licensing/rulemaking discretion drives several PRESENT findings, none rising to CRITICAL since the prohibition core is clearly defined and achievable.

title: Amendment of the Regulations Relating to the Safeguarding of Vessels, Harbors, Ports, and Waterfront Facilities of the United States

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: This partial amendment (checked against EO 10173, which it amends) rewrites §6.10-1 so employment on a merchant vessel is barred unless 'the Commandant is satisfied' the person is not inimical to security, and lets the Commandant designate exempt categories; decision authority over an individual's employment eligibility rests solely with the Commandant with no external check named in the amended provision.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Commandant is the single named responsible actor for the eligibility determination, so responsibility for the amended provision is clearly located rather than diffuse; the broader EO 10173 framework also provides an appeals-board mechanism (§6.10-9) that continues to operate.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order amends a single regulatory section on one subject (employment-document eligibility); there is nothing bundled and no multiple problem frames.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The amended standard turns on whether 'the character and habits of life of such person are such as to authorize the belief that the presence of the individual on board would not be inimical to the security of the United States' — a subjective belief standard under which two officials could reach different good-faith determinations, and the shift to 'would not be' places the burden of clearance on the applicant.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The eligibility screen does not reward non-compliance or agency failure; no perverse behavioral incentive is created by the amended provision. Authority derives from an active security statute, not a lapsed one.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural framework for merchant-vessel employment screening, maintained through ordinary supersession (this order itself amends a numbered predecessor, EO 10173 as amended by EO 10277), so under the standing-procedural-framework carve-out the absence of a sunset does not fire.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The amendment does not remove any pre-existing oversight; the EO 10173 appeals-board review (§6.10-9) remains in place and is not touched by this amendment.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The screening is a government-run eligibility determination rather than a self-reporting compliance regime; the individual seeking employment has direct incentive to establish eligibility and an appeals right exists, so no third-party incentive gap in the sense the flag catches.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: As a narrow amendment to one word-choice-level standard within an existing screening regulation, it produces no meaningful precedent or systemic effect beyond its stated port-security purpose.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the Coast Guard/Commandant is implicated; no second agency's mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The proviso lets 'the Commandant... designate categories of merchant vessels to which the foregoing shall not apply,' concentrating unbounded discretion to exempt entire vessel categories from the security screen in a single official with no stated criteria or external check.

note: Partial amendment of §6.10-1 of EO 10173 (predecessor text available); compared against the baseline, this version shifts the eligibility default toward requiring affirmative clearance and adds a category-exemption proviso, driving the Flag 1/4/11 findings.

title: Designating an Additional Agency Pursuant to Section 103(a) of the Renegotiation Act of 1951

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order merely designates the Bonneville Power Administration as a 'Department' for Renegotiation Act purposes pursuant to a statutory designation power; no discretionary decision authority is pooled in any official — the designation is essentially self-executing and clean.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The designation carries the Renegotiation Act's existing statutory accountability structure; no new failure chain with diffuse responsibility is created by the order's single act.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single-purpose designation of one agency under one statute; nothing is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order creates no ongoing enforcement standard of its own; it triggers application of the Renegotiation Act's pre-existing statutory renegotiation regime to BPA contracts, so there is no order-created compliance rule to assess for vagueness.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A one-time designation extending an existing statute to an agency creates no new behavioral incentive for any regulated actor; the flag's ongoing-conduct precondition is absent. Statute (Renegotiation Act of 1951) is active, not lapsed.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The designation is a one-time, self-executing act with no ongoing grant of discretionary authority to sunset; its effect flows entirely from the Renegotiation Act, which has its own terms.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed, limited, or bypassed; the order adds BPA to a statutory renegotiation regime rather than stripping any check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The order itself establishes no compliance/self-reporting regime; the renegotiation of profits is governed by the statute, so there is no order-level third-party enforcement chain to evaluate.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Designating one power administration as a covered 'Department' is a narrow administrative act with no identifiable precedent or systemic consequence beyond applying an existing statute.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: BPA's contracts become subject to renegotiation, but no second agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded; the designation operates within the Renegotiation Act's own allocation.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order defines coverage inward (bringing BPA into the Act) rather than carving actors out; it establishes no exemption structure of its own.

note: Narrow statutory-designation order; the four compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11) plus Flag 6 are NOT_APPLICABLE because the order creates no ongoing conduct rule of its own, applying an existing statute to one agency. All applicable flags are cleanly ABSENT.

title: Leadership and Coordination of Nondiscrimination Laws

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order delegates the President's approval functions under §602 CRA and §902 Education Amendments to the Attorney General and makes agency implementing directives 'subject to the approval of the Attorney General' (1-402), concentrating coordination, standard-setting, and approval authority over government-wide nondiscrimination enforcement in one official; the check (annual report to the President via OMB) runs back to the appointing authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is clearly located: the Attorney General is the single named coordinator required to report annually to the President (1-304) and each agency retains its own implementation duty (1-402), so the failure chain has identified actors rather than diffuse blame.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order addresses one coherent frame — coordinating enforcement of federal nondiscrimination laws — and its provisions all serve that single purpose; nothing unrelated is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The AG is directed to develop standards, guidelines, and 'reasonable time limits' for securing voluntary compliance and initiating sanctions (1-203, 1-204), but the order itself supplies no substantive standard for when enforcement is triggered or what noncompliance requires, leaving broad good-faith variance in application pending the AG's future guidelines.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The coordination structure does not reward regulated agencies for non-compliance or reward the AG for failure; incentives align with consistent enforcement. Authority rests on active civil-rights statutes, not a lapsed one.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This establishes a permanent administrative coordination structure for an evergreen anti-discrimination mandate with no natural end-state; under the permanence carve-out (rights-mandate/standing-structure), absence of a sunset does not fire, and it includes an annual review-and-report cycle (1-304).

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order revokes EO 11764 and EO 11914 but preserves their regulations in effect (1-501, 1-502) and adds coordination machinery; it does not remove judicial review, IG access, or other pre-existing external checks — it consolidates and adds oversight rather than stripping it.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement of nondiscrimination in federally assisted programs turns substantially on agency self-review and voluntary compliance efforts (1-204, 1-402), and the order supplies no mechanism giving an external actor with standing and information the incentive to surface violations beyond the agencies themselves and DOJ referral.
