flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Centralizing approval authority over all agencies' nondiscrimination rules in the Attorney General establishes an institutional precedent for government-wide substantive-review control by a single department, a structure transferable to coordinate other cross-cutting statutory mandates beyond civil rights.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order subjects every executive agency's implementing directives to Attorney General approval (1-402) and requires agencies to furnish reports 'unless prohibited by law' (1-401), overriding each agency's independent authority to issue its own nondiscrimination regulations and creating a hierarchy that redirects existing agency rulemaking discretion to Justice.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order broadens coverage (1-201(d) sweeps in 'any other provision of Federal statutory law') and its only carve-out — that the AG's authority does not extend to equal-employment provisions (1-503) — is a narrow, defined jurisdictional line, not a discretionary exemption swallowing the rule.

note: Coordination order concentrating cross-agency approval authority in the Attorney General; the Flag 10 cannibalization and Flag 1 concentration findings stem from the same approval-power feature, scored independently per the correlated-flags rule.

title: Creating a Board of Inquiry to Report on a Labor Dispute Affecting the Bituminous Coal Industry of the United States

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order creates a fact-finding Board of Inquiry that only reports to the President under §206 of the Labor Management Relations Act; the Board holds no operative decision authority over the parties, so no authority is pooled — the President retains appointment and termination but exercises a statutorily channeled fact-finding step.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is clearly located in the President (who appoints and receives the report) and the Board (which reports by a date certain); the statutory §206 process defines the accountability chain.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose — inquiry into one labor dispute — with no bundled remedies or multiple problem frames.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Board only investigates and reports; it enforces nothing and imposes no conduct rule on any party, so there is no enforcement mechanism to assess for vagueness.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A fact-finding board creates no ongoing behavioral rule or incentive for regulated parties; the flag's ongoing-conduct precondition is absent. The Labor Management Relations Act invoked is active, not lapsed.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The Board has an explicit termination mechanism — it reports by April 5, 1948 and 'shall continue in existence... until the Board is terminated by the President' — providing a defined wind-down rather than open-ended authority.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds an investigatory body operating under statutory §206 procedures; it removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The order establishes no compliance/enforcement regime dependent on self-reporting; it is a one-off investigatory step, so there is no third-party enforcement chain to evaluate.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Convening a statutory board of inquiry for a single dispute is a routine, narrowly bounded use of the Taft-Hartley emergency-dispute procedure with no identifiable precedent-setting or systemic downstream effect from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Board is a new ad hoc body reporting to the President; it does not redirect or countermand any existing agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order imposes no coverage rule from which anyone could be exempted; a fact-finding board has no population of coverage to carve out.

note: Statutory Taft-Hartley board-of-inquiry order; compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11) are NOT_APPLICABLE because the board only investigates and reports, creating no ongoing conduct rule. All applicable flags clean, including an explicit termination trigger clearing Flag 6.

title: Designating the Chairman of the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order is a single personnel designation of a named individual as Federal Reserve Board Chairman for a fixed four-year term under a specific statutory authority; it exercises an appointment power, not a pooling of decision authority over eligibility, enforcement, or interpretation.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The designation is a discrete act with a clearly identified actor (the President) and appointee; there is no ongoing implementation whose failure would leave responsibility diffuse.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: One appointment, one purpose; nothing bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A personnel designation imposes no enforcement standard or compliance rule on anyone, so there is nothing to assess for enforcement vagueness.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Appointing an individual to a statutory office creates no ongoing behavioral rule or incentive for a regulated class; the flag's precondition is absent. Statute is active, not lapsed.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The designation is expressly time-bounded — 'a term of four years effective February 1, 1944' — so it carries a built-in expiration rather than open-ended authority.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed or bypassed by a designation made under the express statutory appointment authority.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no compliance or enforcement regime; a designation creates no self-reporting structure and thus no third-party enforcement chain to evaluate.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A routine statutory appointment of a named individual has no systemic or precedential architecture beyond filling the office; this is a narrow administrative instrument with no downstream structural implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the Federal Reserve Board is implicated, and only to fill its chairmanship; no second agency's mandate is affected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order sets no coverage rule from which anyone is exempted; a personnel designation has no exemption structure.

note: Pure personnel-designation order with a fixed four-year term; Flags 4,5,8,9,10,11 are NOT_APPLICABLE as no compliance regime, systemic effect, or coverage rule exists. The fixed term cleanly clears Flag 6 (ABSENT).

title: Providing for the Restoration of Law and Order in the Washington Metropolitan Area

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order vests the Secretary of Defense with authority to deploy the Armed Forces, call National Guard units into federal service 'for an indefinite period,' and exercise all the President's D.C.-militia commander-in-chief powers 'as he may deem necessary' (Secs. 2, 3), pooling essentially unchecked domestic military-deployment discretion in one official; the discretionary 'as he may deem' standard with delegation power (Sec. 4) makes the concentration a core design feature.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: While the Secretary of Defense is the named actor, the order provides no standard, review, or reporting mechanism by which responsibility for excessive or improper use of force in restoring 'law and order' could be assigned; if implementation harms occur, the failure chain has no defined corrective actor beyond the discretionary Secretary himself.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single frame (domestic disorder in the Washington area) with one coherent remedy (military restoration of order); nothing unrelated is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The operative standard — 'take all appropriate steps' and use forces 'as he may deem necessary' to 'restore law and order' and protect property, personnel, and functions (Secs. 1-2) — supplies no defined trigger, limit, or standard of conduct, so implementing officers have essentially unbounded good-faith discretion over the scope of force.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The emergency deployment structure does not reward the regulated actors or the agency for failure; no perverse behavioral incentive is identifiable in the text. Authority rests on active statutes (10 U.S.C. Ch. 15) and a same-day proclamation, not a lapsed statute.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is emergency/crisis-response authority with a natural end-state (restoration of order), yet the order sets no expiration, and it authorizes calling Guard units 'for an indefinite period... until relieved by appropriate orders,' leaving the extraordinary authority to persist until the Secretary chooses to relieve it rather than lapsing with the emergency.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order does not remove any pre-existing oversight; it invokes the statutory insurrection/domestic-violence framework and adds no exclusion of judicial or other review.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The order is a one-time emergency military-deployment directive, not an ongoing regulatory-compliance regime; there is no class of regulated parties whose violations a third party would surface, so the flag's precondition is absent.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Authorizing indefinite domestic use of federalized military and Guard forces under an 'as he may deem necessary' standard establishes precedent for broad executive military deployment against civil disorder, a framework readily invoked in future domestic-unrest contexts beyond this single event.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order directs the Secretary of Defense and federalizes Guard units within the statutory structure; it does not countermand or redirect another agency's conflicting statutory mandate — the D.C. militia authority is expressly the President's to delegate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order imposes no coverage rule on a regulated population from which exemptions could be granted; a military-deployment directive has no exemption architecture to map.

note: Emergency domestic military-deployment order; Flag 1 rated CRITICAL because indefinite, standardless deployment discretion pooled in the Defense Secretary is a core concentration. Flags 8 and 11 NOT_APPLICABLE as no ongoing compliance regime or coverage population exists.

title: Regulations Relating to the Right of Members of the Uniformed Services to Incentive Pay for the Performance of Hazardous Duty Required by Competent Orders

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Implementation authority is distributed to 'the Secretary concerned' across the several services under detailed, statutorily grounded rules; no single official holds pooled discretionary authority over eligibility or interpretation — the framework spreads administration and requires uniformity 'to the fullest extent practicable' (Secs. 3(b), 12).

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility for administering incentive-pay determinations rests clearly with each Secretary concerned and named certifying commanding officers; the failure chain has identified actors.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Though it covers several hazardous-duty categories (flight, submarine, parachute, demolition, leprosy duty), all serve the single coherent purpose of implementing statutory hazardous-duty incentive pay; this is comprehensive detail, not bundling of unrelated policy questions.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The eligibility standards are highly specific — precise minimum flight-hour thresholds, defined terms, and quantified requirements (Secs. 4, 7, 8) — so two officials applying them would reach consistent determinations; enforcement/eligibility criteria are clearly defined.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The pay structure rewards actual performance of hazardous duty consistent with its purpose; the certification-and-carryover provisions guard against gaming, and no incentive to undermine the purpose is created. Statute (Career Compensation Act of 1949) is active, not lapsed.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural/administrative framework implementing a permanent statutory pay entitlement with no natural end-state, maintained through ordinary regulatory supersession; under the standing-procedural-framework carve-out, absence of a sunset does not fire.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order implements statutory pay provisions and removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; supplementary service regulations must be 'not inconsistent herewith' (Sec. 12).

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Eligibility turns on documented, order-directed duty and commanding-officer certification rather than unverifiable self-reporting; the service member has direct incentive to establish eligibility and the certifying chain provides internal verification, so no third-party incentive gap in the flag's sense.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A detailed implementation of a specific statutory pay entitlement produces no identifiable precedent or systemic effect transferable beyond its hazardous-duty-pay purpose.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order operates within the military services under the President's commander-in-chief authority and directs uniformity across them; it does not redirect or countermand another agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order's carve-outs (e.g., reduced flight requirements when operations or equipment prevent compliance, Sec. 4(a)(6)) are narrow, condition-specific accommodations serving the statute's purpose, not broad discretionary exemptions that swallow the rule.

note: A long but architecturally clean implementing regulation for statutory hazardous-duty incentive pay; length reflects specificity, and every flag is answerable and ABSENT — precise criteria and distributed administration keep it at zero.

title: Termination of the Wage and Price Regulatory Program

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order revokes EO 12092 and authorizes each agency head to wind down actions taken under it (Secs. 1-2); it disperses termination authority to the existing agency heads rather than pooling any new decision authority in a single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each executive-agency and military-department head is the named actor authorized to terminate their own program actions, so responsibility for the wind-down is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose — terminating the wage-and-price program — with no bundled remedies or multiple frames.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order removes a regulatory program rather than imposing any ongoing conduct standard; there is no enforcement mechanism created to assess for vagueness (it dismantles, rather than establishes, compliance obligations).

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Terminating a program and directing wind-down creates no ongoing behavioral rule or incentive for a regulated class going forward; the flag's ongoing-conduct precondition is absent. No lapsed statute is invoked (authority is active property/procurement and CWPS statutes).

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A revocation/termination order grants no ongoing authority that could require reauthorization; there is nothing to sunset — the order is itself the sunset of a prior program.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Revoking a wage-price regulatory program removes regulatory burden, not any external oversight mechanism over government action; no judicial review, IG access, or reporting requirement is stripped.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The order ends a compliance regime rather than creating one; there is no ongoing enforcement chain for a third party to surface violations within, so the flag's precondition is absent.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Terminating a specific regulatory program is a discrete deregulatory act; the text establishes no new authority claim or precedent with identifiable downstream structural consequences beyond ending the program.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Directing agency heads (including CWPS and OFPP) to terminate their own program actions works with, rather than against, their mandates; no agency's statutory authority is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order imposes no coverage rule from which exemptions could be granted — it eliminates coverage entirely — so there is no exemption architecture to evaluate.

note: Program-termination/revocation order (revokes EO 12092); compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11) and Flag 6 are NOT_APPLICABLE because the order dismantles rather than creates an ongoing rule or authority grant. All applicable flags cleanly ABSENT.

title: Delegating Authority of the President With Respect to Regulations Relating to Certain Allowances and Benefits to Government Personnel on Overseas Duty

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order designates the Secretary of State to perform numerous rule-prescribing functions 'without the approval, ratification, or other action of the President' (Sec. 1), consolidating a broad set of the President's overseas-allowance regulatory authorities in one Secretary; while each is statutorily grounded, the aggregate delegation to a single official with self-executing authority is a real concentration.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of State (and named other officials — Budget Director, Civil Service Commission) are the identified actors for the delegated regulatory functions; responsibility is clearly located rather than diffuse.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Although it touches many statutory provisions and amends several prior orders, all serve the single coherent frame of delegating and consolidating overseas allowance/benefit rulemaking; this is administrative consolidation, not bundling of distinct policy questions.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The delegated functions are to prescribe regulations under specifically cited statutory sections with defined subject matter (e.g., quarters allowances, cost-of-living allowances, unhealthful-post designations); the standards derive from the cited statutes rather than being left undefined.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A delegation of allowance-regulation authority creates no incentive for any actor to undermine the purpose; the framework channels benefit administration consistent with statute. All cited statutes are active, not lapsed.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is a standing administrative delegation framework for permanent overseas-personnel benefit administration, maintained through ordinary supersession (it expressly amends and revokes numbered predecessor orders — EO 10530, 10000, 10011, etc.); under the standing-procedural-framework carve-out, absence of a sunset does not fire.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order reallocates presidential rulemaking functions to the Secretary of State and preserves existing rules 'until hereafter superseded' (Sec. 5(b)); it removes no pre-existing external oversight mechanism such as judicial review or reporting.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The subject is internal allowance administration for government personnel, whose beneficiaries have direct interest in correct application through ordinary personnel channels; no self-reporting compliance regime with a hidden third-party gap is created.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Consolidating overseas-allowance rulemaking delegations is a routine internal reorganization of administrative authority with no identifiable precedent or systemic effect transferable beyond its stated purpose.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order allocates functions to the Secretary of State (with roles for Budget and Civil Service Commission) within their conventional domains and amends prior orders' allocations; it does not countermand another agency's conflicting statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order sets no coverage rule with discretionary carve-outs; it delegates rulemaking authority rather than exempting a population, so no exemption structure swallows a rule.

note: Administrative delegation/consolidation order amending numerous predecessor orders (EO 10530, 10000, 10624, 10853, etc.); the only flag firing is Flag 1 for the aggregate self-executing delegation to the Secretary of State. Diff-aware review confirms it reorganizes rather than weakens existing mechanisms.

title: Amendments to the Manual for Courts-Martial, United States, 1969 (Revised Edition)

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: These are technical amendments to procedural rules of the Manual for Courts-Martial issued under the UCMJ; decision authority remains distributed across military judges, convening authorities, and the appellate courts as the rules specify, with no new pooling of authority in a single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The amended provisions assign responsibilities to defined actors (convening authority, military judge, Judge Advocate General, Court of Military Review); the accountability chain within the courts-martial process remains clearly specified.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Though covering many paragraphs, all are amendments to a single instrument (the Manual for Courts-Martial) serving one frame — procedural updating of military justice rules; this is a routine consolidated amendment, not bundling of distinct policy questions requiring binary accept/reject.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The amendments refine detailed procedural standards (counsel-availability definitions, review timelines, notice requirements) with specific criteria; as diff-aware review against EO 11476 as amended shows, they tighten rather than loosen procedural precision, so enforcement/application remains defined.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The procedural amendments do not reward any actor for undermining military justice; they refine defense-counsel and review procedures. Authority rests on the active UCMJ (Ch. 47 of Title 10), not a lapsed statute.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural framework (military-justice rules) revised through ordinary supersession — the order expressly amends a chain of numbered predecessor orders (EO 11476 as amended by 11835, 12018, 12198, 12233, 12306, 12315); under the standing-procedural-framework carve-out, absence of a sunset does not fire.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Several amendments narrow judicial review: the investigating officer 'will not review any denial of a request for individual military counsel' (Sec. 3) and the convening authority's action on associate-counsel requests 'is subject to review only for abuse of discretion,' with the military judge barred from dismissing charges on the issue (Secs. 4, 6) — these restrict the scope of pre-existing judicial oversight of counsel-availability decisions.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The adversarial courts-martial process supplies built-in adverse parties (defense counsel, trial counsel, accused) with standing and incentive to surface errors, plus appellate review; there is no reliance on self-reporting that leaves violations unsurfaced.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: These are internal procedural refinements to military-justice rules with no identifiable precedent or systemic effect transferable beyond the courts-martial context they govern.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The amendments operate within the military-justice system under the President's UCMJ authority; no other agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The amendments define categories of persons unavailable to serve as individual military counsel (Sec. 6(2)), but these are functional-role exclusions serving the system's operation, not discretionary carve-outs exempting a regulated population from coverage that swallow a rule.

note: Technical amendment package to the Manual for Courts-Martial amending a chain of predecessor orders (EO 11476 et seq.); the sole firing is Flag 7, for provisions expressly limiting judicial/appellate review of counsel-availability denials.

title: United States-Iran Agreement on Release of the American Hostages (Direction to Transfer Iranian Government Assets Held by Domestic Banks)

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order directs and compels banks to transfer Iranian assets to the Federal Reserve Bank of New York 'to be held or transferred as directed by the Secretary of the Treasury' (1-101) and delegates all IEEPA functions to Treasury (1-105), pooling control over the assets and their disposition in the Secretary; it invokes active national-emergency declarations (EO 12170, 12211) as the authority base.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The order grants a sweeping liability shield — 'No person shall be held liable in any court for or with respect to anything done or omitted in good faith' in complying (1-103) — so if the compelled transfers cause improper losses, no actor bears legal responsibility, leaving the failure chain without a corrective party.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order implements a single coherent objective — transferring specified Iranian assets pursuant to the Algiers Accords — with no bundling of distinct policy questions.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The directives are highly specific — precisely which assets (funds, securities, deposits owned by the Government of Iran and its entities), to whom, with interest from a stated date — leaving little room for divergent good-faith application; the compulsion is mandatory and defined.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The one-time compelled asset transfer creates no ongoing incentive for a regulated class to undermine the purpose. The order invokes active, unexpired national-emergency declarations (EO 12170, 12211), not a lapsed statute, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The order rests on declared national emergencies — crisis-response authority with a natural end-state (resolution of the hostage crisis) — yet the asset-transfer directives and nullifications are effective 'immediately' and permanently with no expiration or reauthorization trigger tied to the emergency's resolution.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order revokes and nullifies all court-ordered attachments, injunctions, and litigation-derived rights over the assets after Nov. 14, 1979, and bars all persons from exercising them (1-102), and directs the Attorney General to defend the order's legality (1-104) — this directly strips pre-existing judicial process and remedies over the assets, a core removal of oversight central to the order's purpose.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is a one-time compelled asset transfer, not an ongoing compliance regime with violations for a third party to surface; the banks are directed and compelled to act at once, so the flag's ongoing-enforcement precondition is absent.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Nullifying vested court-ordered attachments by executive order to implement an international agreement establishes precedent that the executive can extinguish private litigants' judicial remedies over foreign-state assets under IEEPA, a claim usable in future sanctions/asset contexts well beyond the hostage settlement.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order tasks Treasury, the Federal Reserve Bank of New York, and the Attorney General within their conventional roles; while it overrides the judiciary's process (scored under Flag 7), it does not redirect another operational agency's conflicting statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order's compelled-transfer directive admits no exemption structure for a regulated population — coverage of the specified assets is mandatory and defined; there is no discretionary carve-out architecture to map.

note: Hostage-settlement asset-transfer order; Flag 7 rated CRITICAL because nullifying vested court-ordered attachments and litigation rights (1-102) directly removes pre-existing judicial oversight, which is central to what the order does. Flags 8 and 11 NOT_APPLICABLE (one-time compelled transfer, no ongoing regime).

title: Grazing Fees

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order directs the Secretaries of Agriculture and the Interior to set grazing fees using a fully specified mathematical formula 'to the extent permitted by law'; because the fee is computed by a defined formula rather than by discretion, no meaningful decision authority is pooled in either official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The two named Secretaries are jointly responsible for applying the formula, and the annual computation is mechanical, so responsibility for the fee-setting is clearly located and its correctness is verifiable against the formula.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose — establishing a grazing-fee formula for public rangelands; nothing bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The fee standard is a precise arithmetic formula with defined indices, a fixed base, a 25% annual change cap, and a $1.35 floor (Secs. 1-2); two officials applying it would reach the same result, so there is no enforcement/standard vagueness.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The formula-based fee does not reward ranchers for non-compliance or agencies for failure; it mechanically tracks forage value and costs. Authority rests on active statutes (Public Rangelands Improvement Act), not a lapsed one.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This establishes a standing fee-setting formula for a permanent administrative function (public-land grazing), computed annually, with Sec. 3 preserving existing rules; as a standing procedural framework it has no natural end-state requiring reauthorization pressure, so absence of a sunset does not fire.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 3 preserves 'any and all existing rules, practices, policies, and regulations' relating to the statutory fee formula; the order removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The fee is set by the agencies via a public formula computed from published statistical indices, not by regulated-party self-reporting; the computation is transparent and externally checkable, so no third-party incentive gap arises.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Setting a grazing-fee formula is a bounded resource-pricing action; the text establishes no authority claim or precedent with identifiable structural consequences beyond public-rangeland fee administration.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order directs Agriculture and Interior to act jointly 'under the various statutes they administer,' aligning with their existing mandates rather than redirecting or countermanding either.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order sets a uniform formula-based fee with no discretionary exemption mechanism; the 25% cap and $1.35 floor are bounded parameters, not carve-outs exempting actors from coverage.

note: Formula-driven grazing-fee order; because the fee is set by a fully specified, externally verifiable formula rather than discretion, every applicable flag is answerable and cleanly ABSENT.

title: Amendments to the Manual for Courts-Martial, United States, 1984

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Technical amendments to the Manual for Courts-Martial issued under the UCMJ; decision authority remains distributed across military judges, convening authorities, staff judge advocates, and appellate review as the rules specify, with no new pooling in a single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The amended provisions assign responsibilities to defined actors (convening authority, staff judge advocate, commanding officer, military judge); responsibility within the courts-martial process remains clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Multiple paragraphs, but all amend a single instrument (the 1984 Manual for Courts-Martial) serving one frame — procedural updating of military-justice rules; a consolidated amendment, not bundling of distinct policy questions.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The amendments refine specific procedural provisions (waiver rules, record-of-trial contents, suspension/vacation procedures, application time limits) with defined criteria; diff-aware review against EO 12473 as amended shows procedural refinement, not the introduction of undefined standards.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The procedural amendments create no incentive for any actor to undermine military justice; provisions like requiring notice-and-opportunity before vacating a suspension (Part V, para 6a) improve fairness. Authority rests on the active UCMJ, not a lapsed statute.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Standing procedural framework (military-justice rules) revised through ordinary supersession — the order amends a chain of numbered predecessor orders (EO 12473 as amended by 12484, 12550, 12586); under the standing-procedural-framework carve-out, absence of a sunset does not fire.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Unlike some earlier MCM amendments, these provisions do not strip judicial or appellate review; the Mil. R. Evid. 506(c) amendment clarifies who may claim the government/IG privilege but does not remove a pre-existing external oversight mechanism over the courts-martial process.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The adversarial courts-martial process supplies adverse parties (defense counsel, accused, trial counsel) with standing and incentive to surface errors, plus appellate review; there is no self-reporting reliance leaving violations unsurfaced.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Internal procedural refinements to military-justice rules with no identifiable precedent or systemic effect transferable beyond the courts-martial context.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The amendments operate within the military-justice system under the President's UCMJ authority; no other agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The amendments set no discretionary exemption mechanism removing a regulated population from coverage; exceptions such as record-content rules for acquittals are functional procedural distinctions, not coverage carve-outs that swallow a rule.

note: Technical amendment package to the 1984 Manual for Courts-Martial amending a chain of predecessor orders (EO 12473 et seq.); unlike order-031, these amendments contain no oversight-narrowing clause, so every applicable flag is cleanly ABSENT.

title: Establishing a Special Emergency Board To Report on the Adjustment of Wages for Employees of the Pacific Electric Railway Company of Los Angeles, California

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: While a fact-finding board is created, Paragraph 3 vests the Economic Stabilization Director with unilateral authority to override the Board's wage recommendations — they 'become effective, unless and except to the extent the Economic Stabilization Director otherwise directs' — pooling final control over the wage outcome in a single official with no external check named.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is clearly located: the Special Emergency Board reports to the President, and the Economic Stabilization Director makes and reports the final effectiveness determination; the failure chain has identified actors.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose — resolving one wage dispute at one company under wartime stabilization; nothing bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Director may direct that recommendations not take effect 'to the extent' he 'otherwise directs' (Para 3) with no stated standard or criteria governing that override, so two directors could reach different good-faith determinations on whether and how much to alter the wage recommendations.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The board-and-review structure for a single wage dispute creates no ongoing incentive for a class of actors to undermine the purpose. The order invokes the active First War Powers Act and Oct. 2, 1942 Act (wartime stabilization), which were unexpired at the time, so no zombie-trap lapse is acknowledged.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The Board is inherently time-bounded — directed to report by October 15, 1943, with the Director's effectiveness determination due within ten days — so the authority has a defined, near-term terminus rather than open-ended persistence.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order operates within the wartime stabilization apparatus (National War Labor Board, Economic Stabilization Director are looped in via filing requirements) and removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The order creates a one-off wage-determination process, not an ongoing compliance regime with violations for a third party to surface; the flag's ongoing-enforcement precondition is absent.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A special board resolving a single company's wage dispute within an established wartime-stabilization procedure produces no identifiable precedent or systemic effect from the text beyond that dispute.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order coordinates the Board, the Economic Stabilization Director, and the National War Labor Board within their existing wartime roles; it does not countermand or redirect another agency's conflicting statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order imposes no coverage rule on a regulated population from which exemptions could be granted; a one-company wage-board determination has no exemption architecture to map.

note: Wartime special-emergency-board order; Flags 1 and 4 fire on the Economic Stabilization Director's standardless override of the board's recommendations (Para 3). Flags 8 and 11 NOT_APPLICABLE (one-off determination, no ongoing compliance regime or coverage population).

title: Transfer of the Functions of the Smaller War Plants Corporation to the Reconstruction Finance Corporation and the Department of Commerce

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order distributes the Smaller War Plants Corporation's functions between the RFC and the Department of Commerce along statutorily defined lines; it disperses functions across two existing bodies rather than pooling authority in a single official, with the Budget Director allocating personnel and property.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The order names the receiving actors (RFC board of directors, Secretary of Commerce) and preserves statutory reporting requirements under the June 11, 1942 act (Sec. 3); responsibility for the transferred functions is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose — reallocating one corporation's functions to two successors; the division of functions serves that one frame and is not a bundling of unrelated policy questions.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A reorganization transferring functions imposes no ongoing conduct standard on any regulated party; the underlying loan/contract-settlement functions carry their own statutory standards, so there is no order-created enforcement rule to assess for vagueness.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A one-time transfer of governmental functions creates no ongoing behavioral incentive for a regulated class; the flag's precondition is absent. Authority is the active First War Powers Act, not a lapsed statute.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The order is a one-time, self-executing transfer of functions and assets effective on a stated date; it grants no ongoing discretionary authority that could require reauthorization, so there is nothing to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order preserves 'all prior regulations, rulings, and other directives' except where in conflict (Sec. 5) and retains statutory reporting duties (Sec. 3); it removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The order establishes no compliance/self-reporting regime; it reassigns governmental functions, so there is no order-level third-party enforcement chain to evaluate.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A wartime agency-function transfer executed under the First War Powers Act is a bounded administrative reorganization; the text establishes no precedent or systemic effect beyond dissolving one corporation into existing bodies.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The transfer moves functions to the RFC and Commerce with a clear division and Budget-Director allocation of personnel/funds, and it amends prior orders in conflict (Sec. 6); the reallocation is orderly with defined hierarchy rather than producing mandate conflict — no cannibalization fires.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order sets no coverage rule on a regulated population from which exemptions could be granted; a function-transfer reorganization has no exemption architecture.

note: Wartime agency-function-transfer reorganization; compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11) and Flag 6 are NOT_APPLICABLE because the order is a one-time transfer creating no ongoing conduct rule or authority grant. Applicable flags cleanly ABSENT, with the transfer's defined division clearing Flag 10.

title: Educational Technology: Ensuring Opportunity for All Children in the Next Century

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order directs each agency to identify and transfer excess computer equipment to schools/nonprofits under existing statutory transfer authorities, with coordination roles for GSA, OSTP, and OPM; authority is distributed across agencies rather than pooled in a single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibilities are assigned to defined actors — each agency, GSA for donations, OSTP for plan coordination (Sec. 3(b)) — so the implementation chain has identified responsible parties rather than diffuse blame.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The stated multi-part goal (equipment transfer plus employee volunteering) coheres under one frame — putting educationally useful federal equipment and expertise into classrooms — and the provisions all serve that single purpose; no distinct unrelated remedies forced together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The core directives are hedged with 'to the extent permitted by law,' 'to the extent possible,' 'where appropriate,' and 'shall attempt to give particular preference' (Secs. 1-2), so whether and how much any agency must transfer equipment is left to broad good-faith discretion with no defined trigger or measurable standard.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order's incentive (donate surplus equipment to schools) aligns with its purpose and creates no reward for undermining it. Authority rests on active statutes (Stevenson-Wydler Act, Federal Property Act), not a lapsed one.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This establishes a standing administrative preference/procedure for routine surplus-equipment disposition, an evergreen administrative function maintained through supersession (it expressly supersedes EO 12821, Sec. 5); under the standing-procedural-framework carve-out, absence of a sunset does not fire, and it includes a 6-month implementation-plan reporting step.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds a preference and reporting machinery (implementation plans to OSTP) and supersedes a prior order; it removes no pre-existing external oversight mechanism, and the 'no rights created' clause (Sec. 6) is a standard non-justiciability provision, not a stripping of existing review.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Compliance depends on each agency voluntarily identifying and reporting excess equipment and giving schools 'highest preference,' with no external actor holding standing and incentive to surface an agency's failure to prioritize schools; enforcement rests on agency self-direction with only internal OSTP-coordinated plans.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A surplus-equipment donation-preference program is a bounded administrative initiative; the text establishes no authority claim or precedent with identifiable structural consequences beyond directing excess-property flows to schools.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order works within existing property-disposal authorities (GSA's surplus process, agencies' Stevenson-Wydler transfer power) and adds a preference; it does not redirect or countermand any agency's conflicting statutory mandate — transfers remain 'to the extent permitted by law.'

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The pervasive qualifiers — 'to the extent permitted by law,' 'to the extent possible,' 'where appropriate' — function as a broad, self-applied escape from the equipment-transfer preference, letting any agency effectively opt out of prioritizing schools without external check, so the exemption terrain is broad and discretionary enough to soften the rule into a preference.

note: Surplus-equipment-to-schools preference order (supersedes EO 12821); the hortatory 'to the extent possible/permitted by law' qualifiers drive the Flag 4, 8, and 11 findings, none rising to CRITICAL since the program's core function still operates within existing transfer authorities.

title: Inspection by Department of Agriculture of Income Tax Returns Made Under the Internal Revenue Code of 1954 of Persons Having Farm Operations

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order authorizes the Department of Agriculture to inspect certain farm-operator tax returns 'for statistical purposes only,' subject to Treasury rules and regulations; it grants a bounded access right rather than pooling discretionary decision authority over eligibility, enforcement, or interpretation in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is clearly located: the Department of Agriculture conducts inspection, and the Secretary of the Treasury prescribes the governing rules (approved the same date), so the actors controlling and conducting the access are identified.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single-purpose authorization of statistical tax-return inspection for one department; nothing bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The access is tightly scoped — returns of persons with farm operations, 'to the extent readily available,' 'for statistical purposes only,' inspected in an IRS office 'in accordance and upon compliance with the rules and regulations prescribed by the Secretary of the Treasury' — leaving little room for divergent good-faith application of the access standard.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Granting statistical inspection access creates no incentive for a regulated class to undermine any purpose; the 'statistical purposes only' limit constrains use. Authority rests on active §6103 of the 1954 Code, not a lapsed statute.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is a standing administrative access authorization for a permanent statistical function, governed by Treasury regulations maintainable through ordinary supersession; as a standing procedural framework it has no natural end-state requiring reauthorization, so absence of a sunset does not fire.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: By opening taxpayers' returns to another department for inspection, the order reduces the confidentiality protection that otherwise shields those returns from access; it expands inter-agency data access in a way that diminishes a pre-existing privacy safeguard over taxpayer information, though bounded by 'statistical purposes only.'

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Whether Agriculture's use stays within the 'statistical purposes only' limit depends on the accessing agency's own compliance with Treasury rules, and the affected taxpayers — who are not notified — have no access to surface misuse, so no external actor with standing and information has incentive to police the access limit.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Authorizing one department to inspect another's confidential taxpayer returns by executive order establishes precedent for expanding inter-agency access to protected tax data, a framework extendable to other departments and purposes beyond farm statistics.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order gives Agriculture read access to IRS-held returns under Treasury-prescribed rules; it operates cooperatively within IRS custody rather than redirecting or countermanding the IRS's statutory mandate — the IRS retains control of the process.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order sets a defined, narrow scope of access (farm-operator returns, statistical use) with no discretionary exemption mechanism; there is no carve-out structure that swallows a coverage rule.

note: Tax-return inspection-access order; Flags 7, 8, and 9 fire around the reduction of taxpayer-return confidentiality and the unverifiable-by-outsiders 'statistical purposes only' limit, none rising to CRITICAL given the bounded, statistically-limited scope.

title: Exclusion of the Defense Mapping Agency Reston Center and Elements Under the Joint Special Operations Command From the Federal Labor-Management Relations Program

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: This partial amendment (checked against EO 12171, which it amends) adds two named DoD organizations to the pre-existing statutory exclusion list under 5 U.S.C. 7103(b)(1); it exercises a specific statutory determination power rather than pooling new discretionary decision authority in an official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The President makes the exclusion determination on stated statutory grounds (primary intelligence/national-security function); the responsible actor and the basis are clearly identified, so no diffuse-blame gap arises from this discrete amendment.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order adds two related organizations under one statutory ground (national-security work); it is a single-frame amendment, not a bundling of unrelated policy questions.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order determines a coverage exclusion; it creates no ongoing enforcement standard governing anyone's conduct, so there is no enforcement mechanism to assess for vagueness.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Adding organizations to a statutory exclusion list creates no ongoing behavioral incentive for a regulated class; the flag's precondition is absent. Authority is the active 5 U.S.C. 7103(b), not a lapsed statute.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This amends a standing exclusion framework (EO 12171) maintained through ordinary supersession/amendment — this very order is an example of that periodic amendment — so under the standing-procedural-framework carve-out, absence of a sunset does not fire.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Diff-aware against EO 12171: the amendment removes the covered DoD organizations from Chapter 71 of Title 5, thereby eliminating the collective-bargaining and Federal Labor Relations Authority oversight regime that would otherwise apply to those units' labor-management relations — a removal of a pre-existing external check over those organizations.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The order sets a coverage exclusion rather than an ongoing compliance regime with violations for a third party to surface; the flag's ongoing-enforcement precondition is absent.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Adding two more DoD units to an established national-security exclusion list, on the same statutory ground already used across EO 12171, is a routine incremental amendment with no identifiable new precedent or systemic effect from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order removes DoD units from the FLRA's labor-relations coverage on a national-security basis; because the exclusion is expressly a statutory determination the President is authorized to make, it does not countermand a mandate the FLRA holds against the President's authority — this is a delegated coverage decision, not a lateral mandate conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order is itself an exemption instrument — it exempts additional organizations from labor-management coverage on a determination that they perform national-security work and cannot apply Chapter 71 consistent with security; the exclusion is granted by executive determination and the framework (EO 12171) it extends allows repeated discretionary additions, expanding who is carved out.

note: Partial amendment adding two DoD units to EO 12171's labor-relations exclusion list (predecessor text available); Flag 7 fires for removing FLRA/Chapter 71 coverage and Flag 11 because the order is itself an exemption grant. Compliance-regime flags 4,5,8 NOT_APPLICABLE (a coverage exclusion, not an ongoing conduct rule).

title: Redistribution of Maritime Functions

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order transfers the Bureau of Marine Inspection and Navigation's functions to the Bureau of Customs and the Coast Guard, to be exercised under the direction of the Treasury and Navy Secretaries respectively; it distributes functions across two agencies under existing supervisory chains rather than pooling them in a single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The order specifies which functions go to which official under whose supervision (Commissioner of Customs under Treasury; Commandant of the Coast Guard under Navy), so responsibility for each transferred function is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single frame — wartime redistribution of maritime functions — with the detailed enumeration serving that one purpose; not a bundling of unrelated policy questions.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A reorganization transferring governmental functions imposes no new ongoing conduct standard on any regulated party; the transferred inspection/licensing functions carry their own pre-existing statutory standards, so there is no order-created enforcement rule to assess for vagueness.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A one-time transfer of governmental functions creates no new ongoing behavioral incentive for a regulated class; the flag's precondition is absent. Authority is the active First War Powers Act, not a lapsed statute.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The order includes an explicit termination trigger — it 'shall become effective on March 1, 1942 and remain in force until the termination of Title I of the First War Powers Act, 1941' (Sec. 9) — tying the wartime reallocation to the sunset of its authorizing statute, so the absence-of-sunset flag does not fire.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The transfer moves operational maritime functions between agencies and preserves the penalty/enforcement provisions of the underlying navigation laws; it removes no pre-existing external oversight mechanism over those functions.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The order establishes no new compliance/self-reporting regime; it reassigns existing inspection and licensing functions, so there is no order-level third-party enforcement chain to evaluate.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A wartime reorganization consolidating maritime inspection and navigation functions into existing bodies, tied to the war-powers statute's duration, is bounded; the text establishes no precedent or systemic effect beyond the transfer itself.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order abolishes the operating role of the Bureau of Marine Inspection and Navigation and redistributes its and the Secretary of Commerce's functions to Customs and the Coast Guard, and also strips training functions from the Maritime Commission (Sec. 5) and vessel-numbering from Collectors of Customs (Sec. 4) — it redirects operational mandates away from agencies that held them, the lateral reassignment the flag targets, though done with a defined hierarchy.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order sets no coverage rule on a regulated population from which exemptions could be granted; a function-transfer reorganization has no exemption architecture, and the waiver authority in Sec. 6 is merely transferred, not newly created.

note: Wartime maritime-function reorganization with an explicit statute-tied sunset (Sec. 9), clearing Flag 6; Flag 10 fires because it redirects operational functions away from the Bureau of Marine Inspection, Maritime Commission, and Collectors of Customs. Compliance-regime flags 4,5,8,11 NOT_APPLICABLE (a function transfer, not an ongoing conduct rule).

title: Authorizing the Appointment of Mrs. Ruth Edna McC. Somers to a Position in the Government Service Without Regard to the Civil Service Rules

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing authorization permitting a single named individual's appointment; no discretionary decision authority is pooled in any official for ongoing exercise.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Nothing to fail administratively; the order simply exempts one named person from Civil Service Rule requirements, with no implementation chain whose failure would raise a responsibility question.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order contains a single provision (one individual appointment); there is nothing to bundle it against, so the flag's concept does not map.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time appointment waiver creates no ongoing behavioral rule for anyone to comply with, so there is no enforcement mechanism to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance or behavioral relationship is created; a single-person appointment waiver produces no incentive structure for any actor class.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing appointment authorization grants no ongoing authority, so there is nothing to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order waives Civil Service Rule requirements for one appointment but removes no external oversight mechanism (judicial review, IG access, reporting) that applied to this action.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory relationship exists for a third party to police; a single appointment creates no violations for anyone to surface.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow, individualized administrative waiver with no systemic or precedential reach beyond the named person; no identifiable second-order effect.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's statutory mandate is implicated, redirected, or countermanded by a single-person appointment waiver.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order is itself a one-off exemption for a named individual, not an instrument with a population of coverage against which an exemption structure could be mapped.

note: Highly individualized administrative waiver (single named appointee); most compliance-regime and systemic flags are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE per the mechanical test, leaving only Flags 1, 2, 7 answerable and clean.

title: Waiver Under the Trade Act of 1974 With Respect to the Soviet Union

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The President exercises a specific statutory waiver under section 402(c)(2) after making the report Congress requires; the authority is a defined, one-time statutory exercise, not pooled discretionary power over an ongoing regime.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The waiver is a discrete presidential act tied to a statutory reporting condition; responsibility rests plainly with the President and the statutory framework, with no diffuse implementation chain to fail.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single-subject waiver of subsections (a) and (b) of section 402 with respect to one country; no distinct remedies or problem frames are packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The waiver lifts a statutory trade restriction; it imposes no ongoing behavioral standard on any party to be enforced, so enforcement clarity does not map.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A statutory trade-restriction waiver creates no ongoing compliance relationship or incentive structure for a regulated party; the flag's concept does not apply.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The waiver of subsections (a) and (b) is granted with no expiration or reauthorization trigger in the order's text; a country-specific trade waiver is the kind of conditional authority whose continuation should track periodic review, and none is provided here (the statutory annual renewal cycle is not restated in the order).

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order recites compliance with the section 402(c)(2) congressional-report requirement rather than bypassing it; it removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Lifting a trade restriction creates no ongoing violations for any external actor to surface; no third-party enforcement relationship exists.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A country-specific statutory waiver operating within the Trade Act's defined framework; no identifiable precedent or authority-claim reaching beyond the stated subject is created by the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded; the waiver is a direct presidential statutory exercise implicating no other agency's operational authority.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order is itself a single statutory waiver, not a coverage instrument with an internal exemption structure to map against an affected population.

note: Narrow statutory trade waiver; compliance-regime flags are NOT_APPLICABLE (the waiver lifts rather than imposes a rule). Flag 6 fires because the order states no expiration for a country-specific waiver whose continuation warrants periodic reauthorization.

title: Continuance of Certain Federal Advisory Committees

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authority under the Federal Advisory Committee Act is distributed to the head of each designated department or agency under GSA guidelines (Sec. 2); no single official gains pooled discretionary control.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each committee is assigned to a named department head accountable for FACA functions, and annual reporting to Congress is expressly preserved (Sec. 2), so responsibility is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Continuing multiple advisory committees and revoking terminated ones is a routine housekeeping instrument; each committee is separately listed and individually revocable in effect, not fused into an accept/reject package that forecloses targeted correction.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Advisory committees issue recommendations, not enforcement; the order creates no compliance standard applied to any regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule or regulated-party relationship is created; continuing advisory bodies produces no incentive to undermine a stated compliance purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Every continued committee carries an explicit termination date of September 30, 1989 (Sec. 1), and the order itself supersedes a numbered predecessor (EO 12534, Sec. 4) — the very reauthorization-by-supersession pattern the sunset flag rewards.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order expressly retains the President's FACA reporting-to-Congress function (Sec. 2) and removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Advisory committees generate no violations to be surfaced; there is no enforcement chain for a third party to enter.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A time-limited continuance/revocation of advisory bodies with no authority claim or definitional framework reaching beyond the listed committees; no identifiable systemic downstream effect.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order assigns FACA functions to the department already housing each committee (per parentheticals) under uniform GSA procedures, creating no conflict with or redirection of any agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order confers no coverage from which anyone could be exempted; it is an internal continuance/revocation of federal advisory bodies.

note: Routine FACA committee-continuance order superseding EO 12534 (predecessor text available and confirms the same continuance-by-supersession pattern); clean across all applicable flags with explicit termination dates and preserved congressional reporting.

title: Authorizing and Directing the Secretary of War to Take Possession of and Operate the Inglewood Plant of North American Aviation, Inc.

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of War is authorized to seize and operate a private plant 'through such person or persons as he may designate' and make 'necessary or appropriate adjustments' to contracts and compensation, concentrating broad operational and property authority in one official with no external check named in the order.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary determines the terms of possession, compensation adjustments, and employee protection with no defined standard or review; if the seizure is mishandled the order provides no actor with an obligation to correct it beyond the same Secretary exercising the authority.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single, coherent purpose (resume defense production at one named plant); the seizure, operation, and compensation provisions are all instrumental to that one aim, not distinct remedies packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Compensation to the company and contract adjustments are left to 'further orders hereafter issued by the Secretary of War' with no stated standard, and 'measures as may be necessary to protect workers returning to the plant' is undefined — two officials could implement these very differently in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The seizure directly serves its stated purpose of restoring war production; no design feature rewards a party for undermining that purpose. The authority rests on the active, unexpired May 27, 1941 unlimited-national-emergency proclamation and wartime powers, not a lapsed statute.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Possession 'shall be terminated by the President as soon as he determines that the plant will be privately operated in a manner consistent with the needs of the national defense' — an explicit end condition tied to resolution of the emergency, satisfying the sunset test for an exceptional-powers grant.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order references certification of the dispute to the National Defense Mediation Board and removes no pre-existing external oversight mechanism; it adds an executive seizure rather than stripping a check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: A one-time plant seizure and government operation creates no ongoing compliance regime for a third party to police; the government itself operates the plant.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Seizing and operating a private plant to break a labor dispute establishes a reusable precedent for executive commandeering of private industry under emergency authority, a claim applicable well beyond this single plant.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order directs the Secretary of War and references cooperating bodies without redirecting or countermanding any other agency's statutory mandate; the National Defense Mediation Board's role is acknowledged, not overridden.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order applies to one named plant and creates no general coverage population with an exemption structure to map.

note: Wartime plant-seizure order; broad discretionary authority in the Secretary of War fires Flags 1, 2, 4, and precedent Flag 9, but an explicit termination condition (Flag 6 ABSENT) and active emergency basis keep it below the CRITICAL threshold on any single flag.

title: Reduction of 100,000 Federal Positions

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The reduction targets are set by the order itself and executed by each department and agency head at their own discretion (Sec. 1); OMB issues implementing instructions but authority is distributed across agency heads, not pooled in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each department and agency head is specifically responsible for achieving its quantified reduction against defined percentage milestones and target dates (Secs. 1, 3), locating responsibility clearly.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent objective (a 4 percent FTE reduction phased over three fiscal years); the SES/GS-15/GS-14 sub-target and milestone dates all serve that one aim rather than packaging distinct policies.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order sets numeric targets but leaves the mechanism to 'attrition or early out programs established at the discretion of the department and agency heads' and delegates 'exemptions necessary for the delivery of essential services' to OMB with no criteria — two agencies could implement and exempt very differently in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Fixed FTE-count targets with discretion over which positions to cut incentivize agencies to eliminate the easiest-to-vacate positions (attrition, low-cost roles) rather than the least-needed ones, potentially degrading capacity in a way disconnected from the stated efficiency purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The mandate is inherently time-bounded: reductions phase to 100 percent by the end of fiscal year 1995 (Sec. 3), giving the authority a defined end-state rather than open-ended continuation.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order 'requests' (does not compel) independent agencies to comply (Sec. 5) and removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism over personnel decisions.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Compliance rests entirely on agency self-reporting of FTE reductions to OMB; no external actor with standing and incentive is given access to verify that reported reductions are genuine rather than reclassifications, so violations depend on self-disclosure.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Mandating uniform percentage cuts across all agencies over 100 employees regardless of function establishes a precedent for across-the-board staffing reductions decoupled from mission need, and can hollow out capacity that later administrations must rebuild.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Each agency reduces its own workforce; the order redirects no agency's statutory mandate or appropriated personnel to another agency, and OMB's coordinating role is consistent with its budget authority.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Sec. 4 delegates to OMB open-ended authority to grant 'exemptions necessary for the delivery of essential services and compliance with applicable law' with no defined criteria or external check, letting a single actor determine the effective scope of the coverage.

note: Government-wide workforce-reduction mandate; fires on vague implementation, self-reporting, undefined OMB exemption authority, perverse cut incentives, and precedential across-the-board-cut effects, but clean on authority location, accountability, and sunset (built-in FY1995 end date).

title: Extension of the Provisions of Executive Order No. 9001 of December 27, 1941, to Contracts of the Department of Commerce

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: This is a full incorporation extending 'all of the functions and powers' of EO 9001 to the Secretary of Commerce, who inherits EO 9001's broad authority to enter and modify war contracts without regard to normal contract law and to delegate that power downward — concentrating wide discretionary contracting authority in one Secretary.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Inheriting EO 9001, the Secretary of Commerce may modify, amend, or settle claims and release accrued obligations 'in the judgment of' the department with the same self-determined standard; if the discretion is misused, no actor other than the exercising department is positioned to correct it.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single-purpose extension of one existing framework to one new department; no distinct remedies or problem frames are packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The extension carries EO 9001's regulatory guardrails (quarterly reporting to the President, Walsh-Healey/Davis-Bacon applicability, fee caps, warranty clauses), which supply defined standards for the exercised contracting authority; enforcement of those terms is not left ambiguous.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The inherited framework serves expedited war procurement and its terms (fee caps, anti-profiteering warranty) work against gaming; no design feature rewards undermining the purpose. Authority rests on the active First War Powers Act, not a lapsed statute.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The extension of emergency war-contracting authority to Commerce carries no termination trigger tied to the war's end; this is exactly the exceptional-powers category the sunset flag targets, and neither this order nor the incorporated EO 9001 supplies an expiration in the text available.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Via EO 9001, contracting is authorized 'without regard to the provisions of law relating to the making, performance, amendment, or modification of contracts,' bypassing the normal competitive-bidding and contract-law checks that would otherwise apply to Commerce procurement.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Compliance with the inherited framework's protections rests on the contracting department's own reporting to the President; no external actor with standing and access is positioned to surface improper contract modifications or settlements, which are made at departmental discretion.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Extending EO 9001 department-by-department establishes a replicable mechanism for spreading extraordinary war-contracting authority across the executive branch, normalizing procurement outside ordinary contract law well beyond Commerce.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order grants Commerce its own parallel authority for its own contracts rather than redirecting or countermanding the statutory mandate of War, Navy, or the Maritime Commission; no jurisdictional conflict is created.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
