flags.justification: Through EO 9001, advertising, competitive bidding, and payment/performance bonds 'need not be required,' and the Secretary of Labor's exemption powers under the labor statutes carry over — broad discretion to exempt contracts from ordinary procurement safeguards resides in the contracting officials.

note: Full incorporation of EO 9001 (predecessor text available) into the Department of Commerce; scored by inheriting EO 9001's flag findings per the incorporation-by-reference rule rather than as a clean one-paragraph extension. The extension itself adds no new safeguards or limits.

title: Amending Executive Order No. 7908 of June 9, 1938, and Executive Order No. 10046 of March 24, 1949

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The amendments make technical, self-executing changes to land jurisdiction (renaming 'General Land Office' to 'Bureau of Land Management,' adding an exchange proviso, transferring jurisdiction to Interior); no discretionary decision authority is pooled in any single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The amended provisions place land administration under the Taylor Grazing Act and the Bankhead-Jones Act with statutory responsibility clearly assigned to the Department of the Interior; no implementation-failure gap arises from these definitional changes.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Two related land-jurisdiction amendments under the same statutory authority; each is independently justified by the stated administrative purpose and could stand alone, so nothing is bundled to foreclose targeted correction.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Per the diff-aware convention, only the amended provisions are assessed; these are land-jurisdiction transfers and definitional substitutions that impose no ongoing behavioral rule to enforce.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The amended provisions create no ongoing compliance relationship or incentive structure; a land-jurisdiction transfer does not reward or penalize any actor's ongoing conduct.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The amendments effect one-time, self-executing jurisdictional transfers and definitional changes with no ongoing grant of authority to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The amendments subject the transferred lands to the Taylor Grazing Act framework and remove no pre-existing oversight mechanism over land administration.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Self-executing land transfers create no ongoing violations for any third party to surface; there is no enforcement chain to enter.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Technical land-jurisdiction amendments confined to specified areas under existing statutes; no authority claim or definitional framework reaching beyond the described lands is created.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The transfer of jurisdiction from Agriculture to Interior is an ordinary, statutorily authorized reallocation of land administration under the Bankhead-Jones and Taylor Grazing Acts, not a conflict with either agency's mandate; predecessors EO 7908, 8531, and 10046 (all available) confirm the amendments are consistent with the existing scheme.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The amendments define which lands fall under which department's jurisdiction; they create no coverage population with an exemption structure to map.

note: Partial amendment of EOs 7908/10046 (predecessors available and reviewed); assessed only the amended land-jurisdiction provisions per the diff-aware convention, which are self-executing transfers/renames making compliance-regime flags genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE and the rest clean.

title: Amending Executive Order No. 11649, Regulations Governing the Seals of the President and the Vice President of the United States

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The amendment merely broadens one permitted-use subsection of EO 11649; it pools no decision authority in any official and leaves the predecessor's use-authorization structure otherwise intact.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The amendment is a definitional expansion of a permitted-use category; there is no implementation chain whose failure would create a responsibility gap.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: A single revision to one subsection under one statutory authority; nothing distinct is packaged with it.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Per the diff-aware convention, only the amended provision is assessed; it expands permitted use of the seals to encyclopedias, dictionaries, books, journals, pamphlets, periodicals, or magazines 'incident to a description or history of seals... or the Presidency,' a bounded and specific enumeration, not a vague standard. The predecessor's Counsel-to-the-President authorization for other uses is untouched.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Widening a permitted-use exception for historical/reference publications creates no incentive for any actor to undermine the seal-protection purpose; the ongoing rule (restriction on seal use) remains and the amendment only relaxes one narrow category.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The amendment is a one-time definitional change to a standing regulation; it grants no new ongoing authority of its own to sunset, and the underlying seal-regulation regime is an evergreen standing procedural framework revised by supersession/amendment.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The amendment removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; it adjusts the substantive scope of a permitted use only.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The underlying seal regulation is enforced through ordinary criminal prohibition (18 U.S.C. 713); publishers and the public have standing/interest in the boundary of permitted use, and the amendment does not create a self-reporting-only gap in that enforcement chain.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow broadening of permitted seal use for reference works; no authority claim or precedent reaching beyond the seal-regulation subject is created.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The amendment implicates no second agency's statutory mandate; it concerns only presidential/vice-presidential seal use.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The amended subsection is one of several specifically enumerated permitted uses; it broadens one narrow, defined category rather than creating a discretionary or rule-swallowing exemption structure.

note: Partial amendment of EO 11649 (predecessor available and reviewed); assessed only the single amended permitted-use subsection per the diff-aware convention, which is a bounded definitional expansion, clean on all applicable flags.

title: Delegating to the Director of Selective Service Authority to Issue Rules and Regulations Under the Military Selective Service Act

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Rulemaking authority under the Selective Service Act is delegated to the single Director of Selective Service (Sec. 1); although a consultation-and-referral process exists, the Director may proceed by certifying that no consulted official objected, so operative rulemaking authority rests substantially in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility for rules is clearly located in the Director, with a defined referral path to the President for disputes (Sec. 2(b)) and preservation of the President's own authority (Sec. 3), so accountability for rulemaking is traceable.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single-purpose delegation of rulemaking authority with an accompanying procedure; no distinct remedies or problem frames are packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order defines the rulemaking procedure precisely: Federal Register publication per section 13(b), mandatory consultation of named officials, minimum comment periods, referral timelines, and certification requirements (Sec. 2), leaving little room for divergent good-faith application of the process itself.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The consultation-and-referral design gives the Director a path to issue rules by certifying no objection, but this is a procedural convenience, not an incentive to undermine the Act's purpose; no actor is rewarded for defeating the stated goal.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The delegation of rulemaking authority to the Director continues indefinitely with no reauthorization trigger; it is a standing grant of discretionary rulemaking power, not a self-revising procedural framework with a supersession pattern, so the flag fires.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order preserves Federal Register publication and comment (Sec. 2(a)), adds an inter-agency consultation and presidential-referral check that did not previously constrain a delegated Director, and expressly does not authorize the Director to waive section 13(b) requirements (Sec. 3) — it adds, rather than removes, oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Rules are published in the Federal Register with notice-and-comment (Sec. 2(a)), giving affected registrants and the public standing and access to identify and contest problematic regulations; the enforcement/challenge chain is not limited to self-reporting.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Delegating presidential Selective Service rulemaking to an agency director establishes a precedent for shifting conscription-related regulatory authority out of direct presidential exercise into agency discretion, a structural change persisting beyond the immediate delegation.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order requires the Director to consult Defense, Justice, Labor, HEW, Transportation, OEP, and the Appeal Board rather than redirecting or countermanding their statutory mandates; it builds coordination, not conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order delegates rulemaking procedure and creates no exemption structure; the Director's authority to waive section 13(b) is expressly withheld (Sec. 3), so no broad discretionary carve-out is established.

note: Rulemaking delegation to a single Director fires Flag 1 (concentration) and Flag 9 (precedent) plus Flag 6 (open-ended discretionary grant), but the robust consultation/referral/publication procedure keeps oversight, enforcement clarity, and third-party access clean.

title: Providing for the Coordination and Control of the Rubber Program

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Chairman of the War Production Board is directed to 'assume full responsibility for and control over the Nation's rubber program in all of its phases,' and the Rubber Director may direct numerous named agencies with 'full compliance...accorded such directives' — near-total control over an entire industrial program pooled in one office with no external check, directly constituting the order's design.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Rubber Director is 'appointed by and responsible to the Chairman' alone, and the sweeping authority to direct other agencies 'in such manner and for such period of time as he deems advisable' is self-defined with no external mechanism to correct misuse; accountability runs only within the same WPB chain.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (assuring adequate rubber supply for war and civilian needs); the enumerated program phases are all facets of that one aim, not distinct policies packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Director may issue directives to listed agencies 'in such manner and for such period of time as he deems advisable' with 'full compliance...accorded,' but the order supplies no standard governing what those directives must contain or how compliance is judged, leaving wide room for divergent implementation.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The concentrated program control serves the stated supply objective; no design feature rewards an actor for undermining rubber supply. Authority rests on the active First War Powers Act, not a lapsed statute.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This wartime program-control authority has no termination trigger tied to the emergency's resolution; an exceptional-powers grant of this breadth should carry a natural end tied to the war, and none is stated, so the flag fires.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: By empowering the Rubber Director to direct the Office of Price Administration, Board of Economic Warfare, Department of Agriculture, and others with mandatory compliance, the order overrides the ordinary independent operation of those bodies' functions within the rubber domain (subject only to the Sec. 5 price-control carve-out), disabling their autonomous decision-making.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is an internal executive-branch program-control order directing government agencies; it creates no regulated-party compliance regime for an external third party to police.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Vesting one 'czar' with directive authority over numerous independent agencies for an entire commodity program establishes a precedent for centralized single-office control cutting across established agency jurisdictions, a model reusable well beyond rubber.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Director is authorized to direct the OPA, Board of Economic Warfare, Office of Defense Transportation, Department of Agriculture, RFC subsidiaries, and Petroleum Coordinator to execute rubber-program tasks with 'full compliance' required — laterally overriding and redirecting the operational mandates of multiple statutory agencies, the core mechanism of the order.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order is an internal program-consolidation instrument directing agencies; it creates no coverage population of regulated parties with an exemption structure to map.

note: Wartime rubber-'czar' order concentrating full program control in one office and laterally directing numerous statutory agencies with mandatory compliance; fires CRITICAL on both Power Concentration and Inter-Agency Cannibalization, the two mechanisms that constitute the order's design.

title: Waiver of the Time Limitations in Civil Service Rule IX To Permit the Reinstatement of Van C. Brodrick to a Position as Rural Carrier in the Post Office at Marysville, Kansas

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing waiver permitting reinstatement of a single named individual; no ongoing discretionary authority is pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The order corrects one erroneous prior removal for one named person, recommended by the Postmaster General and concurred in by the Civil Service Commission; there is no implementation chain whose failure would raise a responsibility gap.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order contains a single provision (one individual reinstatement); there is nothing to bundle it against.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time reinstatement waiver imposes no ongoing behavioral rule for anyone to comply with, so enforcement clarity does not map.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance relationship is created; a single-person reinstatement produces no incentive structure for any actor class.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing reinstatement grants no ongoing authority to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order waives a Civil Service Rule time limitation for one reinstatement (with CSC concurrence) but removes no external oversight mechanism that applied to this action.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory relationship exists for a third party to police; a single reinstatement creates no violations to surface.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow, individualized administrative correction with no systemic or precedential reach beyond the named person.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's statutory mandate is redirected; the Postmaster General and CSC act cooperatively on one personnel correction.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order is itself a one-off waiver for a named individual, not an instrument with a coverage population and exemption structure.

note: Individualized administrative reinstatement waiver for a single named person; most compliance-regime and systemic flags are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE, leaving only Flags 1, 2, 7 answerable and clean.

title: Chemical Warfare Review Commission

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order creates a bipartisan nine-member advisory commission that reports to the President; it exercises only review-and-recommend functions and pools no operational or enforcement authority in any single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Commission has defined reporting deadlines and a designated Chairman, and the Secretary of Defense is responsible for its administrative support (Sec. 3); responsibility for the deliverables is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent charge (review the U.S. chemical-warfare posture, especially binary munitions); the sequential reports address facets of one question rather than bundling distinct policies.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: An advisory commission issues recommendations, not enforcement; the order creates no compliance standard applied to any regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule or regulated-party relationship is created; a time-limited advisory body produces no incentive to undermine a compliance purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The Commission 'shall terminate 30 days after submission of its report, or on September 30, 1985, whichever date is earlier' (Sec. 4(b)) — an explicit, tightly bounded sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order retains the President's FACA reporting-to-Congress function (Sec. 4(a)) and removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: An advisory commission generates no violations to surface; there is no enforcement chain for a third party to enter.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A short-lived study commission on a defined defense question; no authority claim or precedent reaching beyond its advisory role is created by the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Executive agencies are asked to provide information 'to the extent permitted by law' (Sec. 3(a)) and DoD provides support; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order confers no coverage from which anyone could be exempted; it establishes an internal advisory body.

note: Time-limited advisory commission with a tight explicit sunset and preserved congressional reporting; advisory-only nature makes the compliance-regime flags NOT_APPLICABLE and the rest clean.

title: Interagency Task Force on the Roles and Missions of the United States Coast Guard

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A 14-member interagency task force chaired by a member selected by the Secretary of Transportation, exercising only advisory review-and-recommend functions reporting to the President; no operational authority is pooled in a single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Task Force reports to the President through the Secretary of Transportation with a Staff Director appointed by that Secretary (Secs. 2, 4(c)); responsibility for the review is clearly located and its recommendations are explicitly advisory inputs.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent charge (review Coast Guard roles and missions through 2020); the enumerated methodology steps all serve that one review, not distinct bundled policies.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: An advisory task force issues recommendations, not enforcement; no compliance standard is applied to any regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule or regulated-party relationship is created; a study task force produces no incentive to undermine a compliance purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The Task Force 'shall exist for a period of 6 months from its first meeting unless extended by the Secretary of Transportation' and submits a written report (Sec. 5(a)) — an explicit, bounded existence with a defined end.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds an advisory review mechanism and removes no pre-existing oversight; agencies provide information 'to the extent permitted by law' (Sec. 4(a)).

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: An advisory task force generates no violations to surface; there is no enforcement chain for a third party to enter.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: By directing the Task Force to consider whether Coast Guard roles 'might be reduced, eliminated, or moved to other private organizations or Government agencies' (Sec. 3(a)) and feeding into Deepwater investment decisions (Sec. 5(b)), the review's structure could legitimize divestment/privatization of statutory missions, a downstream consequence reaching beyond the stated advisory purpose.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Task Force is composed of representatives from many agencies for coordinated study; it recommends but does not itself redirect or countermand any agency's statutory mandate, and expressly assumes the Coast Guard remains a law-enforcement agency and armed force (Sec. 3(e)).

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order confers no coverage from which anyone could be exempted; it establishes an internal advisory body.

note: Time-limited interagency advisory task force; clean on authority and accountability with an explicit sunset, but Flag 9 fires because the review is structured to consider eliminating or privatizing statutory Coast Guard missions.

title: Waiver under the Trade Act of 1974 with respect to the Hungarian People's Republic

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The President exercises a specific statutory waiver under section 402(c)(2) after making the required congressional report; a defined one-time statutory exercise, not pooled discretionary power over an ongoing regime.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The waiver is a discrete presidential act tied to a statutory reporting condition; responsibility rests plainly with the President and the statutory framework.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single-subject waiver of subsections (a) and (b) of section 402 with respect to one country; no distinct remedies or frames are packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The waiver lifts a statutory trade restriction and imposes no ongoing behavioral standard on any party to be enforced.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A trade-restriction waiver creates no ongoing compliance relationship or incentive structure for a regulated party.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The waiver is granted with no expiration or reauthorization trigger stated in the order's text; a country-specific trade waiver whose continuation should track periodic review provides none in the instrument itself.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order recites having made the section 402(c)(2) report to Congress rather than bypassing it; it removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Lifting a trade restriction creates no ongoing violations for any external actor to surface.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A country-specific statutory waiver operating within the Trade Act framework; no precedent or authority claim reaching beyond the stated subject is created by the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's statutory mandate is redirected; a direct presidential statutory waiver implicating no other agency's operational authority.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order is itself a single statutory waiver, not a coverage instrument with an internal exemption structure.

note: Narrow statutory trade waiver (structurally parallel to order-042); the order text lacks signature/date block internally so date is taken from the record, and Flag 6 fires for the absence of a stated expiration on a country-specific waiver warranting periodic review.

title: Amending Subdivision VIII of Schedule A of the Civil Service Rules

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The amendment adds one position (the Solicitor of the Department of the Interior) to a Schedule A excepted-service list; it pools no discretionary decision authority in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: A single, self-executing classification change to the Civil Service Rules; there is no implementation chain whose failure would raise a responsibility gap.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order contains a single provision (adding one numbered paragraph); there is nothing to bundle it against.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Per the diff-aware convention, only the amended provision is assessed; adding one position to an excepted-service schedule imposes no ongoing behavioral rule to enforce.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The amendment creates no ongoing compliance relationship or incentive structure for any actor class.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing classification change to a standing rule grants no ongoing authority to sunset; the underlying Civil Service Rules are an evergreen procedural framework maintained by amendment.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The amendment removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; it adjusts one entry on a classification schedule.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: A single classification change creates no ongoing violations for any third party to surface.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow administrative reclassification of one position with no systemic or precedential reach.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's statutory mandate is implicated by adding one Interior position to a Civil Service schedule.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Although the amendment excepts one position from competitive-service requirements, it adds a single, specifically named position to a defined schedule rather than creating a broad or discretionary carve-out structure that swallows a rule.

note: Single-provision administrative amendment adding one position to a Civil Service excepted-service schedule; most compliance-regime and systemic flags are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE, and the applicable flags (1, 2, 7, 11) are clean.

title: Nuclear Safety Oversight Committee

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order creates a five-member advisory committee that reports to the President and Cabinet secretaries; it monitors and advises but holds no operational or enforcement authority over the NRC, industry, or states (Secs. 1-2).

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Committee has a designated Chairman, defined reporting obligations (at least annually), and named supporting agencies (DOE, HEW, GSA); responsibility for its deliverables is clearly located (Secs. 1-3).

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent charge (advise on nuclear-safety progress and TMI-recommendation implementation); the enumerated advisory functions all serve that one purpose.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: An advisory committee issues recommendations, not enforcement; it creates no compliance standard applied to any regulated party (it obtains information by agreement, Sec. 1-209).

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule or regulated-party compliance relationship is created; a monitoring/advisory body produces no incentive to undermine a compliance purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The Committee 'shall terminate on September 30, 1980' (Sec. 1-402) — an explicit sunset date roughly six months out.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds an oversight/monitoring layer atop existing NRC functions and preserves the President's FACA congressional reporting (Sec. 1-401); it removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism, and expressly only advises on possible modification of the Advisory Committee for Reactor Safety (Sec. 1-202) rather than eliminating it.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: An advisory monitoring committee generates no violations to surface through an enforcement chain; its information-gathering is by inter-agency agreement, not third-party enforcement.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A time-limited advisory committee monitoring post-TMI reforms; no authority claim or precedent reaching beyond its advisory role is created by the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Committee monitors and reports on NRC, FEMA, DOE, and HEW activities and enters agreements with them for information (Sec. 1-209), but redirects or countermands none of their statutory mandates; it is an overlay advisory body, not a competing authority.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order confers no coverage from which anyone could be exempted; it establishes an internal advisory body.

note: Time-limited nuclear-safety advisory/monitoring committee with an explicit sunset and preserved congressional reporting; advisory-only nature makes compliance-regime flags NOT_APPLICABLE and the rest clean.

title: Authorizing the Secretary of the Navy to Take Possession of and Operate Certain Plants and Facilities Used in the Transportation, Refining and Processing of Petroleum and Petroleum Products

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Navy is authorized to seize and operate the listed petroleum plants 'in any manner that he deems necessary,' hire agents, exercise owners' contractual rights, and take 'any other steps that he deems necessary' — broad discretionary operational and property authority pooled in one official under the War Labor Disputes Act.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary determines the scope of possession and 'appropriate disciplinary action...as may be necessary' against employees with the standard self-defined; if the seizure or its labor measures are mishandled, no actor other than the same Secretary is positioned to correct it.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (maintain wartime petroleum production despite labor disturbances); the seizure, operation, and protection provisions all serve that one aim, applied uniformly to the enumerated facilities.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The authority to take 'such appropriate disciplinary action, not inconsistent with law, as may be necessary' against employees and to take 'any other steps that he deems necessary' supplies no standard, so two officials could apply the seizure's coercive measures very differently in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The seizure directly serves its stated purpose of restoring petroleum production; no design feature rewards undermining that purpose. Authority rests on the Selective Service Act as amended by the active War Labor Disputes Act, not a lapsed statute.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Possession 'shall be terminated by the Secretary of the Navy as soon as practicable, but in no event more than sixty days after he determines that the productive efficiency...has been restored' (Sec. 7) — an explicit end condition tied to resolution of the disruption.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order directs the FBI/DOJ to investigate at the Secretary's request and enlists NLRB, Labor, Interior, and OPA cooperation without removing any pre-existing external oversight mechanism; it operates under the War Labor Disputes Act's terms (Sec. 3).

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: A government seizure and operation of plants creates no ongoing regulated-party compliance regime for an external third party to police; the government itself operates the facilities.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Seizing and operating an entire industry's plants nationwide to override labor disturbances establishes a broad, reusable precedent for executive commandeering of private industry and coercive labor measures under emergency authority, applicable well beyond petroleum.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order enlists the cooperation of Justice, NLRB, Labor, Interior, and OPA 'to the fullest extent of their authority' (Sec. 6) rather than redirecting or countermanding their statutory mandates.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order applies to an enumerated list of named facilities under government operation; it creates no general coverage population with an exemption structure to map.

note: Wartime petroleum-plant seizure (structurally similar to order-044) with broad discretionary Navy authority firing Flags 1, 2, 4, and precedent Flag 9, but an explicit 60-day termination condition (Flag 6 ABSENT) and cooperative rather than override treatment of other agencies keep it below CRITICAL.

title: Amending Executive Order No. 11246, Relating to Equal Employment Opportunity

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Per the diff-aware convention, only the amended provisions are assessed; adding 'sex' to the protected categories and refining the Civil Service Commission complaint-review and contractor-clause language pools no new authority — enforcement authority remains distributed to the Secretary of Labor and contracting agencies as under EO 11246 (predecessor available and reviewed).

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The amendment strengthens accountability by directing the CSC to provide 'prompt, fair, and impartial consideration of all complaints' with at least one impartial review and appeal to the CSC (revised Sec. 104); responsibility for complaint handling is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The amendment addresses a single coherent change (extending the existing EEO framework to sex discrimination); each revised section serves that one purpose and none packages an unrelated policy.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The amended contractor clauses import EO 11246's robust, defined enforcement machinery (Compliance Reports, Secretary of Labor sanctions, cancellation/debarment, hearings, DOJ referral); the amendment adds 'sex' to a precisely specified enforcement standard rather than introducing an ambiguous one.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Extending nondiscrimination coverage to sex creates no incentive for any actor to undermine the equal-opportunity purpose; the amended affirmative-action and compliance-reporting obligations align contractor incentives with the stated goal.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The amended anti-discrimination mandate is evergreen by nature (a rights guarantee with no natural end-state), squarely within the permanence carve-out; its permanence is appropriate, not a design gap, and staggered effective dates show ordinary implementation phasing.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The amendment adds a CSC impartial-review-and-appeal requirement for federal-employment complaints (revised Sec. 104), strengthening rather than removing oversight, and preempts no pre-existing check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The inherited EO 11246 framework gives employees and prospective employees standing to file complaints investigated by the Secretary of Labor (EO 11246 Sec. 206(b)); enforcement does not depend solely on regulated-party self-reporting, and the amendment preserves that complaint channel (revised Sec. 104).

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Extending the federal-contractor and federal-employment nondiscrimination framework to sex established a durable precedent applying the contract-conditioning enforcement model to a new protected class, importing coverage into private employment through federal procurement leverage well beyond the immediate amendment.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The amendment revises CSC and Secretary of Labor / contracting-agency roles within the existing EO 11246 allocation and (revised Sec. 203(d)) references the Secretary of Labor's established authority; it redirects no agency's statutory mandate into conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The amendment adds a protected class and does not touch the exemption provisions; the inherited EO 11246 exemption authority (Sec. 204) is bounded by enumerated categories and a proviso that exemptions must not impede the order's purposes, so no rule-swallowing carve-out is introduced by this amendment.

note: Partial amendment of EO 11246 (predecessor available and reviewed); per the diff-aware convention only the sex-coverage additions and CSC/contractor-clause revisions were assessed. These are coverage expansions with preserved robust enforcement, clean except for the precedential second-order effect (Flag 9).

title: Establishing a Committee and a Council Relating to the Status of Women

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order creates an interdepartmental committee (chaired by the Secretary of Labor, composed of ex officio Cabinet members) and a 17-member citizens' advisory council; both exercise only review, clearinghouse, and advisory functions with no operational or enforcement authority pooled in any single official (Secs. 102, 202).

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Committee is chaired by the Secretary of Labor with defined annual reporting to the President (Sec. 103) and named financing/administration responsibilities (Sec. 301); responsibility for the bodies' work is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (advancing the status of women through coordinated review and advice); the Committee and Council are complementary facets of that one aim, not distinct bundled policies.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Both bodies review, coordinate, and advise; they enforce nothing and impose no compliance standard on any regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule or regulated-party compliance relationship is created; advisory/coordinating bodies produce no incentive to undermine a compliance purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Neither the Interdepartmental Committee nor the Citizens' Advisory Council carries a termination date or reauthorization trigger; unlike the fixed-sunset advisory bodies in this batch, these are established as standing bodies continuing indefinitely until revoked, so the absence-of-sunset flag fires for this discretionary administrative structure.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds coordinating and advisory machinery and removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; agencies cooperate 'to the extent practical and not inconsistent with law' (Sec. 301).

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Advisory/coordinating bodies generate no violations to surface; there is no enforcement chain for a third party to enter.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The bodies review progress and stimulate cooperation with no authority claim or definitional framework reaching beyond their advisory/coordinating role; no identifiable systemic downstream effect from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Committee is composed of Cabinet members for coordinated review and asks agencies to furnish information/assistance (Sec. 301); it redirects or countermands no agency's statutory mandate. This order establishes new bodies rather than amending the referenced EO 10980 (predecessor available), whose Commission had submitted its report.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order confers no coverage from which anyone could be exempted; it establishes internal coordinating and advisory bodies.

note: Establishes a standing interdepartmental committee and citizens' advisory council (following up EO 10980's Commission, whose text was available); advisory-only nature makes compliance-regime flags NOT_APPLICABLE, and the only fired flag is Flag 6 because, unlike the batch's other advisory bodies, these carry no termination date.

title: Authorizing the Secretary of the Interior to Take Possession of and Operate the Mines, Collieries, and Preparation Facilities of the Ford Collieries Company of Curtisville, Pennsylvania, and Rochester and Pittsburgh Coal Company of Indiana, Pennsylvania

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Interior is authorized to seize and operate the named mines 'in such manner as he deems necessary,' take possession of related property, and act 'through or with the aid of such public or private instrumentalities or persons as he may designate' — broad discretionary operational authority pooled in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary determines the scope of operation and 'appropriate disciplinary action, not inconsistent with law, as may be necessary' against employees with the standard self-defined; if mishandled, no actor other than the same Secretary is positioned to correct it.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (maintain wartime coal production despite labor disturbances at the two named companies); the seizure and operation provisions all serve that one aim.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The authority to 'take such appropriate disciplinary action, not inconsistent with law, as may be necessary' against employees (Sec. 2) supplies no standard, so two officials could apply the seizure's coercive labor measures very differently in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The seizure directly serves its stated purpose of restoring coal production; no design feature rewards undermining that purpose. Authority rests on the Selective Training and Service Act as amended by the active War Labor Disputes Act, not a lapsed statute.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Possession 'shall be terminated by the Secretary of the Interior within sixty days after he determines that the productive efficiency...has been restored' (Sec. 5) — an explicit end condition tied to resolution of the disruption.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order directs the War Manpower Commission, Selective Service, War Department, and DOJ to cooperate (Sec. 3) and operates under the War Labor Disputes Act's terms (Sec. 2) without removing any pre-existing external oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: A government seizure and operation of the mines creates no ongoing regulated-party compliance regime for an external third party to police; the government itself operates the facilities.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Seizing and operating private mines to override labor disturbances establishes a reusable precedent for executive commandeering of private industry and coercive labor measures under emergency authority, applicable beyond these two named companies.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order directs named agencies to cooperate 'to the fullest extent possible' (Sec. 3) rather than redirecting or countermanding their statutory mandates.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order applies to two named companies' facilities under government operation; it creates no general coverage population with an exemption structure to map.

note: Wartime coal-mine seizure (structurally parallel to orders 044 and 057) with broad discretionary Interior authority firing Flags 1, 2, 4, and precedent Flag 9, but an explicit 60-day termination condition (Flag 6 ABSENT) and cooperative treatment of other agencies keep it below CRITICAL.

title: Declassification of Selected Records Within the National Archives of the United States

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Declassification is self-executing by the order itself; the only discretion (withholding under a FOIA exemption) requires joint agreement of the affected agency head and the Archivist, not a single unchecked official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Archivist is named and obligated to make records public within 30 days, a defined actor with a defined duty and deadline.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent subject—declassifying a listed set of records; no distinct remedies or problem frames are packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The withholding standard ties to an 'authorized exemption to the Freedom of Information Act...other than the exemption that pertains to national security information'—a defined, cross-referenced legal standard.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No actor benefits from undermining the declassification; the design pushes toward disclosure with a narrow, statute-bound exception.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is a one-time, self-executing declassification with a 30-day completion window; there is no ongoing grant of authority to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed; the order expands public access and preserves FOIA processes.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Public researchers gain direct access and standing under FOIA to contest improper withholding, creating a genuine external check on withholding determinations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order applies only to a specific enumerated list of records and disclaims creating any enforceable right; no precedent usable in unrelated contexts is established.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Archivist and affected agency heads act within their existing statutory roles; no agency mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Withholding is available for 'specific information' under any FOIA exemption other than national security, controlled jointly by the agency head and Archivist with no defined ceiling on how much can be withheld—giving the affected agency discretion to carve out material from the ordered disclosure.

note: Predominantly a one-time declassification, so Flag 6 is NOT_APPLICABLE; but the FOIA-exemption withholding creates an ongoing disclosure-determination relationship, so Flags 4/5/8/11 were assessed on the merits rather than waved off.

title: Establishing a Flag for the United States Navy

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order simply approves an already-recommended flag design; no discretionary decision authority is pooled anywhere.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: A symbolic adoption with nothing to fail; no implementation chain requiring a responsible actor.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single-provision symbolic order with nothing to bundle against—only one action, adopting a flag design.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule or compliance obligation exists; adopting a flag creates nothing to enforce.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No regulated actor or incentive structure is created by the adoption of a flag design.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: No ongoing grant of authority exists; a flag adoption is a one-time, self-executing symbolic act.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is touched or removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance relationship exists for any third party to have standing over.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Narrow symbolic instrument with no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the Navy/Defense are involved in their own recommended action; no second agency's mandate is implicated.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: There is no coverage population from which anyone could be exempted.

note: Purely symbolic flag-adoption order; Flags 1, 2, and 7 remain answerable (all ABSENT) per the decision-structure rule, while the compliance-regime and systemic flags are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE.

title: Placing an Additional Position in Level IV of the Federal Executive Salary Schedule

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A ministerial pay-classification adding one named position (Deputy Administrator, FHWA) to Level IV; no discretionary authority is concentrated.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: A self-executing schedule amendment with no implementation to fail and no ambiguous responsibility.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single-item amendment adding one position; nothing to bundle against per the one-provision NOT_APPLICABLE rule.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance obligation—a fixed pay-level assignment creates nothing to enforce against anyone's conduct.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No regulated-party incentive structure is created by placing a position in a salary level.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: One-time, self-executing salary-schedule amendment; no ongoing authority grant to sunset (and, per the diff-aware check, it merely appends to EO 11248's continuing pay-schedule framework).

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No existing oversight mechanism is removed; the amendment operates within the statutory salary-reform framework.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance relationship exists for a third party to police.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Narrow administrative pay action with no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: It touches one DOT position within the government-wide salary schedule; no agency's operational mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population and no exemption structure exists.

note: Partial amendment of EO 11248 adding one Level IV position; predecessor read and confirms a continuing pay-schedule framework revised by routine supersession/amendment. Decision-structure flags (1, 2, 7, 10) scored ABSENT; compliance-regime and systemic flags NOT_APPLICABLE.

title: Establishing the President's Foreign Intelligence Advisory Board

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Board is purely advisory—it 'shall advise the President' and 'report thereon to the President'—holding no eligibility, enforcement, or implementation authority; decision authority remains with the President.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Board reviews and reports on CIA and other agencies but has no corrective authority; if intelligence functions fail, the Board can only report, and no actor is assigned obligation-plus-capacity to correct—responsibility diffuses back to the President.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose—standing up an advisory review board; no distinct remedies or problem frames are packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Board enforces nothing against any regulated party; it advises and reports, so there is no enforcement standard to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Members drawn from outside government reviewing the intelligence community create no evident incentive to undermine the advisory purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The Board is created with no termination date or reauthorization trigger—unlike its revoked predecessor EO 10656 (also open-ended)—and an ongoing advisory body is not evergreen by nature the way a rights mandate is, so the absence of any sunset is a design gap.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The Board adds a new external review layer over the intelligence community rather than removing any existing oversight; per the direction rule, adding an imperfect check does not fire this flag.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No regulated-party compliance regime exists; the Board itself is the reviewing party, so there is no separate violation for a third party to surface.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Creating an advisory board reporting to the President establishes no authority claim or definitional precedent usable to expand power in unrelated contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Board reviews CIA and other intelligence agencies but does not redirect or countermand their statutory operational mandates; agencies retain their functions and merely furnish information.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population is defined and no exemption structure exists—the order creates a body, not a rule with exemptions.

note: Revokes EO 10656 (read); order 064 largely re-creates a similar open-ended advisory board with broad information-access rights. Flag 2 fires on report-only accountability and Flag 6 on the absence of any termination mechanism for a standing (non-evergreen) advisory body.

title: Authorizing the Secretary of War to Take Possession of and Operate the Plants and Facilities of Cudahy Brothers Company of Cudahy, Wisconsin

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of War is authorized to take possession, operate 'in any manner that he deems necessary,' exercise all contractual rights, employ persons, and 'take any other steps that he deems necessary'—sweeping seizure and operating authority pooled in one official with no external check specified in the order.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary decides when to seize, how to operate, and (Sec. 4) himself determines when productive efficiency has been restored to trigger termination—self-judged success with no external actor able to correct him.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose—seizing one named plant to prevent a war-effort production interruption; no distinct remedies bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The operative standards—'any manner that he deems necessary,' 'any other thing that he may deem necessary,' 'such action, if any, as he may deem necessary'—are entirely discretionary, so two officials could reach very different operating decisions in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Termination is keyed to the Secretary's own determination that pre-disturbance efficiency 'has been restored,' giving the operating authority an incentive to define restoration on its own terms and prolong control; the underlying War Labor Disputes Act was an active wartime statute, so the zombie sub-flag does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Sec. 4 supplies a defined termination trigger—possession must end within 60 days after the Secretary determines pre-interruption productive efficiency is restored—so an end-condition tied to the emergency's resolution exists.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed; the order directs operation 'pursuant to the War Labor Disputes Act' and observance of the NWLB directive, adding rather than stripping constraints.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Whether the plant is properly operated and timely returned depends entirely on the Secretary's own assessment; no external party has standing or access to surface mis-operation or premature/delayed termination.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The seizure asserts a broad presidential/War-Department precedent to take and operate private industrial property on a single official's findings, a template readily extendable to other plants and labor disputes beyond this one facility.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order channels operation through the War Labor Disputes Act and NWLB directive; it does not redirect or countermand another agency's statutory operational mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: A one-time seizure of a single named facility defines no broad coverage population from which exemptions could be carved.

note: Wartime single-plant seizure; the emergency statute (War Labor Disputes Act) was active, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire, and Sec. 4's efficiency-restoration termination trigger clears Flag 6 despite the broad grant.

title: Amending the Selective Service Regulations

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Occupational deferment now turns on activities 'identified as essential by the Director of Selective Service upon the advice of the National Security Council,' and the Director may unilaterally 'temporarily suspend' the new preinduction-exam right for whole States—discretionary eligibility and suspension authority pooled in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Director both defines essential-activity criteria and controls suspension of the examination right, with the same office judging necessity; if deferment administration miscarries, no external actor is assigned obligation and capacity to correct it within these amendments.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The single order packages substantively distinct changes—tightening occupational/agricultural deferments, phasing out paternity deferments, and creating a new voluntary preinduction-examination right—that a reviewer cannot accept or reject separately.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Deferment hinges on undefined judgments—'necessary to the maintenance of the national health, safety, or interest' and 'material loss of effectiveness'—that different local boards could apply to reach materially different classifications in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The amendments tighten and phase out deferment categories rather than rewarding gaming; the underlying Military Selective Service Act was active, so the zombie sub-flag does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: These amend a standing procedural framework (the Selective Service Regulations) maintained through ordinary supersession/amendment—the order itself amends a long chain of numbered predecessor EOs—so it fits the standing-procedural-framework carve-out.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The Director's power to 'temporarily suspend the provisions of this paragraph for particular States' lets one official switch off the newly granted preinduction-examination right without any review process, effectively disabling a check on his own workload judgment.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Deferment determinations run between the registrant and his local board with no external actor holding standing and access to surface erroneous essential-activity or occupational classifications.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Tying occupational deferment to activities the Director designates 'upon the advice of the National Security Council' embeds NSC influence into individual draft classification, a precedent linking national-security discretion to who is exposed to induction.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The NSC role is advisory to the Director and the armed-forces examining stations act in their normal capacity; no agency's statutory operational mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order restructures the deferment (exemption-from-induction) architecture, with essential-activity carve-outs controlled by the Director's discretion and grandfather clauses, i.e., coverage from the draft turning on discretionary occupational designations.

note: Partial-but-substantial amendment of the standing Selective Service Regulations (predecessor framework EO 10001 read); scored as modifications to an ongoing conscription compliance regime. Flag 6 cleared via the standing-procedural-framework carve-out given the explicit chain of superseded predecessor EOs.

title: Modification of Executive Order No. 6910 of November 26, 1934, as Amended, Withdrawing Public Lands in Certain States

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order self-executingly modifies EO 6910's blanket withdrawal only to the extent needed to let the Secretary of the Interior withdraw described tracts for reclamation under a specific statute; no open-ended discretionary authority is pooled.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The action is a defined land-status modification enabling a statutorily bounded reclamation withdrawal; no implementation chain with diffuse responsibility exists.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single-purpose land-status modification; nothing distinct is bundled against it.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A land-status modification creates no ongoing behavioral rule for anyone to comply with, so there is no enforcement standard to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No regulated-party incentive structure arises from re-designating specific tracts for reclamation.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: One-time, self-executing land-status modification; there is no ongoing grant of authority to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is limited or removed; it operates within existing withdrawal/reclamation statutes.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance relationship exists for a third party to police.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Narrow tract-specific land modification with no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: It enables Interior to act under its own reclamation authority within the EO 6910 framework; no other agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population and no exemption structure exists.

note: Partial modification of EO 6910 (predecessor read: a broad temporary land withdrawal); this order narrowly carves specific tracts out to enable a statutorily bounded reclamation withdrawal. Decision-structure flags ABSENT; compliance-regime/systemic flags NOT_APPLICABLE for a one-time land action.

title: Revoking Rhodesian Sanctions

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Revocation authority is exercised by the President and residual enforcement/implementation is distributed among the Secretaries of State, Treasury, Commerce, and Transportation—no single official holds concentrated discretion.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Named Secretaries retain responsibility for enforcing the prior orders as to pre-revocation transactions and for implementing this order, with State designated as coordinator—responsibility is clearly assigned.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose—terminating Rhodesian/Zimbabwe-Rhodesia trade sanctions—by revoking three related predecessor orders; no distinct remedies bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The savings clauses precisely preserve pre-existing liabilities and violations 'as if the revocation had not been made,' and continuing determinations remain 'until amended...by appropriate authority'—defined enforcement terms during wind-down.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Preserving liability for pre-revocation violations removes any incentive to have raced to violate before the effective date; the design avoids rewarding evasion.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This terminates authority rather than granting ongoing authority; a revocation is self-executing with nothing to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order preserves all pending suits, proceedings, and enforcement for prior-period conduct; it removes a sanctions program but does not disable any oversight or review mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The order winds down a coverage regime rather than creating an ongoing compliance rule; residual enforcement is for closed past conduct, leaving no forward compliance relationship for third parties to police.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The revocation is tightly scoped to Zimbabwe-Rhodesia sanctions with careful savings clauses; it establishes no authority claim or definition usable to expand power in unrelated contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The multiple named Secretaries act within their existing enforcement roles and are directed to consult with State; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or set in conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Removing sanctions eliminates a coverage regime rather than creating exemptions within one; there is no ongoing rule from which actors are carved out.

note: Revokes EO 11322/11419/11978 (11322 read, confirming a detailed sanctions regime); this is a clean, carefully-savings-claused wind-down, so compliance-regime and sunset flags are NOT_APPLICABLE and the decision-structure flags all clear ABSENT.

title: Reserving Certain Public Lands as Administrative Sites for the Cabeza Prieta Game Range and the Kofa Game Range

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order self-executingly withdraws 80 described acres and reserves them for Interior's use as game-range administrative sites; no discretionary decision authority is created or pooled.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: A one-time land reservation with no implementation chain that could fail and no ambiguous responsibility.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single-purpose land reservation; nothing distinct is bundled against it.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A land withdrawal/reservation creates no ongoing behavioral compliance rule, so there is no enforcement standard to evaluate.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No regulated actor or incentive structure arises from reserving specific tracts.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: One-time, self-executing land reservation; no ongoing grant of authority to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight is removed; the reservation merely supersedes EO 6910's broader temporary withdrawal as to these specific lands, subject to valid existing rights.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance relationship exists for any third party to police.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Narrow, tract-specific reservation with no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The reservation is for the Department of the Interior's own use and supersedes only a prior withdrawal's coverage of these tracts; no other agency's statutory operational mandate is redirected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population and no exemption structure exists.

note: Supersedes EO 6910 (predecessor read) only as to specific described tracts; a clean one-time land reservation. Decision-structure flags (1, 2, 7, 10) ABSENT; compliance-regime/systemic flags NOT_APPLICABLE.

title: Prescribing Rates of Charges for Certain Hospitalization and Dispensary Services and Delegating Authority to Prescribe Such Rates

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Beyond setting fixed rates, Sec. 2 delegates the President's statutory rate-setting authority (via amendment to EO 10530) to be exercised 'without the approval...of the President,' and Sec. 1's rates become subject to amendment/revocation at any time under that delegated authority—an ongoing discretionary rate power vested downward.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The delegation is to a defined officer/function performing the President's Act-of-May-10-1943 authority, and the fixed rates are self-executing; responsibility for future rate changes is clearly located in the delegated authority.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order combines two distinct actions under one instrument—prescribing specific dollar rates (Sec. 1) and creating a standing delegation of rate-setting authority (Sec. 2)—which are separable policy questions packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The rates are precise fixed dollar amounts per service class and the delegated authority is a bounded statutory rate-prescribing function; a reader gets consistent, defined charges rather than an ambiguous standard.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Fixed charges for hospitalization/dispensary services with a delegated adjustment mechanism create no evident incentive for any actor to undermine the order's purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is a standing rate-administration function maintained through ordinary supersession—the order itself revokes EO 9411, sets an effective date, and makes Sec. 1 rates expressly 'subject to amendment, supersedure, or revocation...at any time'—fitting the standing-procedural-framework carve-out.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed; delegating a routine rate-setting function under EO 10530 does not disable any external review that previously applied.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The order sets published charges rather than a compliance rule policed by self-report; the fixed public rates give paying parties direct notice and a basis to contest overcharging, so no third-party detection gap exists.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Institutionalizing rate-setting as a standing delegated function (added as EO 10530 Sec. 1(w)) makes future rate changes routine and low-visibility, shifting them out of presidential-level action—an institutional precedent that persists beyond this specific rate schedule.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The delegation slots a rate-setting function into the existing EO 10530 delegation scheme; no other agency's statutory operational mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The rate schedule distinguishes payer classes (non-citizen US employees vs. all others) as pricing tiers, not as carve-outs from coverage; no discretionary exemption structure swallows the rule.

note: Combines a fixed rate schedule with a standing delegation of rate-setting authority (amending EO 10530, read) and revokes EO 9411 (read). The ongoing delegation gives it a live regulatory relationship, so all 11 flags apply; Flags 1, 3, and 9 fire on the standing delegation.

title: Authorizing the Secretary of the Interior to Take Possession of and to Operate Certain Coal Mines

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Secretary of the Interior alone is authorized to seize and operate 'any and all such mines' and property 'to the extent that he may deem necessary,' with no external check on the seizure decision.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The implementing Secretary determines what possession is necessary and when to terminate it; no external actor is charged with correcting operational failure.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single remedy (seizure/operation of coal mines) under a single war-effort frame; nothing unrelated is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Operation 'in such manner as he may deem necessary' and possession of property 'to the extent he may deem necessary' set no defined standard; two officials could act quite differently.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Authority rests on an active, unexpired war/labor-disputes statute; the order ties termination to restored productive efficiency, creating no incentive to prolong seizure. No acknowledged statutory lapse.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Sec. 8 requires termination 'in no event more than sixty days after the restoration of the productive efficiency' — a defined end-condition tied to the emergency's resolution.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight is removed; wage changes remain subject to the National Wage Stabilization Board and Economic Stabilization Director.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Operation is government-run with recognized collective-bargaining and grievance procedures for workers, providing external actors with standing to surface problems.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Establishes and normalizes executive seizure of private industry under labor-dispute conditions, a precedent usable in contexts beyond coal.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Directs all federal agencies and the Secretary of War to cooperate/provide protection; no agency's statutory mandate is countermanded or redirected against its purpose.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No carve-outs or waiver structure that would exempt actors from coverage.

note: War-powers seizure order with a defined termination trigger, keeping Flags 5 and 6 clean despite broad discretionary language firing Flags 1, 2, and 4.

title: Establishment of the Retraining and Reemployment Administration

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Administrator acts 'subject to the general supervision of the Director of War Mobilization' and with a nine-member Retraining and Reemployment Policy Board; authority is distributed, not pooled.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Administration is directed to 'advise with the appropriate committees of the Congress,' and the Director supervises; responsibility lines are traceable.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent frame — reconversion retraining/reemployment; the subsidiary functions all serve that one purpose.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Grants 'general supervision and direction of the activities of all Government agencies' on retraining and power to 'issue necessary regulations and directions' with no defined standard or trigger.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No design feature rewards undermining the reemployment purpose; authority derives from an active First War Powers Act, no acknowledged lapse.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Creates a standing administration exercising discretionary supervisory authority tied to war-transition, an inherently temporary condition, yet provides no expiration or reauthorization mechanism.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 5 amends prior orders only 'so far as they are in conflict'; it adds a coordinating body rather than removing any pre-existing external check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Order creates an inter-agency coordinating body developing programs, not an ongoing compliance rule for regulated parties, so no third-party enforcement chain exists to assess.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Erects a new standing bureaucracy with directive authority over multiple agencies' activities, an institutional change that persists and expands centralized program control.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Assigns the Administration 'general supervision and direction of the activities of all Government agencies' on retraining, overlaying existing statutory mandates of Labor, VA, Selective Service, etc., creating jurisdictional friction.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population subject to exemptions; the order structures a program office rather than a rule from which actors could be carved out.
