note: A coordinating-agency creation; compliance-regime Flags 8 and 11 are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE because it creates no ongoing regulated-party relationship, while Flag 10 fires on its directive authority over other agencies' mandates.

title: Evaluation of Flood Hazard in Locating Federally Owned or Financed Buildings, Roads, and Other Facilities, and in Disposing of Federal Lands and Properties

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Duties are distributed across 'the heads of the executive agencies,' each acting within its own domain; no single official holds concentrated authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each agency self-evaluates flood hazards 'as far as practicable' and reports to itself; no named actor is responsible when an agency simply declines to preclude a hazardous use.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single frame (reduce federal flood-plain exposure) applied across construction, grants, disposal, and planning; provisions are variants of one purpose.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Standards are 'as far as practicable' and 'whenever practical and economically feasible'; two agency heads could reach very different determinations in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Agencies both evaluate the hazard and decide whether avoidance is 'practicable,' incentivizing declaring compliance impractical to avoid restricting their own projects or sale prices.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Establishes an open-ended discretionary evaluation regime with no expiration or reauthorization mechanism, persisting until revoked.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Adds a new appropriations-request statement requirement to the Bureau of the Budget; removes no pre-existing oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Compliance rests entirely on agency self-evaluation and self-reporting; no external actor has standing and information to surface an agency's failure to weigh flood hazards.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Institutionalizes federal influence over flood-plain land-use patterns via disposal restrictions and mortgage/loan conditioning, shaping private development beyond the stated purpose.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Each agency acts under its own authority; the Army/TVA provide information cooperatively, with no redirection or countermanding of another agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The repeated 'as far as practicable' qualifier functions as a self-granted exemption each agency can invoke to opt out of precluding hazardous use, undermining the coverage.

note: Self-evaluated, self-reported 'as far as practicable' discretion drives a cluster of firings (Flags 2, 4, 5, 8, 11) even though authority is distributed rather than concentrated.

title: American Heritage Rivers, Amending Executive Orders 13061 and 13080

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The amendment only changes a numeric cap ('ten' to 'up to 20'); the President's designation authority in the parent orders is unchanged and no new authority is pooled.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: No responsibility chain is altered; the parent orders' Committee/CEQ structure continues to operate.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single, targeted amendment to one number in two orders; nothing is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time numeric amendment creates no enforcement standard or ongoing compliance rule of its own to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule is created by raising a designation ceiling; no incentive structure attaches.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The amendment is a one-time, self-executing edit to a number; it grants no new ongoing authority that could be sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No oversight mechanism of the parent orders is removed or bypassed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement chain is established by the numeric change; the flag has nothing to assess.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Raising the designation cap from ten to twenty has no identifiable precedential or cross-domain consequence beyond the initiative's own scope.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The number change implicates no additional agency and redirects no mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population or exemption structure is touched by changing a designation count.

note: Diff-aware: predecessors EO 13061 and 13080 were available and read; this is a partial amendment of a single numeric provision, so only the always-answerable decision-structure flags (1, 2, 7) plus 3 and 9 apply, all cleanly ABSENT.

title: Excusing Federal Employees From Duty on December 24, and for One-Half Day on December 31, 1954

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A self-executing one-time closure on named dates; no discretionary decision authority is concentrated in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Nothing to fail administratively; the order is self-executing with no implementation outcome to hold anyone responsible for.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single-provision order (a specific holiday closure) with nothing to bundle against.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing closure creates no ongoing enforcement standard or compliance relationship.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule exists from which perverse incentives could arise.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The order is inherently self-terminating (fixed 1954 dates); there is no ongoing grant of authority to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight is removed; it references EO 10358's holiday definitions without disabling any check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime exists for any third party to police.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Narrow one-time administrative closure with no systemic or precedential implication.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's mandate is implicated or redirected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Although agency heads may exclude employees 'for special public reasons,' this is a one-time closure with no ongoing coverage population, so the exemptions flag does not apply.

note: Predecessor EO 10358 read (referenced only for holiday definitions, not amended). A one-time self-executing closure: only the always-answerable Flags 1, 2, 7 apply, all ABSENT; the discretionary-exclusion clause is noted but does not create an ongoing coverage regime for Flag 11.

title: Authorizing the Secretary of War to Take Possession of and Operate the Plants and Facilities of the American Enka Corporation Located at or Near Enka, North Carolina

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Sole authority to seize and operate the Enka plants 'in such manner as he may deem necessary' rests in the Secretary of War with no external check on the seizure.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary alone determines both the need to take and the point to relinquish possession; no external actor corrects operational failure.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single remedy and single war-effort frame directed at one named facility.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Operation 'in such manner as he may deem necessary for the effective prosecution of the war' supplies no defined standard for how the plant is run.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Authority rests on an active War Labor Disputes Act; termination tied to determination that possession is no longer necessary, creating no perverse incentive. No acknowledged lapse.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Possession 'shall be terminated by the Secretary of War within sixty days after he determines that such possession is no longer necessary,' a defined end-condition.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Operation is 'in accordance with the provisions of all applicable laws'; no pre-existing oversight is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Government-operated seizure under applicable laws; no self-reporting compliance regime that lacks external actors.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Reinforces the precedent of executive seizure of a specific private plant over a labor dispute, usable in analogous contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the Secretary of War acts; no other agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No exemption or waiver structure.

note: Single-facility war seizure; broad 'as he may deem necessary' discretion fires Flags 1, 2, 4, but the 60-day termination trigger keeps Flag 6 (and 5) clean.

title: Philadelphia Transportation Company, Possession and Control by the Secretary of War

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Sole authority to seize and control the transit systems and to use them 'for such purposes connected with the war emergency as he may deem needful or desirable' rests in the Secretary of War.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary alone judges both the taking and the termination ('when he determines that such possession and control are no longer necessary'); no external correction mechanism.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single remedy (seizure of one transit company) under a single war-emergency frame.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: 'Such purposes connected with the war emergency as he may deem needful or desirable' is an open-ended standard permitting widely divergent uses.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Authority rests on active wartime statutes with no acknowledged lapse; no structural incentive rewards prolonging control.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Authorized to terminate control 'when he determines that such possession and control are no longer necessary for purposes connected with the war emergency' — an end-condition tied to the emergency.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed or bypassed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Government-operated seizure; no self-reporting compliance regime lacking an external checker.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Extends the precedent of federal seizure to a municipal transit system over a labor dispute, a template applicable to other essential-service takeovers.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the Secretary of War is directed to act; no other agency's mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No exemption or waiver structure.

note: Terse war seizure with self-judged termination; the broad 'needful or desirable' discretion fires Flags 1, 2, 4, but the emergency-linked end-condition clears Flag 6.

title: Authorizing the Acquisition of Land Near Petersburg, Virginia, for Emergency Conservation Work

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authorizes a specific land acquisition under statutory authority, with expenditure directed by the Secretary of the Interior or the National Park Service; no discretionary power is pooled unaccountably.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Named actors (Director of ECW, Interior, NPS) handle the fixed-sum transfer and acquisition; responsibility is traceable.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single acquisition action serving one stated conservation purpose; nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time land acquisition with a fixed $30,000 allocation creates no ongoing enforcement standard or compliance relationship.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A fixed-sum, one-time acquisition establishes no ongoing behavioral rule from which perverse incentives could arise.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Self-executing one-time acquisition and fixed allocation; there is no ongoing grant of authority to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance regime exists for a third party to police.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Narrow, one-off land purchase for a specific park with no systemic or precedential implication.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The inter-agency fund transfer (ECW to Interior/NPS) is statutorily authorized and cooperative; no agency's mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population or exemption structure exists in a one-time acquisition.

note: Fixed-sum one-time land acquisition: compliance-regime and systemic flags are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE; only the decision-structure flags (1, 2, 7) plus bundling apply and are ABSENT.

title: Federal Civilian and Military Pay Increases

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Rates are adjusted 'pursuant to' specified statutory pay systems and cost-of-living statutes; no discretionary authority is concentrated.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: A self-executing schedule of statutorily prescribed adjustments; nothing to fail and no discretion requiring accountability.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Civilian, military, and executive/congressional/judicial pay are adjusted under one coherent statutory pay-adjustment frame; not an obscuring bundle.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Self-executing fixed pay schedules create no ongoing enforcement standard or compliance relationship.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A fixed schedule adjustment establishes no ongoing behavioral rule from which perverse incentives arise.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing rate adjustment with fixed effective dates; there is no ongoing grant of authority to sunset (and it is itself superseded by routine successors).

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Supersedes EO 12010 (the prior year's identical-type adjustment); removes no oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime exists for a third party to police.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A routine statutory pay adjustment with no identifiable precedential or cross-domain consequence.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No agency mandate is redirected or countermanded by adjusting pay schedules.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population subject to exemptions; the order sets rates, not a rule from which actors are carved out.

note: Diff-aware: predecessor EO 12010 read; EO 12087 supersedes it with a structurally identical fixed-schedule adjustment. A one-time statutory pay adjustment, so compliance-regime flags are NOT_APPLICABLE and the applicable decision-structure/frame flags are ABSENT.

title: Reaffirming Policy of Full Participation in the Defense Program by All Persons, Regardless of Race, Creed, Color, or National Origin, and Directing Certain Action in Furtherance of Said Policy

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Implementation is distributed across all departments, contracting agencies, and a five-member Committee on Fair Employment Practice; no single official holds concentrated authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Committee may 'receive and investigate complaints' and 'take appropriate steps to redress grievances' and 'recommend' measures, but no actor is given enforcement power or is clearly responsible when discrimination persists.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single anti-discrimination frame; the training, contract-clause, and committee provisions all serve that one purpose.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: 'Take appropriate steps to redress grievances' specifies no defined consequence for non-compliance or standard triggering enforcement, so implementing officials could act very differently.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No design feature rewards undermining the non-discrimination policy; contractors are obligated by contract clause. No acknowledged statutory lapse.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: An anti-discrimination rights mandate is evergreen by the nature of the problem (per the permanence carve-out, akin to EO 9981), so permanence is not a design gap here.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Creates new machinery (a complaints-investigating Committee and contract clauses); removes no pre-existing oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The Committee 'shall receive and investigate complaints,' giving affected workers (third parties) standing and a channel to surface violations, so enforcement does not rest solely on regulated-party self-reporting.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Establishes the precedent of federal anti-discrimination conditions in government contracting and a standing fair-employment body, a framework later extended broadly across federal contracting.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Directs contracting and training agencies within their own functions; no agency's statutory mandate is countermanded or redirected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No carve-outs or waivers; the non-discrimination duty applies to defense industries and government without exception.

note: Evergreen anti-discrimination mandate: Flag 6 clears under the permanence carve-out; the main weaknesses are the toothless 'appropriate steps' enforcement and undefined accountability of the FEPC (Flags 2, 4), with a real second-order contracting precedent (Flag 9).

title: Termination of Urban and Community Impact Analyses

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A one-sentence self-executing revocation concentrates no authority; it removes an internal OMB analysis procedure rather than pooling decision power.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: No implementation chain is created; the order simply extinguishes EO 12074, leaving no ambiguous responsible actor.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single provision (revocation) with one stated purpose; nothing is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A revocation creates no ongoing compliance relationship or enforcement mechanism to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule is created for any actor, so no incentive structure exists.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing revocation grants no continuing authority that could be sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The revoked EO 12074 was itself an internal management analysis procedure, not an external oversight check; no pre-existing oversight is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement chain or compliance regime exists over which a third party could have standing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Removing an internal analytical procedure produces no identifiable precedential or systemic downstream effect beyond ending the analysis itself.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's statutory operational mandate is redirected or countermanded; only an internal OMB procedure is ended.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population or exemption structure exists in a bare revocation.

note: Predecessor EO 12074 (revoked) was read; it was an internal OMB impact-analysis procedure, confirming the revocation removes no external oversight. Diff-aware review supports ABSENT on Flag 7.

title: Establishing the Union Slough Migratory Waterfowl Refuge

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A self-executing land reservation exercises no discretionary decision authority; lands are simply set apart for Agriculture use.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: No implementation chain that could fail; the reservation is complete on signing.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single-purpose land reservation with one frame; nothing bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time land reservation creates no ongoing compliance relationship to enforce.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule for any actor is established.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A one-time land-status action grants no continuing authority to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is limited or bypassed; the order is subject to valid existing rights.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime exists over which a third party could surface violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow land reservation has no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the Department of Agriculture is implicated as beneficiary; no conflicting agency mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population or exemption structure in a land reservation.

note: Self-executing one-time land reservation under the Migratory Bird Conservation Act; compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11), sunset (6), effects (9), and cannibalization (10) genuinely inapplicable.

title: Inspection of Income, Estate, and Gift Tax Returns by the Senate Committee on Government Operations

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Inspection authority is statutory (26 U.S.C. 6103(a)) and bounded by TD 6132 rules; no single official gains unchecked discretion.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Commissioner of Internal Revenue is named as specifically responsible for ensuring Privacy Act compliance.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose (committee inspection of returns) under one frame; nothing bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Conditions are precise: written statement of purpose, showing of jurisdiction, or written taxpayer consent, per TD 6132; two officials would apply consistently.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The conditions align actor behavior with the privacy-protective purpose; no reward for gaming the rule.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Authority is expressly time-bound to the Ninety-fourth Congress and return years 1955-1975, a built-in expiration.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds Privacy Act safeguards and written-statement checks rather than removing any pre-existing oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The Commissioner independently gatekeeps each inspection against defined conditions, providing an external check on the requesting committee.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Access is narrowly cabined by statute, year range, and one named committee; no precedent usable in unrelated contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: IRS/Treasury are implicated but their statutory mandate is not redirected or countermanded; the order operates within 6103.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The conditions are narrow gatekeeping requirements, not carve-outs that swallow coverage.

note: Time-bound, statutorily bounded access order with a named responsible official and precise conditions; clean across all applicable flags.

title: Drug Policy Functions

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Associate Director for Drug Policy is made 'primarily responsible' for coordinating and overseeing 'international as well as domestic drug abuse functions by all Executive agencies,' pooling cross-agency coordination in one staff official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The role is defined as assisting the President within the Domestic Policy Staff, so accountability runs cleanly to the President.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose (clarifying drug-policy responsibility within Domestic Policy Staff); nothing bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: An internal staff-organization order creates no external compliance relationship to enforce.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule for regulated parties or beneficiaries is established.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The coordinating/oversight authority is granted permanently with no review or reauthorization mechanism; discretionary coordination authority is not evergreen by nature.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed; the order reassigns already-transferred functions internally.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime exists over which a third party could have standing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Internal reassignment of drug-policy staff responsibility produces no identifiable precedent usable in unrelated contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: A White House coordinating role over agency drug functions does not countermand any agency's statutory operational mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population or exemption structure exists in an internal staff-organization order.

note: Internal EOP reorganization implementing Reorg Plan No. 1 of 1977 and EO 12045; Flag 1 fires modestly on single-official cross-agency oversight, Flag 6 on permanent discretionary coordination authority. Cited EO 12045 available but not material to the diff.

title: Partial Revocation of Certain Executive Orders Withdrawing Public Lands for Use as Air Navigation Sites

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A self-executing partial revocation of land withdrawals exercises no discretionary decision authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: No implementation chain that could fail; the restoration is complete on signing.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single-purpose land restoration; nothing bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time land-status revocation creates no ongoing compliance relationship to enforce.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule for any actor is established.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A one-time self-executing revocation grants no continuing authority to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is limited or bypassed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime exists over which a third party could surface violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow land restoration has no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only prior Commerce air-navigation withdrawals are ended for lands no longer required; no conflicting agency mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population or exemption structure in a land revocation.

note: Self-executing partial revocation restoring public lands no longer needed; predecessor withdrawal orders (EOs 4652, 4872, 4873) are pre-numbering-scheme and not required since this is a clean one-time land action.

title: Amending Executive Order Nos. 11803, 11837, and 11842 to Provide Authority to Increase the Number of Members of the Presidential Clemency Board

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The amendment grants the President unbounded discretion to appoint 'such additional members... as he shall from time to time determine to be necessary,' with no cap, replacing EO 11803's fixed 9-member board size.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Board membership and its work remain the President's responsibility; the amendment introduces no diffuse or circular accountability.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: A single membership-provision amendment; nothing bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The amended provision governs board size only, creating no enforcement relationship of its own to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The membership provision establishes no behavioral rule for any actor class.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The parent Clemency Board (EO 11803, Sec. 9) has an explicit termination date (Dec. 31, 1976), so the framework this provision expands already sunsets.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed by an authorization to enlarge the board.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The membership provision creates no compliance regime for third-party surfacing of violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Expanding a time-limited clemency board's size has no identifiable precedent usable in unrelated contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's statutory mandate is implicated by an EOP board's membership change.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: A membership-size provision defines no coverage population or exemption structure.

note: Diff-aware: predecessor EO 11803 was read (bounded, time-limited clemency board); this amendment swaps its fixed size for unbounded presidential discretion, firing Flag 1. Co-named predecessors EO 11837 and EO 11842 are NOT in referenced-predecessors, so their specific amended provisions could not be compared; assessment rests on the EO 11803 baseline and the amending text.

title: Establishing the National Wage Stabilization Board and Terminating the National War Labor Board

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The new Board is made 'independent with respect to its policies, decisions, and control of personnel' (Sec. 1(d)) and holds subpoena power (Sec. 3), concentrating wage-stabilization authority in one body.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Board is tripartite (public, employee, employer) and situated within the Department of Labor with a defined quorum; responsibility is locatable.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Coherent single frame (transition of wage-stabilization machinery from war to post-war); provisions serve that purpose.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Board operates under existing War Labor Board rules, regulations, and procedures continued in effect (Sec. 5), giving defined standards.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No design feature rewards non-compliance; the Board simply continues stabilization functions under existing statutory objectives.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Authority rests on the First War Powers Act and wartime Stabilization/Emergency Price Control Acts, an emergency-powers category that should track a natural end, yet the Board is created with no expiration or reauthorization trigger.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order reorganizes labor boards but removes no pre-existing external oversight; it continues prior rules and preserves the Wage Adjustment Board.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Wage disputes involve tripartite parties and dispute-case machinery, giving employees and employers standing to surface issues.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order continues an existing wage-stabilization framework into peacetime rather than establishing new precedent usable in unrelated domains.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: It terminates the National War Labor Board and transfers its 'personnel, records, property, funds, and outstanding obligations' (Sec. 4) to the new Board, reassigning an existing body's functions and resources.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No carve-out structure; the building-and-construction Wage Adjustment Board continuance is a defined jurisdictional allocation, not an exemption swallowing the rule.

note: War-powers reorganization; Flag 6 fires on emergency-derived authority lacking a natural-end mechanism (Zombie Trap does not fire, as the War Powers/Stabilization statutes were still active, not lapsed). Flag 10 fires on the termination-and-transfer of the War Labor Board. Predecessors EO 9017 and EO 9617 read; this order amends/substitutes para 2(b) of EO 9617.

title: Authorizing the Department of Defense and the Department of Commerce to Exercise the Functions and Powers Set Forth in Title II of the First War Powers Act, 1941, as Amended by the Act of January 12, 1951, and Prescribing Regulations for the Exercise of Such Functions and Powers

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Secretaries may exercise the authority and 'delegate such authority to any other military or civilian officers,' who may make 'further delegations' (Sec. 2), pooling broad contracting discretion in delegable single-official hands.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Comptroller General access (Sec. 11) and a complete-data requirement (Sec. 6) preserve locatable responsibility for contracting decisions.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent frame (defense-facilitating contracting authority); provisions serve it.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The operative standard is open-ended: contracts for anything 'necessary, appropriate or convenient for the national defense,' with modifications made whenever 'in the judgment of the Secretaries... the national defense will be thereby facilitated' (Secs. 3-4).

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Anti-kickback warranty (Sec. 10), bar on cost-plus-percentage (Sec. 12), and fee ceilings (Sec. 13) counter incentives to game the contracting authority.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Authority derives from wartime First War Powers Act (as extended Jan. 12, 1951), an emergency-powers category with a natural end, yet the order sets no expiration or reauthorization trigger.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: It authorizes contracting 'without regard to the provisions of law relating to the making, performance, amendment, or modification of contracts' (Sec. 1) and dispenses with advertising, competitive bidding, and bonds (Sec. 5), disabling standard procurement checks.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The Comptroller General's right of access to contractor and subcontractor books (Sec. 11) supplies an external actor with standing and access to surface irregularities.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Establishing broadly delegable authority to contract 'without regard to' procurement law creates precedent and institutional machinery usable well beyond any single procurement, normalizing bypass of ordinary contracting safeguards.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order extends parallel authority to Commerce under statutory grant (Part II) and preserves other Commerce procurement authority; it countermands no existing statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Sweeping waivers ('Advertising, competitive bidding, and bid, payment, performance or other bonds... need not be required,' Sec. 5) let officials bypass default procurement protections at discretion.

note: Waiver-heavy defense-contracting authority under active (non-lapsed) war-powers statute: Flags 1,4,6,7,9,11 fire; partial counterweights (Comptroller access, anti-kickback, fee caps) hold Flags 2,5,8 clean. Zombie Trap does not fire since the First War Powers Act was active/just-extended, not expired.

title: Establishing the President's Economic Policy Board, and for Other Purposes

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Board 'will oversee the formulation, coordination, and implementation of all economic policy' and 'serve as the focal point for economic policy decision-making,' with the Treasury Secretary as Chairman and 'principal spokesman' (Sec. 3), pooling economic-policy authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Board advises the President with a named Chairman and Executive Director (Secs. 3, 5); accountability runs to the President.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single frame (centralized economic-policy coordination); the conforming amendments to other councils serve that coordination purpose rather than bundling unrelated matter.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: An internal advisory/coordinating body creates no external compliance relationship to enforce.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule for regulated parties or beneficiaries is established.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The Board is created permanently with no review, expiration, or reauthorization mechanism; this discretionary coordinating structure is not evergreen by nature.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed; the order adds a coordinating layer and amends council memberships without disabling checks.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime exists over which a third party could have standing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Creating a coordinating board and adjusting council memberships produces no identifiable precedent usable in unrelated domains.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: A body positioned to oversee 'all economic policy' as the 'focal point for decision-making' sits atop cabinet departments and existing councils holding their own mandates, creating parallel authority with unclear hierarchy over agency economic functions.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population or exemption structure exists in an internal coordinating-body order.

note: Internal coordinating board; Flag 1 (focal point over all economic policy), Flag 6 (permanent discretionary authority), and Flag 10 (parallel authority over agencies' economic mandates) fire. Amended predecessors EO 11269 and EO 11789 available and reviewed; those amendments are minor membership changes not material to the diff.

title: Restoring Certain Lands Reserved for Military Purposes to the Jurisdiction of the Territory of Hawaii

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A self-executing restoration of lands to the Territory of Hawaii exercises no discretionary decision authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: No implementation chain that could fail; the restoration is complete on signing.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single-purpose land restoration; nothing bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time land-status restoration creates no ongoing compliance relationship to enforce.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule for any actor is established.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A one-time self-executing restoration grants no continuing authority to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is limited or bypassed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime exists over which a third party could surface violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow land restoration has no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Lands no longer needed for military purposes are returned to the Territory; no conflicting agency mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population or exemption structure in a land restoration.

note: Self-executing one-time restoration of surplus military lands to the Territory of Hawaii; compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11), sunset (6), effects (9), and cannibalization (10) genuinely inapplicable. Cited prior EOs describing the parcels (5240, 4679, 6408) are descriptive references, not amended baselines.

title: Reinstatement of Maxwell M. Hamilton, a Former Foreign Service Officer

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A self-executing one-time reinstatement of a single named officer under a specific statute; no discretionary authority is pooled anywhere, and the reinstatement is even conditioned on Senate confirmation.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Nothing to fail: the order effects a single personnel action with no implementing program or ongoing obligation whose failure would require a responsible actor.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single-provision order (one reinstatement) with nothing to bundle against; the flag's concept does not map onto it.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: One-time reinstatement creates no ongoing compliance relationship to enforce against anyone.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule or incentive structure for any actor class; a single personnel restoration.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing reinstatement grants no ongoing authority, so there is nothing to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Removes no pre-existing oversight; reinstatement is expressly subject to Senate confirmation, a retained external check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement chain exists for a completed personnel action; there is no violation for any third party to surface.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow individual personnel action under an express statutory reinstatement authority carries no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's mandate is redirected or countermanded; the order restores one officer within the Foreign Service.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population or exemption structure; the order applies to one named individual only.

note: Cited authority is a statute (46 Stat. 1207), not a numbered EO; no predecessor lookup needed. Per the compliance-regime test, Flags 4/5/8/11 and 3/6/9/10 are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE to a one-time individual reinstatement, leaving the three always-answerable decision-structure flags clean.

title: American Heritage Rivers Initiative Advisory Committee

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Committee only reviews nominations and recommends up to 20 rivers; the President makes final designations. Advisory authority with the decision retained by the President is not concentration.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Roles are clear: the Committee recommends through the CEQ Chair and the President designates; the DOD/Army Assistant Secretary is the named administrative/financial support with a designated Federal officer.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (establishing a river-nomination advisory committee); no distinct remedies or problem frames forced together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A purely advisory committee imposes no compliance standard on any regulated party, so there is no enforcement mechanism to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory relationship or behavioral rule is created; the Committee only appraises voluntary community nominations.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Sec. 4 provides the Committee 'shall terminate no later than 2 years from the date of this order'—an explicit sunset, a positive finding.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Removes no existing oversight; it only adds a FACA advisory body, and the direction rule bars firing this flag on newly-added machinery.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement chain exists—the committee makes recommendations, not compliance determinations subject to violation.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A time-limited advisory committee recommending honorific river designations establishes no reusable authority claim or precedent identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Agencies provide information 'to the extent practicable and permitted by law' and the Army provides support; no existing statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: There is no regulated coverage population from which anyone could be exempted; participation is by voluntary community nomination.

note: FACA advisory committee with a decision retained by the President and an explicit 2-year sunset; the compliance-regime flags (4/5/8/11) are NOT_APPLICABLE because no ongoing behavioral rule is created.

title: Appointment of James P. Davis, Director, Division of Territories and Island Possessions, Department of the Interior, as Administrator of the Puerto Rico Reconstruction Administration

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: This order only substitutes the named Administrator (Davis vice Arnold); per the partial-amendment convention only that provision is assessed, and swapping the name of an official does not itself pool new authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is clearly fixed in the named Administrator within the Interior Department; the appointment leaves the pre-existing PRRA accountability chain intact.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: One coherent action (naming an administrator and conforming the cited EOs accordingly); no distinct remedies or frames are packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A personnel-substitution amendment creates no new enforcement standard; it modifies who holds an office, not any compliance rule.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Naming a successor administrator establishes no new incentive structure for any actor class.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The amendment itself is a one-time appointment that grants no ongoing authority of its own to sunset; the underlying PRRA framework continues under its predecessors' terms.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight is removed; the order changes only the identity of the officeholder and conforms the cited orders accordingly.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: A personnel amendment creates no violation for a third party to surface; no compliance chain is established.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A routine successor appointment carries no identifiable precedential or systemic effect beyond continuity of the PRRA leadership.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The appointee already sits in Interior's Division of Territories; no second agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded by the substitution.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population or exemption structure is created or altered by naming an administrator.

note: Partial amendment: EO 9901 substitutes the PRRA Administrator and amends EOs 7057/7180/7554/7689 'accordingly.' Predecessor EO-7057 (establishing the PRRA framework) was available and confirms this diff touches only the officeholder, not the underlying grant; EOs 7180/7554/7689 were not present in referenced-predecessors, but the amendment operates only on the appointee name, so the missing texts do not change the finding.

title: Amending Paragraph 1 of Title II of Executive Order No. 9001 of December 27, 1941 Concerning Reports of Contracts and Purchases Made Under Authority of Title II of the First War Powers Act, 1941

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authority to decide what to disclose is spread across three named officials (Secretaries of War and Navy and the Maritime Commission Chairman) rather than pooled in one; the amendment itself concentrates nothing new.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: By deleting the requirement to report contracts to the President and leaving only self-maintained data disclosed 'as they may deem compatible with the public interest,' the amendment removes the external actor (the President) to whom the departments were accountable for these war contracts, leaving each department to judge its own transparency.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: A single-provision amendment to one reporting paragraph; nothing distinct is bundled together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The retained disclosure standard is 'so much of such data as does not cover restricted, confidential, or secret contracts,' released as each official 'may deem compatible with the public interest'—an undefined discretionary standard two officials could apply very differently in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Though disclosure discretion widens, no structural feature rewards the departments for undermining the war-production purpose the order serves; the incentive to conceal is a transparency gap captured under Flags 2/7, not a perverse-incentive design.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This amendment grants no operative authority of its own—it only edits a reporting clause—so there is no new authority to attach a sunset to; the parent war-powers grant continues under EO 9001.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: EO 9001 Title II Para 1 required 'All contracts and all purchases...shall be reported to the President...at least quarter annually'; this order replaces that mandatory presidential reporting with mere internal data 'maintained' and discretionary public disclosure, removing the pre-existing oversight channel to the President—the paragraph's core function—and thus collapsing the very accountability mechanism the amended provision existed to provide.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: After the amendment, disclosure depends entirely on the contracting departments' own discretion with no external actor given standing or access to compel reporting of contracts they choose to withhold as against 'the public interest.'

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Making mandatory contract reporting discretionary for war agencies establishes a transparency-reduction precedent for emergency procurement that makes later scrutiny of that spending structurally harder, an effect beyond the stated purpose of merely amending a report format.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Each department retains its own contracts and data; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded by the reporting change.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The clause lets each department carve out from public disclosure whatever it deems 'restricted, confidential, or secret...or for other reasons affecting the public interest,' a self-controlled, undefined exemption broad enough that any contract can be withheld at the discloser's discretion.

note: Diff-aware: predecessor EO-9001 was available. The amendment's key mechanism is a reporting requirement, and the diff weakens it—deleting mandatory quarterly reporting to the President in favor of self-maintained data and discretionary disclosure—driving the Flag 7 CRITICAL and the accountability/exemption findings.

title: Amendment of Executive Order No. 10841, Relating to International Cooperation Under the Atomic Energy Act of 1954, as Amended

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The amendment inserts a presidential-referral step onto a determination previously made jointly by the Secretary of Defense and the AEC; it disperses authority back toward the President rather than pooling it in one actor.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is clearly assigned: determinations under paragraph (2) of section 2(a) are referred to the President, who may disapprove within fifteen days, adding a named accountable actor.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: One discrete procedural change (adding a referral proviso to a single paragraph); nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The added standard is precise—each determination is referred to the President and 'unless disapproved by him' becomes effective fifteen days after referral—leaving no ambiguity about the trigger or timing.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Adding presidential review to atomic-energy cooperation determinations creates no incentive to undermine the order's purpose; it strengthens control over the determination.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The amendment grants no new ongoing authority of its own—it adds a review proviso—so there is nothing new to sunset; the parent framework continues under EO 10841.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Per the direction rule, this ADDS a check: EO 10841 let the Secretary of Defense and AEC act jointly 'without the approval, ratification, or other action of the President,' and this order inserts a presidential-disapproval review over section-2(a)(2) determinations—new oversight, not removal.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The added referral gives the President direct visibility over each determination, supplying an external actor with both standing and information—the opposite of a third-party gap.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Narrowly reinserting presidential review over one class of atomic-cooperation determinations has no identifiable adverse downstream or precedential effect from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Defense/AEC joint role and State Department negotiating role are preserved; the amendment layers presidential review atop them without countermanding any agency mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No exemption or carve-out is created; the amendment adds a universal review step to the covered determinations.

note: Diff-aware: predecessor EO-10841 was available. Baseline let Defense/AEC act 'without...action of the President'; this order adds a presidential-disapproval referral—an oversight addition, so Flag 7 is ABSENT under the direction rule. Flag 6 NOT_APPLICABLE because the amendment adds only a procedural proviso, not new standing authority.

title: Amendments Relating to the National Research Council

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Council operates under the NAS congressional charter as its principal operating agency; the order stimulates and coordinates research and vests no concentrated discretionary authority in any single federal official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Cost recovery is bounded by the NAS charter and OMB Circular A-122, with the OMB Director able to specify limits in writing; responsibility for cost oversight is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: A single coherent purpose—updating the NRC's functions and cost/acquisition terms; no distinct remedies or problem frames are forced together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Cost terms are anchored to OMB Circular A-122 and any additional written OMB requirements, and sole-source acquisition is gated by a defined 'only source' uniqueness finding 'in accordance with applicable law and regulations'—not an open-ended standard.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order bars the Academy from receiving 'compensation whatever for any services to the Government' and subjects it to A-122 cost principles, structurally checking any incentive to inflate cost recovery.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Standing procedural framework maintained through ordinary supersession, evidenced by this order expressly amending numbered predecessor EO 2859 'to read as follows'; the coordination function is evergreen and revised by amendment, not left frozen, so the permanence carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds cost oversight (A-122, OMB Director's written limits) rather than removing any pre-existing check; no oversight mechanism is excluded or bypassed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Cost recovery runs through federal contracting instruments subject to OMB cost principles and audit, giving contracting agencies and OMB standing and access to scrutinize the Academy's charges.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Updating the Council's functions and reaffirming a sole-source path bounded by an 'only source' finding produces no identifiable adverse precedent beyond the stated coordination purpose.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Agency participation in the Council is voluntary ('encouraged...to the extent authorized by the head of the...agency and permitted by law'); no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The noncompetitive-acquisition provision is a bounded exception, triggered only by an agency's 'only source' uniqueness determination and still requiring compliance with applicable law—not a broad discretionary carve-out that swallows competition rules.

note: Full restatement of EO 2859 (predecessor EO-2859 available). The diff modernizes functions and adds A-122/OMB cost controls; the standing-procedural-framework carve-out applies to Flag 6 because the order maintains the framework through explicit supersession of a numbered predecessor.

title: Amendment of Executive Order No. 1888 of February 2, 1914, as Amended, Relating to Conditions of Employment in the Service of the Panama Canal and the Panama Railroad Company on the Isthmus of Panama

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The amended paragraphs repeatedly vest unchecked discretion in the Governor—to raise up to 200 aliens' pay above the cap, to grant passes 'in his discretion,' to set medical charges 'as may be prescribed,' and to defer and commute leave 'in his discretion'—pooling employment-condition authority in one official with no external check named.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility for each employment decision is clearly fixed in the Governor of the Panama Canal; the accountable actor is identifiable even where his discretion is broad (that breadth is a Flag 1/11 issue).

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: All amended paragraphs address a single coherent subject—conditions of Canal/Railroad employment; distinct pay, leave, and medical provisions of one employment code are not impermissible bundling of unrelated frames.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Key standards are undefined: aliens may be paid above the cap 'if such action is deemed necessary by reason of an emergency' and replaced 'as early as practicable,' and leave commutation turns on whether an employee can 'be spared...without detriment'—criteria two administrators could apply very differently in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The pay and leave rules create no structural incentive for employees or the Governor to undermine Canal operation; the citizen-preference and replacement clauses point toward the order's own purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Standing procedural framework for permanent Canal-Zone employment administration, maintained through ordinary supersession—this order amends numbered predecessors (EO 1888, 2514) and revokes EOs 8986/9164—so the procedural-framework carve-out applies; the war-limited leave-commutation clause even carries its own express expiration.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order revises pay/leave/medical rules and revokes two prior EOs but removes no external oversight mechanism (judicial review, IG, or reporting) that previously applied to Canal employment.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: As an internal federal-employment code, compliance runs through the ordinary civil-employment administrative chain; affected employees have standing and incentive to contest pay, leave, and benefit determinations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Routine updates to a localized employment code (pay caps, leave, medical charges) carry no identifiable systemic or precedential effect reasoned from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Authority stays within the Panama Canal administration under the Governor; no other agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The pay-cap rule is undercut by Governor-controlled carve-outs—up to 200 aliens may be lifted above the cap and others exempted by 'emergency' need or prior-construction service—concentrating exemption discretion in one official over the citizen-preference default.

note: Partial amendments to specific paragraphs of EO 1888 (predecessor EO-1888 available); assessed against the ongoing Canal-employment relationship the code governs. Governor discretion drives the Power/Vague/Exemptions findings; Flag 6 is ABSENT under the standing-procedural-framework carve-out given the supersession pattern.

title: President's Economic Policy Advisory Board

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Board is purely advisory—it 'shall advise the President' and reports 'whenever requested'—and holds no decision authority over anyone; nothing is concentrated.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Roles are clear: a President-designated Chairman, the Assistant to the President for Policy Development as Secretary, and GSA providing reimbursable administrative services.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose—establishing an economic-policy advisory board; no distinct remedies or frames are packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: An advisory board imposes no compliance obligation on any regulated party, so there is no enforcement mechanism to evaluate.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule or incentive structure is created; the Board only advises the President.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Sec. 4(b) provides the Board 'shall terminate on December 31, 1982, unless sooner extended'—an explicit sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Removes no pre-existing oversight; the Sec. 4(a) reassignment of FACA responsibilities to the President is a routine advisory-committee administrative arrangement, not the removal of an external check on this Board.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement chain exists—an advisory board produces recommendations, not compliance determinations subject to violation.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A time-limited private-member advisory board with a confidentiality provision for information supplied to it has no systemic or precedential effect identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Board works alongside the Cabinet Council on Economic Affairs and draws information from agencies 'to the extent permitted by law'; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: There is no regulated coverage population from which to exempt anyone; the Board advises rather than regulates.

note: FACA advisory board with an explicit fixed-date sunset; cites no numbered predecessor EO. Compliance-regime flags (4/5/8/11) are NOT_APPLICABLE because the Board creates no ongoing behavioral rule.

title: Establishing the National War Labor Board

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Board 'shall finally determine the dispute' and may take jurisdiction 'on its own motion,' vesting binding, final adjudicatory authority over war-related labor disputes in a single tripartite body with no external appeal or review identified in the text.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Board is the clearly named actor responsible for adjusting disputes, with a defined tripartite composition, presidential appointment, quorum rules, and a specified certification path through the Secretary of Labor.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: A single coherent purpose—creating a board for peaceful settlement of war-labor disputes; the transfer of the predecessor Mediation Board's assets is incidental continuity, not a distinct bundled policy.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Board may 'finally determine the dispute' using 'mediation, voluntary arbitration, or arbitration under rules established by the Board,' but the order specifies no consequence for non-compliance with a determination and no standard governing which method or outcome applies—two administrators could reach very different enforcement postures.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The design channels disputes toward peaceful settlement consistent with the no-strike/no-lockout agreement it implements; no structural feature rewards parties for undermining that purpose. The wartime authority rests on an active, declared state of war, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire—no lapsed statute is acknowledged.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Emergency/wartime dispute-settlement authority is exactly the kind that should end with the emergency, yet the order sets no expiration or reauthorization trigger—it recites the disputes are settled 'for the duration of the war' but attaches no sunset to the Board's own authority, so it persists until affirmatively revoked.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 7 expressly preserves the Railway Labor Act, NLRA, FLSA, and other statutes rather than displacing them, and Sec. 2 defers where other adjustment procedures exist; no pre-existing external oversight is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The dispute mechanism is inherently multi-party—labor and employer representatives sit on the Board and either party or the Secretary of Labor can bring a dispute forward—so external actors with standing and incentive to surface disputes are built in.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Vesting a federal board with binding final authority to settle private labor disputes establishes an institutional precedent for federal wage/dispute control reusable beyond the immediate no-strike pledge, an effect beyond the order's stated adjustment purpose.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order supersedes the National Defense Mediation Board (EO 8716) with an orderly transfer of its staff, records, and funds, and expressly does not conflict with the NLRA/Railway Labor Act machinery; it consolidates rather than countermands existing mandates.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Sec. 2 exempts from the Board's reach all 'labor disputes for which procedures for adjustment or settlement are otherwise provided until those procedures have been exhausted,' and Sec. 7's broad statutory carve-outs (Railway Labor Act and others) remove significant categories of disputes from coverage.

note: Supersedes EO 8716 (predecessor EO-8716 available), which it replaces with orderly asset transfer—hence Flag 10 ABSENT. Wartime authority under an active declared war (not a lapsed statute), so the Zombie Trap does not fire; Flag 6 PRESENT because emergency dispute authority carries no sunset tied to the war's end.

title: President's Private Sector Survey on Cost Control in the Federal Government

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Committee only conducts a survey and 'shall advise' the President, the Secretary of Commerce, and agency heads; it holds no decision authority, so nothing is concentrated.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Roles are clear: a President-designated Chairman, the Secretary of Commerce responsible for support and for FACA responsibilities, and GSA guidelines governing the arrangement.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: A single coherent purpose—a private-sector cost-control survey; no distinct remedies or problem frames are forced together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: An advisory survey committee imposes no compliance standard on any regulated party, so there is no enforcement mechanism to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule or incentive structure is created; the Committee reviews agency operations and recommends.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Sec. 4(b) provides the Committee 'shall terminate on December 31, 1982, unless sooner extended'—an explicit sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Removes no pre-existing oversight; the Sec. 4(a) FACA reassignment preserves the President's annual report to Congress and only routes other FACA duties through the Secretary of Commerce under GSA guidelines.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement chain exists—the Committee surveys and recommends rather than making compliance determinations subject to violation.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A time-limited survey committee that only recommends, with agency information shared 'to the extent permitted by law,' has no systemic or precedential effect identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Agencies provide information 'to the extent permitted by law' and the Committee reviews operations without directing them; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: There is no regulated coverage population from which anyone could be exempted; the Committee advises rather than regulates.

note: FACA advisory/survey committee (the Grace Commission structure) with an explicit fixed-date sunset; cites no numbered predecessor EO. Compliance-regime flags (4/5/8/11) are NOT_APPLICABLE because the Committee creates no ongoing behavioral rule; the private-sector funding arrangement is administrative, not a regulated relationship.

title: President's Commission on Executive Exchange

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Directs the Director of OPM to terminate the Commission's functions by a fixed date; a wind-down task with a clean, defined answer as to where authority rests, no discretionary pooling.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: OPM Director is the single named actor obligated to complete termination by September 30, 1991; responsibility for the action is clear.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single-purpose abolition/revocation order with nothing distinct bundled against it.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: One-time abolition creates no ongoing behavioral or compliance rule to enforce against anyone.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory relationship or incentive structure created by a wind-down.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The order is self-terminating with a hard completion deadline (Sept 30, 1991); it grants no continuing authority that could persist without reauthorization.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Revokes EO 12493 and abolishes a commission but removes no external oversight mechanism; per predecessor, the Commission itself was an advisory body, not an oversight check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement chain or ongoing compliance relationship exists in a one-time abolition.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Abolishing an internal exchange commission establishes no precedent or definitional framework usable in unrelated contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: OPM is the only agency implicated and is assigned a wind-down task consistent with its role; no mandate conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population or exemption structure exists in an abolition order.

note: Revokes EO 12493 (predecessor read); the revoked order was an advisory personnel-exchange commission, so revocation removes no oversight and creates no ongoing relationship, supporting NA on the compliance-regime flags.

title: Amending Executive Order No. 10923 to Extend the Time Within Which the Board Created Therein Shall Report its Findings to the President

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Merely authorizes an existing Emergency Board to report by an alternate date under Section 10 of the Railway Labor Act; no discretionary authority is pooled in any single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Board's reporting obligation to the President remains clear; the amendment only adjusts timing.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single-provision timing amendment with nothing to bundle.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A reporting-deadline extension creates no ongoing enforcement standard against any party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance relationship or actor incentive structure is created by a deadline change.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The board and the statutory 30-day standstill are inherently time-limited under the Railway Labor Act; the order fixes a report date and the underlying process self-terminates.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed; the amendment operates within the Railway Labor Act's statutory process.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: A one-off deadline amendment establishes no enforcement chain requiring third-party surfacing of violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Extending one board's report date carries no precedential or definitional spillover beyond the named dispute.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No agency mandate is redirected or countermanded; the National Mediation Board's role under the statute is unaffected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage or exemption structure exists in a timing amendment.

note: Partial amendment of EO 10923 (predecessor read): touches only the Board's reporting-deadline mechanism; the parent emergency-board framework continues under the Railway Labor Act, so assessed against that single provision per the diff-aware convention.

title: Amending Executive Order 8588 and Prescribing Regulations Governing the Payment of Expenses of Transportation of the Immediate Family of Civilian Officers and Employees of the Government

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Repeatedly vests unbounded discretion in the head of the department or establishment to grant extensions and specifically authorize express shipments, with no external check on those determinations.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The agency head is the clearly named responsible actor for approvals; responsibility for the routine reimbursement determinations is traceable.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The two parts (household-goods amendment and immediate-family transportation) share a single transportation-reimbursement frame; nothing distinct is forced together to foreclose targeted review.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Standards like 'when the head of the department determines it to be in the interest of the Government' and 'most economical route' leave extension and authorization decisions open to substantially different good-faith application by different officials.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Weight caps (500/250 lbs), cost ceilings, and time limits constrain gaming; no actor is rewarded for undermining the reimbursement purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Standing procedural framework for routine employee-transfer reimbursement maintained through ordinary supersession (it itself amends EO 8588 as amended by EO 9122); permanence is appropriate for an evergreen administrative function.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
