flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed; it references and works within the Standardized Government Travel Regulations.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Compliance rests on agency-internal approval of employees' claims with no external actor holding standing or incentive to surface improper reimbursements.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Routine internal reimbursement rules carry no meaningful precedential or cross-domain consequence identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Each department administers its own employee reimbursements; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or duplicated in conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Time limits and extension provisions are bounded (max two years, defined conditions) and do not create carve-outs broad enough to swallow the reimbursement rule.

note: Partial amendment of EO 8588 (as amended by EO 9122); both predecessors present. Creates an ongoing reimbursement-eligibility relationship for employees, so the compliance-regime flags apply and are scored on the merits rather than NA.

title: Establishment and Administration of the Peace Corps in the Department of State

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Broad delegation of Mutual Security Act functions vested in the President is channeled to the Secretary of State and onward to a Director 'as the Secretary shall deem necessary,' pooling program authority with minimal external check.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary self-defines the scope of delegated functions and finances the agency from his own discretionary funds; if the program fails, responsibility loops back to the same official who set its terms.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent frame (establishing and financing one agency); no distinct policy questions forced together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Delegation of 'such of the functions... as the Secretary shall deem necessary' provides no defined standard, so two officials could reasonably delegate very different scopes of authority.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No design feature rewards undermining the assistance mission; establishment of a service agency creates no clear counter-incentive.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Creates a permanent agency and open-ended delegation with no review, reauthorization, or expiration trigger; a new program grant of this kind should carry reauthorization pressure and none exists.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Adds a new agency without removing any pre-existing oversight; expressly preserves EO 10893 rather than displacing it.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Program administration and financing are wholly internal to State with no external actor given standing or incentive to surface misuse of the delegated funds or functions.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Establishing an open-ended foreign-assistance agency by self-defined delegation of broad statutory authority sets an institutional precedent for standing up further programs through discretionary sub-delegation.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Locates the Peace Corps within State and expressly provides it shall not supersede or derogate from EO 10893, avoiding conflict with existing mandates.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No exemption or waiver structure carving parties out of coverage; the order is a grant of authority, not a coverage rule with exceptions.

note: References EO 10893 (predecessor read) only to preserve it, not to amend or incorporate it wholesale; scored on its own terms as an agency-establishment order with broad self-defined delegation.

title: United States Circuit Judge Nominating Commission

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The President appoints all panel members and chairs, controls when panels activate, and retains sole discretion over nominations; authority over the process pools in the President with no external check.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Panels have defined, reported functions (public notice, inquiry, recommendation) and named standards; the advisory chain and its reporting to the President are clear.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (structuring circuit-judge nominating panels); no distinct unrelated matters packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Selection standards ('outstanding legal ability,' 'judicial temperament,' 'sound health') and the authorization for panels to 'adopt such additional criteria... as it considers appropriate' allow substantially divergent good-faith application across the thirteen panels.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The one-year post-service ineligibility rule (Sec. 5) actively guards against self-dealing; no design feature rewards undermining the merit-selection purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Sec. 8 sets an explicit Commission termination date (December 31, 1978) and Sec. 7 terminates appointments 30 days after each report; strong built-in reauthorization/expiration pressure, a positive finding.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Revokes prior nominating-commission orders (EO 11972, 11993) but installs a comparable advisory structure; no external oversight of judicial nominations is removed, since the President's constitutional selection role is unchanged.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Panels must give public notice inviting suggestions, creating an external channel for surfacing candidates and objections rather than relying solely on internal self-assessment.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: An advisory nominating structure recommending candidates for the President's discretionary constitutional appointment power carries no identifiable cross-domain precedent from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Commission is a standalone advisory body; no agency's operational statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The only carve-out is the narrow member-ineligibility rule, which tightens rather than guts coverage; no broad exemption swallows the process.

note: Revokes EO 11972 and EO 11993 (both present) and continues a nominating-commission framework; the explicit termination and post-service ineligibility provisions clear several flags that would otherwise fire.

title: Setting Aside Submerged Lands of the Continental Shelf as a Naval Petroleum Reserve

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Transfers administration of the reserve to the Secretary of the Navy and gives him unilateral control of a Treasury suspense account with discretion to refund or deposit proceeds 'as the Secretary of the Navy may deem to be proper,' with no external check.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Navy both administers the reserve and decides how remitted funds are handled, and provisions 'continue in effect until changed by the Secretary of the Navy,' making him the sole arbiter of his own performance.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (reserving continental-shelf lands and transferring their administration); no distinct unrelated matters packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The standard for handling remittances ('refunded... or deposited... as the Secretary of the Navy may deem to be proper') supplies no defined trigger or criteria, permitting divergent good-faith application.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No design feature rewards undermining the reserve's oil-and-gas conservation purpose; the reservation is protective in structure.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Establishes an ongoing, permanent administrative regime ('shall continue in effect, until changed by the Secretary of the Navy') with no review, expiration, or reauthorization trigger over discretionary control of the reserve and its funds.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Substitutes the Secretary of the Navy for the Secretary of the Interior throughout the 1950 operating notice, removing Interior's pre-existing administrative role, and makes the successor's provisions changeable at his own discretion without external process.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Administration and the suspense-account handling are internal to the Navy with no external actor holding standing or incentive to surface improper fund disposition.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Asserting paramount federal dominion over continental-shelf lands and moving them under military control establishes a precedent usable to reassign resource jurisdiction, and it operates against the backdrop of the pending U.S. v. California tidelands litigation it expressly references.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Redirects functions the Secretary of the Interior held under the December 1950 notice to the Secretary of the Navy and revokes EO 9633 which had placed these resources under Interior, reassigning an existing operational mandate laterally.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The 'subject to valid existing rights' and non-interference clauses are narrow savings provisions, not broad discretionary carve-outs that swallow the reservation.

note: Revokes EO 9633 (present), which had placed the same continental-shelf resources under Interior; the diff shows a lateral reassignment of an operational mandate from Interior to Navy, driving Flags 7 and 10. Establishes an ongoing discretionary fund-control relationship, so compliance-regime flags are scored on merits.

title: Committee on Selection of the Director of the Federal Bureau of Investigation

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Amends only the reporting deadline in Sec. 3(a) of EO 11971; no decision authority is concentrated, the advisory committee's role is unchanged.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Committee's obligation to report to the President and Attorney General remains clearly assigned; only the date shifts.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single-provision deadline amendment with nothing to bundle.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A reporting-date change creates no ongoing enforcement standard against any party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Extending a report date by 30 days creates no ongoing compliance relationship or actor incentive to game.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The parent EO 11971 already terminates the Committee 30 days after its report; this amendment operates within that self-limiting, time-bound structure.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed; a deadline is merely reset.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: A one-off deadline amendment establishes no enforcement chain requiring third-party surfacing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Resetting one committee's report date has no precedential or cross-domain consequence.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No agency mandate is redirected; the Attorney General's supporting role under EO 11971 is unaffected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage or exemption structure exists in a deadline amendment.

note: Partial amendment of EO 11971 (predecessor read): touches only the Sec. 3(a) reporting deadline; the parent advisory-committee framework, including its 30-day termination, continues, so the deadline is assessed alone per the diff-aware convention.

title: Computer Software Piracy

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Responsibility is distributed across each agency head, the CIO Council, and OMB rather than pooled in a single official; authority over compliance is diffuse but not concentrated.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each agency polices its own software compliance and self-assesses via its own performance measures with no named external actor obligated to correct a failing agency; Sec. 9's no-rights clause removes any enforceable accountability.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent frame (federal-government software copyright compliance); the national-security and law-enforcement carve-outs are scope limits, not bundled distinct policies.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Agencies 'shall take such measures... as the agency head deems appropriate,' and procedures 'may include' listed steps, leaving trigger, standard, and consequence undefined enough that agencies could implement very differently in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The agency that must comply is also the beneficiary of clean licensing; no design feature rewards non-compliance or expansion-through-failure.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Standing procedural framework for an evergreen administrative function (ongoing lawful software use); permanence is appropriate and revision runs through ordinary agency practice and OMB guidance, not a fixed-term grant.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 9 states the order creates no right or benefit enforceable against the government, and Secs. 6-7 shield intelligence and law-enforcement sources/methods from disclosure, foreclosing external and judicial oversight of compliance.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement depends on agency self-inventory and self-certification with no external actor given standing or access to surface an agency's copyright violations; contractor controls rest on 'certifications or written assurances.'

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: By tying domestic government practice to WTO/TRIPS and Berne compliance, the order builds precedent leveraging federal procurement policy to advance international IP-enforcement positions in trade negotiations, a use beyond mere internal housekeeping.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Builds on the existing CIO Council (EO 13011) and OMB's Clinger-Cohen role rather than redirecting or duplicating any agency's statutory mandate; roles are additive.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The national-security (Sec. 6) and law-enforcement (Sec. 7) carve-outs are broad and self-invoked, letting large swaths of covered agencies decline disclosure obligations at their own discretion.

note: References EO 13011 (predecessor read) only to identify the pre-existing CIO Council it uses as a forum; not an amendment or incorporation, so scored on its own text. Creates an ongoing agency-compliance relationship, so compliance-regime flags apply on the merits.

title: Establishing the Cabinet Committee on Economic Policy

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A multi-member advisory committee that only advises and assists the President; it holds no operative decision authority over eligibility, enforcement, or implementation to concentrate.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: As a purely advisory body chaired-in-coordination by the CEA Chairman and presided over by the President, there is no implementation failure chain for which responsibility would be diffuse.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (coordinating national economic policy advice); no distinct unrelated matters packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: An advisory committee imposes no behavioral rule on any party, so there is no enforcement mechanism to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Purely advisory coordination creates no ongoing compliance relationship or incentive for any regulated actor to game.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Establishes a standing committee with no termination date, review, or reauthorization trigger; it continues until revoked, and an advisory-coordination body of this kind carries no natural evergreen justification for indefinite permanence.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Adds an advisory forum without removing any pre-existing oversight; Sec. 4 expressly preserves all existing agency functions and hierarchies.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement or compliance relationship exists over which a third party would need standing; the committee only formulates advice.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A cabinet advisory committee with no operative authority produces no identifiable precedent or cross-domain consequence from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Sec. 4 expressly disclaims subjecting any agency or its functions to the authority of another, preventing mandate conflict; the committee only coordinates.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population or exemption structure exists for an advisory body.

note: No predecessor amended or revoked. Advisory-coordination body with no operative compliance relationship, so Flags 4, 5, 8, 11 are NA; Flag 6 fires because the standing committee has no termination or reauthorization mechanism.

title: Providing for the Screening of the Ready Reserve of the Armed Forces Established Under the Provisions of Part II of the Armed Forces Reserve Act of 1952, as Amended

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Vests broad discretionary authority in the Secretary of Defense (and Treasury for the Coast Guard) to define hardship, determine critical military skills, and set transfer regulations governing individual reservists, with no external check on those determinations.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The 'Service concerned' both applies the screening criteria and determines eligibility (e.g., who is 'not immediately available'), and the only feedback loop is the Secretary's own periodic reports to the President, so the implementing actor also judges its own performance.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent frame (continuous screening of the Ready Reserve); the several transfer categories all serve that one purpose and are not distinct unrelated policies forced together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Standards like 'extreme personal or community hardship as defined by the Secretary,' 'critical military skill as determined by the Secretary,' and 'proper balance of such skills' leave transfer decisions to substantially divergent good-faith application across services.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The one-year voluntary-agreement exception lets a reservist avoid Standby transfer by pledging to stay, and hardship/critical-occupation categories give individuals reason to characterize their status to obtain or avoid transfer, creating gaming pressure on the screening definitions.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Prescribes a 'continuously screening' regime with no expiration, review, or reauthorization trigger; an ongoing discretionary personnel-mobilization authority of this kind should carry reauthorization pressure and none exists.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Implements the Armed Forces Reserve Act's screening mandate and adds periodic presidential reporting; no pre-existing external oversight mechanism is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Screening determinations are made internally by each Service with only self-generated periodic reports to the President; no external actor has standing or access to surface misclassification of reservists.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The critical-occupations screening framework, keyed to a Secretary-of-Labor list, embeds a standing civilian-manpower-allocation mechanism whose definitions could shape mobilization and occupational-deferment policy well beyond the immediate reserve-balance purpose.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Distributes screening authority across Defense, Treasury (Coast Guard), and the Secretary of Labor (critical-occupations list) with no clear hierarchy resolving conflicts between the Labor list and the Services' critical-skill determinations, creating cross-agency friction over who controls a reservist's status.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Multiple discretionary carve-outs from Standby transfer (critical military skill, one-year voluntary agreement, apprenticeship/academic study, elective officials) are broad and partly self-invoked, letting significant categories opt out of the default transfer rule.

note: Cites only statutes (Armed Forces Reserve Act of 1952 as amended, Reserve Forces Act of 1955), not a numbered predecessor EO, so no predecessor comparison was required. Creates an ongoing reservist-classification compliance relationship, so all compliance-regime flags apply on the merits.

title: Establishing Apache Migratory Waterfowl Refuge

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A one-time self-executing land reservation for the Department of Agriculture; no discretionary decision authority is pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The order simply reserves described lands as a refuge; there is no implementation chain that could fail without a responsible actor.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single subject (one refuge, one described tract) under one stated purpose; nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A land reservation creates no ongoing compliance rule for any actor; enforcement of game laws is left to existing law, so this flag's question does not map.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral regime is established that could reward wrong conduct.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing land set-aside grants no continuing authority to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed; the order expressly preserves the land's national-forest status and forest conservation primacy.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory relationship exists over which a third party would surface violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Narrow acreage reservation with no precedential or systemic implication beyond the described tract.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Refuge use is expressly made consistent with and non-interfering with the Forest Service's primary forest use; no agency mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage-population rule exists from which anyone could be exempted; the private-lands proviso is an inclusion mechanism, not a carve-out.

note: Classic one-time land reservation; compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11) plus Flag 6 are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE per the concrete test, while decision-structure flags score ABSENT.

title: Federal Space Management

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The GSA Administrator holds broad authority to select, acquire, and manage federal space and issue standards; discretion is bounded only by criteria the Administrator himself weighs, with President approval reserved as the sole external check.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: A single named actor (the Administrator) is responsible for implementation and must annually submit plans for Presidential approval, giving a clear responsibility chain.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order fuses two distinct frames under one purpose: efficient federal space management and an urban-revitalization/social-policy agenda (strengthening cities, low-income housing availability), obscuring which problem drives a given siting decision.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Criteria like 'serious consideration,' 'first consideration to a centralized community business area,' and multifactor social/economic 'consideration' lists let two officials reach different siting decisions in good faith without a defined standard.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No design feature rewards undermining the stated space-management or urban-strengthening purpose; incentives run toward the stated goals.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A standing procedural framework for the permanent function of managing federal space, maintained by ordinary supersession (it revokes EO 11512, itself a supersession of EO 11035) rather than requiring reauthorization; the procedural-framework carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Compared to superseded EO 11512, this order preserves the President-approval and inter-agency consultation checks and adds OMB coordination; it removes no pre-existing oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: State, regional, and local officials are given a consultation role with recommendations and objections, providing external parties with standing and interest in siting outcomes.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Directing federal siting toward urban cores as a lever for redevelopment establishes a precedent for using property-management authority to pursue unrelated social-policy ends, an effect beyond mere space efficiency.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Administrator must consult affected agencies, give written notice, and coordinate with OMB; existing agency space authorities under other statutes are directed to conform only 'to the extent they have the authority to do so,' avoiding mandate conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The only carve-out ('except where such selection is otherwise prohibited' and agencies acting under other authority) is narrow and law-bounded, not a discretionary waiver that swallows coverage.

note: Diff-aware comparison to superseded EO 11512 shows preserved/strengthened oversight (OMB added); scored PRESENT on Flags 1/3/4/9 for concentrated GSA discretion and the bundled urban-policy frame.

title: Employee-Management Relations in the Foreign Service of the United States

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The head of each foreign affairs agency may 'in his sole judgment' suspend any provision, and Board decisions are 'final, unless overruled by the head of the foreign affairs agency concerned,' pooling ultimate authority in the very management the order regulates.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Board's final decisions can be unilaterally overruled by the agency head, and agencies determine emergency suspensions themselves, so the party responsible for compliance also judges its own compliance.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order addresses one coherent subject (Foreign Service employee-management relations) with interlocking recognition, consultation, and grievance provisions; no distinct unrelated matter is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Key triggers turn on undefined 'sole judgment' and 'sole discretion' standards (agency-head suspension, Commission recognition of 'acceptable democratic and ethical standards'), permitting divergent good-faith application.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Because agency management can override the Board and suspend provisions in 'emergency' or 'national interest' situations it defines, resistant management is incentivized to invoke those escape clauses rather than consult in good faith.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A standing procedural labor-relations framework for a permanent administrative function, revisable through ordinary amendment (it amends EOs 11491 and 11264 and provides its own periodic-review mechanism in Sec. 11); the procedural-framework carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order creates a new oversight architecture (Board, Commission, disputes/grievance panels with public members) where none existed for the Foreign Service; it adds checks rather than removing pre-existing ones.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Recognized organizations, a Commission empowered to decide unfair-practice complaints and issue cease-and-desist orders, and public panel members provide external actors with standing and incentive to surface violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Extending federal-sector labor-relations machinery to the Foreign Service (amending EO 11491) sets an institutional precedent for structuring bargaining rights while embedding a management-override escape valve usable in adjacent contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Commission draws on Labor, CSC, and OMB representatives in a coordinated committee structure with defined roles; no agency's existing statutory mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Sec. 3(b) lets an agency head suspend 'any provision' for any post or activity in his 'sole judgment' whenever he deems it necessary in the national interest, a broad discretionary carve-out concentrated in one actor that can swallow coverage.

note: Amends EOs 11491 and 11264 (both present); the agency-head override of Board decisions plus 'sole judgment' suspension power drive Flags 1/2/4/5/11. No CRITICAL: the order still substantially stands up a functioning recognition/grievance regime.

title: Authorizing the Secretary of the Interior To Withdraw and Reserve Lands of the Public Domain and Other Lands Owned or Controlled by the United States

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order delegates to the Secretary of the Interior the President's own power to withdraw, reserve, modify, or revoke public-land reservations 'to the same extent,' concentrating a broad discretionary land-control authority in a single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary is the named responsible actor and each order requires prior approval of the Budget Director and Attorney General plus publication, so responsibility is traceable.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single subject: delegation of land withdrawal/reservation authority; nothing distinct is packaged with it.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order delegates a governmental power and imposes no ongoing compliance rule on any regulated party whose enforcement could be vague.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A pure intra-executive delegation of land authority creates no behavioral incentive regime for any actor class.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A standing delegation of a permanent land-administration function, maintained through ordinary supersession (it supersedes EO 9146); the procedural-framework carve-out applies rather than an emergency/temporary grant.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Relative to superseded EO 9146, this order adds checks (retains Budget-Director/AG prior approval per EO 7298 and adds a concurrence requirement for other agencies' lands); no pre-existing oversight is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory relationship exists over which an external party would surface violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Standing delegation of the President's land-withdrawal power lets the Secretary make and unmake reservations indefinitely by administrative order, a durable transfer of an executive prerogative usable across countless future tracts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order expressly requires the prior concurrence of any other department whose lands are affected, deliberately avoiding conflict with other agencies' jurisdiction.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population and no exemption structure; the order is an authority delegation, not a rule applied to a regulated class.

note: Supersedes EO 9146 (present); diff shows preserved Budget/AG approval plus an added inter-agency concurrence safeguard. Compliance-regime flags NOT_APPLICABLE; broad discretionary delegation drives Flags 1 and 9.

title: Relating to Voluntary Agreements

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The amendment vests the Title I §708(c)(1) voluntary-agreement authority (which confers antitrust immunity) exclusively in the GSA Administrator, concentrating that gatekeeping power in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Named officials (the Administrator and specified Secretaries) hold the delegated functions, and OMB-Director guidelines govern advisory committees, giving a traceable responsibility chain.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single-purpose amendment redistributing one section's delegated functions; nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order redelegates DPA §708 voluntary-agreement/advisory-committee authority without defining standards for approving agreements or granting the attendant antitrust immunity, leaving the operative approval standard undefined in the amended text.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Section 708 voluntary agreements confer antitrust immunity on participating industry; concentrating approval without defined criteria creates an incentive for regulated firms to seek immunized coordination under the government's cover.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This absence flag does not fire: DPA §708 authority is periodically reauthorized by Congress on its own terms, supplying the reauthorization pressure the flag looks for, and this order merely re-delegates within that framework.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The amendment adds an oversight step, requiring advisory-committee functions to be exercised only after consultation with and per guidelines of the OMB Director; it removes no pre-existing check versus EO 10480's section 501.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Voluntary agreements rely on participant cooperation with no external actor given standing or access to surface abuse of the antitrust immunity; the amended text creates no third-party enforcement interest.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Delegating antitrust-immunized voluntary-agreement authority entrenches a mechanism by which competitor coordination can be legitimized under DPA cover, a precedent extending beyond any single agreement.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order coordinates rather than conflicts, subjecting other Secretaries to section 101 and channeling Title I authority to GSA and advisory-committee functions through OMB; no agency mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The instrument allocates authority among named officials and does not create discretionary carve-outs from a coverage population.

note: Partial amendment of section 501 of EO 10480 (present): it re-delegates DPA §708 voluntary-agreement authority (antitrust immunity) to GSA/named Secretaries and adds OMB consultation. Assessed against that specific provision; the antitrust-immunity domain drives Flags 1/4/5/8/9.

title: Garnishment of Federal Employees' Pay

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Rulemaking authority is split between OPM (in consultation with the Attorney General) and the Postmaster General, and is bounded by the implementing statute (5 U.S.C. 5520a); no unchecked pooling of discretion.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Two named actors (OPM and the Postmaster General) are clearly assigned responsibility for promulgating the regulations, so accountability is traceable.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose: designating who issues garnishment regulations; the two designations serve one coherent statutory scheme.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order merely designates rule-issuers to implement a detailed statute; the enforcement standard is supplied by 5 U.S.C. 5520a, not left undefined here.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A designation to write garnishment regulations creates no incentive for any actor to undermine the statute's purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A standing designation implementing a permanent statutory function; no emergency or temporary authority that would call for reauthorization pressure, so the permanence carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The regulations remain subject to ordinary notice-and-comment rulemaking; no pre-existing oversight is removed or bypassed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The garnishment scheme is court/creditor-driven under 5 U.S.C. 5520a, giving private creditors external standing and incentive to enforce; no gap is created.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A routine statutory-implementation designation with no precedential or systemic downstream effect identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: OPM and the Postmaster General are given non-overlapping domains (executive-branch civilians vs. USPS employees), deliberately avoiding jurisdictional friction.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The USPS 'exception' is a clean jurisdictional split, not a discretionary carve-out that undermines coverage.

note: Clean statutory-implementation designation; every flag answerable and ABSENT (compliance regime exists via 5 U.S.C. 5520a, so 4/5/8/11 score ABSENT rather than NOT_APPLICABLE).

title: Revocation of Executive Order No. 5791 of February 2, 1932, Withdrawing Public Lands in Colorado

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A self-executing revocation of a prior land withdrawal, effective upon filing of the resurvey plat; no decision authority is concentrated in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The revocation is automatic upon a defined event; there is no implementation chain that could fail without a responsible actor.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single action affecting one described township; nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Revoking a land withdrawal imposes no ongoing compliance rule on anyone whose enforcement could be vague.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A one-time land-status change creates no behavioral incentive regime.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing revocation grants no continuing authority to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed; the order simply restores lands to the public domain on resurvey.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory relationship exists over which a third party would surface violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Narrow revocation of a single township's withdrawal with no precedential or systemic implication.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's mandate is implicated or redirected by restoring the tract to the public domain.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population and no exemption structure exist.

note: Revokes EO 5791 (present); a clean self-executing land-withdrawal revocation. Decision-structure flags ABSENT; compliance-regime flags and Flag 6 NOT_APPLICABLE per the concrete test.

title: Modification of Executive Order No. 8507 of August 8, 1940, Withdrawing Public Lands for Use of the War Department as an Anti-Aircraft Firing Range

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A self-executing modification that eliminates two described sections from a prior withdrawal (preserving an access road); no discretionary authority is concentrated in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The land-status change is automatic and definite; there is no implementation chain with a missing responsible actor.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single action modifying one prior withdrawal; nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A land-withdrawal modification imposes no ongoing compliance rule whose enforcement could be vague.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A one-time land-status change establishes no behavioral incentive regime.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing modification grants no continuing authority to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed; the order narrows a prior withdrawal's footprint.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory relationship exists over which a third party would surface violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Narrow acreage adjustment with an express road-access preservation; no precedential or systemic implication.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order carves land out of the War Department's range while preserving the access road; it does not countermand another agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population and no exemption structure exist; the road exception is a geographic reservation, not a discretionary waiver.

note: Modifies EO 8507 (present); a clean self-executing land-withdrawal modification. Decision-structure flags ABSENT; compliance-regime flags and Flag 6 NOT_APPLICABLE.

title: Further Amendments to Executive Order No. 12757—Implementation of the Enterprise for the Americas Initiative

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The amendment adds delegations of new Public Law 104-107 functions to the Treasury Secretary but requires them to be exercised per Council recommendations and in consultation with State; per the incorporated EO 12757 framework, authority remains distributed across a multi-agency Council and Board, not pooled.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary is the named responsible actor operating within the EO 12757 Council/Board structure with defined disagreement-referral to a Cabinet-level body; responsibility is traceable.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: A single-purpose technical amendment updating statutory citations and adding related delegations under one coherent Enterprise-for-the-Americas framework.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The delegated functions are tied to specific statutory sections and exercised per Council recommendations; the amendment supplies no vague new enforcement standard.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The debt/environmental-framework delegations and the default-condition proviso inherited from EO 12757 do not reward undermining the Initiative's purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A standing administrative framework maintained through ordinary supersession/amendment (this is itself a further amendment of EO 12757, as amended by EO 12823); the procedural-framework carve-out applies, and the incorporated forward-reference clause shows routine updating.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight is removed; the amendment adds consultation requirements (Council recommendations, consultation with State) rather than stripping checks.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The inherited EO 12757 structure includes an Environment for the Americas Board with private nongovernmental members and fiscal-audit review, providing external actors with standing over program administration.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The new Section 6 deems references to section 571 to include 'any hereinafter-enacted provision of law that is the same or substantially the same,' auto-extending delegated authority to future unenacted statutes without a fresh order, a precedent that broadens delegation beyond the cited text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The amendment coordinates Treasury, State, and the multi-agency Council; no agency's existing statutory mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No discretionary exemption structure is created; the order allocates delegated functions and updates citations.

note: Further amends EO 12757 (present), as amended by EO 12823 (present); largely technical citation updates within a clean multi-agency Council/Board framework. Only the forward-incorporation clause (new Sec. 6) fires Flag 9.

title: Implementing the Omnibus Trade and Competitiveness Act of 1988 and Related International Trade Matters

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Numerous presidential trade functions are delegated to single officials (primarily the USTR), and the amended CFIUS structure lets a unanimous Committee decision not to investigate a foreign takeover 'conclude action' with only advisory referral to the President, pooling sensitive review authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each function is assigned to a named official or committee with defined reporting to the President and Congress; responsibility for each delegation is traceable.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order packages many substantively distinct trade matters (GATT accession, wine barriers, steel, telecom, Toshiba/Kongsberg sanctions, CFIUS review, superconductivity commission, Buy American, offsets) into one omnibus instrument, though united by implementing a single statute.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Toshiba/Kongsberg sanction exceptions delegate broad authority to determine and implement exceptions 'by regulation or otherwise' to multiple department heads without defined criteria, allowing divergent good-faith application of the sanctions.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No design feature rewards undermining the trade-policy purpose; delegations and sanctions run toward the stated objectives.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A standing framework implementing a permanent statute; the time-bounded elements (three-year sanctions, the Superconductivity Commission which 'shall disband within a year') carry their own defined termination, so no inappropriate open-ended emergency grant exists.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 5-102 exempts Export Administration Act rules from EO 12291 regulatory review 'notwithstanding' its normal terms, and the amended CFIUS process lets a unanimous no-investigation decision conclude action, reducing pre-existing review of covered actions.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Sanctions are implemented against named entities by government departments with import/procurement bans, and CFIUS review involves multiple agencies with dissent-referral rights; enforcement does not depend solely on self-reporting.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Standing up the CFIUS national-security review machinery (new Secs. 7-8 under Defense Production Act §721) creates a durable, expandable foreign-investment-blocking apparatus whose precedent extends well beyond the immediate Omnibus Trade Act context.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order coordinates numerous agencies through interagency groups, consultation requirements, and the multi-member CFIUS committee; it allocates rather than countermands existing statutory mandates.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The Toshiba/Kongsberg sanctions carry broad exception authority delegated to Defense, Treasury, and every department head to grant exceptions 'by regulation or otherwise,' a discretionary carve-out dispersed across many actors that could erode the sanctions' coverage.

note: Amends EO 11858 (present, CFIUS) and references EO 12291; a broad omnibus trade order. Flags 1/3/4/7/9/11 fire on the aggregated delegations, sanction-exception discretion, EO 12291 review carve-out, and the durable CFIUS apparatus. No CRITICAL: each mechanism is individually bounded and reports to the President/Congress.

title: Designating the Secretary of the Interior to Accept on Behalf of the United States Retrocession by any State of Certain Criminal and Civil Jurisdiction Over Indian Country

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Sole authority to accept retrocession of civil/criminal jurisdiction is delegated to the Secretary of the Interior to exercise 'without the approval, ratification, or other action of the President or of any other officer,' pooling discretion in one official with only a consultation (not concurrence) check with the Attorney General.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is unambiguously assigned to the Secretary of the Interior, whose acceptances must be published in the Federal Register, so the responsible actor and the record of each action are clearly identifiable.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order addresses a single subject—delegation of authority to accept jurisdictional retrocession under 25 U.S.C. 1323(a)—with no distinct remedies or problem frames packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The mechanism is precise: retrocession is effected by publishing a notice specifying the jurisdiction retroceded and its effective date, with criminal-jurisdiction acceptances requiring prior consultation with the Attorney General; two officials would apply this the same way.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order merely enables acceptance of jurisdiction voluntarily ceded back by states; no actor is rewarded for undermining its purpose, and the underlying statute (25 U.S.C. 1323(a)) is active, not lapsed.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is a standing delegation implementing a permanent statutory function (accepting retrocession) with no natural end-state; the authority is evergreen procedural machinery, not emergency or temporary-program authority requiring reauthorization pressure.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order strips the default check of presidential review by expressly authorizing the Secretary to act 'without the approval, ratification, or other action of the President or of any other officer,' removing a pre-existing layer of executive oversight over these acceptances.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Retrocession is state-initiated and each acceptance is published in the Federal Register, giving affected tribes, states, and the public visibility to surface any irregularity; there is no self-reporting compliance regime hidden from third parties.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Vesting a single Secretary with unreviewable discretion to reshape criminal and civil jurisdiction over Indian country sets a precedent for consequential jurisdictional shifts affecting tribal sovereignty and law-enforcement coverage, effects extending beyond any individual retrocession.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Attorney General is drawn in only through a consultation requirement for criminal-jurisdiction acceptances; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded, so coordination is defined rather than conflicting.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order creates no exemptions, waivers, or carve-outs from any coverage; its 'provided' clauses are procedural conditions on the Secretary's own action, not exemptions swallowing a rule.

note: Cites section 403(a) of the Act of April 11, 1968 (25 U.S.C. 1323(a)) as its authority, not a prior EO, so no predecessor comparison was required; power concentration and preemption both fire on the express 'without approval of any other officer' clause.

title: Amendment of Section 3 of Executive Order No. 8802 of June 25, 1941, Establishing the Committee on Fair Employment Practice

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The amendment only enlarges the Committee from a chairman and four members to a chairman and six members; a partial amendment expanding a multi-member body diffuses rather than concentrates authority, and touches no eligibility, enforcement, or interpretive discretion.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Membership composition is the only mechanism amended; the predecessor EO 8802 already fixes responsibility in the appointed Committee, and this change leaves that clear accountability line intact.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The single provision changing Committee size stands alone, with no distinct remedies or problem frames packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The amended term is unambiguous—the Committee 'shall consist of a chairman and six other members'—leaving no room for divergent good-faith application of the amended provision itself.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Adding two committee seats creates no incentive for any actor to undermine the fair-employment purpose; the change is purely one of composition.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Under the diff-aware convention, this partial amendment touches only membership count, not the Committee's duration; the Committee's evergreen anti-discrimination mandate has no natural end-state warranting reauthorization pressure, so no sunset is owed.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The amendment removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; expanding membership neither excludes nor bypasses any judicial, legislative, or administrative check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Under EO 8802 the Committee 'receives and investigates complaints,' a third-party complaint mechanism the amendment leaves undisturbed; this composition change creates no self-reporting-only enforcement gap.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Enlarging an advisory-investigatory committee by two members produces no identifiable precedent, definitional import, or downstream authority claim beyond its stated administrative effect.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's statutory mandate is implicated, redirected, or duplicated; the amendment operates entirely within the existing Committee's own composition.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No exemptions, waivers, or carve-outs are created or altered; the change is confined to the number of committee seats.

note: Partial amendment of EO 8802 (read; predecessor 8823, cited only as a prior amendment also touching composition, likewise merely changes membership). Per diff-aware convention only the amended mechanism (Committee size) is assessed, and it is structurally clean; full framework of EO 8802 is not inherited because this modifies one identified piece.

title: Adjustments of Certain Rates of Pay and Allowances

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order self-executes statutorily-mandated pay adjustments under named USC pay-system provisions; it vests no discretionary authority in any single official, so no authority is concentrated.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The adjustments follow fixed statutory formulas set out in attached schedules with defined effective dates, leaving no failure chain or diffuse responsibility to trace.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Although it covers several pay systems, each is an independently statutorily-required annual adjustment under a single coherent purpose; no unrelated remedy or problem frame is smuggled in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time schedule-based pay adjustment creates no ongoing compliance rule for any actor to comply with, so there is no enforcement standard to be vague about.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order sets fixed pay rates with no ongoing behavioral or compliance relationship, so there is no incentive structure for any regulated party to game.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is a fixed, self-executing rate adjustment that is superseded and replaced annually (it here supersedes EO 12330 and will itself be superseded), evidencing maintenance through routine supersession rather than indefinite frozen authority; no sunset is owed.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Superseding the prior year's identically-structured pay order (EO 12330) removes no oversight mechanism; congressional and judicial roles in pay-setting statutes are untouched.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The order imposes no compliance obligation on any regulated party, so there is no violation for a third party to surface and the flag does not map onto it.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A routine implementation of statutorily-required pay adjustments establishes no precedent, definitional expansion, or institutional change usable in adjacent contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Multiple pay systems across agencies are adjusted per their own statutes without redirecting or countermanding any agency's mandate; no jurisdictional conflict arises.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order creates no coverage population and no exemptions from any rule; it is a fixed schedule adjustment with nothing to exempt.

note: Supersedes EO 12330 (read); the two are structurally identical annual pay-adjustment orders, so the diff introduces no new mechanism. Compliance-regime flags (4, 5, 8, 11) are NOT_APPLICABLE because a fixed-schedule pay adjustment creates no ongoing regulatory relationship.

title: Historically Black Colleges and Universities

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Implementation is distributed: the Secretary of Education sets goals in consultation with OMB and agency heads, while each agency reviews its own programs and designates its own implementing official; no single actor holds pooled discretionary authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each agency designates 'an immediate subordinate' responsible for implementation and the Secretary reports annually, but nothing names who is responsible or empowered to correct a failure if participation goals are not met, leaving a self-assessing agency as the only judge of its own success.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order pursues a single coherent purpose—increasing HBCU participation in federally sponsored programs—with mutually reinforcing directives; no distinct remedy or unrelated problem frame is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Directives to 'identify, reduce, and eliminate barriers' and set 'annual goals' supply no metric, threshold, or standard for what counts as adequate compliance, so two agency officials could in good faith reach substantially different implementation decisions.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Because each agency both self-reviews its programs and reports its own participation levels, an agency has an incentive to define barriers narrowly and report favorably, since it grades its own performance with no independent check.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The order establishes a permanent anti-discrimination/remedial initiative whose subject matter is evergreen by nature ('overcome the effects of discriminatory treatment'), with an annual reporting cycle providing recurring review moments; no sunset is owed under the permanence carve-out.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order only adds machinery—annual reports to the President, agency liaisons, and comment opportunities for HBCU presidents—and removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism, so the removal-direction flag does not fire.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement rests on agency self-review and self-reported participation levels; although HBCU presidents may 'comment on implementation,' they are given no standing or mechanism to compel correction, so no external actor has both access and leverage to surface non-compliance.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The initiative directs internal executive-branch effort toward a defined beneficiary class; no precedent, definitional import, or authority claim usable in unrelated contexts is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Each agency acts within its own existing statutory authorities—the order expressly has agencies 'identify the statutory authorities under which they can provide relief'—so no agency's mandate is redirected or countermanded and coordination through the Secretary is defined.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order creates no exemptions, waivers, or carve-outs from its coverage; it directs affirmative agency effort rather than defining anyone out of an obligation.

note: No predecessor EO amended or superseded, so no diff comparison required. The self-review/self-report design drives the accountability-gap, vague-enforcement, perverse-incentive, and third-party cluster (Flags 2, 4, 5, 8).

title: Providing for the Conduct of the Mobilization Effort of the Government

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order pools sweeping authority in a single Director of Defense Mobilization to 'direct, control, and coordinate all mobilization activities of the Executive Branch...including but not limited to production, procurement, manpower, stabilization, and transport,' with subordinate agencies subject to his direction and no external check—an all-of-government concentration that is the order's core design.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Director need only 'from time to time report to the President concerning his operations,' with no defined standard, trigger, or correction mechanism if mobilization fails; accountability runs solely to the President at the Director's own discretion.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order pursues the single coherent purpose of unifying mobilization direction; the enumerated activity areas are all facets of that one mobilization frame, not distinct bundled remedies.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Director may 'issue such directives, consonant with law, on policy and operations...as may be necessary,' with agencies duty-bound to execute them, but no defined standard bounds what directives are 'necessary,' so the scope of enforceable command is left to unilateral discretion.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The design centralizes command for wartime mobilization; no actor is rewarded for undermining the purpose, and the authorizing Defense Production Act was active and unexpired, so no zombie-trap incentive arises.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is an emergency/crisis-response mobilization grant—exactly the category with a natural end-state tied to the emergency's resolution—yet it contains no expiration or reauthorization trigger, continuing until actively revoked.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: By subordinating functions previously delegated under EOs 10161 and 10172 to the Director's 'direction and control' and providing that any inconsistent prior order 'shall control' in favor of this one, the order displaces the pre-existing distributed control structure over those mobilization functions.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Oversight of the Director's exercise of authority depends on his own discretionary reports to the President ('as may be required'); no external actor is given standing or access to surface a failure of the mobilization program.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Establishing a single Executive Office official empowered to direct all executive-branch mobilization creates a durable institutional precedent for centralized peacetime/wartime economic command extendable well beyond the immediate mobilization occasion.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order subjects functions already delegated to 'the respective officers concerned' under EOs 10161 and 10172 to the Director's direction and control and declares this order controls over any inconsistent prior order, redirecting existing agency mandates under one lateral authority.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order creates no exemptions, waivers, or carve-outs from coverage; it universally binds executive agencies to the Director's directives.

note: References EOs 10161 and 10172 (both in predecessors folder), whose delegated functions are subordinated to the new Director's direction and control—driving the Flag 7 and Flag 10 findings. Power Concentration rises to CRITICAL given the 'direct, control, and coordinate all mobilization activities' grant that is the order's entire architecture.

title: Planning, Acquisition, and Management of Federal Space

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Administrator of General Services is given authority to 'plan, acquire, and manage space' upon his own determination, but this is checked: an aggrieved agency head may request presidential review within thirty days, so authority is concentrated yet subject to a defined external appeal, warranting PRESENT not CRITICAL.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is clearly assigned to the Administrator, who must advise agency heads in writing and annually submit plans for presidential approval, providing a defined chain and correction point (the thirty-day review to the President).

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order coherently addresses one subject—federal space planning, acquisition, and management—and its enumerated siting policies all serve that single purpose without packaging an unrelated remedy or frame.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The siting criteria (e.g., 'positive economic and social influence on...development or redevelopment,' consideration of 'adequate low and moderate income housing') are open-ended standards on which two administrators could in good faith reach different acquisition decisions, and the Administrator's determination hinges on his own judgment that action 'will serve to improve...management.'

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order directs economical, consolidated use of government-owned space first; the incentives created (favor existing owned space, limit private rentals to prevailing rates) align with, rather than undermine, its efficiency purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural framework for the permanent administrative function of managing federal space, maintained through ordinary supersession (it expressly supersedes EO 11035); under the standing-procedural-framework carve-out no sunset is owed.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Compared to superseded EO 11035, this order preserves and arguably broadens the agency-head appeal to the President and adds consultation requirements with HUD, HEW, Commerce, and local officials; it removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The framework builds in structural cross-checks—agency-head written appeal to the President, mandatory consultation with other named departments and with governors/local officials—so multiple external actors have standing and access to contest space determinations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Directing federal siting decisions toward area development/redevelopment and social-economic outcomes leverages space management as a tool of urban policy, a second-order use of GSA authority reaching beyond the stated space-efficiency purpose into community development.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order requires the Administrator to consult and coordinate with other agencies rather than override their mandates, and agency heads retain their space requirements subject to defined review; coordination is structured, not conflicting.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order contains no exemptions, waivers, or carve-outs removing any agency or facility from its coverage; it applies uniformly to federally owned and leased space in the United States.

note: Supersedes EO 11035 (read); the diff adds development/redevelopment siting criteria and expanded inter-agency consultation while preserving the agency-head appeal-to-President check, so Flag 7 stays ABSENT. Flags 1 and 4 fire on the Administrator's determination authority and open-ended siting standards.

title: Suspending Certain Statutory Provisions Relating to Employment in the Canal Zone

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order is a self-executing presidential suspension of specified statutory provisions under express appropriation-act authority; it vests no ongoing discretionary decision authority in any official, so nothing is concentrated.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The action and its author are fully identified—the President suspends named statutory provisions with a defined effective date and a defined savings proviso—leaving no failure chain requiring a responsible corrector.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order does one thing—suspend compliance with two named appropriation-act sections regarding Canal Zone employment—with a single carve-out proviso; no distinct remedies or frames are packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time suspension of statutory compliance imposes no ongoing behavioral rule for any actor to comply with, so there is no enforcement standard to be vague about.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order lifts a compliance requirement rather than creating an ongoing incentive structure; no regulated party's conduct is governed going forward, so the flag does not map onto it.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The suspension is tied to the operative periods of the named 1952 appropriation acts (it applies 'from and including the effective date of the said acts'), so its duration is defined by the underlying statutes rather than left as open-ended standalone authority.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order exercises a suspension power that Congress itself granted in the appropriation acts; it removes no pre-existing external oversight mechanism, and the compensation-limitation provisions are expressly preserved.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Because the order creates no ongoing compliance obligation, there is no violation for any third party to surface, and the flag's ongoing-enforcement premise does not apply.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The suspension is a narrow, statutorily-authorized employment-provision waiver for the Canal Zone; no precedent, definitional import, or authority claim usable in unrelated contexts is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's statutory mandate is implicated, redirected, or duplicated; the order operates directly on statutory employment provisions without reassigning any agency's authority.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order's operative act is itself an exemption—suspending compliance with named statutory employment provisions for Canal Zone employment—and while it preserves the compensation-limitation carve-out, the suspension's boundaries (which employment relationships are covered by 'directly or indirectly by any branch' and the company-stock ownership language) are broad enough to raise a coverage-scope question.

note: Cites appropriation-act sections (not a prior EO) as authority, so no predecessor comparison required. Compliance-regime flags 4, 5, 8 are NOT_APPLICABLE (a compliance suspension creates no ongoing conduct rule); Flag 11 is scored on the merits because the order's core act is defining an employment carve-out.

title: Extension of the Provisions of Executive Order No. 9001 of December 27, 1941, to Contracts of the Immigration and Naturalization Service, Department of Justice

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Full incorporation of EO 9001 extends its broad war-contracting authority to the Attorney General, who may contract 'without regard to the provisions of law relating to the making, performance, amendment, or modification of contracts' and delegate that power downward—inheriting EO 9001's concentration of discretionary contracting authority in a single department head.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Inherited from EO 9001, oversight runs only through the department's own periodic reports to the President, and the Attorney General is empowered to settle, modify, or release contract obligations 'in his judgment' with no named external corrector, extending that diffuse accountability to INS contracting.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The extending order does one thing—apply the EO 9001 framework to INS feeding-and-care contracts—under a single war-facilitation frame, with no distinct remedies bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The inherited EO 9001 framework lets the department enter contracts 'without any restriction of any kind' as to type, character, location, or form and dispenses with advertising and competitive bidding, leaving what contracting conduct is permissible largely to unbounded departmental judgment.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: By waiving competitive bidding and permitting after-the-fact modification, amendment, or release of accrued obligations 'with or without consideration,' the inherited framework creates incentives for non-arm's-length contracting; the First War Powers Act was active and unexpired, so the zombie sub-flag does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This extends emergency wartime contracting authority ('deeming that such action will facilitate the prosecution of the war')—the kind of exceptional-powers grant with a natural end-state at the war's resolution—yet contains no expiration or reauthorization trigger tied to that end-state.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The extended framework operates 'without regard to the provisions of law relating to the making, performance, amendment, or modification of contracts' and waives advertising and competitive-bidding requirements, disabling the pre-existing procurement-oversight regime for INS contracts.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement of contracting propriety rests on the department's own quarterly reports to the President (with restricted/confidential contracts reported separately and disclosure at the department's discretion); no external actor is given standing or access to surface improper INS contracts.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Extending the sweeping EO 9001 contracting-waiver framework to a civilian Justice Department component establishes a precedent for exporting emergency war-contracting authority into non-military agencies, a downstream expansion beyond the order's immediate INS subject.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order grants new authority to the Attorney General over INS contracts rather than redirecting or countermanding another agency's existing statutory mandate; no lateral jurisdictional conflict with another agency is created.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The inherited framework exempts INS contracting wholesale from the normal statutory contracting rules ('without regard to the provisions of law'; advertising and competitive bidding 'need not be required'), a broad carve-out from the default procurement regime concentrated in the contracting department's discretion.

note: Full incorporation of EO 9001 (read): the order extends 'the provisions of Executive Order No. 9001...with respect to contracts,' so per the incorporation-by-reference convention it inherits EO 9001's flag profile (broad no-restriction contracting authority, self-reporting oversight, statutory-rule waivers) despite its brevity.

title: United States Advisory Council on the National Information Infrastructure

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order creates a purely advisory council that 'shall advise the Secretary'; it vests no decision authority over eligibility, enforcement, or implementation in any single actor, so nothing is concentrated.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
