flags.justification: Responsibility is clearly placed: the Secretary of Commerce appoints and receives advice, the Council reports, and the Department provides support; as an advisory body under the Federal Advisory Committee Act there is no failure chain requiring an independent corrector.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order coherently establishes one advisory council on a single subject (the National Information Infrastructure); the enumerated advisory topics are all facets of that mandate, not distinct bundled remedies.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Council has only advisory functions and no enforcement power over any party, so there is no enforcement standard capable of being applied inconsistently; its non-binding role is defined clearly.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: An unpaid advisory council issuing recommendations creates no incentive for any actor to undermine the order's purpose; the FACA authority invoked is active and unexpired.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The order contains an explicit sunset: the Council 'shall exist for a period of two years...unless the Council's charter is subsequently extended,' requiring affirmative reauthorization—a positive finding.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The Council operates under the Federal Advisory Committee Act, and the order preserves congressional reporting ('except that of reporting to Congress'); it removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism and adds a FACA-governed body.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: As a FACA advisory committee, the Council's proceedings are subject to that Act's transparency requirements and it may invite outside experts, so there is no hidden self-reporting compliance regime lacking third-party visibility.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Creating a time-limited advisory council with recommendation-only functions establishes no precedent, definitional expansion, or authority claim usable to reach beyond its advisory subject.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order provides that 'notwithstanding any other Executive order, the functions of the President under the Act...applicable to the Council...shall be performed by the Secretary' and that agency heads 'shall...provide to the Council such information as it may require,' reassigning presidential FACA functions and imposing information-supply duties across agencies, creating lateral friction with GSA's and other agencies' roles.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order carves Council members out of federal ethics coverage—they 'shall not be considered special government employees for any purpose or for purposes of 18 U.S.C. 201-203, 205, 207-209, and 218-219'—an exemption from conflict-of-interest statutes for the very private-sector advisers shaping infrastructure policy.

note: No predecessor EO amended or superseded (invokes FACA and 3 U.S.C. 301). Clean advisory architecture with an explicit two-year sunset; the only flags firing are the cross-agency reassignment of presidential FACA functions (Flag 10) and the ethics-statute exemption for Council members (Flag 11).

title: Providing for the Termination of the Office of War Information, and for the Disposition of Its Functions and of Certain Functions of the Office of Inter-american Affairs

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order concentrates significant discretionary authority in the Secretary of State (who designates the Interim Service head and may transfer or terminate any of its functions at will) and in the Director of the Bureau of the Budget, who unilaterally 'shall determine' which personnel, records, property, and appropriation balances relate to transferred functions.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility for each step is specifically assigned—the Service head is 'responsible to the Secretary of State,' and named officials handle winding-up—so the failure chain is traceable to identified actors.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Though it touches several offices, the order pursues a single coherent reorganization purpose—terminating OWI and disposing of its and certain OIAA functions—with all provisions serving that one frame.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: This is a one-time reorganization/termination that transfers and abolishes functions on fixed dates; it establishes no ongoing behavioral rule for any actor to comply with, so there is no enforcement standard to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A structural transfer-and-abolition of offices creates no ongoing compliance relationship or incentive for any regulated party to game; the First War Powers Act invoked was active, not lapsed.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The order is self-liquidating with built-in fixed dates (functions abolished as of September 15, 1945; entities abolished as of December 31, 1945), so it terminates on its own terms and there is no open-ended authority left to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order revokes and amends specified paragraphs of prior EOs to accomplish reorganization but removes no external oversight mechanism (judicial review, IG, or congressional reporting) over any ongoing function.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Because the order creates no ongoing compliance obligation, there is no continuing violation for a third party to surface; the flag's ongoing-enforcement premise does not apply to a one-time reorganization.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Consolidating OWI's overseas information functions into a State Department 'Interim International Information Service' seeds the institutional precedent and machinery for a permanent peacetime foreign-information apparatus, a downstream consequence beyond the immediate wartime-office termination.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order redistributes functions, personnel, records, and appropriation balances across the State Department, Bureau of the Budget, and Treasury and amends/revokes provisions of prior EOs (8840, 9182), reassigning existing agency functions and giving the Budget Director control over allocation—classic reorganization-driven inter-agency reassignment.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order defines no coverage population subject to an ongoing rule and creates no exemptions or waivers from such a rule; it is a self-executing reorganization with nothing to exempt.

note: References EOs 9182 (OWI), 8840/9532 (OIAA) as the offices being terminated/transferred (all in predecessors folder); the diff-relevant question is the reorganization architecture, not those offices' internal design. Compliance-regime flags 4, 5, 8, 11 are NOT_APPLICABLE because a self-executing one-time reorganization creates no ongoing regulatory relationship.

title: Authorizing the Appointment of Russell Sturgis to the Position of Assistant Territorial Representative in the Wage and Hour Division, Department of Labor, Without Regard to the Requirements of the Civil Service Rules

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A one-time authorization to appoint a single named individual; no discretionary authority is pooled in any official, and the appointment expressly 'shall not thereby confer a competitive classified civil-service status.'

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Self-executing, one-person authorization with nothing to implement or fail; no failure chain exists to leave unassigned.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order contains a single provision (one appointment authorization); there is no second matter against which bundling could occur.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time appointment authorization creates no ongoing behavioral rule for anyone to comply with or enforce.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory relationship or actor whose incentives the order shapes; a single fixed personnel action.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing appointment grants no continuing authority that could be subject to reauthorization pressure.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed; the appointee simply does not receive competitive classified status, and civil-service protections are neither disabled nor bypassed for others.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance regime exists over which a third party would have standing to surface violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow single-person administrative appointment with no systemic or precedential implications identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the Department of Labor is implicated; no second agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order is itself a fixed one-time exemption of a named person from civil-service rules, not an ongoing exemption structure carving actors out of a coverage regime.

note: Narrow one-time single-person civil-service exemption appointment; per the concrete NOT_APPLICABLE test, only decision-structure Flags 1, 2, 7 remain answerable (all ABSENT), while the compliance-regime and systemic flags are genuinely inapplicable.

title: Federal Compliance With Fuel Use Prohibitions

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Compliance responsibility rests with each agency head individually; the Secretary of Energy only provides advice, guidelines, and coordination, so authority is distributed rather than pooled.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each agency head is expressly named as 'responsible for compliance' with the fuel-use prohibitions and for funding requests, giving a clear responsible actor for implementation failure.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order addresses a single coherent problem (federal compliance with the Fuel Use Act) with no unrelated remedies or competing problem frames packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The standard is compliance with statutory fuel-use prohibitions in Titles II/III of the Act, with the Secretary setting survey and plan guidelines; the enforcing actor and standard are defined by reference to the statute.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Funds are locked to their conversion purpose (Sec. 1-402), and no actor is rewarded for non-compliance; the incentive structure aligns agencies toward the stated compliance goal.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The order grants an ongoing, indefinite compliance-and-reporting program (annual plans continuing until revoked) with no review-and-reauthorization mechanism; it is not an evergreen rights mandate but discretionary program authority that persists by default.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds OMB submission and a statutorily-required congressional report (Sec. 1-502) rather than removing any pre-existing oversight; direction is additive, not subtractive.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement rests entirely on agency self-survey, self-reported annual plans, and internal OMB review; no external actor with standing and independent access is given a mechanism to surface non-compliance.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order confines itself to internal federal facility compliance with an existing statute; no precedent or definitional expansion usable in unrelated contexts is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: DOE and OMB are given coordinating and guideline roles that supplement, not countermand, agencies' existing statutory duties under the Fuel Use Act; no mandate conflict is created.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Sec. 1-501 confines exemptions strictly to those 'granted under the provisions of the Power plant and Industrial Fuel Use Act,' foreclosing discretionary carve-outs that could swallow the rule.

note: Ongoing internal federal-compliance regime; references EO 12088 (present in referenced-predecessors) as an incorporated pollution-control standard, a partial cross-reference not a wholesale extension, so flags are assessed on this order's own architecture.

title: Exemption of Abraham Straus From Compulsory Retirement for Age

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A single self-executing exemption of one named employee for a fixed one-year period; no discretionary authority is pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The action is complete on issuance with a defined end date (January 31, 1943); there is no implementation chain that could fail and leave responsibility unassigned.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order contains a single provision (one retirement exemption); there is nothing to bundle against.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A fixed one-year personnel exemption creates no ongoing behavioral rule to enforce against anyone.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory relationship or actor class whose incentives the order shapes; a single fixed action.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The exemption is expressly time-limited to 'a period of one year ending January 31, 1943,' so it carries a built-in expiration rather than lacking one.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed; the order operates the statutory exemption authority without disabling any review process.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance regime exists for any third party to have standing to police.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow single-person exemption with no systemic or precedential implications identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the Treasury Department/Customs Service is implicated; no other agency's mandate is affected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order is a single fixed statutory exemption of one named person, not an ongoing exemption structure creating discretion to carve out a class.

note: Narrow one-time single-person retirement exemption with a defined end date; Flag 6 scores ABSENT (not N/A) because the order does contain an explicit expiration, and the compliance-regime flags are genuinely inapplicable.

title: Modification of Executive Order No. 6957 of February 4, 1935, Withdrawing Certain Public Lands

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order narrowly authorizes the Secretary of the Interior to issue one coal-prospecting permit to a named individual under a specific leasing act; this is a bounded, statutorily-channeled authorization, not discretionary pooling of authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Interior is the identified actor for issuing the permit under defined statutory leasing procedures; responsibility is clear.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order contains a single modification of one prior withdrawal for one permit; there is nothing to bundle against.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time land-status modification authorizing a single permit under a named leasing act creates no ongoing enforcement standard.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory relationship whose incentives the order shapes; a single land-status modification.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing modification of a land withdrawal grants no continuing authority that could be subject to reauthorization pressure.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Comparison to predecessor EO 6957 (present in referenced-predecessors) shows the withdrawal framework is merely relaxed to permit one leasing-act permit; no oversight mechanism is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance regime exists over which a third party would have standing to surface violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow modification of a land withdrawal for a single permit with no systemic or precedential implications identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the Department of the Interior is implicated; no second agency's statutory mandate is redirected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order is a single fixed carve-out from one land withdrawal for one permit, not an ongoing exemption structure with discretion to expand coverage exceptions.

note: Diff-aware check against predecessor EO 6957 (available): this partial amendment relaxes the prior land withdrawal only to allow one named permittee's leasing-act permit; the withdrawal framework otherwise continues, so flags are assessed on this narrow modification, and only decision-structure flags remain applicable.

title: Designating the Honorable Jorge L. Cordova as Acting Judge of the District Court of the United States for Puerto Rico

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A single designation of one named individual as an acting judge; no discretionary decision authority is pooled, so far as the (unpublished) title indicates.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: A self-executing single designation with no implementation chain that could fail and leave responsibility unassigned.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: A single designation with one provision; nothing to bundle against.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time judicial designation creates no ongoing behavioral rule to enforce.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory relationship or actor class whose incentives the designation shapes.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A one-time acting-judge designation grants no continuing programmatic authority subject to reauthorization pressure.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Nothing in the designation removes any pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance regime exists for a third party to police.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow single-person judicial designation with no systemic or precedential implications identifiable from the available text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only one judicial office is implicated; no second agency's statutory mandate is redirected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage regime and thus no exemption structure exists.

note: Order text was not published; only the title is available, indicating a narrow single-person acting-judge designation. Scoring is based on that title; the body text was unavailable, and only decision-structure flags 1/2/7 remain answerable (all clean/ABSENT).

title: Establishing an Emergency Board To Investigate a Dispute Between The Long Island Rail Road and Certain of Its Employees Represented by the United Transportation Union

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authority is exercised by a three-member board appointed under a statutory mandate (Sec. 9A of the Railway Labor Act); the board only investigates and reports, so no unchecked authority is pooled in one actor.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The board is named and tasked to report findings to the President within 30 days (Sec. 2); responsibility for the investigative function is clearly assigned.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order addresses a single labor dispute under one statutory mechanism; no distinct remedies or problem frames are packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The status-quo requirement is precise: 'no change, except by agreement of the parties, shall be made...in the conditions out of which the dispute arose' for a defined 120-day window (Sec. 3), tracking the statute.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Board members must have no financial or other interest in any railroad organization or carrier, and the process is a neutral investigate-and-report cooling-off mechanism; no actor is rewarded for undermining it.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The board 'shall terminate upon the submission of the report' (Sec. 4), with the status-quo obligation fixed at 120 days; the order has an explicit built-in expiration.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order executes the statutory emergency-board process without removing any pre-existing oversight; it reports to the President as the Act requires.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The Act's structure gives both parties (carrier and union) standing and incentive to participate and surface issues to the neutral board; enforcement does not depend on one party's self-reporting.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A routine, statutorily-prescribed emergency board for a single dispute; no precedent or authority expansion usable in unrelated contexts is identifiable.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The ad hoc board is created under the Railway Labor Act and does not redirect or countermand any existing agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No exemption or waiver structure is created; the status-quo requirement applies uniformly to the parties, with the only carve-out being mutual agreement of both sides.

note: Standard statutory emergency-board order under Sec. 9A of the Railway Labor Act; clean architecture with a defined membership, a defined reporting deadline, and a built-in termination trigger, so all applicable flags score ABSENT.

title: Protection and Enhancement of the Cultural Environment

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Duties are distributed across all federal agency heads, the Secretary of the Interior, and the independent Advisory Council on Historic Preservation; no single actor holds unchecked authority over historic-preservation determinations.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each agency head both decides whether to transfer/demolish its own properties and controls the referral, and there is no actor with power to compel preservation once the Advisory Council has merely 'been provided an opportunity to comment' (Sec. 2(b)); accountability for a wrong demolition is diffuse.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order coherently pursues a single purpose (federal stewardship of historic/cultural properties) across four cited preservation statutes; the provisions are all facets of that one frame, not distinct bundled problems.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Key standards are undefined: agencies must 'exercise caution' and act only after a 'reasonable period' of review, and the Advisory Council's comment is non-binding, so two agency heads could in good faith reach different decisions on the same property.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Because an agency need only afford the Advisory Council 'an opportunity to comment' and may then still proceed to demolish, an agency wishing to avoid preservation burdens is incentivized to move properties through before nomination or to treat comment as a formality.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is a permanent evergreen stewardship mandate implementing standing preservation statutes; like an anti-degradation policy, its subject matter has no natural end-state that reauthorization pressure would track, so the permanence carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds oversight (Interior review, Advisory Council comment, annual procedure submissions) rather than removing any pre-existing check; direction is additive, so Flag 7 does not fire.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement depends on each agency self-inventorying and self-referring its own properties; the Advisory Council and Interior only see what agencies choose to refer, and no external actor has independent access to surface a demolition before it occurs.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order builds interagency preservation procedure within existing statutory authority; no precedent or definitional expansion usable in unrelated domains is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Interior and Advisory Council are given advisory/coordinating roles consonant with the cited statutes; agencies retain their own property authority, so no statutory mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No waiver or exemption structure removes properties from coverage; the review procedures apply across all federally owned properties that appear to qualify for the Register.

note: Broad standing preservation-procedure order; the recurring weakness is that agencies self-police their own properties and the Advisory Council's role is limited to non-binding comment, driving PRESENT on Flags 2, 4, 5, and 8 without any single core collapse rising to CRITICAL.

title: Creating a Board of Inquiry To Report on Certain Labor Disputes Affecting the Maritime Industry of the United States

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A three-member board with named members is created under Sec. 206 of the Labor-Management Relations Act to inquire and report; it holds fact-finding powers set by statute, not concentrated discretionary authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The board is a named actor tasked to report to the President by a fixed date (October 10, 1959) under Sec. 206; responsibility for the investigative function is clear.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order addresses a single set of related maritime labor disputes under one statutory mechanism; no distinct remedies or problem frames are packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The board's 'powers and duties as set forth in Title II' of the Act and its reporting obligation are defined by the statute; the standard is not left to divergent good-faith readings.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The board is a neutral statutory fact-finding body reporting to the President; no actor is rewarded for undermining the inquiry's purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Unlike a clean emergency board, this order provides that after reporting the board 'shall continue in existence to perform such other functions as may be required...until the Board is terminated by the President,' creating open-ended authority with no natural end-state tied to reauthorization pressure.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order executes the statutory board-of-inquiry process without removing any pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Under the Act both employer associations and the union have standing and incentive to participate before the neutral board; enforcement of the inquiry does not depend on one party's voluntary self-reporting.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A statutorily-prescribed board of inquiry for a specific dispute; no precedent or authority expansion usable in unrelated contexts is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The ad hoc board is created under the Labor-Management Relations Act and does not redirect or countermand any existing agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No exemption or waiver structure is created; the order simply constitutes a fact-finding board over the described disputes.

note: Statutory Taft-Hartley board of inquiry, otherwise clean; the sole flag is Flag 6, because the board is directed to 'continue in existence...until...terminated by the President' rather than dissolving on submission of its report, giving it an open-ended tail.

title: Abolishing the Government Patents Board and Providing for the Performance of its Functions

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Comparison to predecessor EO 10096 (available) shows functions formerly held by a multi-agency Government Patents Board and its Chairman are transferred to the single Secretary of Commerce, including the Chairman's power to 'determine with finality any controversies or disputes...concerning the ownership of inventions'; that dispute-resolution authority now pools in one cabinet officer.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of Commerce is named as the responsible actor for the transferred functions and for disposition of property, personnel, records, and funds (Sec. 3); responsibility is clearly assigned.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order does one coherent thing (abolish the Board and reassign its functions); no distinct unrelated remedies or problem frames are packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The substantive patent-ownership standards continue under EO 10096's detailed paragraph 1 criteria, which persist except as inconsistent; the transfer does not itself introduce an undefined enforcement standard, and existing rules 'continue in full force' (Sec. 4).

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A reorganization moving an existing dispute-resolution and patent-policy function into Commerce creates no reward for undermining the policy's purpose; incentives are unchanged from the predecessor scheme.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is a permanent administrative-structure reorganization that itself amends/supersedes numbered predecessors (EO 10096), evidencing a category of rule maintained through ordinary supersession rather than reauthorization; the standing-procedural-framework carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: EO 10096's multi-agency Government Patents Board provided a built-in consultative check on the Chairman's patent-policy administration (Sec. 3(b): the Board 'shall advise and confer with the Chairman'); abolishing the Board removes that pre-existing interagency oversight layer, folding advice-and-consult into a single secretary.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The underlying regime's disclosure/indexing and dispute-submission mechanisms from EO 10096 continue; the reorganization does not remove any third-party mechanism for surfacing issues, and the dispute process is invoked by employees and agencies as parties.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A contained reorganization of one board's functions into an existing department; no precedent or definitional expansion usable in unrelated domains is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order dissolves a standing interagency body composed of representatives from ten departments/agencies (EO 10096 Sec. 3(a)) and consolidates its cross-cutting functions into Commerce alone, redirecting a coordination structure that other agencies participated in into a single agency's hands.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No exemption or waiver structure is created; the order reassigns functions without carving actors out of coverage.

note: Diff-aware scoring against predecessor EO 10096 (available): abolishing the multi-agency Government Patents Board and consolidating its functions (including final dispute-resolution authority) into the Secretary of Commerce fires Flag 1 (upward pooling), Flag 7 (removal of the Board's built-in interagency advisory check), and Flag 10 (dissolving a ten-agency coordination body into one).

title: Establishment of a Fetal Tissue Bank

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of Health and Human Services is given sole authority to establish the bank, set the procedures, run the peer-review selection of 'meritorious research projects,' and 'develop human fetal cell lines,' with no external check specified over these determinations.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary both designs the program and is the sole judge of whether projects are 'meritorious' and whether tissue sourcing complies, reporting only to the President; there is no independent actor with obligation and capacity to correct a failure of implementation.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order pursues a single stated purpose (a fetal-tissue bank sourced from ectopic pregnancies and spontaneous abortions to support research); no distinct unrelated remedies or problem frames are packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Standards are undefined: tissue is to be released for 'meritorious research projects selected through an appropriate peer review process,' with 'meritorious' and 'appropriate' left unspecified, so two administrators could apply the selection standard very differently in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Restricting sourcing exclusively to ectopic pregnancies and spontaneous abortions is designed to avoid creating demand-driven incentives; no actor class is rewarded for undermining the stated research purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The order grants ongoing discretionary program authority to build and operate a tissue bank and develop cell lines indefinitely, with only a one-time progress report (Sec. 4) and no review-and-reauthorization mechanism; this is discretionary program authority, not an evergreen rights mandate.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order expressly requires the program to be carried out 'in accordance with all other applicable legal requirements related to fetal tissue' (Sec. 3), preserving existing legal constraints rather than removing any pre-existing oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Compliance with the sourcing restriction and peer-review process depends on HHS's own internal administration; no external actor is given standing or access to verify that tissue truly comes only from ectopic pregnancies and spontaneous abortions.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: By creating a federal fetal-tissue bank and directing development of 'human fetal cell lines,' the order establishes durable institutional infrastructure and a policy precedent in a contested domain that reaches beyond the immediate research-supply purpose and shapes future federal fetal-tissue policy.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The program is housed within HHS's own health-research remit; no other agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No waiver or exemption structure is created; the sourcing restriction ('exclusively from ectopic pregnancies and spontaneous abortions') is a narrowing limit on coverage, not a carve-out that could swallow the rule.

note: Standalone program-creation order concentrating design, selection, and sourcing-compliance authority in one Secretary with only a single progress report; PRESENT on Flags 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, and 9, none rising to CRITICAL since the sourcing restriction and legal-compliance clause keep the program's stated purpose largely intact.

title: Exclusions From the Federal Labor-Management Relations Program

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The President unilaterally 'determined' the excluded units have intelligence/national-security functions and that Chapter 71 'cannot be applied' consistent with national security, a self-certified determination resting solely in the President with no external check (Sec. 1).

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The order is a discrete amendment adding named DEA/Marshals sub-offices to an exclusion list; responsibility for the determination rests clearly with the President and the exclusion is definite, so no diffuse-blame chain arises.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single subject matter (curing the form defect of EO 12559 by restating determinations and amending EO 12171's Sec. 1-209); no distinct remedies or problem frames are packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The operative standard ('cannot be applied in a manner consistent with national security requirements and considerations') is a conclusory label; two officials could disagree in good faith on which sub-units qualify, though here it is applied to specifically enumerated offices.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order simply removes named units from collective-bargaining coverage; it creates no ongoing incentive rewarding conduct that undermines its purpose. No lapsed statute is invoked.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural amendment to an exclusion list maintained through ordinary supersession/amendment (it amends EO 12171 and explains the EO 12559 history), fitting the standing-procedural-framework carve-out; no natural end-state exists.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: By excluding these units from Chapter 71, the order removes the statutory labor-relations framework (including FLRA processes) that otherwise applied to them; this is a removal of a pre-existing external check, not a new addition.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The order is a coverage exclusion, not an ongoing compliance regime; it creates no violations for any external actor to surface, so third-party enforcement incentives do not apply.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order legitimizes curing a court-invalidated EO by re-issuance reciting the same determinations post hoc (AFGE v. Reagan), establishing a precedent for expanding national-security labor exclusions via self-certified determinations reusable in future contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order names DOJ sub-units but merely removes them from a labor program; it does not redirect or countermand any agency's statutory operational mandate, so no lateral jurisdictional conflict is created.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order is itself an exemption instrument carving named units out of the labor-relations program on a discretionary national-security determination; the exclusion mechanism is concentrated in the President with a broad, self-defined trigger.

note: Partial amendment of EO 12171 (predecessor read from referenced-predecessors); order swaps Sec. 1-209 to expand the excluded DEA/Marshals units. Flag 8 NOT_APPLICABLE as a one-time coverage exclusion with no ongoing compliance relationship.

title: Blocking Iraqi Government Property and Prohibiting Transactions With Iraq

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Sec. 5 vests the Secretary of the Treasury with broad authority to promulgate rules, prohibit/regulate any transaction of economic value, and redelegate, with the scope of blocking and prohibitions defined by the President's IEEPA emergency finding and no external check on implementation.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is clearly assigned: Treasury (in consultation with State) administers, and all agencies are directed to carry out the order; a named actor bears the obligation to implement.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent frame (responding to Iraq's invasion of Kuwait under the EO 12722 emergency); the enumerated prohibitions all serve the one stated sanctions purpose and are not distinct unrelated remedies forced into a package.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Enforcement rests on regulations 'that may hereafter be issued'; the anti-evasion clause (Sec. 2(h)) and undefined 'promotes or is intended to promote' language leave trigger conditions and standards to be filled in later, permitting divergent good-faith application.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The blocking-and-licensing structure creates an ongoing agency interest in maintaining broad discretionary licensing authority, and blanket prohibitions with donation carve-outs incentivize routing transactions through exempt channels; but the emergency (EO 12722) is active and unexpired, so the Zombie Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: An emergency/exceptional-powers grant is exactly the authority that should track the emergency's resolution, yet the order contains no sunset or reauthorization trigger of its own; it persists until revoked (the NEA's annual renewal is statutory, not in this text).

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order transmits itself to Congress and operates under IEEPA/NEA reporting frameworks; it does not remove any pre-existing oversight mechanism over the newly created sanctions authority.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Compliance depends on U.S. persons refraining from prohibited transactions and on agency detection; no external private actor is given standing or incentive to surface violations, and offshore/overseas-branch conduct is hard for third parties to observe.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order expands IEEPA blocking authority (adding export/finance/transport prohibitions and 'anything of economic value') beyond EO 12722, entrenching a comprehensive-sanctions template reusable against other targets and normalizing broad economic-emergency authority.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order directs all agencies to cooperate and centralizes administration in Treasury/State without countermanding any agency's statutory operational mandate; suspension of licenses under existing authorizations is within the emergency framework, not a lateral mandate conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Exemptions (donations for human suffering, journalism, official business, and any exception 'provided in regulations that may hereafter be issued') are discretionary and open-ended, with Treasury able to expand carve-outs via licensing, creating scope for exemption to swallow coverage.

note: Extends/supersedes-in-part EO 12722 (predecessor read); this order adds substantially broader prohibitions and Treasury authority to the active EO 12722 emergency. Emergency is active and unexpired, so Zombie Trap does not fire despite emergency-derived authority.

title: To Provide a Guide on Ethical Standards to Government Officials

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order sets conduct standards binding on covered officials themselves and leaves review to each department/agency head (para. 6); it pools no eligibility, enforcement, or interpretive authority in any single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each department/agency head is to 'review or issue internal directives' to assure standards, but no actor is named with obligation and capacity to correct a failure, and the covered official also being the regulated party leaves the failure chain diffuse.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent frame (ethical standards for senior officials); the enumerated prohibitions are facets of one conflict-of-interest purpose, not distinct unrelated policies bundled for accept/reject.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Standards turn on open-textured tests such as 'create the appearance of' private gain and 'incompatible with the full and proper discharge,' with enforcement left to each agency head's internal directives; two officials could reach different good-faith enforcement decisions.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The incentive created (avoid outside compensation and conflicts) aligns with the stated integrity purpose; no design feature rewards conduct that undermines it. No lapsed statute is invoked.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: An ethics/integrity standard is evergreen by nature (like an anti-discrimination mandate) with no natural end-state that reauthorization pressure would track, so permanence is appropriate and the flag does not fire.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Para. 7 expressly states nothing supersedes, alters, or interprets existing law or regulation; the order adds ethics directives without removing any pre-existing external check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement depends on agency-head internal review of officials' own conduct; no external actor is given standing, access, or incentive to surface violations of these ethics rules.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: As an internal-standards guide expressly not superseding law (para. 7) and self-limited to senior officials, it establishes no authority claim or definitional precedent extending into unrelated domains.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Implementation is decentralized to each department/agency head over its own personnel; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded and no parallel authority conflict is created.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Paragraph 5 carves out reimbursements, participation in charitable/civic/political affairs, and awards from the compensation bars; these exceptions are defined but the 'bona fide'/'to the extent permitted by law' qualifiers give room to characterize activities into the exempt categories.

note: Standalone ethics-standards order with no numbered predecessor to compare; scored on its own text. Enforcement/exemptions flags fire on open-textured standards and self-review structure.

title: Authorizing the Secretary of War to Take Possession of and to Operate Certain Plants and Facilities of Montgomery Ward & Co., Incorporated

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Para. 1 vests the Secretary of War with sweeping seizure and operating authority ('any manner that he deems essential,' exercise the company's contractual rights, take 'any other steps that he deems necessary') over private property, concentrated in one official with no external check; this discretionary takeover authority is the core of the order.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary determines when 'productive efficiency ... has been restored' (para. 4) triggering termination and reports to the President only on wage-payment shortfalls (para. 2); the actor exercising the power also judges when its predicate is satisfied.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single frame (wartime seizure to end labor disturbances impeding war production at Montgomery Ward); the operative provisions all serve that one purpose and are not distinct policies packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The termination trigger ('productive efficiency ... prevailing prior to the ... interruptions has been restored') and the enforcement standard ('deems essential'/'deems necessary') are Secretary-defined, allowing wide good-faith variance in what compliance and completion mean.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order lets the Secretary pay court-ordered NWLB wage increases only 'out of the net operating income' of the seized plants (para. 2), creating an incentive to under-realize operating income or delay wage relief; but the underlying War Labor Disputes Act is an active wartime statute, so the Zombie Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The order contains an explicit end-state: possession terminates within sixty days after productive efficiency is restored (para. 4), tying the emergency seizure authority to resolution of the disturbance rather than leaving it permanent.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The seizure moves operation of the plants into War Department control under the Secretary's discretion, displacing the ordinary private/judicial governance of the company's operations and enabling him to 'exercise any contractual or other rights' of the company outside normal review.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is a one-time seizure-and-operate directive to a cabinet officer, not an ongoing compliance regime for a regulated population; there are no recurring violations for an external third party to surface.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order establishes precedent for presidential seizure and operation of a private retailer/distributor (not a munitions plant) under wartime labor statutes, extending the seizure template into commercial-distribution contexts and normalizing broad Commander-in-Chief takeover claims.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Para. 3 directs other agencies to cooperate with and assign personnel to the Secretary of War; this is coordination, not a redirection or countermanding of another agency's statutory operational mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order applies wholesale to the enumerated Montgomery Ward plants with no exemption or waiver structure carving actors out of coverage; the exemptions concept does not map onto this seizure directive.

note: Wartime seizure order with no numbered EO predecessor; references NWLB directive orders, not amended EOs. Flags 8 and 11 NOT_APPLICABLE as this is a one-time seizure directive with no ongoing compliance relationship or exemption structure. Power Concentration rated CRITICAL given unbounded discretionary takeover authority.

title: Presidential Advisory Committee on Gulf War Veterans' Illnesses

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Committee is advisory only (Sec. 2(c): 'shall not ... conduct scientific research'; provides 'advice and recommendations'), reports to three Cabinet secretaries, and holds no decision authority over eligibility or enforcement, so no authority is pooled.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Roles are clearly assigned: members appointed by the President, reporting through three named Secretaries, with DoD performing FACA functions (Sec. 4(a)); as an advisory body with defined reporting lines, the accountability chain is coherent.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single frame (advising on Gulf War veterans' illnesses); the enumerated review topics (research, treatment, outreach, risk factors) are all facets of that one subject, not distinct policies bundled for accept/reject.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order creates a purely advisory committee with no regulatory or enforcement mechanism applied to any party, so there is no enforcement standard whose clarity could be assessed.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: An advisory committee that only reviews and recommends creates no ongoing behavioral rule or compliance relationship, so no actor-class incentive to undermine a stated compliance purpose arises. No lapsed statute is invoked.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The order has explicit termination triggers: a final report due by December 31, 1996, and termination 30 days after that report (Secs. 2(f), 4(b)), and it is FACA-subject; the temporary body has a defined end.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order subjects the Committee to FACA and preserves the annual congressional-reporting function (Sec. 4(a)); it adds an advisory check without removing any pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no regulated party or compliance regime; the advisory committee itself generates public reports under FACA, so the third-party-enforcement concept does not apply.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A time-limited, FACA-subject advisory committee that expressly cannot address legal liability (Sec. 2(d)) or conduct research establishes no authority claim or definitional precedent extending into unrelated domains.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Committee reviews and coordinates around existing bodies (Persian Gulf Veterans Coordinating Board) and reports to DoD/VA/HHS without redirecting or countermanding any agency's statutory mandate; DoD merely performs FACA administrative functions.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order establishes an advisory body with no regulatory coverage population and thus no exemption or waiver structure to assess.

note: Standalone advisory-committee order, no numbered EO predecessor. Compliance-regime flags (4, 5, 8, 11) NOT_APPLICABLE because a purely advisory, FACA-subject, self-terminating body creates no ongoing regulatory relationship; scores cleanly (0) across all applicable flags.

title: Continuation of Export Control Regulations

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order revives and continues the entire Export Administration Act framework under IEEPA in the President and his delegate (Secretary of Commerce), continuing broad export-licensing and rulemaking authority (Secs. 1-3) with control over exports pooled in the executive under an emergency finding.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Administration is clearly assigned to the Secretary of Commerce (continuing existing regulations and delegations); responsibility for implementation is definite rather than diffuse.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The stated threat frames three distinct concerns together (national-security export controls, anti-foreign-boycott foreign-policy cooperation, and protecting the domestic economy from 'excessive drain of scarce materials'), bundling separate problem areas under one emergency continuation.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order continues an entire body of regulations 'to the extent permitted by law' and layers IEEPA sections 203(b)(2)/206 to 'control over any inconsistent provisions,' leaving the operative enforcement standard as a cross-referenced composite that officials could reconcile differently in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Sec. 1 continues the EAA framework 'Notwithstanding the expiration of the Export Administration Act of 1979' by invoking IEEPA, and Sec. 4 states intent to terminate only 'upon the enactment ... of a bill reauthorizing' the EAA -- an incentive to keep exercising lapsed-statute authority indefinitely rather than press for clean reauthorization.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: An emergency continuation of expired statutory authority is exactly the kind of grant that should track resolution, yet it 'shall remain in effect until terminated' (Sec. 4) with termination only aspirational upon future reauthorization -- no self-executing sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order routes export-control authority through IEEPA rather than the lapsed EAA, bypassing the periodic congressional reauthorization and sunset review that the EAA's own expiration structure supplied, effectively disabling that legislative check on the authority.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Export-control compliance rests on exporters and Commerce administration via licensing and self-certification; no external third party is given standing or access to surface non-compliance, and the continued administrative-sanctions regime operates on agency detection.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Using IEEPA's emergency power to resurrect an expired regulatory statute wholesale establishes a powerful precedent for continuing any lapsed authority by declaring a national emergency, undermining Congress's power to sunset delegated authority -- a structural effect far beyond export control.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order continues existing Commerce (and section 38(e) Arms Export Control Act) administration through the prior delegations without redirecting or countermanding another agency's statutory operational mandate; it preserves the status quo allocation.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The continued regulatory regime is administered through export licensing where the Secretary of Commerce grants case-by-case exceptions with broad discretion, and the order lets all such licenses 'remain in full force ... until amended or revoked,' concentrating exemption control in the agency.

note: Full incorporation by reference of EO 12002/12214/12131/12551 export-control delegations, which were NOT in referenced-predecessors (INDEX lists them cited by other orders only), so the diff comparison to those delegations could not be made; scored on the incorporating order's own text. Classic Zombie Emergency Trap: continues authority 'notwithstanding the expiration' of the EAA, firing Flag 5 CRITICAL.

title: Designation of Under Secretary and Assistant Secretaries of the Interior to Act as Secretary of the Interior

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order merely establishes a succession order for who performs the Secretary's duties during absence/sickness; it distributes an existing office's duties by defined ordering rather than pooling new discretionary authority in one actor.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The line of acting authority is clear and sequential (Under Secretary, then named Assistant Secretaries), so responsibility for who acts as Secretary is unambiguous.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single, self-contained subject (Interior Department acting-succession); nothing distinct is bundled, and it supersedes one predecessor (EO 7277) on the same narrow matter.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A succession-designation order creates no compliance rule or enforcement mechanism for any party, so there is no enforcement standard to evaluate.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order establishes an internal acting-succession with no ongoing behavioral rule for any regulated actor, so no incentive to undermine a compliance purpose exists. No lapsed statute is invoked.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A departmental acting-succession rule is a standing procedural framework revised through ordinary supersession (it expressly supersedes EO 7277, itself a successor to EO 7198), evidencing maintenance by routine amendment rather than needing a sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes no existing oversight; it simply designates acting officials under R.S. 179 and does not touch any review mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no regulated party or compliance regime in a succession designation, so third-party enforcement standing does not apply.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow internal acting-succession designation with no systemic or precedential implications beyond Interior's own operations; no second-order effect is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the Department of the Interior is implicated; no second agency's mandate is redirected or countermanded, so the cannibalization concept does not apply.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population and no exemption/waiver structure exist in a succession-designation order.

note: Supersedes EO 7277 (predecessor read); this is a partial update simply changing the named acting officials and adding a second Assistant Secretary fallback, otherwise identical in structure. Narrow administrative succession order: Flags 9, 10, 11 and compliance-regime Flags 4, 5, 8 NOT_APPLICABLE; scores clean 0 on the answerable decision-structure flags.

title: Establishing the Necedah Migratory Waterfowl Refuge

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order reserves described lands as a refuge for the Department of Agriculture and authorizes the Secretary to prescribe rules for entry/hunting; this is a bounded land-management delegation, not a pooling of broad discretionary authority in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Administration is clearly assigned to the Secretary of Agriculture over a defined refuge; responsibility for the reservation and its rules is unambiguous.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single subject (establishing one named wildlife refuge on described lands); nothing distinct is bundled with the reservation.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The prohibition on hunting/trapping/entering applies 'except under such rules or regulations as may be prescribed by the Secretary of Agriculture,' leaving the operative standard of what is permitted entirely to future, unspecified regulations.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The refuge prohibition aligns with the conservation purpose and creates no ongoing incentive rewarding conduct that undermines it. No lapsed statute is invoked.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A permanent wildlife refuge is evergreen by nature (a standing conservation reservation with no natural end-state), so the absence of a sunset is appropriate and the flag does not fire.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order reserves lands 'subject to valid existing rights' and amends EO 6964 only to exclude this area from a prior land-classification withdrawal; it removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement of the anti-hunting/entry prohibition depends on Agriculture's own wardens and future regulations; no external third party is given standing or incentive to surface violations occurring on the remote 40,500-acre refuge.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A discrete land reservation for a named refuge with described boundaries establishes no authority claim or definitional precedent extending into unrelated domains.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order reserves lands already transferred to Agriculture (per EO 7908) and adjusts a prior public-land withdrawal (EO 6964) to accommodate this refuge; it does not countermand another agency's operational statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Coverage is qualified 'subject to valid existing rights,' the Provided clause excludes private inholdings until acquired, and entry/hunting bans yield to whatever rules the Secretary prescribes -- exemptions bounded but resting on Secretary discretion.

note: Cites EO 7908 (read; land transfer to Agriculture) and amends 'EO 6964 of February 5, 1935' (public-land withdrawal for Wisconsin) -- but the file present for EO 6964 is EO 6964-A (a Knox County painters' agreement), so the actual cited land-withdrawal predecessor was not recoverable; that specific comparison could not be made. Establishes an ongoing entry/hunting regulatory relationship, so compliance-regime flags 4/8/11 apply and fire on the delegation to future Secretary rules.

title: Central Intelligence Agency Retirement and Disability System

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Director of Central Intelligence maintains the System and Fund and, under Sec. 1(b), may permit annuity allotments/assignments 'for such purposes as the Director in his sole discretion considers appropriate,' pooling discretionary authority over fund payments in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is clearly assigned to the Director, who maintains the Fund and prescribes regulations (Sec. 3); the administering actor is unambiguous.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single frame (conforming the CIA retirement system to civil-service rules on assignability, application deadlines, and deposits); the provisions all serve that one conforming purpose.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The core discretionary standard for permitting annuity allotments -- purposes 'the Director in his sole discretion considers appropriate' (Sec. 1(b)) -- supplies no criteria, so administration could vary widely in good faith; other provisions (deadlines, deposits) are, by contrast, precise.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The conforming rules on assignability and application deadlines create no incentive rewarding conduct that undermines the retirement system's purpose. No lapsed statute is invoked -- authority rests on the CIA Retirement Act of 1964.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A permanent retirement-and-disability administrative system is evergreen by nature (a standing benefit-administration framework maintained through ordinary amendment), so the absence of a sunset is appropriate.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order implements the statutory retirement system and preserves legal exceptions ('except as otherwise may be provided by Federal laws,' Sec. 1(a)); it removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Benefit administration runs entirely through the Director's determinations on applications and allotments within CIA, an inherently closed intelligence-agency system; no external third party has standing or access to surface improper fund determinations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow order conforming a specific agency's retirement fund to civil-service rules establishes no authority claim or definitional precedent extending into unrelated domains.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order concerns only the CIA's own retirement fund administered by the Director; no other agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded (it conforms to, rather than displaces, civil-service retirement rules).

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Sec. 1(b) permits the Director in 'sole discretion' to authorize assignments as an exception to the general non-assignability rule of Sec. 1(a), a discretionary carve-out concentrated in one official with no defined criteria or external check on grants.

note: Implements the CIA Retirement Act of 1964; no numbered EO predecessor to compare. Creates an ongoing benefit-administration relationship, so compliance-regime flags apply; the 'sole discretion' allotment power fires Flags 1, 4, and 11.

title: Transfer of Jurisdiction Over Certain Lands Containing Oil and Gas Deposits From the War Department to the Department of the Interior

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order transfers oil-and-gas jurisdiction over described parcels to Interior and directs the Secretary to take protective action; authority is a bounded, defined land-management assignment, not a pooling of broad discretionary power in one actor.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is clearly placed on the Secretary of the Interior to protect against drainage (para. 2), with the War Department retaining primary jurisdiction for flood-control/navigation (para. 3); the division of responsibility is explicit.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single subject (transferring oil-and-gas jurisdiction over described Sacramento River parcels to prevent drainage); nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time jurisdictional transfer of land creates no ongoing compliance rule or enforcement mechanism for any regulated party to be measured against.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A jurisdictional land transfer with royalties paid to Treasury miscellaneous receipts (para. 4) establishes no ongoing behavioral rule for any actor and creates no gaming incentive. No lapsed statute is invoked.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is a one-time, self-executing jurisdictional transfer with nothing ongoing to sunset; no continuing grant of authority requires periodic reauthorization.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The transfer expressly preserves the War Department's primary jurisdiction for flood-control and navigation (para. 3) and removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No regulated party or compliance regime exists in a land-jurisdiction transfer, so third-party enforcement standing does not apply.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A discrete transfer of oil-and-gas jurisdiction over specifically described parcels, subordinate to existing War Department purposes, establishes no authority claim or precedent extending into unrelated domains.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order reassigns oil-and-gas jurisdiction from the War Department to Interior over the same parcels while War retains primary flood-control/navigation jurisdiction (para. 3), creating overlapping dual jurisdiction on one tract with potential for coordination friction between the two agencies.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population and no exemption/waiver structure exist in a one-time land-jurisdiction transfer.

note: One-time land-jurisdiction transfer with no numbered EO predecessor to compare. Compliance-regime flags (4, 5, 8, 11) and Flag 6 NOT_APPLICABLE as no ongoing regulatory relationship or continuing authority grant exists; Flag 10 fires on the deliberately overlapping War/Interior jurisdiction over the same parcels.

title: Assigning Authority to Order Certain Persons in the Ready Reserve to Active Duty

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Sec. 1 delegates the President's involuntary call-up authority to the Secretary of Defense (and, at his designation, service secretaries) to order any qualifying Ready Reserve member to active duty, with no external check on the individual determination.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Authority is named and traceable to the Secretary of Defense exercising a specific statutory power; responsibility for call-up decisions is not diffuse.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single subject: exercising the DoD Appropriation Act call-up authority. No distinct remedies or problem frames are packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The criteria for who may be ordered to active duty are precise and objective (Sec. 1(1)-(3): not in a unit, unfulfilled obligation, under 24 months served); little room for divergent good-faith application.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: By targeting reservists 'not participating satisfactorily in a unit,' the order creates a call-up threat that pressures compliant unit participation, but also incentivizes minimal 24-month service-counting; a modest incentive tension with purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Sec. 1 expressly limits the delegated authority to actions 'until June 30, 1968,' an explicit built-in expiration.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is excluded, bypassed, or disabled; the order operates within the statutory scheme of the DoD Appropriation Act.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Determinations rest entirely within DoD; no external actor has standing or incentive to surface an erroneous or unfair call-up of an individual reservist.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The delegation implements a defined, time-limited statutory authority and establishes no precedent or definitional framework usable beyond ordering reservists to active duty.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Authority runs only within DoD to the Secretary and service secretaries; no other agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Sec. 3 requires 'appropriate consideration' of family responsibilities and essential employment without defined criteria or bounds, giving DoD discretionary, unreviewable latitude to spare or select individuals.

note: Cites no numbered EO predecessor; scored on own text. Ongoing individualized call-up relationship makes compliance-regime flags applicable; explicit June 30, 1968 sunset clears Flag 6.

title: Continuing the Regulation of Exports

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order continues broad export-control administration in the Secretary of Commerce and the President under TWEA section 5(b), a sweeping delegated power with no meaningful external check on its exercise.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of Commerce is the named administrator of the continued rules and penalties; responsibility for export-control administration is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The single frame 'continuing the regulation of exports' bundles three distinct purposes (national security vigilance, foreign-policy furtherance, and protecting the domestic economy from scarce-material drain/inflation), obscuring which justifies the continuation.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order carries forward an existing, detailed CFR regulatory scheme (15 CFR Parts 368-399) with specified penalty caps; the enforcement standard is the pre-existing, defined regulatory apparatus.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Sec. 1 continues export-control authority 'Notwithstanding the expiration of the Export Administration Act of 1969' by re-basing it on TWEA section 5(b), exercising a lapsed statute's framework indefinitely and removing pressure to let Congress reauthorize the EAA on its own terms.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The continuation of export controls under an emergency/wartime statute has a natural end-state (resolution of the invoked emergencies) but is granted to 'continue in full force and effect' with no expiration or reauthorization trigger.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: By substituting TWEA section 5(b) authority for the expired EAA, the order bypasses the statutory reauthorization and congressional-oversight process that a lapsed EAA would otherwise force, sustaining the regime outside its intended legislative check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The continued CFR scheme relies on the Commerce licensing apparatus with penalties for violations; the order preserves rather than removes existing enforcement structures over exporters.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Re-grounding an expired trade statute on TWEA section 5(b) establishes a reusable precedent for keeping lapsed regulatory frameworks alive under emergency authority, a device applicable well beyond export controls.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Sec. 4 expressly preserves other agencies' delegations under section 5(b); the order coordinates rather than countermands existing agency mandates.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order carves out no broad discretionary exemptions from coverage; the only exclusions are the specific medicine/medical-supply statutory exemptions carried forward, not a swallow-the-rule structure.

note: Textbook Zombie Emergency Trap: Sec. 1 continues authority 'notwithstanding the expiration of the Export Administration Act of 1969' by re-basing on TWEA 5(b). Predecessor EO 11533 (available) delegated EAA administration to Commerce; this order preserves that scheme past the parent statute's lapse.

title: American Defense Service Medal

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Award regulations are split among the Secretaries of War, Navy, and Treasury; no single official pools discretionary authority, and the subject (a service medal) concentrates no consequential power.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The three service secretaries are the named, identifiable authorities for prescribing award regulations; responsibility is clear.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose: establishing one medal and authorizing its award. Nothing distinct is packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A medal-establishment order creates no compliance rule or enforcement mechanism against any regulated party; there is no ongoing behavioral standard to apply.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order creates no ongoing regulatory relationship or behavioral incentive; a decoration for qualifying service imposes no rule any actor is meant to comply with.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Establishing an award is an evergreen administrative act with no natural end-state requiring reauthorization; the standing recognition of qualifying service does not call for a sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed, limited, or bypassed by creating a medal.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no compliance obligation for any third party to surface; the order simply authorizes award of a decoration under agency regulations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
