flags.justification: A narrow ceremonial award order carries no precedential or systemic downstream consequence identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Award-regulation authority is jointly assigned to War, Navy, and Treasury over their own personnel with no conflict or redirection of any agency's existing statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: There is no coverage population subject to a rule from which exemptions could be carved; the order confers eligibility for a medal rather than regulating conduct.

note: Narrow ceremonial medal-establishment order with no ongoing compliance relationship; compliance-regime flags (4, 5, 8, 11) are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE, while decision-structure flags remain answerable and clean. Cites no numbered EO predecessor.

title: Export of Banned or Significantly Restricted Substances

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authority is distributed across Commerce, State, EPA, FDA, CPSC, and USTR through an interagency task force and a State-Commerce concurrence process, with disagreements referred to the President; no single official holds unchecked control.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each agency is assigned specific, named duties (list publication, notification, licensing review), and licensing decisions rest with Commerce after defined consultations; responsibility is traceable.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order coherently addresses one problem frame (notification and controlled export of hazardous substances) through complementary mechanisms; no unrelated remedies are forced together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Export controls turn on undefined qualitative standards—'extremely hazardous,' 'substantial threat,' 'clear and significant harm to foreign policy interests' (1-301)—on which two officials could reach different good-faith export-control decisions.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The design's stated intent to 'resort to the imposition of export controls only in a very few instances' and rely on notification aligns actor incentives with disclosure rather than rewarding evasion or agency empire-building.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The order grants ongoing discretionary export-control and licensing authority derived from the EAA of 1979—a program with a natural reauthorization end-state—yet contains only reporting/evaluation duties (1-601), which the rubric notes are not equivalent to a sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order preserves EAA procedures 'in all their particulars' including industry consultation (1-307) and adds notification and evaluation requirements; it adds rather than removes checks.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement of the notification and control regime depends on agency self-assessment and internal review; recipient foreign governments may respond but no domestic third party has standing and information to surface non-notification or evasive exports.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order imports an expansive cross-statutory definition of 'banned or significantly restricted substance' (1-101, spanning FDCA, CPSA, TSCA, FIFRA, etc.) into export policy, creating a framework that legitimizes leveraging domestic regulatory findings to control foreign trade in adjacent domains.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order routes multiple agencies' existing statutory notification and regulatory roles through State and a Commerce-concurrence chokepoint (1-303, 1-306), creating parallel authority and referral friction over substances each agency independently regulates by statute.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Sec. 1-301 categorically exempts several substance classes (1-101(b)(3), (e)(4), (m)(1)(E)) and 'medicine or medical supplies' from export controls, and confines controls to 'exceptional cases,' a structure that leaves most covered substances outside the control default.

note: Detailed multi-agency export-notification framework under the EAA of 1979; cites no numbered EO predecessor as amended/revoked. Distributed authority clears Flags 1-2 despite the ongoing coverage relationship that makes compliance-regime flags applicable.

title: Establishing the President's Committee on Civil Rights

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authority rests in a 15-member advisory committee that only inquires and recommends (paras. 2, 5); it holds no eligibility, enforcement, or implementation power to concentrate.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Committee is a named body charged to report to the President; as a purely advisory panel with no operational duties, there is no failure chain with diffuse responsibility.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose: creating a committee to study civil-rights safeguards and recommend improvements; no distinct remedies or frames are packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order creates a study-and-report body with no enforcement mechanism or compliance standard applied to any regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: An advisory fact-finding committee imposes no ongoing behavioral rule creating incentives for any regulated actor class.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Para. 6 provides that 'upon rendition of its report to the President, the Committee shall cease to exist'—an explicit built-in termination trigger.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight is removed; the order directs agencies to cooperate with a new advisory body and disturbs no existing review process.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no compliance obligation or violation to surface; the Committee only studies and recommends, creating no regulated conduct for a third party to police.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: An advisory committee that only makes recommendations establishes no self-executing precedent or authority reusable in unrelated contexts; any downstream effect depends on later, separate action on its report.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Paras. 3-4 direct agencies to furnish information and testimony to the Committee but redirect no agency's statutory operating mandate; cooperation is coordinative, not countermanding.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: There is no regulatory coverage population from which exemptions could be carved; the order regulates no one's conduct.

note: Advisory study committee with an explicit self-termination clause (para. 6); compliance-regime flags (4, 5, 8, 11) are NOT_APPLICABLE because no ongoing behavioral rule is created. Cites no numbered EO predecessor as amended/revoked.

title: Foreign Intelligence Electronic Surveillance

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: 1-101 authorizes the Attorney General to approve warrantless electronic surveillance for foreign intelligence 'without a court order' upon his own certifications, pooling significant surveillance authority in a single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Attorney General and enumerated Senate-confirmed certifying officials (1-103) are specifically named and answerable for their statutory certifications; responsibility is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order coherently implements one subject—FISA authorization mechanics—with no distinct remedies or problem frames packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The standards and certifications are those defined by FISA sections 102 and 104, incorporated by reference; the order adds no independently vague enforcement trigger.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: By enabling AG-approved warrantless surveillance (1-101), the design gives the executive an incentive to route foreign-intelligence collection through the no-court-order channel rather than the judicial-order process of 1-102, favoring the less-checked path.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The delegation is a standing procedural framework implementing a permanent statute (FISA) and is maintained through ordinary amendment/supersession of prior EOs (it amends EO 12036 here), fitting the standing-procedural-framework carve-out.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sections 1-104 and 1-105 amend EO 12036 to require that any electronic surveillance be conducted in accordance with FISA 'as well as this Order'—adding a check rather than removing one; the warrantless channel derives from FISA itself, not from any oversight removal by this order.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: For the 1-101 warrantless track, no external party has standing or access to the classified certifications to surface improper surveillance; enforcement depends on executive self-compliance.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Formalizing an executive warrantless-surveillance authorization channel and a roster of designated certifying officials establishes durable institutional machinery and precedent for expanding foreign-intelligence collection authority beyond the immediate designations.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order allocates FISA certification roles among named officials and amends EO 12036's surveillance sections without countermanding any agency's separate statutory operating mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order carves out no exemptions from FISA coverage; 1-104/1-105 in fact extend FISA compliance to monitoring under EO 12036 rather than exempting anyone.

note: Partial amendment of EO 12036 (predecessor available): 1-104/1-105 add a FISA-compliance cross-reference to sections 2-202/2-203, which strengthens the check, so Flag 7 does not fire. Balance of order implements FISA's own delegation scheme; the warrantless channel is statutory, not created by this EO.

title: Authorizing the War Department, the Navy Department, and the United States Maritime Commission to Perform the Functions and Exercise the Powers Described in Title II of an Act Approved December 18, 1941

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Title I authorizes War, Navy, and the Maritime Commission to contract 'without regard to the provisions of law relating to the making, performance, amendment, or modification of contracts,' with broadly redelegable authority and no external check—an open-ended pooling of war-procurement contracting power.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The three agencies both exercise and self-report on the contracting authority (Title II para. 1), and may withhold restricted/confidential/secret contracts from public reports at their own discretion, leaving no independent actor to determine failure.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single frame—expediting war procurement through relaxed contracting authority; the provisions are all instrumental to that one purpose.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Title I para. 3 permits modification/settlement of claims and releases 'with or without consideration' 'whenever, in the judgment of' the department the war is thereby facilitated—an open, standardless discretion two officials could apply very differently.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Waiving advertising, competitive bidding, and bonds (Title I para. 4) while allowing large advance payments and no-consideration contract modifications creates incentives for cost inflation and favoritism, partly checked but not eliminated by the anti-profiteering warranty and cost-plus limits.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is exceptional war-procurement authority with a natural end-state (the war's conclusion), yet the order sets no expiration or reauthorization trigger, granting the sweeping contracting power open-endedly.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Title I displaces the pre-existing statutory contracting safeguards ('without regard to the provisions of law relating to' contracts) and dispenses with competitive bidding and bonding, removing established procurement checks.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Oversight rests on the departments' own quarterly reports to the President, with confidential/secret contracts exempt from public disclosure; no external party has standing and information to surface abuse.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Establishing that agencies may contract 'without any restriction of any kind' and outside ordinary contracting law sets a durable precedent for emergency procurement exceptionalism reusable in later crises.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Authority is conferred in parallel on War, Navy, and the Maritime Commission for their respective procurement, and they are directed to cooperate with OPM; no agency's statutory mandate is countermanded or redirected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order exempts this contracting from advertising, competitive-bidding, and bonding requirements and from ordinary contract law wholesale (Title I paras. 1, 4), with limited carve-backs (Walsh-Healey, profit limits) that leave the broad exemptions dominant.

note: Broad WWII procurement order granting contracting authority 'without regard to' ordinary contract law; cites the underlying Dec. 18, 1941 Act rather than a numbered EO predecessor as amended. Anti-discrimination, Walsh-Healey, and profit-limit clauses provide partial checks that keep several flags at PRESENT rather than CRITICAL.

title: Revocation of Executive Order No. 9165, Providing for the Protection of Essential Facilities from Sabotage and Other Destructive Acts

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A one-line revocation exercises and pools no ongoing authority; it dissolves a facility-security program rather than concentrating any decision power.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The action is self-executing and complete on signature; there is no implementation failure chain and thus no diffuse responsibility.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single action—revoking EO 9165 with a savings clause preserving War Department facility-security functions; nothing distinct is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A revocation creates no compliance rule or enforcement mechanism; there is no ongoing behavioral standard to apply to anyone.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Removing a prior program's coordinating framework establishes no new ongoing incentive structure for any regulated actor class.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A self-executing, one-time revocation grants no continuing authority, so there is nothing to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Per EO 9165, that program was itself a coordinating/security scheme, not an oversight check on other actors; its revocation removes no pre-existing external review mechanism, and the savings clause preserves cooperative facility-security arrangements.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: With no ongoing compliance obligation created, there is no violation for any third party to surface.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The revocation expressly preserves War Department facility-security functions and necessary cooperative arrangements, foreclosing meaningful gap or precedent effects beyond winding down the specific 1942 program.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Revoking the Office of Civilian Defense-centered coordination scheme and expressly preserving War Department functions and cooperative arrangements avoids redirecting or countermanding any agency's remaining statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: A revocation defines no coverage population from which exemptions could be carved.

note: Self-executing one-time revocation of EO 9165 (predecessor available and reviewed: a multi-agency facility-security coordination program, not an oversight check). Compliance-regime and sunset flags NOT_APPLICABLE; savings clause preserves War Department functions, clearing Flags 9-10.

title: Excusing From Duty on Wednesday, September 23, 1936, Until 1 p.m. Employees of the Executive Departments, Establishments and Other Agencies of the Federal Government in the District of Columbia, Who Desire to Show Honor to the Veterans of the Civil War Upon the Occasion of the Parade of the Grand Army of the Republic

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A one-time leave excusal exercises no consequential discretionary authority; the President directs a scheduling accommodation with no power pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The directive is self-executing for a single morning; there is no implementation failure chain to assign responsibility for.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single, trivial subject—excusing D.C. federal employees until 1 p.m. on one day; nothing distinct is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-day leave order creates no enforcement mechanism or compliance standard applied to any regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A single scheduling accommodation establishes no ongoing behavioral rule or incentive structure.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The order is inherently self-limiting to one morning and grants no continuing authority; there is nothing to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed or bypassed by a one-time leave excusal.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance obligation is created, so there is no violation for any third party to surface.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A trivial one-day administrative scheduling order carries no precedential or systemic downstream consequence.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The excusal applies uniformly to D.C. federal employees and redirects no agency's statutory mandate or resources.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order excuses employees 'except those who may for special public reasons be excluded,' an undefined, discretionary exclusion that supervisors could invoke without bounded criteria, though the stakes are trivial.

note: Trivial one-day leave-excusal order; compliance-regime and sunset flags NOT_APPLICABLE. Flag 11 remains applicable because the order defines a covered employee population and carves out an undefined 'special public reasons' exclusion, however low-stakes. Cites no numbered EO predecessor.

title: Defining Additional Functions and Duties of the War Production Board

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order vests in the WPB Chairman the OPM's and SPAB's powers plus Section 120 National Defense Act authority, exercisable through any official 'as he may determine,' and declares in para. 3 that 'his decisions shall be final'—sweeping authority pooled in one official with no external check.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: With the Chairman's decisions declared final and no review mechanism, if the war-production program fails there is no independent actor with obligation and capacity to correct him.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single frame—consolidating war-production functions in the WPB Chairman; the several transfers all serve that one purpose.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The transferred powers are exercised through whatever 'officials or agencies and in such manner as he may determine' (para. 3), with no defined standard governing how the consolidated procurement/production authority is applied.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order consolidates and clarifies fragmented war-production authority; nothing in its incentive structure rewards behavior undermining effective war procurement.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This grants exceptional wartime procurement/production authority with a natural end-state (the war), yet sets no expiration or reauthorization trigger for the Chairman's consolidated powers.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order reorganizes and consolidates operational authority but does not remove any pre-existing external oversight or review mechanism from the war-production function.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is an internal executive-reorganization order transferring functions among boards; it creates no external compliance obligation for a third party to surface.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Consolidating multiple agencies' powers in a single Chairman whose 'decisions shall be final' establishes precedent and institutional machinery for centralized, unreviewable wartime economic control usable beyond the immediate mobilization.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Para. 4 abolishes the Office of Production Management and transfers its personnel, records, property, and funds to the WPB, and para. 5 rescinds EO 8629 and amends EO 9024/8942 accordingly—redirecting existing agencies' functions and resources into the WPB Chairman (confirmed against predecessors EO 9024, 8942, 8629).

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: An internal reorganization defining a board's functions creates no external coverage population from which exemptions could be carved.

note: Amends EO 9024 (para. 1/2), absorbs OPM and abolishes EO 8629, per para. 4-5—all three predecessors (9024, 8942, 8629) available and reviewed. Internal reorganization order, so third-party and exemptions flags are NOT_APPLICABLE; the 'decisions shall be final' consolidation drives Flag 1 to CRITICAL and Flag 10 to PRESENT.

title: Rules Governing the Granting and Issuing of Passports in the United States

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Para. 124 concentrates unbounded discretion in the Secretary of State to 'refuse to issue,' 'withdraw or cancel' passports, and para. 126 lets him make further regulations, with no external check named.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility for issuance rests clearly and solely with the Secretary of State (para. 1); the failure chain points to a single named officer, so no diffuse-blame gap arises.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order addresses a single coherent subject—passport issuance rules—with no distinct unrelated remedies or problem frames packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Para. 124 authorizes refusal, restriction, and cancellation entirely 'in his discretion' with no stated standard, so two officials could reach opposite denial decisions in good faith; para. 125 withdraws 'protection' on undefined terms.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The evidentiary and fee structure rewards accurate documentation; no design feature rewards non-compliance or gaming by applicants or officials.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Standing procedural framework for permanent passport administration; para. 127 explicitly supersedes EO 5860 as amended by EO 6650, evidencing maintenance through ordinary supersession rather than freeze—the procedural evergreen carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order supersedes a predecessor procedural rule but removes no pre-existing judicial, congressional, or administrative oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Compliance rests on applicant self-attestation and supporting-witness affidavits (paras. 40-43); no external actor has standing or incentive to surface false statements beyond the issuing agency's own review.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The discretionary refusal/cancellation power in para. 124 establishes precedent for executive control over citizens' international travel, a precedent usable well beyond routine documentation.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Authority is exercised entirely within State; clerks of court act ministerially and no other agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The fee exemptions (paras. 91-99) are narrow, defined, and documented (officials on duty, seamen, grave-visitors); they do not swallow the coverage rule and are not discretionary carve-outs.

note: Long procedural order; supersedes EO 5860/6650 (both present in predecessors) so Flag 6 scores ABSENT under the standing-procedural-framework carve-out. Core weight comes from broad discretionary refusal/cancellation authority in paras. 124-126.

title: Excluding Land From Sitgreaves National Forest and Reserving it for Townsite Purposes

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Self-executing land-status change ('is hereby, excluded'/'reserved'); no ongoing decision authority is concentrated in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: A one-time metes-and-bounds reservation has no implementation to fail and thus no responsibility chain to break.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single action—exclusion and townsite reservation of one tract—with nothing unrelated bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order creates no ongoing behavioral rule or compliance relationship for anyone; a one-time land reservation has no enforcement mechanism to define.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory relationship exists, so there is no actor for whom incentives could be assessed.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing land-status change grants no ongoing authority, so there is nothing to sunset or reauthorize.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is excluded, limited, or bypassed by the reservation.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime exists over which any third party would need standing or incentive to surface violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Narrow 3.35-acre administrative land action with no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Acts on the recommendation of Agriculture within statutory forest-modification authority; no second agency's mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population and no exemption structure exist in a one-time land reservation.

note: One-time self-executing land exclusion/reservation; compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11) and 6,9,10 are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE per the decision-structure-vs-compliance-regime test, leaving Flags 1,2,3,7 cleanly ABSENT.

title: Suspension of Eight-hour Law as to Work by the Alaska Railroad, Department of the Interior

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Interior unilaterally determines for which work the suspension is 'essential,' with no external check on that determination, pooling discretionary reach in a single officer.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Interior is the single named actor responsible for applying the suspension; the failure chain points to an identifiable officer.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose—suspending the eight-hour limit to permit railroad rehabilitation in the short season—with no unrelated matter packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: 'Essential to the accomplishment of the purpose' is the only trigger for applying the suspension, an undefined standard on which two officials could differ in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Overtime is expressly paid at time-and-one-half beyond 40 hours, so the design does not reward the wrong behavior; no zombie trap—the eight-hour statute is active, not lapsed.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The suspension is expressly limited to 'a period of five months,' a built-in expiration that requires affirmative renewal—a clean sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order suspends a statutory hours limit under the statute's own emergency exception but removes no judicial, congressional, or administrative oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The wage/overtime protection is a concrete entitlement affected workers themselves have direct standing and incentive to enforce; no reliance on regulated-party self-reporting.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Invoking the statute's 'extraordinary emergency' exception on rehabilitation/economic-development grounds establishes a precedent for treating ordinary operational backlogs as emergencies to lift labor protections.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Authority is exercised within Interior over its own Alaska Railroad; no other agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order is itself a bounded exemption from the hours law tied to specified work; it contains no further discretionary carve-outs that swallow its own coverage.

note: Invokes an active 'extraordinary emergency' under the statute's own exception with a hard five-month sunset, so Flag 6 is ABSENT and the zombie trap does not fire (active, not lapsed, authority).

title: Creating an Emergency Board To Investigate a Dispute Between the Trans World Airlines, Inc., and Certain of its Employees

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authority is exercised under the statutory Railway Labor Act sec. 10 procedure; the President appoints a three-member fact-finding board that reports back, with no discretionary implementation authority pooled anywhere.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Board has a defined obligation (report findings within thirty days) and named responsibility; the statutory process makes responsibility clear.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose—investigate one carrier-employee dispute—with nothing unrelated bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Railway Labor Act sec. 10 supplies a precise mechanism: a 30-day investigation and a 30-day status-quo period, with clear triggers and consequences.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The board is barred from members 'pecuniarily or otherwise interested' in any carrier or employee organization, structurally removing the obvious perverse incentive.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The board self-terminates: it must report within thirty days, and the status-quo obligation ends thirty days after the report—an inherent expiration.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds a fact-finding board and a temporary status-quo requirement; it removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The neutral board and the reporting-to-President structure supply an external actor with standing and incentive to surface the dispute's facts; enforcement does not rest on party self-reporting.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A routine, statutorily prescribed emergency board for one labor dispute establishes no precedent or authority claim usable beyond its immediate context.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The National Mediation Board's statutory role is respected as the trigger; no agency's mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The status-quo obligation applies fully to the carrier and its employees with no carve-outs; nothing is exempted from coverage.

note: Standard statutory Railway Labor Act emergency board; tightly bounded by the Act's 30-day mechanism and a conflict-of-interest bar, scoring cleanly ABSENT across all eleven flags.

title: Generalized System of Preferences for Developing Countries

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order makes technical annex modifications to eligible-article lists under the Trade Act's structured process (Sec. 503, ITC advice, public comment); no discretionary implementation authority is newly pooled in one officer.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The GSP program's responsibility rests with the President and Treasury under the Trade Act's defined framework; this amendment adjusts item lists without obscuring who is responsible.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose—modifying GSP eligible-article designations and limitations—with no unrelated matter bundled in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Duty-free treatment turns on the precise 35%/50% value-content rules and item-number designations inherited from EO 11888's General Headnote 3(c), leaving little room for divergent good-faith application.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The value-content and country-of-origin definitions create standing incentives to engineer transshipment or minimal-processing arrangements to capture duty-free treatment—an inherited feature of the GSP eligibility architecture this order adjusts.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is a partial amendment of a standing tariff-schedule framework maintained through ordinary supersession/amendment (it further amends EO 11888's annexes); the procedural-framework carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order amends TSUS annexes and removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; the Trade Act's congressional-notification and ITC-advice checks remain intact.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Origin/value-content compliance rests on importer documentation administered by Treasury regulations, with no external actor structurally incentivized to surface misstated content claims.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A routine technical adjustment of eligible-article lists within an existing program; no new precedent or authority claim usable beyond the GSP context is created.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Treasury and the ITC operate in their existing statutory roles; the amendment redirects no agency's mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The A*/General Headnote 3(c)(iii) structure carries competitive-need and country-specific exclusions that this order adjusts; such definitional country/article carve-outs shape coverage but are rule-based rather than swallowing it, so PRESENT not CRITICAL.

note: Partial amendment of EO 11888's GSP annexes (predecessor present); scored diff-aware against the ongoing trade-preference compliance regime it modifies, which carries inherited origin-definition and exemption-architecture features.

title: Creating an Emergency Board to Investigate a Dispute Between Trans World Airlines, Inc., and Certain of its Employees

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Exercised under Railway Labor Act sec. 10; a three-member board is appointed to investigate and report, with no discretionary implementation authority pooled in any single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The board bears a defined duty to report findings within thirty days; responsibility is clear under the statutory process.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose—investigate one carrier-employee dispute—with nothing unrelated bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Act supplies precise 30-day investigation and 30-day status-quo mechanics with clear triggers and consequences.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Members must not be 'pecuniarily or otherwise interested' in any carrier or employee organization, structurally excluding the obvious perverse incentive.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The board self-terminates via the 30-day report requirement and the status-quo period ending thirty days after the report.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Adds a fact-finding board and temporary status-quo requirement; removes no pre-existing oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The neutral board reporting to the President supplies an external actor with standing and incentive to surface the facts; no reliance on party self-reporting.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Routine statutory emergency board for one dispute; establishes no transferable precedent or authority claim.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The National Mediation Board's statutory triggering role is respected; no agency mandate is redirected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The status-quo obligation applies fully to carrier and employees with no carve-outs from coverage.

note: Materially identical statutory Railway Labor Act emergency board (later dispute/union than order-164); tightly bounded by the Act, scoring cleanly ABSENT across all flags.

title: Termination of Emergency Authority for Export Controls

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order revokes EO 12470 and rescinds the economic-emergency declaration once the Export Administration Act was reauthorized (Pub. L. 99-64); it de-concentrates rather than pools authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Authority returns to its normal statutory home under the reauthorized Act and the standing EO 12002/12214 delegations; responsibility is clear.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose—terminate the emergency and revoke the predecessor order—with continuity savings clauses; nothing unrelated bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The savings clauses in Secs. 2-3 precisely preserve existing rules 'until amended or revoked by the proper authority,' leaving no ambiguity about what continues.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order removes the very zombie-emergency incentive its predecessor created by terminating authority once the statute was reauthorized on its own terms, so no perverse incentive persists.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This order is itself the termination event for the emergency authority; it grants no new ongoing authority that would require its own sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Restoring statutory rather than emergency authority removes no oversight; if anything it returns the program to ordinary statutory checks.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The order preserves the existing export-control enforcement apparatus (penalties, administrative sanctions) intact under the reauthorized Act rather than creating a new self-reporting-only regime.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Terminating an emergency upon statutory reauthorization models the correct wind-down and creates no adverse precedent; savings clauses merely prevent a coverage gap.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Commerce's administration under the Act continues in its statutory role; no agency mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order creates no exemption structure; its savings clauses uniformly preserve prior administrative actions until amended by proper authority.

note: Revokes EO 12470 (present in predecessors)—the order that had continued export controls 'notwithstanding the expiration' of the EAA; this order is the clean anti-zombie termination that rescinds the emergency once the Act was reauthorized, so the zombie trap does not fire and all flags are ABSENT.

title: Termination of the Air Coordinating Committee

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order abolishes a committee and assigns only ministerial wind-up tasks to the FAA Administrator; no ongoing decision authority is concentrated.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The FAA Administrator is the single named actor responsible for winding up outstanding affairs (para. 3); responsibility is clear.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose—terminate the committee and revoke its establishing orders—with nothing unrelated bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Termination of a committee creates no ongoing behavioral rule or compliance relationship whose enforcement could be defined.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory relationship exists after termination, so there is no actor for whom incentives could be assessed.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The order is itself a termination and grants no ongoing authority; the sole delegated task (wind-up) is inherently self-limiting, so no sunset is missing.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Dissolving a coordinating committee and revoking EO 10655/10796 removes an operational body, not any external oversight mechanism over the executive.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime remains over which a third party would need standing to surface violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Abolishing a defunct coordinating committee makes no new capability possible and sets no transferable precedent identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Removing an inter-agency coordinating body ends jurisdictional overlap rather than creating it; the FAA's own statutory role is unaffected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population or exemption structure exists in a committee-termination order.

note: Terminates the Air Coordinating Committee and revokes EO 10655/10796 (present in predecessors); compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11) are NOT_APPLICABLE as no ongoing relationship survives, while decision-structure flags 1,2,7 and 6,9,10 are cleanly ABSENT.

title: Delegation of Functions of the President Respecting the Transfer of Wheat to Pakistan

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Sec. 1 empowers the Director for Mutual Security to perform the President's statutory functions 'without the approval, ratification, or other action of the President,' pooling the operative authority in one official—though bounded by the State foreign-policy responsibility in Sec. 4 and the retained termination function in Sec. 2.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Director for Mutual Security is the single named delegate, subject to the Secretary of State's foreign-policy responsibilities (Sec. 4); responsibility is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose—delegating and structuring the specific wheat-transfer functions—with nothing unrelated bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time delegation to effectuate a specific statutory wheat transfer to Pakistan creates no ongoing behavioral rule with an enforcement standard to define.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A fixed, one-time price-support-wheat transfer establishes no ongoing regulatory relationship in which perverse incentives for a regulated class could arise.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The delegation is tied to a single statutory transfer that is inherently self-exhausting, and the Act retains a separate termination-of-assistance function (Sec. 2), so no sunset is missing.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 1 lets the Director act 'without the approval, ratification, or other action of the President,' expressly removing the presidential-review step that would otherwise attach to exercise of these functions.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The order allocates executive functions rather than regulating an outside party, so there is no compliance chain over which a third party would need standing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow delegation for one country-specific statutory transfer under the ordinary Title 3 mechanism creates no precedent or authority claim usable beyond this transaction.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The delegation is expressly subordinated to the Secretary of State's foreign-policy responsibilities (Sec. 4), coordinating rather than countermanding any agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order establishes no coverage population and thus no exemption structure.

note: One-time delegation of specific statutory wheat-transfer functions; compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11) are NOT_APPLICABLE, but the 'without approval/ratification of the President' clause fires both Flag 1 (concentration) and Flag 7 (removal of the presidential-review step).

title: Prescribing Regulations Governing the Exportation of Articles and Materials Designated in the President's Proclamation of February 25, 1941

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: By amending EO 8640 to add these materials, the order extends the Secretary of State's authority to issue general export licenses under presidential directives (EO 8640 para. 1), concentrating export-control discretion in that officer under wartime defense authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of State (through the Administrator of Export Control) is the identifiable actor for licensing; responsibility for the extended coverage is clearly located within the existing EO 8640 framework.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose—adding specified drugs and leather items to the export-control list—with no distinct unrelated matter packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Coverage is extended into EO 8640's general-license regime, under which the Secretary issues licenses per 'specific directives as may from time to time be communicated,' an open-ended standard on which enforcement application is undefined.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A defense-era export-restriction list does not reward non-compliance; exporters gain nothing structurally by evading licensing, and no zombie trap arises—the July 2, 1940 Act authority was active in 1941.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The order extends emergency/defense export-control authority tied to national-defense-strengthening statute, a subject with a natural end-state at the emergency's resolution, yet supplies no expiration or reauthorization mechanism—the permanence gap fires here (contrast the evergreen carve-out).

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds items to the licensing list within the existing framework and removes no pre-existing judicial, congressional, or administrative oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Compliance with export licensing depends on exporters seeking licenses; no external actor is given standing or incentive to surface unlicensed exports beyond the administering agency.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Incorporating additional commodity classes into the general-license export-control regime extends a broad executive precedent for restricting exports of any 'designated' material by proclamation, usable well beyond the specific drug/leather items listed.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Authority runs through State and the Administrator of Export Control per EO 8640; Commerce schedule numbers are used only for statistical classification, so no agency's mandate is redirected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Under the incorporated EO 8640 framework the Secretary of State 'may issue general licenses authorizing the exportation to all or certain areas' with paras. 5, 10, 11 of the base regulations made inapplicable, creating broad discretionary carve-outs from the restriction concentrated in one officer.

note: Full incorporation into EO 8640's export-control regime (predecessor present); scored by inheriting EO 8640's general-license architecture for the newly added items, which drives the discretionary-licensing and exemptions findings despite the amending order's brevity.

title: Declaring a Public Policy Against Discrimination on the Basis of Age

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: No single official gets discretionary authority; it directs 'the head of each department and agency' to enunciate the policy and amend procurement regulations, distributing implementation rather than pooling it.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The order declares a policy and directs regulation amendments but names no actor responsible for enforcing the non-discrimination requirement against contractors or correcting failures, so if the policy is ignored no one in particular is answerable.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent subject (age discrimination by federal contractors); no distinct remedies or problem frames bundled together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order creates a compliance rule for contractors but specifies no enforcement trigger, enforcing actor, or consequence for non-compliance; the 'bona fide occupational qualification, retirement plan, or statutory requirement' exceptions leave discretion for divergent good-faith application.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No design feature rewards non-compliance or gaming; the merit/anti-discrimination incentive aligns with the stated purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Anti-discrimination mandate is evergreen by nature (permanence carve-out, per EO 9981 anchor); ending age discrimination has no natural end-state making continued authority inappropriate.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes no pre-existing oversight; it only adds a continuous-notice statement to procurement regulations.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement depends on contractor self-adherence with no external actor (e.g., aggrieved worker, IG) given standing or a mechanism to surface violations; older applicants denied jobs have no procedure created here to raise it.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Precedent is confined to age-discrimination policy for federal contractors; no identifiable authority claim or definitional framework usable in unrelated domains.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Each agency amends its own procurement regulations within its existing mandate; no reassignment or countermanding of another agency's statutory authority.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Broad, self-declared exceptions ('bona fide occupational qualification, retirement plan, or statutory requirement') are controlled by the contractor with no defined criteria or external check, potentially allowing sophisticated actors to engineer 'BFOQ' justifications for age limits.

note: Contractor-facing non-discrimination policy creates an ongoing compliance relationship, so Flags 4/5/8/11 are applicable and scored on the merits rather than NOT_APPLICABLE; no numbered predecessor is amended (Kennedy's cited memo is not a numbered EO).

title: Authorizing the Secretary of the Interior to Take Possession of and Operate the Mines, Collieries, and Preparation Facilities of Certain Coal Companies

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Sole authority to seize, operate, and 'do all things necessary' for the named mines is pooled in the Secretary of the Interior, who 'deems necessary' the manner of operation and takes 'appropriate disciplinary action,' with no external judicial, legislative, or administrative check on the seizure or its conduct.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary determines when possession is warranted and when 'productive efficiency has been restored' to terminate it; the same actor exercising and self-judging the seizure means no independent party is answerable if the takeover fails.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single remedy (possession/operation) for a single stated problem (wartime interruption of coal production); no distinct policies bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Standards like 'such manner as he deems necessary' and 'appropriate disciplinary action... as may be necessary' give the Secretary open-ended discretion two officials could apply very differently, though bounded by 'not inconsistent with law' and existing employment terms.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The seizure is tied to restoring pre-interruption production and terminates on that condition; no design feature rewards prolonging the takeover or undermining production. Authority rests on an active, unexpired wartime statute (War Labor Disputes Act), not a lapsed one.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Sec. 5 sets an explicit termination trigger: possession ends within sixty days after the Secretary determines productive efficiency is restored, tying authority to resolution of the emergency.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; it directs other agencies to cooperate but disables no external check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Compliance with operating conditions rests with the Secretary and cooperating agencies; no external party (workers, company owners, courts) is given standing or a mechanism to surface mismanagement of the seized property.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The seizure establishes precedent for executive takeover of private industry under the war-powers/Selective Service framework, an authority claim usable to justify future property seizures beyond coal.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Named agencies (War Manpower Commission, Selective Service, War Department, Justice) are directed only to 'cooperate'; no existing statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded, and managements keep managerial functions.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Coverage is a fixed, named list of four companies with no waiver or carve-out mechanism that could swallow the rule.

note: Wartime property-seizure order; authority derives from an active, unexpired wartime statute, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire per v1.2.2. The explicit efficiency-restoration termination trigger clears Flag 6 despite the emergency subject matter.

title: Establishing the National Space Council

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Vice President as Chairman is 'the President's principal advisor on national space policy,' sets the agenda, and controls committee/working-group creation; authority pools in the Chairman, though the Council is advisory and Sec. 2(c) preserves existing agency lines of authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Council 'monitors and coordinates implementation' but its recommendations are advisory and it 'shall not interfere with existing lines of authority'; if national space policy fails, no actor here holds both obligation and enforcement capacity to correct it.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (coordinated national space policy process); the advisory board and Council are components of one framework, not distinct bundled policies.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Council is advisory and coordinative with no compliance rule imposed on regulated parties to enforce; agency conformity is directed but this is an internal EOP coordination body, not an enforcement instrument.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No design feature rewards actors for undermining the stated coordination purpose; membership and reporting align incentives toward information exchange.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The Council and its planning process are established permanently with an annual report but no termination or reauthorization trigger; a review/reporting requirement is expressly not a sunset. (Note: the private-citizen advisory Board falls under FACA, which carries its own charter renewals, but the Council itself has none.)

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds coordination machinery and expressly preserves existing lines of authority (Sec. 2(c)); it removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism. Sec. 6's amendment to EO 12660 merely reassigns the Microgravity Board's reporting body from the Economic Policy Council to the Council, adding no removal of oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: As an advisory/coordination body there is no regulated-party compliance regime requiring third-party surfacing of violations; the annual report to the President and the private-citizen advisory Board provide external visibility.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Placing space-policy coordination under the Vice President and directing all agencies to 'conform' to national space policy centralizes cross-sector (civil, national-security, commercial) direction in the EOP, a precedent that shapes agency space autonomy beyond the immediate coordination aim.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Council directs that each agency 'ensure that its space-related activities conform to national space policy' and monitors implementation across NASA, DoD, Commerce, DCI, etc.; per the diff-aware amendment, Sec. 6 also strips the Economic Policy Council of the Microgravity Board reporting relationship established in EO 12660 and reassigns it, a lateral reassignment of an existing coordination role.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No exemption or waiver structure carves any agency out of the coordination framework; coverage is uniform across space-related executive agencies.

note: Sec. 6 partially amends EO 12660 (available in referenced-predecessors) by swapping the Microgravity Board's reporting body from the Economic Policy Council to the new National Space Council; assessed as a partial amendment of one provision, contributing to Flag 10. Flags 4/8/11 scored ABSENT (not N/A) since an advisory coordination body still answers those questions cleanly.

title: Preservation and Display of Enemy Flags Captured by the Navy and the Coast Guard

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order directs the Secretary of the Navy to deposit captured flags for preservation at fixed locations (Naval Academy / Coast Guard Academy); it is a self-executing custodial directive concentrating no discretionary authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is clearly assigned to the named Academy Superintendents under the Secretary of the Navy; the custodial chain is explicit.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single custodial subject with a proviso allocating Coast Guard captures; no distinct policies bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time/standing custodial deposit directive creates no ongoing compliance rule for any regulated party to enforce; the flag's underlying concept does not map onto the instrument.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No behavioral incentive structure exists for any actor class; the order simply designates where captured flags are stored.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Standing procedural custodial framework maintained through ordinary supersession (it expressly supersedes the 1849 Polk order), evidencing revision by routine amendment rather than needing a sunset; no discretionary authority granted.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: It supersedes the prior Polk custodial directive but removes no external oversight mechanism (judicial review, IG, reporting); the superseded order was itself a custodial rule, not an oversight check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No regulated-party compliance regime exists, so there is no violation for a third party to surface; the flag does not apply to a custodial deposit directive.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Narrow custodial matter with no systemic, precedential, or definitional consequences reasoning forward from implementation.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Both the Navy and Coast Guard (when operating as part of the Navy) act within existing custodial roles; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population with an ongoing rule to be exempted from; the Coast Guard proviso is an allocation of custody, not an exemption from a compliance regime.

note: Custodial deposit directive with no ongoing compliance relationship, so compliance-regime Flags 4/5/8/11 are NOT_APPLICABLE while decision-structure Flags 1/2/7 are answerable and clean (ABSENT). It supersedes an 1849 Polk order (not a numbered EO), which supports the Flag 6 standing-procedural-framework carve-out.

title: Extension of Trust Periods on Indian Lands Expiring During the Calendar Year 1949

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order is self-executing: it extends by operation of law all 1949-expiring trust periods for twenty-five years, concentrating no discretionary authority in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: No implementation actor with decision authority is created whose failure could go unaddressed; the extension is automatic upon the order's terms.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single subject (blanket trust-period extension) with one carve-out for congressionally reserved cases; no distinct policies bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A self-executing status extension creates no ongoing compliance rule for any party to enforce; the flag's concept does not map onto this instrument.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No behavioral incentive is created for any actor class; the order automatically extends land-trust status.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The extension is itself time-bounded (a defined twenty-five-year period from each patent's expiration), so it carries an intrinsic end-state rather than lacking one.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: It removes no pre-existing oversight and expressly preserves cases 'in which Congress has specifically reserved to itself authority to extend the period of trust,' leaving Congress's role intact.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No regulated-party compliance regime exists, so there is no violation for a third party to surface; the flag does not apply to an automatic status extension.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A routine annual trust-period extension of a defined class of expiring patents; no identifiable authority claim or precedent usable in unrelated contexts beyond the recurring extension practice itself.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's statutory mandate is redirected; the congressional-reservation carve-out avoids conflict with Congress's retained authority.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The single carve-out (congressionally reserved cases) narrows coverage but there is no ongoing compliance rule to be exempted from; it is a jurisdictional boundary, not an exemption from a behavioral regime.

note: Self-executing, one-time trust-period extension for a fixed class of 1949-expiring patents; compliance-regime Flags 4/5/8/11 are NOT_APPLICABLE per the land-status/fixed-action guidance, and the decision-structure flags are clean. The extension is intrinsically time-bounded, clearing Flag 6.

title: Inspection by the Office of Price Administration of Corporation Statistical Transcript Cards Prepared from Income and Declared Value Excess-Profits Tax Returns

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order authorizes OPA inspection access to specified transcript cards under rules the Secretary of the Treasury already prescribed (Treasury Decision 5173); no discretionary authority is pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Access is bounded by defined records and the Treasury's existing rules; responsibility for governing inspection sits with the Secretary of the Treasury per the incorporated Treasury Decision.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single narrow subject (OPA inspection of one class of tax-transcript cards for one tax year); nothing bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The inspection is governed 'in accordance and upon compliance with the rules and regulations prescribed by the Secretary of the Treasury in Treasury Decision 5173,' incorporating a defined compliance standard rather than leaving it open.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Granting inter-agency inspection access creates no incentive for any actor to undermine the stated purpose; the incentive (price-administration oversight) aligns with the aim.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The grant is intrinsically bounded to transcript cards for taxable years ending after June 30, 1942 and before July 1, 1943, a fixed and closed data set rather than open-ended authority.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds an inspection access right subject to existing Treasury rules; it removes no pre-existing oversight or confidentiality safeguard beyond what the incorporated regulations govern.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Access is exercised by a government agency (OPA) subject to Treasury's prescribed rules and the underlying tax-return confidentiality framework; the compliance conditions are defined and monitorable under Treasury Decision 5173.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Extending confidential corporate tax-return-derived data to a non-tax agency (OPA) for price administration sets a precedent for cross-agency access to taxpayer information, a definitional pathway usable to import tax data into other regulatory domains.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Bureau of Internal Revenue prepares the cards and Treasury retains rulemaking control; OPA merely gains inspection access, with no redirection or countermanding of either agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Coverage is a fixed, narrowly-defined set of transcript cards for one tax year with no waiver or carve-out mechanism.

note: Narrow inter-agency data-inspection authorization incorporating an existing Treasury Decision by reference; compliance-regime flags are applicable (an ongoing inspection relationship governed by defined rules) and scored ABSENT on the merits. Only the cross-agency taxpayer-data precedent (Flag 9) fires.

title: Establishing the Operations Coordinating Board

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A single Board (chaired by the Under Secretary of State) is tasked with coordinating execution of all national-security policy across agencies and reports to the NSC; coordinating authority pools in one body, though its role is advisory and it acts only on the President's direction.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Board 'advises' and 'coordinates' execution but Sec. 6 disclaims any authority to abrogate functions vested by law in agencies; if coordinated policy execution fails, responsibility diffuses across the Board and the several agencies with no single accountable actor.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (integrated implementation of national-security policy); the abolition of the Psychological Strategy Board is a consequential wind-up, not an unrelated bundled policy.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: As a coordination/advisory body the Board imposes no compliance rule on regulated parties to enforce; its functions (advise, coordinate, report) are defined and internal to the executive branch.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No design feature rewards actors for undermining the coordination purpose; membership and reporting align incentives toward interdepartmental coordination.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The Board is established as a standing coordination body with reporting to the NSC but no termination date or reauthorization trigger; reporting is expressly not equivalent to a sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 6 preserves all functions vested by law in agencies and heads; the order abolishes the Psychological Strategy Board (an operational predecessor, not an external oversight check) and removes no judicial, legislative, or IG oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No regulated-party compliance regime exists requiring third-party surfacing of violations; the Board reports to the NSC and President, providing internal accountability appropriate to a coordination body.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Creating a standing interagency board to coordinate the 'timely and coordinated execution' of national-security policy and 'the particular climate of opinion the United States is seeking to achieve' institutionalizes covert/psychological-operations coordination machinery whose precedent extends beyond any single policy.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Board abolishes the Psychological Strategy Board and winds up its affairs, and inserts a cross-agency coordination layer above State, Defense, FOA, and CIA operational planning; Sec. 6's savings clause mitigates but does not eliminate the friction of a new coordinating authority over existing mandates.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No exemption or waiver structure carves any agency out of the coordination framework; Sec. 6 is a savings clause preserving statutory functions, not a coverage exemption.

note: Interagency national-security coordination board; advisory/coordination nature makes compliance-regime Flags 4/8/11 answerable and clean (ABSENT), while Flag 10 fires on abolition of the Psychological Strategy Board plus the new coordination layer over existing agency mandates. No numbered EO predecessor is amended.

title: Partial Revocation of Executive Order No. 5214 of October 30, 1929, Withdrawing Public Lands for Naval Purposes

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A self-executing partial revocation of a land withdrawal for a metes-and-bounds-described 18-acre tract; no discretionary authority is concentrated in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The action is automatic upon issuance; the only continuing duty (providing ingress/egress) is clearly assigned to the Secretary of the Navy, so no accountability chain is left open.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single subject (revoking the withdrawal as to one described tract); nothing bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time land-status revocation creates no ongoing compliance rule for any party to enforce; the flag's concept does not map onto this instrument.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No behavioral incentive is created for any actor class by revoking a land withdrawal.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing revocation grants no ongoing authority, so there is nothing to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The revocation removes no external oversight mechanism; it restores land status and directs the Secretary of the Navy to provide access under his regulations.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No regulated-party compliance regime exists, so there is no violation for a third party to surface.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow 18-acre land-status change with no systemic or precedential implications reasoning forward from implementation.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the Navy is implicated (retaining access regulation authority over the tract); no second agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population with an ongoing rule to be exempted from; the ingress/egress provision is an access allocation, not an exemption from a compliance regime.

note: Self-executing partial land-withdrawal revocation; compliance-regime Flags 4/5/8/11 and Flag 6 are NOT_APPLICABLE per the one-time land-status-action guidance, and the decision-structure flags are clean. It revokes EO 5214 in part, but 5214's text was not needed given the revocation is a fixed metes-and-bounds tract carve-out.

title: Maintaining Unofficial Relations with the People on Taiwan

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Broad functions under the Taiwan Relations Act are delegated to the Secretary of State (with certain functions reserved to the President), and Sec. 1-203(b) lets the Secretary determine 'in each case' whether contract modifications 'protect the foreign policy interests of the United States'; discretion pools in the Secretary, though ultimate functions are reserved to the President.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Contract amendments 'with or without consideration' turn on the Secretary of State's case-by-case foreign-policy determinations with no defined review; if such determinations produce bad outcomes, responsibility rests with the same discretionary actor who makes them.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Coherent single purpose (implementing the Taiwan Relations Act through unofficial relations); delegations and law-specifications are components of one statutory framework, not distinct bundled policies.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order allocates delegated functions and specifies enumerated statutory provisions by citation (Sec. 1-201, 1-202); the standards are the underlying statutes' own, not left undefined by this order.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No design feature rewards actors for undermining the stated purpose of maintaining unofficial Taiwan relations; the American Institute in Taiwan mechanism aligns with the Act's aims. Authority derives from the active Taiwan Relations Act, not a lapsed statute.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is a standing delegation implementing a permanent statute (the Taiwan Relations Act), revised through ordinary supersession (it supersedes the December 1978 memorandum); the underlying diplomatic-relations function is evergreen, so the procedural-framework carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 1-202 specifies that procurement of services 'may be effected without regard to' numerous statutory provisions (competitive-bidding, advertising, fee-limitation, and Contract Disputes Act sections), affirmatively displacing pre-existing procurement oversight and dispute mechanisms for these contracts.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: With standard procurement competition, advertising, and Contract Disputes Act review set aside (Sec. 1-202), external actors (competing contractors, GAO-style challengers) lose the access and standing they would ordinarily have to surface irregularities in AIT contracting, leaving compliance largely to the Secretary's own determinations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Creating a template for conducting foreign relations and contracting through an 'unofficial instrumentality' (AIT) free of numerous procurement statutes establishes precedent for off-the-books quasi-diplomatic arrangements usable in other non-recognition contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Functions are delegated to State (and a narrow set to OPM), each within its competence and 'in consultation with the Secretary of State'; no agency's existing statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded against its will.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Sec. 1-202 carves AIT contracting out of an extensive list of procurement statutes, and Sec. 1-203 allows uncompensated contract modifications on the Secretary's discretion, creating a broad exemption from ordinary contracting rules controlled by a single actor with limited external check.

note: Delegation order implementing the Taiwan Relations Act; it supersedes a 1978 presidential memorandum (not a numbered EO) and cross-references EO 10789 incidentally rather than amending it, so no predecessor comparison was required. Flag 7/8/11 fire on the Sec. 1-202 procurement-statute waivers.

title: Statistical Policy Functions

flags.name: Power Concentration
