flags.justification: The order transfers statistical-policy functions from the Secretary of Commerce to the Director of OMB and requires redelegation to the OIRA Administrator, pooling statistical-policy authority in OMB/OIRA; Sec. 6 gives the Director broad power to 'take all steps necessary or appropriate' to effectuate transfers, though this operates within existing statutory delegations.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility for the transferred functions is clearly located in the Director of OMB and the OIRA Administrator, and Commerce retains a defined residual reporting function (Sec. 4(b)); the accountability chain is explicit, not diffuse.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (reassigning statistical-policy functions to OMB/OIRA under the Paperwork Reduction Act); the several amended predecessor orders are all instruments of that one reassignment, not distinct bundled policies.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order makes mechanical agency-name substitutions and revocations across named predecessor orders and ties functions to Section 3(a) of the Paperwork Reduction Act; no open-ended compliance standard is left for divergent good-faith application.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Reassigning statistical-policy functions creates no incentive for any actor to undermine the stated purpose; the transfer aligns statistical-policy coordination with OMB/OIRA's paperwork-reduction role.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Standing administrative reassignment of a permanent statistical-policy function, maintained through ordinary supersession (it amends and revokes numbered predecessors, evidencing routine revision); the procedural-framework carve-out applies. Notably Sec. 1 replaces a fixed 1977 termination phrase with an ongoing Paperwork-Reduction-Act linkage.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Per the diff-aware convention against EO 12013 (available), this order largely reverses 12013's Commerce-to-OMB direction back toward OMB/OIRA; it revokes EO 12013 and prior sections but the reassignments transfer operational functions rather than removing any external oversight mechanism (judicial review, IG, congressional reporting).

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is an internal reallocation of statistical-policy functions among executive officials, not a regulated-party compliance regime requiring third-party surfacing of violations; accountability runs through OMB's ordinary budget and reporting channels.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Consolidating statistical-policy authority in OMB/OIRA under the Paperwork Reduction Act strengthens centralized OMB control over federal statistics and information-collection, a precedent extending OIRA's reach over agency data practices beyond this specific transfer.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order redirects functions, records, property, personnel, and appropriations from the Secretary of Commerce to the Director of OMB (Sec. 5) and revokes/amends Commerce's role across EO 11541, 10253, 10033, 12013, and 11961, laterally reassigning an existing agency's operational mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No exemption or waiver structure is created; the order reassigns functions uniformly without carving any actor out of a compliance regime.

note: Diff-aware: this order partially amends EO 11541, 10253, 10033, and 11961 and revokes EO 12013 (available in referenced-predecessors), reversing 12013's earlier Commerce-ward transfer back to OMB/OIRA; assessed as partial amendments plus an internal function transfer, driving Flag 10. Compliance-regime flags scored ABSENT since this is an internal reallocation, not a public compliance rule.

title: Revocation of Executive Order No. 6014 of February 6, 1933, Withdrawing Public Lands in Oregon

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A self-executing revocation of a prior land withdrawal effective upon plat filing; no discretionary authority is pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The action is automatic and complete on filing; there is no implementation to fail and thus no responsibility chain to trace.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single provision revoking one prior order; nothing distinct is bundled and no dual problem-frame is present.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time land-status revocation creates no ongoing compliance rule for any actor to enforce.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral regime exists, so no incentive is created for any actor class.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The order is a one-time, self-executing action; it grants no continuing authority that could be subject to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Restoring lands to public availability removes no pre-existing external oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement chain exists; the revocation is self-executing with no violations to surface.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow revocation restoring one township to settlement; no precedent or systemic downstream effect is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only Interior's resurvey/plat process is implied; no second agency's mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order creates no coverage regime from which exemptions could be carved.

note: Predecessor EO-6014 (Hoover land withdrawal) was available and reviewed; this is a clean diff-aware revocation of a one-time withdrawal, so compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11) and Flags 6/10 are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE.

title: Possession and Operation of the Transportation System, Plants, and Facilities of the Scranton Transit Company Located in and About the City of Scranton, Pennsylvania

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Director of ODT is authorized to seize, control, and operate the system and 'do anything that he may deem necessary,' with no external check; this discretionary seizure power is the order's core and is unbounded.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Director both operates the property and unilaterally determines when possession is 'no longer necessary' (Sec. 4), so the actor judging success is the actor exercising the power.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent remedy (seizure to sustain war-effort transit); no distinct policy questions or dual frames are packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Authority to operate 'in such manner as he may deem necessary for the successful prosecution of the war' supplies no defined standard; two officials could reach very different operating decisions in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Because the Director alone decides when seizure is 'no longer necessary,' the operating agency controls the duration of its own expanded authority, an incentive to prolong possession.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Emergency seizure authority is exactly the kind that should track a resolution condition, but termination rests solely on the Director's discretionary determination, not any automatic lapse tied to the war's end.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 3 bars attachment/garnishment absent the Director's consent, but the order preserves existing terms of employment and ODT orders (Sec. 2); it does not remove a pre-existing external oversight check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Direct government possession and operation, not a self-reporting compliance scheme; no third-party enforcement mechanism is needed or absent.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Transfers the Secretary of War's transportation-seizure powers (1916 Act) to the ODT Director, establishing a precedent for pooling wartime seizure authority in an emergency-transport office beyond the immediate dispute.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Sec. 5 expressly transfers to the Director functions vested by statute in the Secretary of War, redirecting another office's statutory authority into the ODT.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No exemption or waiver structure carves anyone out of coverage; the seizure applies wholesale to the named property.

note: Wartime seizure order under active War Powers/War Labor Disputes authority; Flag 5 zombie trap does not fire because the statutes invoked were active, not lapsed. Sec. 5's statutory transfer from the Secretary of War drives the Flag 10 finding.

title: President's Foreign Intelligence Advisory Board

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Board serves 'at the pleasure of the President,' reports directly to him, and all appointments (members, chair, staff director) rest with the President alone, concentrating control of the oversight body in the office it partly checks.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Board only advises and recommends; no named actor is obligated to act on its findings, so failure of intelligence performance triggers no correction responsibility.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose (an advisory board to assess intelligence quality); no distinct remedies or problem-frames are bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Board has no enforcement role at all; it assesses and recommends, so there is no enforcement standard to be vague about.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: As an unpaid advisory body reporting to the President, no incentive to game outcomes or undermine the stated purpose is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The Board is established with no termination date or reauthorization requirement; it continues until a future President revokes it, and no member-committee sunset (unlike EO 9981's advisory committee) is provided.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds an advisory review layer over the intelligence community; it removes no pre-existing external check, and new machinery does not fire Flag 7.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The Board itself is a standing external reviewer with access to agency information (Sec. 5); it functions as the third-party check rather than lacking one.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A President-facing advisory board with no operative authority over agencies produces no identifiable precedent or downstream effect beyond its advisory remit.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Board reviews and advises but does not redirect or countermand any agency's statutory operational mandate (Sec. 2-4).

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No exemption or waiver structure exists; the Board's review authority extends to all intelligence agencies.

note: Advisory-only board; power/accountability findings flow from its serve-at-pleasure, advise-and-recommend design, but its lack of enforcement authority keeps most compliance-regime flags clean. Flag 6 fires for lack of any sunset on a standing advisory structure.

title: Authorizing the Secretary of the Navy to Take Possession of and Operate the Plant of the Howarth Pivoted Bearings Company at Philadelphia, Pennsylvania

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Navy is directed to seize and operate the plant, may 'exercise any existing contractual or other rights of the said company,' and 'take such other steps as may be necessary or desirable,' with no external check on this unbounded seizure authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Termination rests on the President's own determination that the plant can be privately operated 'consistent with the war effort,' with no independent actor accountable for how the seizure is administered.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single remedy (seizure of one named plant to secure war-materials production); nothing distinct is packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Directing operation to 'produce effectively essential war materials' and take 'all things necessary or incidental' supplies no defined operating standard; officials could implement very differently in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Termination is tied to the plant being operable privately in a manner consistent with the war effort, an external condition, not an incentive for the operating agency to prolong control.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Emergency seizure authority should track a resolution condition, but ends only when the President discretionarily decides private operation is feasible, with no automatic lapse.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes no pre-existing external oversight mechanism; it directs a discrete seizure.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Direct government possession and operation, not a self-reporting compliance regime; no absent third-party enforcer.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Authorizing a cabinet secretary to seize and operate a private plant and exercise its contractual rights establishes precedent for executive takeover of private production usable beyond this single facility.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Authority is vested directly in the Secretary of the Navy; no other agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No exemption or waiver structure; the seizure applies wholesale to the named plant.

note: Wartime plant seizure; similar architecture to order-182 but narrower (single plant, authority vested directly in the Navy Secretary rather than transferred from another office), so Flags 5 and 10 stay clean.

title: Performance of Commercial Activities

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: OMB's Director controls implementing guidance, cost-comparison methodology, savings-estimate exceptions, and the tracking system (Sec. 2), pooling program direction and the power to grant exceptions in one office.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Agencies self-report progress quarterly to the President through OMB (Sec. 1(h)), and OMB, which sets the methodology, also monitors compliance, so the actor judging success administers the program.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent frame (A-76 cost-comparison privatization of commercial activities); provisions all serve that stated purpose.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Key triggers turn on undefined standards ('private industry costs are unreasonable,' activities 'performed more economically'), and 'to the extent permitted by law' qualifies every agency duty, so agencies could comply very differently in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Sec. 1(e) lets agencies retain negotiated first-year savings as incentive compensation, rewarding contracting-out volume rather than genuine efficiency and incentivizing studies that favor conversion.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The order mandates recurring annual studies of at least 3 percent of civilian staff 'thereafter' with no termination or reauthorization point; it is permanent by default over a program that is not evergreen by nature.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds OMB tracking and public-review publication requirements (Sec. 2(b)); it removes no pre-existing oversight, and Sec. 5 preserves existing procurement procedures.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement runs on agency self-reporting and OMB self-tracking; Sec. 5 bars any private right of action, so no external actor has standing and incentive to surface non-compliant or gamed cost comparisons.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A standing government-wide presumption that commercial activities go to private industry, with retained-savings incentives, entrenches contracting-out as the default across all agencies well beyond any single study.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: OMB and OPM act within their existing management/personnel coordination roles; no agency's statutory operational mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Coverage is qualified 'to the extent permitted by law,' exempts where 'statute or national security requires government performance or where private industry costs are unreasonable,' and OMB may authorize exceptions (Sec. 1(e)), giving discretionary carve-outs to the same office running the program.

note: Standing government-wide privatization program with an ongoing compliance/study regime, so all compliance-regime flags apply; OMB concentration plus self-reporting plus discretionary exemptions drive the score. No CRITICAL: the failure modes degrade but do not defeat the stated efficiency purpose.

title: Chemical and Biological Weapons Proliferation

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of State unilaterally determines which persons and countries are sanctioned, whether sanctions apply, are terminated, or are waived for 'foreign policy or national security reasons' (Secs. 4-6), concentrating the entire trigger-and-relief architecture in one office with no external check.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The same Secretaries who impose sanctions also determine when to lift or not apply them (Sec. 6, 5(c)), and Sec. 8 bars any enforceable right, so no external actor can hold implementation to account.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent frame (nonproliferation of chemical/biological weapons via export controls and sanctions); the eight sanction types all serve that one stated purpose.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Sanctions turn on determinations that a person 'knowingly and materially contributed' or a country made 'substantial preparations,' undefined standards on which two Secretaries could reach different good-faith conclusions.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The sanctions target foreign proliferators; no design feature rewards regulated parties or implementing officials for undermining the nonproliferation purpose. The IEEPA emergency invoked is a fresh, active declaration, not a lapsed statute.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The order declares a national emergency and sanctions 'remain in force' until the Secretary discretionarily decides to lift them (Sec. 6); an emergency grant of this kind should track a resolution condition but has no automatic lapse or reauthorization mechanism.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 8 provides the order creates no right 'enforceable at law,' insulating these determinations from judicial review that would otherwise attend such executive action.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Identification of sanctionable persons/countries depends entirely on the Secretary of State's own determinations; no external actor has standing and access to surface a proliferator the Secretary declines to designate.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Building a permanent IEEPA-based sanctions and export-control apparatus keyed to executive determination establishes precedent and standing machinery for broad discretionary trade sanctions reachable in adjacent foreign-policy contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: State, Commerce, and Treasury act through their own statutory authorities (Arms Export Control Act, export jurisdiction, import controls) in a consultative structure (Sec. 7); no agency's mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Sec. 4(5) and 5(c) let the Secretaries exempt procurement contracts, decline any individual country sanction for 'significant foreign policy or national security reasons,' and grandfather existing contracts, giving broad discretionary carve-outs controlled by the same office imposing sanctions.

note: Cited EO 12730 (available, reviewed) is invoked only as a Commerce delegation source, not amended; order-186's own authority is a fresh, active IEEPA national-emergency declaration, so the zombie trap does not fire despite 12730's own expiration language. Sanction-imposition-and-relief power concentrated in State drives the CRITICAL on Flag 1.

title: Tribal Colleges and Universities

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authority is distributed among the Secretary of Education, a 15-member advisory Board, participating agencies, and OMB; no single official holds unchecked discretionary control over the program's substance.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The scheme relies on each agency's own Five-Year Plan and self-scored Annual Performance Report (Sec. 5), with no named actor obligated or empowered to correct an agency that fails its self-set objectives.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The six stated purposes all coherently serve federal support of tribal colleges; this is a unified frame, not distinct problems bundled to force acceptance.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Duties are hortatory ('shall strive to include,' 'to the extent permitted by law and regulation') with agency-set 'annual goals' rather than binding standards, so compliance is undefined and could vary widely across agencies.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Agencies self-set and self-report goals, but the incentive runs toward the stated purpose (funding tribal colleges); no design feature rewards undermining it.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Sec. 7 terminates the Board 2 years after the order 'unless renewed by the President,' an explicit affirmative-reauthorization sunset that scores this flag as a positive finding.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds reporting, an advisory Board, and integrated planning; it removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Progress depends on agencies' own performance reports reviewed by the White House Initiative Office; no external actor with standing and independent access is positioned to surface an agency's inflated or unmet goals.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A coordinating advisory-and-planning structure for existing tribal-college funding programs; no authority claim or definitional change with identifiable precedential reach beyond its stated remit.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Agencies collaborate through liaisons and plans consistent with their own statutory authority (Sec. 3-4); no agency's mandate or appropriated funds are redirected against statute.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No exemption or waiver structure; all participating agencies as determined by the Secretary are covered by the planning and reporting obligations.

note: Cleanly designed coordination order with an explicit 2-year Board sunset (Sec. 7), distributed authority, and no oversight removal; the residual PRESENT findings (Flags 2, 4, 8) all trace to the hortatory, agency-self-report enforcement model.

title: Exemption of Edward M. Weeks From Compulsory Retirement for Age

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A one-time statutory exemption of a single named employee for a fixed six-month period; no ongoing discretionary authority is pooled anywhere.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The action is self-executing and complete on issuance; there is no implementation that could fail and no responsibility chain to trace.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single provision exempting one individual; nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A fixed, one-time personnel exemption creates no ongoing compliance rule for any actor to enforce.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral regime is created, so no incentive is established for any actor class.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The exemption self-expires on a stated date (February 29, 1940), so it carries its own built-in termination and scores this absence flag as a positive finding.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Granting one time-limited exemption removes no pre-existing external oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement chain exists; the exemption is self-executing with no violations to surface.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A single named individual's six-month retirement deferral, expressly authorized by statute; no precedent or systemic downstream effect is identifiable.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the Treasury's Bureau of Engraving and Printing is implicated; no second agency's mandate is affected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order is itself a single statutory exemption of one person, not a coverage regime with a carve-out structure to assess.

note: Cited predecessors EO 7413 (available), 7635 and 7945 (not in referenced-predecessors) are prior identical exemptions of the same person and do not alter scoring; this is a fixed, self-expiring one-time action, so compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11) and Flag 10 are NOT_APPLICABLE and Flag 6 is a clean ABSENT (built-in expiry).

title: Modifying Executive Order of June 8, 1926, Creating Reservoir Site Reserve No. 17, Pacific Slope Basins, California

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A land-status modification enabling the Federal Power Commission to license transmission-line projects on described lands under fixed conditions; no discretionary authority is concentrated in a single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The modification is self-executing with a defined condition (licensees remove works within 30 days of Interior's notice); responsibility for removal is clearly assigned.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single land-modification action for one reserve; nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The one behavioral condition is precise: licensees must remove project works at their own expense within thirty days of notice from the Secretary of the Interior when lands are needed for reservoir purposes.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The removal-at-own-expense condition aligns licensee incentives with the reserve's protection; no feature rewards undermining the reservoir purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A one-time modification of land-reserve status; it grants no continuing discretionary authority that could be subject to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes no pre-existing oversight; it conditionally enables FPC licensing that was previously foreclosed by the reserve status.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Interior triggers removal by notice when lands are needed, providing a defined actor with both standing and reason to enforce the condition.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow, condition-bounded accommodation of transmission lines on specified reserve lands; no precedent or systemic downstream effect is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The FPC's licensing authority and Interior's reserve-management role coexist without conflict; the order coordinates them rather than redirecting either mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order modifies coverage of a land reserve under a stated condition, not through a discretionary waiver structure that could swallow the rule.

note: Predecessor is the unnumbered Executive Order of June 8, 1926 (not a numbered EO in referenced-predecessors); the modification is condition-bounded and creates an ongoing but precisely-defined removal obligation for FPC licensees, so compliance-regime flags apply and score cleanly ABSENT, while Flag 6 is NOT_APPLICABLE (one-time land action).

title: Revoking Executive Order No. 8458 of June 27, 1940, as Amended by Executive Order No. 8532 of September 4, 1940, and Authorizing Persons Appointed from the Emergency Replacement List Established Pursuant to Such Orders to Acquire a Classified Civil Service Status

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order revokes the Emergency Replacement List effective a fixed date and lets a defined class of already-appointed persons acquire classified status under existing statute and EO 8743; no discretionary authority is pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The transition follows fixed, self-executing criteria (dates of appointment, six months' continuous service); eligibility is rule-determined, not left to unaccountable discretion.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The revocation and the status-conferral for those already appointed under the revoked list are a single coherent wind-down, not distinct policies forced together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Eligibility criteria are precise (appointment after June 30, 1941, entry on duty by December 31, 1942, six months' continuous service in 1941-1942), leaving little room for divergent good-faith application.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The one-time status conferral on a closed, dated class of appointees creates no ongoing incentive to game the rule; the list itself is being terminated.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The order is itself a termination (revoking the list effective December 31, 1941) with fixed closing dates on the conferral window; it winds authority down rather than granting open-ended authority.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Revoking an emergency appointment list and conferring status under existing civil-service law removes no external oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Status acquisition runs through the Civil Service Commission under EO 8743 and the 1940 Act, an existing external gatekeeper, so no third-party enforcement gap is created for this closed class.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A closed, dated transition of a defined group into classified status as an emergency list is retired; no precedent or systemic downstream effect beyond the affected appointees is identifiable.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the Civil Service Commission's own machinery is implicated, consistent with its statutory mandate; no other agency's authority is redirected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The one carve-out (census-force employees appointed via examination) is narrow and specific, not a broad discretionary waiver capable of swallowing the rule.

note: Predecessors EO 8458, 8532, and 8743 were available and reviewed; diff-aware reading confirms this is a clean rule-bound wind-down of the Emergency Replacement List plus a fixed-class status conferral, with an ongoing but precisely-bounded eligibility determination that scores every applicable flag ABSENT.

title: Amending the Selective Service Regulations Concerning the Ordering of Registrants for Induction

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authority rests in local boards operating under an existing distributed statutory framework (Military Selective Service Act); the amendment adds a procedural rule, not new concentrated discretion in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility remains with the local board and Selective Service chain that already administers induction; the amendment does not diffuse who acts.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single-provision amendment addressing one issue (induction of registrants who refuse a physical exam); nothing distinct is packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The trigger 'has refused or otherwise failed to comply' is broadly worded, and two boards could differ on whether a non-appearance was a refusal or an excusable failure, producing inconsistent application against the baseline in EO 10001 §631.7.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The amendment closes an evasion loophole by removing the incentive to skip the physical exam to delay induction; it aligns incentives with the statute's purpose rather than undermining it.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural amendment to a permanent regulatory framework maintained through ordinary supersession (a long chain of amending EOs is cited), fitting the standing-procedural-framework evergreen carve-out.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed; the registrant's acceptability is still determined before induction, only the sequence changes.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement runs through the compulsory induction machinery, not self-reporting; the board initiates action against the registrant, so no third-party gap arises.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow procedural change to induction sequencing with no discernible precedential reach into unrelated domains.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the Selective Service System is implicated; no second agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The amendment expands rather than narrows coverage, removing a de facto escape route; no carve-out or waiver structure is created.

note: Diff-aware partial amendment to EO 10001 §631.7 (predecessor text available); it closes an evasion loophole rather than weakening the mechanism. Cited older SSS EOs (10202, 10659, etc.) not in referenced-predecessors, but the operative baseline (10001) was available.

title: Establishing the War Relocation Authority in the Executive Office of the President and Defining Its Functions and Duties

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A single Director 'appointed by and responsible to the President' is authorized to effectuate removal, relocation, maintenance and supervision of designated persons with broad power to make delegations, employ personnel, and prescribe regulations, with no external judicial, legislative, or administrative check.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Director need only 'keep the President informed'; a Liaison Committee merely 'assists' with no enforcement role, so if the program harms those relocated there is no named actor obligated and able to correct it.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order coherently addresses a single subject (standing up the relocation authority) under one frame; distinct remedies are not force-packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Director may 'prescribe the terms and conditions' of employment and 'safeguard the public interest,' 'provide for their needs in such manner as may be appropriate,' and 'supervise their activities' — open-ended standards under which officials could act very differently in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Compulsory enlistment of removed persons in a 'War Relocation Work Corps' at Director-set compensation gives the Authority an institutional interest in sustaining the relocated population as a labor pool rather than resolving their status.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: An emergency/wartime exceptional-powers grant is exactly the category that should end with the emergency, yet only the Work Corps is limited 'for the duration of the present war'; the Authority itself has no sunset (cf. EO 9066 calibration anchor scoring PRESENT).

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 11 expressly preserves the authority of prior orders/proclamations and the FBI's functions; no existing external check is removed by this order.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The affected persons are subjects of the program, not parties who can surface abuses, and no external actor is given standing or access to police the Director's treatment of them; oversight depends entirely on the Authority's own conduct.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order operationalizes mass removal and detention of a civilian population by class, establishing durable institutional machinery and precedent for exercising sweeping control over designated persons far beyond any single military necessity.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Director may 'secure the cooperation, assistance, or services of any governmental agency' and directs departments (War, Justice, Treasury, Agriculture, Labor) to provide services, redirecting agency resources and creating overlap with the Secretary of War's parallel EO 9066 authority requiring a deconfliction clause (Sec. 10).

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Coverage is defined by military designations under EO 9066; this order creates no waiver or carve-out structure that would let covered persons escape the program.

note: Full incorporation of the EO 9066 framework (predecessor available) for the relocation/maintenance phase; scored on the sweeping single-Director architecture it builds atop an active wartime exclusion power. CRITICAL on Flags 1 and 9 reflects core design collapse of any accountable limit on control over a class of persons.

title: Liquidation of War Agencies

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A single presidentially-appointed Director directs liquidation planning and may establish uniform practices, but authority is meaningfully tempered by a mandatory Liquidation Advisory Committee of seven agencies he must consult, so pooling is partial.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Director's functions are advisory/coordinating ('further,' 'suggest,' 'advise and assist') and Sec. 3 disclaims divesting any agency of its own functions, leaving no single actor clearly responsible if liquidation goes poorly.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (orderly winding-up of war agencies); no distinct remedies or problem frames are packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Standards like 'proper preparations,' 'orderly and timely winding up,' and 'when deemed appropriate' are undefined, so two officials could reasonably reach different liquidation actions.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The office's purpose is to accelerate liquidation and it has no budget or authority that expands through delay; incentives track the stated goal.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The task is inherently temporary (winding up war agencies) yet the order sets no termination date or reauthorization trigger for the Director's office — a natural end-state exists but no mechanism tracks it.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 3 expressly preserves each named agency's own functions (including GAO's); no pre-existing external check is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The Advisory Committee (including GAO and the Bureau of the Budget) receives periodic status reports, providing external actors with access and incentive to surface liquidation problems.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A time-limited internal coordination function for dismantling temporary agencies; no precedent or authority claim with reach beyond the stated purpose.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Director overlays a coordinating role across Treasury, Surplus Property Administration, Civil Service Commission, GAO and others and may 'establish uniform liquidation practices,' creating potential friction with those agencies' own liquidation roles despite the Sec. 3 non-divestiture savings clause.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Sec. 3 carves agencies whose affairs transfer to Treasury out of the Director's reach, but this is a jurisdictional boundary, not a discretionary waiver structure that guts coverage.

note: No numbered predecessor amended; scored on own text. Coordination-heavy but advisory design with a mandatory multi-agency committee keeps most flags low; Flag 6 fires because the temporary subject matter lacks a termination trigger.

title: President's Commission on Strategic Forces

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Commission is a purely advisory body ('provide appropriate advice'); it holds no eligibility, enforcement, or implementation authority, so no decision power is concentrated.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is clearly located: the President appoints and designates the Chairman, the Secretary of Defense provides administration and performs the President's FACA functions, and the Commission reports by a fixed date.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose (review the strategic modernization program and advise); no distinct remedies or frames are packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: An advisory commission that only reports to the President enforces nothing and creates no ongoing compliance relationship, so vague-enforcement analysis does not apply.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No regulated party, beneficiary, or ongoing behavioral rule exists; a one-time advisory report creates no incentive structure to assess.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Sec. 4(b) provides the Commission terminates 30 days after its report and Sec. 2(b) sets a report deadline — an explicit, well-designed sunset, a positive finding.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 3(a) provides information supplied to or developed by the Commission 'shall not, to the extent permitted by law, be available for public inspection,' shielding its record from ordinary public-disclosure oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no enforcement chain or violation for any third party to surface; the body only advises.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A short-lived advisory commission with a fixed report; no precedent or authority claim with reach beyond its charge is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Secretary of Defense supplies support and other agency heads provide information 'to the extent permitted by law'; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order imposes no coverage regime on any population, so there is no rule for exemptions to swallow.

note: Advisory FACA commission with explicit termination; the four compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11) are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE because no ongoing regulatory relationship exists. Flag 7 fires narrowly on the non-public-inspection clause.

title: Providing for Stabilization of Prices, Rents, Wages, and Salaries

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Sec. 3(a) delegates to the Cost of Living Council 'all of the powers conferred on the President by the Economic Stabilization Act of 1970,' and Sec. 4 lets the Council define terms, grant exemptions, issue binding regulations, and redelegate to any official — sweeping economy-wide authority pooled in one Council with no external check.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Council both administers the freeze and controls its own exemptions, records requirements, and enforcement referrals, so it effectively judges its own performance with no independent actor obligated to correct failures.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: A single instrument bundles distinct controls over prices, rents, wages, and salaries plus a new standing Council and a mandate to design a post-freeze regime, so the sound and questionable elements cannot be accepted or rejected separately.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Ceilings are pegged to 'the highest of those pertaining to a substantial volume of actual transactions' in a base period and the Council may 'take such other actions as it determines to be necessary,' terms open enough that officials could reach different enforcement outcomes in good faith, despite the fixed $5,000 penalty.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Pegging ceilings to each party's own highest base-period price rewards those who had inflated prices during the 30-day base window and penalizes restraint, incentivizing gaming of the reference period.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The 90-day freeze is time-limited, but the Cost of Living Council and its broad delegated stabilization powers have no termination or reauthorization trigger and are directed to design ongoing post-freeze policy — the standing authority is permanent by default.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing external oversight mechanism is removed; enforcement runs through the courts via Justice Department injunction actions under Sec. 205 of the Act.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement depends on the Council detecting violations from records that regulated parties self-maintain; while sellers must keep base-price records 'available for public inspection,' no external actor is given standing or incentive to pursue violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Standing up an economy-wide wage-price control apparatus with delegated presidential power and a mandate to develop a durable successor regime establishes machinery and precedent for peacetime economic control extending well beyond the 90-day freeze.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Sec. 4(b)-(d) direct all executive departments to furnish information and assistance and let the Council redelegate to and utilize any agency, redirecting agency resources into stabilization work outside their own statutory missions.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Secs. 4(a)(ii) and 5 give the Council unbounded discretion to 'make exceptions or grant exemptions' and to exempt parties 'from any requirement otherwise imposed,' with no defined criteria — a concentrated, open-ended waiver power over its own coverage.

note: No numbered EO predecessor; scored on own text. Broad delegation of 'all' statutory powers to one Council plus unbounded exemption discretion drives CRITICAL on Flag 1 and a wide spread of PRESENT findings.

title: Science and Technology Policy Functions

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order re-pools numerous statutory functions transferred to the President and redelegates them to the Directors of OSTP, OMB, and NSF, concentrating science-policy authority in a few EOP officials, though bounded by the underlying Act and Reorganization Plan.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each delegated function is assigned to a named Director with specified coordination duties, so responsibility for each function is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (effectuating the transfer/redelegation of science-and-technology functions under one Reorganization Plan); no unrelated remedies packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A reorganization/delegation order allocating functions among EOP officials creates no ongoing compliance rule for any regulated party to enforce.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No regulated party or beneficiary behavior is governed; the order only reassigns internal functions, so there is no incentive structure to assess.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A permanent administrative reallocation of standing statutory functions implementing a Reorganization Plan; the underlying functions are evergreen and revised through ordinary supersession, so absence of a sunset is not a design gap.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing external oversight is removed; the order re-establishes the Federal Coordinating Council and an advisory panel under FACA rather than disabling checks.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement regime or violation exists for a third party to surface; the order is an internal transfer of functions.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A bounded implementation of an already-enacted Reorganization Plan; no precedent or authority claim extending beyond the stated science-policy reorganization is identifiable.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order splits related functions between OSTP and OMB (e.g., R&D-transfer policy and reorganization/Federal-State liaison to OMB while related science functions go to OSTP), creating parallel authority and coordination friction between the two offices over overlapping subject matter.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order imposes no coverage regime on any population, so there is no rule from which exemptions could be granted.

note: Implements Reorganization Plan No. 1 of 1977 (a statutory plan, not a numbered EO predecessor, so no diff-comparison needed). Internal delegation order: compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11) NOT_APPLICABLE; Flag 10 fires on the OSTP/OMB function split.

title: Authorizing the Secretary of War to Take Possession of and Operate the Plants and Facilities of the Springfield Plywood Corporation, Located At Springfield, Oregon

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of War is authorized to seize and operate the plant, exercise the corporation's contractual rights, employ persons, and 'take any other steps that he deems necessary,' concentrating broad operational discretion in one official, though under a specific statutory seizure authority (War Labor Disputes Act).

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is squarely on a single named official (the Secretary of War) for the seizure and operation, so accountability is clearly located rather than diffuse.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose (seize and operate one named plant to end a war-effort production interruption); nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: 'To the extent that he may deem necessary,' 'in any manner that he deems necessary,' and 'any other steps that he deems necessary' set no objective standard bounding the seizure's scope or conduct, leaving operation to unreviewable executive judgment.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Sec. 5 ties termination to restoration of pre-interruption productive efficiency and Sec. 3 preserves existing management, so the design incentivizes prompt normalization rather than prolonged control.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Sec. 5 provides possession 'shall be terminated' within 60 days after the Secretary determines pre-interruption efficiency is restored — an explicit end-condition tied to the resolving event, a positive finding.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing external check is removed; Sec. 2 keeps existing terms of employment (subject to WLDA Sec. 5) and the seizure operates within that statutory framework.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: A one-time government takeover of a single named plant creates no ongoing compliance regime over regulated parties for a third party to police.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Authorizing seizure and operation of private facilities to override a labor dispute establishes and reinforces a precedent for government requisition of private industry usable in future disputes, an effect beyond ending this single interruption.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Other federal agencies (War Manpower Commission, Selective Service, Justice) are only directed to 'cooperate'; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order applies to a single facility and creates no coverage rule with exemptions to grant.

note: No numbered EO predecessor; scored on own text against the War Labor Disputes Act framework. A defined 60-day termination trigger and clear single-official accountability keep the score moderate despite broad 'deem necessary' discretion. Flags 8 and 11 NOT_APPLICABLE (single one-time seizure, no ongoing regulatory relationship).

title: Waiver Under the Trade Act of 1974 With Respect to Tajikistan and Turkmenistan

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The President exercises a specific waiver authority expressly conferred by section 402(c)(2) of the Trade Act; it is a self-executing statutory determination, not the pooling of open-ended discretion in an unchecked official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The action and responsibility rest with the President under a named statutory provision, and the statute conditions the waiver on a report to Congress, which the order recites was made.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: A single action (waiving sections 402(a)/(b) for two named countries) under one statutory frame; nothing distinct is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time trade waiver imposes no ongoing behavioral rule on any regulated party to enforce.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The waiver removes a trade restriction for two countries; it creates no compliance regime and thus no incentive structure for regulated actors to game.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The Trade Act's section 402(c)(2) waiver framework carries statutory renewal/review conditions, and the waiver is a discrete determination rather than a standing grant of discretionary authority left permanent by default.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight is removed; the statute's congressional-report requirement is expressly satisfied, preserving the built-in check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no enforcement chain or violation for a third party to surface; the order is a discrete waiver determination.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A routine Jackson-Vanik-style waiver applying an established statutory mechanism to two named countries; no precedent or authority claim beyond the stated action is identifiable.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency is implicated; the order is a direct presidential statutory determination.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order is itself a waiver of a statutory restriction; it establishes no coverage regime with a further exemption structure to assess.

note: One-time statutory waiver under the Trade Act of 1974 (statute, not a numbered EO predecessor). Compliance-regime and lateral flags (4,5,8,10,11) NOT_APPLICABLE because no ongoing regulatory relationship or second agency exists; decision-structure flags score ABSENT, yielding a clean 0.00.

title: Creating an Emergency Board to Investigate Disputes Between the Carriers Represented by the National Railway Labor Conference and Certain of Their Employees

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A three-member investigatory board appointed by the President only reports findings; it holds no eligibility, enforcement, or implementation authority, so no decision power is concentrated.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The board's obligation is specific and bounded (report within thirty days), and the statutory status-quo obligation runs on defined actors (carriers and employees), so responsibility is clear.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose (investigate the named rail disputes under the Railway Labor Act); nothing distinct is bundled together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The status-quo requirement is precise — 'no change, except by agreement' for thirty days after the report — with a defined trigger and duration, leaving little room for divergent enforcement.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The cooling-off/status-quo design channels parties toward mediated agreement and imposes a conflict-of-interest bar on board members ('no member shall be pecuniarily or otherwise interested'); incentives track the stated purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The board's authority is inherently time-limited: it reports within thirty days and the freeze runs thirty days past the report, a built-in expiration tied to the resolving event.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds an investigatory board within the Railway Labor Act's existing procedure; it removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The dispute is bilateral and public (carriers vs. named unions) under the National Mediation Board's process, so violations of the status-quo obligation are readily surfaced by the opposing party.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A routine emergency-board creation under section 10 of the Railway Labor Act; no precedent or authority claim beyond the stated dispute is identifiable.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The National Mediation Board's role is respected (its judgment triggers the order) and the new board operates within, not against, that statutory scheme; no agency mandate is redirected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The status-quo obligation applies uniformly to the listed carriers and employees with the only exception being mutual agreement; no discretionary carve-out structure exists.

note: No numbered EO predecessor; scored on own text against the Railway Labor Act §10 framework. A tightly time-bounded, statutorily-channeled investigatory board with a conflict-of-interest bar scores cleanly ABSENT across all eleven applicable flags.

title: Delegating Certain Functions of the President Under the Defense Production Act of 1950

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Sweeping presidential Defense Production Act powers (priorities, allocations, requisitioning, price/wage stabilization, subpoena) are delegated to several officials, and a new Economic Stabilization Administrator may 'establish price ceilings and stabilize wages' — significant pooling, but distributed across multiple Senate-confirmed officials with coordination checks rather than one hand.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each function is delegated to a specifically named officer or agency (e.g., Interior for fuels, Commerce for other materials, the ESA Administrator for stabilization), with the NSRB Chairman coordinating, so responsibility for each function is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: A single order packages nine distinct programs — priorities/allocations, requisitioning, capacity expansion and loan guarantees, economic stabilization, real-estate credit, labor supply, voluntary agreements — that could each stand alone, foreclosing targeted acceptance or rejection.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Delegates exercise powers 'in such manner as will in his judgment promote adequate supplies' and may create agencies and issue orders with broad discretion, so different officials could reach materially different enforcement decisions in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The claimant structure of Sec. 103 lets each delegate press competing claims for scarce materials before the others, creating an incentive to over-claim on behalf of one's own sector rather than optimize aggregate supply.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The delegated emergency defense-production and stabilization authorities are the kind that should track the emergency's resolution, yet the order sets no termination or reauthorization trigger and even creates a 'new and independent' standing Economic Stabilization Agency that persists until revoked.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing external check is removed; the order layers in oversight (Federal Reserve Board supervision of fiscal agents, Attorney General/FTC consultation before voluntary agreements, Civil Service classification review) rather than disabling checks.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Implementation runs largely through agency determinations and industry voluntary agreements and self-reported requirements; apart from the Attorney General/FTC antitrust consultation, no external actor is given standing and incentive to surface non-compliance across the sprawling program.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Standing up an economy-wide mobilization apparatus — including a new independent Economic Stabilization Agency, subpoena power, and industry loan-guarantee machinery — establishes durable institutions and precedent for peacetime economic control extending well beyond the immediate defense-production need.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order redirects functions and resources across Interior, Agriculture, Commerce, ICC, GSA, the Federal Reserve, RFC, HHFA and others, creates overlapping claimant and guarantee roles, and requires the NSRB Chairman to 'resolve interagency issues,' evidencing built-in lateral mandate friction.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order allocates and delegates powers but does not establish a discretionary waiver/exemption structure that would carve regulated parties out of coverage; exemption authority for the substantive controls is left to the underlying Act and later rulemaking.

note: No numbered EO predecessor amended; scored on own text against the Defense Production Act of 1950. Broad multi-program mobilization delegation; Flag 1 stays PRESENT (not CRITICAL) because authority is distributed across many named, Senate-confirmed officials with substantial coordination and consultation checks rather than pooled in one office.

title: Creating an Emergency Board to Investigate a Dispute Between the United Air Lines, Inc., and Certain of its Employees

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authority is a three-member fact-finding board appointed by the President under Sec. 10 of the Railway Labor Act; the board only investigates and reports, holding no decisional or enforcement power, so no authority is pooled in one unaccountable hand.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is clear: the board must report findings to the President within thirty days, and the statutory framework (RLA) governs the process; the named board and President carry the obligations.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single, discrete purpose—create a board for one named dispute; nothing distinct is packaged with it.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The statutory status-quo freeze (no change except by agreement for thirty days after the report) is a precise, self-executing consequence defined by Sec. 10 of the RLA.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The cooling-off structure incentivizes maintaining conditions and negotiating; no actor is rewarded for undermining the purpose. The board is barred from members with pecuniary interest.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The order is inherently time-limited: the board reports within thirty days and the freeze lasts thirty days past the report, so authority naturally lapses.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed; the order operates within the RLA emergency-board process and adds a status-quo freeze rather than stripping any check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The dispute is bilateral (carrier and union) with the National Mediation Board and President involved; interested parties have direct standing and incentive to surface non-compliance with the freeze.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A routine, statutorily prescribed emergency-board creation for a single labor dispute establishes no precedent or authority claim usable in unrelated contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The National Mediation Board's judgment triggers the order and no agency's statutory mandate is countermanded or redirected; the ad hoc board does not conflict with existing agency authority.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No carve-outs or waivers; the status-quo freeze applies to both parties without exemption.

note: Standard RLA Sec. 10 emergency-board order; time-limited fact-finding with a defined statutory freeze, clean across all flags.

title: Possession, Control, and Operation of the Transportation System, Plants, and Facilities of the Great Lakes Towing Company, Cleveland, Ohio

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Director of the Office of Defense Transportation is authorized to take possession, operate, and terminate the seizure in such manner 'as he may deem necessary,' with transferred war-power functions and no external check on his operational discretion.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Director both operates the facilities and unilaterally determines when possession is 'no longer necessary' (para. 4), so the same actor judges its own success with no independent correction mechanism.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose—seize and operate one named company's facilities for the war effort; no distinct policies packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The operating standard is 'such manner as he may deem necessary' with terms/conditions of employment maintained but no defined trigger, metric, or bounded standard for the Director's actions—two officials could act very differently in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The Director terminates when possession is no longer necessary; the wartime-seizure design does not reward undermining the purpose, and this invokes an active wartime emergency, not a lapsed statute.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is emergency/wartime seizure authority whose subject matter has a natural end-state (the emergency), yet termination depends solely on the Director's own determination with no fixed sunset or reauthorization trigger.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Para. 3 bars any attachment, garnishment, or execution against seized property absent the Director's prior written consent, disabling ordinary judicial/creditor process over the property.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Not a compliance regime over regulated parties reliant on self-reporting; the government operates the facility directly, so surfacing violations is not the operative structure.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Transferring the Secretary of War's Act of 1916 seizure functions to the ODT Director and shielding seized assets from legal process establishes precedent for executive commandeering of private transportation infrastructure beyond this single company.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Para. 5 expressly transfers functions vested by statute in the Secretary of War to the ODT Director, redirecting an existing statutory mandate to a different official.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No exemption/waiver structure carving actors out of coverage; the order applies to one named company's assets in full.

note: Wartime seizure with broad discretionary operating authority, self-judged termination, anti-attachment shielding, and a statutory-function transfer from the Secretary of War; invokes an active wartime emergency so the zombie trap does not fire.

title: Possession and Operation of Coal Mines

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Interior is authorized to take possession of any and all struck/threatened coal mines and operate them 'in such manner as he deems necessary,' concentrating broad discretionary seizure authority in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary both operates the mines and determines when possession is 'no longer required,' judging his own performance; however a sixty-day-after-restoration outer limit and WLB contract-approval role partly constrain him.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose—seize and operate coal mines to sustain wartime production; no distinct policies bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Operation 'in such manner as he deems necessary' and possession 'so far as may be necessary or desirable' set no bounded standard; two officials could implement very differently in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The design preserves collective bargaining and requires WLB contract approval, aligning incentives with resuming production; it invokes an active wartime statute, not a lapsed one.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A concrete termination mechanism exists: possession ends when no longer required for the war program and in no event more than sixty days after productive efficiency is restored, giving a defined end-state.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight is removed; the order preserves grievance procedures, collective bargaining rights, WLB approval, and expressly leaves EO 9370 intact.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Government directly operates the mines rather than relying on regulated-party self-reporting; the enforcement-chain concern does not map onto this seizure structure.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order establishes a broad precedent for federal commandeering of an entire private industry ('any and all mines' in which a strike is even threatened) to override labor stoppages, usable beyond this instance.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Secretary of War assists on request and WLB retains its role; the order expressly does not repeal or amend EO 9370, so no agency's statutory mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No waiver/carve-out structure; coverage extends to all mines with a strike or threatened stoppage without exemptions.

note: Wartime coal-mine seizure; broader discretion fires Flags 1/2/4/9, but a defined 60-day termination cap, preserved bargaining/WLB oversight, and explicit non-repeal of EO 9370 keep Flags 6/7/10 clean. Active wartime statute, so zombie trap absent.

title: Environmental Effects Abroad of Major Federal Actions

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Each agency's own officials determine, largely unreviewably, whether an action has significant effects, which document (if any) to prepare, and whether an additional exemption applies (Sec. 2-3, 2-4, 2-5(c)); discretion pools in each acting agency with no external check.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The order creates internal procedures only and Sec. 3-1 bars any cause of action, so if an agency fails to consider effects there is no external actor with obligation and capacity to correct it.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Coherent single frame—procedures for considering extraterritorial environmental effects; no unrelated remedies packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Agencies choose among document types 'as determined by the agency,' and 'significantly affects' turns on the agency's own judgment (Sec. 3-4), so two agencies could reach very different compliance decisions in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Because an agency self-determines significance and can invoke its own additional exemptions, the rational actor can define an action out of coverage to avoid the review burden, undermining the stated purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The order grants ongoing procedural/discretionary authority with no expiration, review, or reauthorization mechanism; it continues until revoked.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 1-1 declares this the government's 'exclusive and complete determination' of NEPA procedures abroad and Sec. 3-1 forecloses any cause of action, cutting off judicial review that might otherwise attach to environmental review obligations.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: With no cause of action and reliance on agency self-assessment, no external party has standing and incentive to surface an agency's failure to prepare the required review.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Declaring an 'exclusive and complete' executive determination of NEPA's procedural reach abroad and barring litigation establishes precedent for insulating extraterritorial executive action from environmental-review challenge generally.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: State and CEQ get coordination/consultation roles and a lead-agency rule resolves multi-agency actions (Sec. 3-3); no existing agency's statutory mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Sec. 2-5 exempts a broad sweep (actions by/under direction of the President or Cabinet officers on national-interest grounds, intelligence, arms transfers, most export/nuclear activities, international-organization votes, disaster relief) AND lets agencies create further categorical exclusions and exemptions 'as may be necessary'—discretionary, self-controlled carve-outs broad enough to swallow the coverage, defeating the stated purpose.

note: Self-implementing agency-discretion review scheme; the exemptions structure (Sec. 2-5, including agency power to add its own exemptions) is broad enough to swallow the rule (CRITICAL), compounded by the no-cause-of-action clause removing external oversight.

title: Amending Executive Order No. 11030 of June 19, 1962, with Respect to the Preparation of Presidential Proclamations

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The amendment only adds a boilerplate closing recitation for proclamations and adjusts copy-routing/seal rules within the existing EO 11030 framework; no decision authority is concentrated in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Purely clerical drafting/publication conventions with responsibilities resting on the Office of the Federal Register as before; nothing to fail in a way that lacks a responsible actor.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Multiple sections all concern the same narrow subject—form and filing of proclamations; they are variations on one drafting-convention purpose, not distinct bundled policies.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The amended provisions are precise formatting/routing rules (fixed recitation text, number of copies to forward); no ambiguous enforcement standard exists.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A drafting-convention amendment creates no behavioral incentive for any actor to undermine its purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural framework maintained through ordinary supersession/amendment (it amends EO 11030, which itself superseded EO 10006); per the procedural-framework carve-out, absence of a sunset is appropriate.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed; the diff against EO 11030 only adds/adjusts formatting and routing steps and narrows applicability of EO 3577 to certain proclamations.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance regime over regulated parties; the rules govern internal document preparation, so third-party enforcement standing is not implicated (clean, not inapplicable).

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A formatting/filing amendment has no systemic or precedential downstream consequences beyond document style.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Roles of the Federal Register office (and the earlier EO 10347 seal item) are adjusted within their existing functions; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Sec. 4 carves treaty/international-agreement proclamations out of EO 3577's application, but this is a narrow, defined subject-matter distinction, not a discretionary exemption swallowing any rule.

note: Partial amendment of EO 11030 (its text was available); purely clerical proclamation-drafting/filing conventions, clean across all flags. Flag 6 ABSENT under the standing-procedural-framework carve-out (maintained via supersession).
