title: Amendment of Executive Order No. 8389 of April 10, 1940, as Amended

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: By extending all provisions of EO 8389 to Rumania, this order inherits that framework's concentration of licensing and definitional authority in the Secretary of the Treasury, who alone authorizes exceptions to sweeping asset-freeze prohibitions under Sec. 5(b).

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Inherited from EO 8389: the Treasury Secretary administers the freeze, defines coverage, and grants licenses with no independent actor obligated to correct wrongful blocking or over-broad application.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose—extend the existing freeze regime to Rumanian-interest property; nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Inherited: EO 8389 hinges coverage on property in which the target country/national had 'any interest of any nature whatsoever, direct or indirect' and 'reasonable cause to believe' domicile tests—standards broad and indeterminate enough to yield divergent good-faith enforcement.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The blocking regime's incentives run toward disclosure and license-seeking; no design feature rewards undermining the freeze. This invokes active Sec. 5(b) emergency authority, not a lapsed statute.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is emergency economic-warfare authority (Sec. 5(b), Trading with the Enemy Act) whose subject matter has a natural end tied to the crisis, yet neither this order nor the inherited EO 8389 provides any expiration or reauthorization trigger.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds a country to the freeze list; it does not remove any pre-existing oversight mechanism over foreign-exchange regulation.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Inherited: enforcement depends on required reports under oath from holders of blocked property (EO 8389 Sec. 10) and on banking-institution compliance; no external actor with independent standing and information exists to surface concealment of covered interests.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The one-sentence extension mechanism demonstrates and entrenches a template for adding whole countries to a sweeping asset-freeze regime by executive order alone, a precedent readily reusable across nations.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Authority rests with Treasury throughout; no other agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded by extending coverage to Rumania.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Inherited: all prohibitions apply 'except as specifically authorized in regulations or licenses issued by the Secretary of the Treasury,' vesting unbounded, single-actor discretion to license actors out of coverage.

note: Full incorporation: this brief order extends all provisions of EO 8389 (text available) to Rumania, so it inherits that freeze regime's architecture—Treasury-concentrated licensing discretion, broad interest/domicile coverage tests, self-reporting enforcement, and unbounded licensing exemptions.

title: Prescribing Regulations Governing the Enforcement of the Neutrality of the United States

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order distributes neutrality-enforcement duties across War, Navy, Treasury, Commerce, Justice, the Panama Canal Governor, and all departments; authority is dispersed by function rather than pooled in one hand.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each department is assigned a specific, named enforcement responsibility, so if neutrality enforcement fails within a domain the responsible department is identifiable.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent frame—allocating enforcement of the existing neutrality proclamation among departments; no unrelated matters bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Duties are assigned by broad subject area (e.g., 'enforcement of the neutrality of the United States as prescribed in the above-mentioned proclamation') without defined triggers, standards, or consequences, leaving considerable good-faith variance in how each department enforces.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The duty-allocation scheme creates no incentive for any department to undermine neutrality enforcement; it invokes an active war/neutrality proclamation, not a lapsed statute.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The order is expressly tied to the duration of the declared war ('during said war'), giving it a natural end-state as the emergency resolves.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Duties are 'in addition to the duties now prescribed, or hereafter prescribed, by law'; nothing removes or limits any existing oversight or statutory function.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement rests on government departments (including DOJ prosecution of violations), not on regulated-party self-reporting, so the third-party-standing concern does not fire.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: An internal allocation of existing enforcement duties among departments creates no identifiable precedent or authority claim reusable in unrelated contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order carefully partitions duties by domain (with cooperation clauses and mutual-agreement divisions between Treasury and Commerce) to avoid conflict rather than countermanding any agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Provisions are jurisdictional carve-outs among departments (e.g., Navy vs. Treasury vessels, Panama Canal Zone), not exemptions removing regulated actors from neutrality coverage.

note: Inter-departmental allocation of neutrality-enforcement duties; largely clean, with only the broadly-worded duty assignments firing Flag 4 (Vague Enforcement). Tied to the duration of the declared war, so no sunset gap.

title: Prescribing Arrangements for Developing and Coordinating a National Program For Minority Business Enterprise

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of Commerce coordinates, promotes, and convenes but is given no new binding authority over others; Sec. 4 expressly disclaims subjecting any agency's statutory functions to Commerce's authority, so no decision authority is concentrated.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of Commerce is the named actor responsible for coordinating the program; responsibility for the coordination function is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose—coordinate federal efforts on minority business enterprise; no distinct unrelated remedies bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order creates coordination and advisory machinery, not an enforcement regime with compliance triggers; there is no enforcement standard to be vague about, and duties on agencies are 'to the extent permitted by law and funds available.'

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A coordination/advisory structure creates no incentive for actors to undermine the stated purpose; no compliance gaming is enabled.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The order establishes an ongoing coordination program and a standing Advisory Council (two-year renewable terms) with no expiration or reauthorization trigger; this is a discretionary program grant, not an evergreen rights mandate, so absence of a sunset fires.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds coordination machinery and an advisory council; it removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism, and Sec. 4 preserves existing agency functions.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No regulated-party compliance regime dependent on self-reporting exists; the program is internal federal coordination, so the third-party-standing concern does not fire.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A coordination-and-advisory program with an express savings clause creates no identifiable precedent or authority claim reusable to reshape agency mandates elsewhere.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Sec. 4 expressly provides that nothing subjects any agency's statutory functions to another's authority or abrogates them; agencies furnish information 'to the extent permitted by law,' avoiding mandate conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No exemption or waiver structure; the order imposes no coverage from which actors are carved out.

note: Coordination/advisory program via Commerce with an explicit Sec. 4 savings clause protecting other agencies' mandates; clean except Flag 6, since the standing program and advisory council are permanent with no reauthorization trigger.

title: Extension of Trust Periods on Indian Lands Expiring During Calendar Year 1941

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order is self-executing—it extends by its own terms all 1941-expiring trust periods 25 years—vesting no discretionary decision authority in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: There is no implementation chain that can fail: the extension operates automatically by the order's text, so no responsible actor is left undefined.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: A single action—extending expiring trust periods—with one carve-out for cases Congress reserved to itself; nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing extension of land trust periods creates no ongoing behavioral rule for anyone to comply with, so there is no enforcement mechanism to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory relationship or compliance behavior is created by a self-executing extension, so there is no incentive structure to evaluate.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The action is inherently bounded—a fixed 25-year extension from each trust's expiration date—so it carries its own defined term rather than open-ended authority.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight is removed; the order expressly does not apply where Congress reserved extension authority to itself, preserving legislative prerogative.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime or regulated party exists whose violations a third party would need standing to surface; the extension is self-executing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A routine annual-style trust-period extension under longstanding statutory authority establishes no new precedent or authority claim usable in unrelated contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency is implicated; the self-executing extension does not redirect or countermand any agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No ongoing coverage from which actors are exempted; the single Congress-reservation carve-out is a jurisdictional boundary on a one-time action, not a discretionary exemption regime.

note: One-time, self-executing extension of Indian-land trust periods; compliance-regime flags (4,5,8) and Flags 10/11 are NOT_APPLICABLE because no ongoing regulatory relationship, second agency, or coverage/exemption structure exists. Decision-structure flags 1/2/7 answered ABSENT per convention.

title: Providing for the Screening of the Ready Reserve of the Armed Forces

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order revokes the detailed EO 10651 and replaces it with a bare delegation to the Secretary of Defense of the President's authority to 'prescribe regulations for the screening'—shifting from President-set criteria to unbounded rulemaking discretion pooled in one Secretary.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Where EO 10651 required the Secretary to make periodic reports to the President (Sec. 10), this order removes that reporting obligation and delegates open-ended regulatory authority with no accountability mechanism specified.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Two provisions—delegate screening authority and revoke the predecessor—serve one purpose; nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Diff against EO 10651: the predecessor supplied detailed screening criteria (age/fitness, hardship, critical-occupation list, transfer preferences); this order supplies none, leaving the entire screening standard to be defined later at the Secretary's discretion, so the standard is undefined on the face of the order.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A delegation of Ready-Reserve screening rulemaking creates no identifiable incentive for an actor to undermine the purpose; it invokes active Title 10 authority, not a lapsed statute.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The order grants ongoing discretionary regulatory authority to the Secretary with no expiration or reauthorization trigger; screening is a continuing administrative function but the delegation is open-ended discretion, not a self-revising rule, so absence of a sunset fires.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: By revoking EO 10651, the order eliminates the President's periodic-report oversight mechanism (Sec. 10) that previously let screening be 'evaluated in relation to overall manpower mobilization requirements,' removing a pre-existing check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Reserve screening is an internal military-personnel process, not a regulated-party compliance regime reliant on self-reporting, so the third-party-standing concern does not fire.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Replacing a fully specified, President-issued screening rule with a blank delegation to a Secretary sets a precedent that detailed executive criteria can be swept away and re-vested as open-ended agency discretion, weakening presidential-level control over reserve mobilization policy.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Authority is delegated to Defense (and Treasury for the Coast Guard) consistent with their existing roles; no agency's statutory mandate is countermanded or redirected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order itself contains no exemption structure; the detailed transfer categories of EO 10651 are revoked rather than replaced with any new carve-out regime.

note: Diff-aware: this order revokes the detailed EO 10651 (text available) and replaces its President-set screening criteria and periodic-reporting requirement with a bare, open-ended delegation to the Secretary of Defense—weakening the standard (Flag 4), removing the report-to-President oversight (Flag 7), and pooling discretion (Flags 1/2).

title: Administration of Arms Export Controls

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authority is distributed across the Secretaries of State, Defense, Treasury, and Commerce with numerous cross-concurrence and consultation requirements (e.g., Sec. 1(l)(1) requires Defense concurrence in State's designations); no single official holds unchecked authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each delegated function is assigned to a specifically named Secretary, so responsibility for any failure is traceable to an identified official; Sec. 2(b) names the Secretary of State as continuously responsible for supervision.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order addresses a single coherent subject (delegation of Arms Export Control Act functions); no unrelated remedies or distinct problem frames are packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Sec. 1(a) authorizes the Secretary of State to 'find... whether the proposed transfer will strengthen the security of the United States and promote world peace' with no defined standard or criteria, allowing wide good-faith divergence in application.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The delegation structure with mandatory inter-secretary concurrences does not create an incentive for any actor to undermine the Act's purpose; no lapsed statute is continued.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural delegation framework maintained through supersession (it revokes EO 11501, evidence of routine amendment), with no natural end-state; per the standing-procedural-framework carve-out it scores ABSENT.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Compared to predecessor EO 11501, this order adds rather than removes checks (more consultation/concurrence requirements); it removes no pre-existing external oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Sec. 25 congressional-notification functions aside, the licensing and export-control determinations rely on executive self-assessment with no external actor given standing and access to surface non-compliance in individual transfer decisions.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order allocates existing statutory functions among departments; it establishes no new authority claim or definitional framework usable as precedent in unrelated domains.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order carefully partitions functions and requires concurrence rather than countermanding any agency's statutory mandate; e.g., Treasury import functions are guided by State's views (Sec. 1(l)(2)), avoiding jurisdictional conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order carves specified subsections out of each delegation (reserving them to the President), but these are jurisdictional reservations, not coverage exemptions that swallow a regulatory rule; no discretionary waiver structure exists.

note: Diff-aware comparison to predecessor EO 11501 (available): EO 11958 expands the delegation scope but adds substantial cross-concurrence checks, so it strengthens rather than weakens oversight architecture.

title: Establishing an Advisory Board on Just Compensation and Prescribing Its Functions

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authority to set just-compensation standards rests in a three-member Board of named judges (para. 2) acting collectively per constitutional/statutory limits (para. 4); no single official holds concentrated discretion.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Board is the named body responsible for establishing the standards and the War Shipping Administration is the named actor applying them (para. 5), so responsibility for any failure is traceable.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose: guidance on just-compensation standards for requisitioned vessels; no distinct problems or remedies bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Board is to 'establish fair and equitable standards, rules and formulae of general applicability' (para. 4) with no defined content, and the WSA 'will be guided by' them (para. 5) without a binding compliance mechanism, leaving application indeterminate.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: An advisory standard-setting board creates no incentive for any actor to undermine the compensation objective; no lapsed statute is continued.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The Board has an explicit built-in termination: members 'serve for a period of three months or for such lesser period... as may be required for the completion of the Functions' (para. 2).

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order creates a new advisory body and removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; compensation remains subject to 'applicable provisions of the Constitution and the laws' (para. 4).

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Compensation determinations are made internally by the WSA guided by the Board, and vessel owners (the affected third parties) are given no mechanism or standing in the order to contest or surface underpayment.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A temporary wartime standard-setting board for vessel compensation has no identifiable precedential or systemic downstream effect beyond its stated task.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the WSA is implicated and the Board advises it; no second agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The standards are of 'general applicability' (para. 4) to all requisitioned vessels; no exemption or waiver structure exists.

note: Advisory standard-setting body with a built-in short duration; the guidance-not-binding structure produces the two mild PRESENT findings on Flags 4 and 8.

title: Coordination of Federal Activities Affecting the Fishery Industry

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Interior is designated Fishery Coordinator with authority to designate a Deputy and delegate 'any of the functions, duties, and powers' (para. 1) and to determine the scope of the coordinating office, concentrating coordination authority in one official, though the functions are only liaison/advisory.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Fishery Coordinator (Secretary of the Interior) is the single named responsible official, so responsibility for coordination failure is clearly assigned.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose of coordinating federal fishery activities for the war effort; no unrelated matters bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The office's functions are to 'make specific recommendations' and 'advise' (para. 2(b)) with no enforcement mechanism, trigger, or standard of compliance for the agencies it coordinates, leaving any 'enforcement' wholly undefined.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A liaison/coordination office creates no incentive to undermine sustained fishery production; authority is tied to the active, unexpired First War Powers Act, so no zombie trap.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Para. 4 provides the order 'shall continue in force and effect so long as Title I of the First War Powers Act, 1941, remains in force,' tying its life to the emergency statute's duration.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds a coordinating office and liaison-officer designations; it removes no pre-existing oversight, and confidential/secret war operations are expressly exempted from disclosure to it (para. 2(a)).

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The order imposes no compliance obligation on regulated parties to be enforced; it is a coordination/advisory function, so no third-party enforcement gap arises.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A wartime fishery-coordination office produces no identifiable precedential or systemic downstream effect beyond its coordination task.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Office of Fishery Coordination coordinates with agencies via voluntary liaison and only advises the War Production Board 'when and to the extent requested' (para. 2(b)); it does not redirect or countermand any agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order imposes no coverage rule with exemptions; the only carve-out (confidential/secret war plans, para. 2(a)) is an information-sharing limit, not an exemption from a regulatory obligation.

note: Wartime coordination office tied explicitly to the First War Powers Act's duration; mild power-concentration and vague-authority findings offset by the clear termination trigger and single named official.

title: Partial Revocation of Executive Order of March 21, 1914

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Self-executing partial revocation of a public water reservation over a specific described tract; no decision authority is concentrated in any official because none is exercised going forward.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The action is complete on signature; there is no implementation to fail and thus no accountability chain to be missing.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single provision revoking one reservation as to one described tract; nothing is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time self-executing land-status revocation creates no ongoing compliance relationship for anyone, so vague-enforcement analysis does not apply.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule or incentive is created by a one-time land-status revocation.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing revocation grants no continuing authority, so there is nothing to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; it merely alters the reservation status of a described parcel.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing enforcement or compliance obligation exists over which any third party would have standing to surface violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow land-status revocation with no systemic or precedential implication; per the flag's own note it is not applicable to such actions.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency is implicated in any way by a self-executing partial revocation of a water reservation.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage rule with exemptions is created; the order simply removes land from a prior reservation.

note: One-time, self-executing land-status revocation; the compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11) plus 6/9/10 are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE because no ongoing regulatory relationship or continuing authority is created. Predecessor is an unnumbered 1914 EO, not recoverable by number, but its full text is not needed since this is a clean partial revocation.

title: Withdrawal of Public Land for Use of Department of Agriculture as Addition to Agricultural Field Station

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Self-executing withdrawal of described lands reserved for Agriculture's use; no decision authority is pooled in any official because the action is complete on signature.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The action is fully executed by the order itself; there is no implementation chain that could fail without a responsible actor.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single provision withdrawing one described tract for one purpose; nothing bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time land withdrawal creates no ongoing compliance relationship to be enforced.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule or incentive structure is created by a one-time land withdrawal.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The withdrawal is self-executing and grants no continuing discretionary authority to sunset; its 'continue in force until revoked' clause is a land-status statement, not an ongoing authority grant.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; it only reserves described land for a field station.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing enforcement or compliance obligation exists for any third party to surface.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow land withdrawal with no systemic or precedential implication.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Land is reserved for Agriculture's use but no second agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage rule with exemptions is created by a land withdrawal.

note: One-time, self-executing land withdrawal; compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11) and 6/9/10 are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE as no ongoing regulatory relationship or continuing authority is created. No numbered predecessor cited.

title: Delegating to the Secretary of Agriculture Certain Authority Relating to Emergency Livestock Feed

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Sec. 1(a) empowers the Secretary of Agriculture to act 'without the approval, ratification, or other action of the President,' and Sec. 1(b)/Sec. 2 vest sole discretion in him to declare acute-distress areas and terminate assistance 'upon notice,' with no external check on either determination.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of Agriculture is the single named official responsible for the determinations and their termination, so responsibility is clearly assigned.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose: delegating emergency livestock-feed authority for distressed Indian tribal lands; nothing bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Sec. 1(b) turns on the Secretary's finding that distress is 'materially increased by severe drought, flood... or other uncontrollable catastrophe' and that feed 'will not displace or interfere with normal marketing,' standards loose enough that two officials could reach opposite determinations in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The single-Secretary discretion could invite over- or under-declaration, but no structural feature rewards undermining the relief purpose, and the authority derives from an active statute (7 U.S.C. 1427), so no zombie trap.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Each assistance instance terminates on the Secretary's notice (Sec. 2), and the specific 1965 declaration is capped 'until January 1, 1968' (Sec. 4), providing built-in termination triggers.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 1(a) explicitly removes the pre-existing requirement of Presidential 'approval, ratification, or other action' over the exercise of this section-407 authority, eliminating a standing external check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The distress determination and the finding that feed 'will not displace or interfere with normal marketing' rest entirely on the Secretary's self-assessment; no external actor (e.g., affected marketers or tribal members) is given standing or access to surface an erroneous determination.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The delegation applies a specific statutory relief authority to a defined situation; it establishes no precedent or definitional framework usable in unrelated domains.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Sec. 3 requires the Secretary of Agriculture to 'maintain liaison with' and 'consult' the Secretary of the Interior; the order coordinates rather than countermanding Interior's mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No exemption or waiver structure is created; the order defines a coverage trigger (acute distress) rather than carve-outs from a rule.

note: The 'without the approval... of the President' delegation removes an existing check (Flag 7) and pairs single-official discretion (Flag 1) with self-assessed loose findings (Flags 4, 8); offset by explicit termination triggers on Flag 6.

title: Exemption of Arthur H. Chase From Compulsory Retirement for Age

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A one-time exemption of one named individual from compulsory retirement, exercised by the President under section 204 authority; no ongoing decision authority is pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The action is self-executing and complete; there is no implementation chain that could fail without a responsible actor.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single provision addressing one individual's retirement exemption; nothing bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time individual retirement exemption creates no ongoing compliance relationship to be enforced.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule or incentive structure arises from a single-person exemption.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The exemption is expressly time-limited to 'a period of one year ending November 30, 1940,' so any continuing effect self-terminates.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; it applies a statutory exemption authority to one person.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing enforcement or compliance obligation exists over which any third party would have standing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A single-individual, one-year retirement exemption has no systemic or precedential implication.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's statutory mandate is implicated; the action concerns one Navy Department employee's retirement status.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: This is a single statutory exemption applied to one named person, not an exemptions structure carving actors out of a coverage rule.

note: One-time individual exemption extending EO 8011 (predecessor available and read); the extension is a fixed one-year renewal for one person, so compliance-regime flags (4,5,8) and 9/10/11 are NOT_APPLICABLE. Flag 6 scores ABSENT because the exemption is expressly time-bounded.

title: Directing the Federal Works Administrator to Take Possession of and Operate a Certain Project of the Grand River Dam Authority

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Federal Works Administrator is directed to seize, manage, and operate the project 'through such person or persons as he may designate' (para. 1) and may exercise 'any existing contractual or other rights of the Authority' (para. 4), concentrating broad operational control in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Federal Works Administrator is the single named official responsible for possession, management, and restoration of the project, so responsibility is clearly assigned.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose: wartime seizure and operation of one identified power project; nothing bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time possessory seizure of a specific project directed at a single government official creates no ongoing regulatory-compliance relationship with a regulated class to be enforced.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The seizure is tied to war-safety purposes with a restoration duty; no structural feature rewards undermining the purpose, and it invokes an active statute (section 16 of the Federal Power Act), so no zombie trap.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Retention continues indefinitely 'until such time as it shall appear to the President that further retention... is unnecessary' (para. 2); an emergency/exceptional-powers seizure of this kind should have a natural end tied to the emergency, and the only termination trigger is the seizing authority's own discretion, so the flag fires.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Para. 4 authorizes employment of personnel 'without compliance with the requirements of the Civil Service Rules,' expressly bypassing a pre-existing external check on federal hiring.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance obligation on a regulated party exists; the affected Authority's compensation is fixed by the Federal Power Commission, not left to self-reporting, so no third-party enforcement gap applies.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order asserts a Presidential power to seize and operate a privately-licensed FPA project 'without compliance with the Civil Service Rules,' establishing precedent for executive takeover of licensed facilities usable in contexts beyond this single project.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order redirects operation of a project licensed and regulated by the Federal Power Commission to the Federal Works Administrator (with FPC only fixing compensation), overriding the normal FPC-licensee operational relationship and creating friction with the FPC's statutory role.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage rule with exemptions is created; the order is a targeted seizure of one project. The Civil Service carve-out is a preemption of oversight (Flag 7), not a coverage-exemption structure.

note: Wartime seizure invoking active FPA section 16; concentrates operational control in one Administrator (Flag 1), bypasses Civil Service Rules (Flag 7), lacks an emergency-tied end (Flag 6), overrides the FPC-licensee relationship (Flag 10), and sets a seizure precedent (Flag 9). Compliance-regime flags 4/8/11 are NOT_APPLICABLE as no ongoing regulated-party relationship is created.

title: Presidential Advisory Committee on Federalism

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Committee is purely advisory (Sec. 2, 'shall advise the President'); members and Chairman are appointed by and serve at the pleasure of the President, so no operative decision authority is pooled anywhere.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: As an advisory body with no operational mandate, there is no implementation to fail; the President who appoints and can remove members retains clear responsibility.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose of advising on federalism policy; nothing bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A purely advisory committee imposes no compliance obligation on any actor, so there is no enforcement mechanism to assess for vagueness.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: An advisory committee with no operative authority creates no behavioral incentive for regulated parties or beneficiaries to game.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Sec. 4(b) provides the Committee 'shall terminate on December 31, 1982, unless sooner extended,' an explicit sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order creates a new advisory body under the Federal Advisory Committee Act and removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement or compliance obligation exists over which any third party could have standing; the body only advises.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A time-limited advisory committee established under FACA has no identifiable precedential or systemic downstream effect beyond offering policy advice.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Sec. 3(a) has agencies provide information 'to the extent permitted by law'; the Committee coordinates/advises and redirects no agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage rule with exemptions is created by an advisory committee.

note: Purely advisory FACA committee with an explicit sunset (Sec. 4(b)); compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11) are NOT_APPLICABLE because the body imposes no ongoing rule on anyone's conduct. Clean architecture across all applicable flags.

title: Federal Consumer Programs

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Sec. 1-303 vests the Chairperson with authority to 'establish such policies, definitions, procedures, and standards to govern the implementation, interpretation, and application of this Order,' and Sec. 1-502 makes the Chairperson the sole approver of every agency's consumer program, concentrating interpretive and approval authority in one designated official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Chairperson both directs and monitors implementation (Secs. 1-504, 1-505) and reports 'on behalf of the President,' so the actor responsible for correcting failure is also the actor judging whether the programs succeeded, with no independent check named.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order pursues a single coherent aim (coordinating and strengthening federal consumer programs) across coherent sub-parts; no unrelated remedies or distinct problem frames are packaged for binary acceptance.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Compliance turns on undefined qualitative standards—agencies must ensure 'consumer needs and interests are adequately considered' (Sec. 1-401) and 'early and meaningful participation' (Sec. 1-401(b))—with the Chairperson approving programs against no fixed metric, so two officials could reach different compliance judgments in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The self-assessment structure is weak but no design feature affirmatively rewards agencies for undermining consumer protection; authority derives from no lapsed statute, so no zombie trap.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The order establishes a permanent standing coordination framework and Chairperson authority with no expiration or reauthorization requirement—only a recurring report (Sec. 1-505), which the flag expressly notes is not a substitute for a sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds oversight machinery (Council monitoring, public comment periods, OMB budget review under Sec. 1-601); it removes no pre-existing external check, so the removal-directed flag does not fire.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement of the five required program elements rests on agency self-reporting and internal Chairperson monitoring (Secs. 1-501 to 1-505); no external actor with standing and access—consumers included—is given a mechanism to surface an agency's non-compliance beyond commenting on the agency's own draft.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order restructures internal agency consumer-program procedures; it establishes no authority claim or definitional framework usable as precedent in unrelated domains beyond consumer affairs.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Sec. 1-601 directs OMB to share every agency's budget consumer-exhibit with the Chairperson, who then supplies OMB 'an analysis of the adequacy of the... funding and staff levels,' inserting the Chairperson into OMB's statutory budget-review function and creating parallel authority over program funding assessment.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Sec. 1-902 exempts independent regulatory agencies, EO 12044 Sec. 6(b) categories, and—critically—any agency that demonstrates 'to the satisfaction of the Chairperson' that its activities have 'no substantial impact upon consumers,' a discretionary, single-actor-controlled carve-out with an easily-argued trigger that can shrink coverage.

note: Partial reference to EO 12044 (available and read) only for the Sec. 6(b) exemption categories incorporated into Sec. 1-902; assessed independently. Concentrates interpretive/approval authority in the Chairperson (Flags 1,2), relies on undefined qualitative standards and self-reporting (Flags 4,8), permanent with no sunset (Flag 6), inserts into OMB budget review (Flag 10), and includes a discretionary self-swallowing exemption (Flag 11).

title: Continuing the Regulation of Exports

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Continues delegation (via incorporated EO 11533) of full export-control power to the Secretary of Commerce with power of successive redelegation; discretion pools in one official, with the Board being advisory only.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Incorporated EO 11533 keeps the President as accountable overseer (Sec. 4: President may prescribe rules, issue directives, receive reports); a named actor retains correction authority.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (continue export controls notwithstanding EAA expiration); no distinct remedies or problem frames forced together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Continues an ongoing regulatory regime (15 CFR parts 368-399) but the underlying standards rest on broad national-security/foreign-policy/economic-drain criteria (Sec. preamble) that two officials could apply differently; enforcement mechanics carried over unchanged.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Sec. 1 expressly continues export-control authority 'Notwithstanding the expiration of the Export Administration Act of 1969,' substituting the 1917 Trading With the Enemy Act 5(b) emergency power; the agency exercises lapsed-statute authority, removing pressure to reauthorize the EAA on its own terms.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Emergency-derived authority tied to an expired statute and 1950/1971 emergency proclamations has a natural end-state but no sunset or reauthorization trigger; it continues 'in full force and effect' indefinitely.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing external check is removed; Sec. 2 actually lowers maximum fines and disapplies a civil penalty, and Sec. 3 preserves other delegations. No oversight mechanism is stripped.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Export-control enforcement runs through Commerce licensing/self-reporting by exporters; no external actor with standing and access to surface non-compliance is created or preserved.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Establishes the precedent of running a lapsed statutory framework on residual 1917 emergency power, legitimizing indefinite continuation of expired trade-control authority usable in future lapses.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Commerce retains its existing role; Sec. 3 expressly preserves other agencies' delegations under 5(b). No agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Order continues coverage of the existing CFR export-control scheme; it creates no new waiver or carve-out structure that would swallow the rule.

note: Full incorporation of EO 11533 (predecessor available); scored inheriting its Commerce delegation. Zombie Emergency Trap fires: text acknowledges EAA expiration yet continues authority under 1917 TWEA 5(b).

title: Implementing the Generalized System of Preferences

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Designations are made by the President under statutory Title V criteria after ITC advice, public comment, and congressional notification; authority is checked and distributed, not pooled in one unaccountable official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The President is the named responsible actor and duty rates flow from statutory Trade Act criteria administered via customs and Treasury regulations; responsibility is traceable.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose: implement the GSP in the Tariff Schedules. Multiple sections are all mechanical steps of one program, not distinct bundled remedies.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Eligibility is governed by precise, quantified rules-of-origin (35%/50% value-content tests in new headnote 3(c)) under Treasury regulations, leaving little room for divergent good-faith application.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The 35% domestic-content requirement is designed to prevent gaming; no actor class is rewarded for undermining the duty-free program's purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: GSP designation is a discretionary trade-preference grant with a natural periodic-review character, but this order sets no expiration or reauthorization trigger for the eligibility grants it establishes; predecessor EO 11844 noted continuing review but this order embeds no sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight is removed; ITC advice and congressional notification checks are retained per statute. Sec. 11 supersedes EO 11844 (a country-designation order) but adds implementation machinery rather than stripping review.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Rules of origin are verified through customs entry/appraisal at the border by the Treasury/Customs, an external enforcer with access to shipment data, not mere self-reporting.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A mechanical tariff-schedule implementation of a statutorily-mandated program; no meaningful precedent or authority claim extending beyond the stated GSP purpose is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Roles for ITC, Treasury/Customs, and Commerce statistics are coordinated within existing statutory mandates; no agency's authority is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The 'A*' designation (Sec. 7, headnote 3(c)(iii)) carves specific countries out of otherwise-eligible items, and the President 'may modify at any time' the beneficiary list; a discretionary exclusion structure exists, though bounded by statutory criteria.

note: Supersedes EO 11844 (predecessor available), a country-designation order; this order is a partial-amendment/implementation adding TSUS machinery, assessed on its own terms. Statutorily-constrained trade program with precise origin rules scores near-clean.

title: Regulating Transactions in Foreign Exchange and Foreign-Owned Property, Providing For the Reporting of All Foreign-Owned Property, and Related Matters

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Sweeping control over all foreign-exchange, banking, and foreign-owned-property transactions is vested in the Secretary of the Treasury, whose licensing decisions Sec. 7 makes 'final,' with power to deem any person a 'national' (Sec. 5) — authority pooled in one official with no external check.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Treasury (and Attorney General) both administer and adjudicate, determining coverage, granting/denying licenses, and investigating; no independent actor is responsible when the regime misfires, and Sec. 7 forecloses review.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent frame (block Axis-connected foreign property and transactions); the many prohibitions are facets of one regulatory purpose, not distinct unrelated remedies.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Coverage turns on elastic standards — 'reasonable cause to believe,' 'purporting to act directly or indirectly for the benefit' (Secs. 5D-E) — and Treasury may 'deem' anyone a national; two officials could reach materially different enforcement decisions in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The prohibition-plus-license structure aligns regulated parties toward compliance to obtain licenses; no design feature rewards undermining the blocking purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Emergency/wartime property-control authority premised on an 'unlimited national emergency' is exactly the kind of exceptional power that should end with the emergency, yet Sec. 9 makes it amendable/revocable at will with no expiration tied to the emergency's resolution.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 7 provides that the Secretary's decision on any license 'shall be final,' eliminating judicial/administrative review of licensing determinations — a direct removal of an external check on the concentrated authority.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement depends on regulated parties' recordkeeping and Treasury investigation (Sec. 4); no external third party with standing and access to surface concealed foreign interests is created.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Establishes an expansive, self-defining 'national'/'foreign country' framework with unreviewable licensing that becomes a durable template for later blocking/sanctions regimes (e.g., its terms are carried forward and relied on in EO 8389/9095 successor orders).

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Sec. 4 assigns overlapping investigative/enforcement authority jointly to the Secretary of the Treasury 'and/or the Attorney General' without a clear hierarchy, creating parallel authority over the same subject matter.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The entire regime operates through Treasury-controlled licenses granted 'except as specifically authorized by the Secretary' (Secs. 1-2), an unbounded, single-actor discretion to exempt any transaction with no defined criteria or external check on grants.

note: Amends EO 8389 (predecessor available; restates it in full). Full incorporation of the 8389 framework for a new consolidated schedule of countries; scored as the operative blocking regime, not a narrow one-provision tweak.

title: Revoking Executive Order 9301 of February 9, 1943, Establishing a Minimum Wartime Workweek of Forty-Eight Hours

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A one-sentence revocation exercises no ongoing authority and concentrates power nowhere; it removes the War Manpower Commission Chairman's discretionary power that predecessor EO 9301 had pooled.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Self-executing revocation with the President as the single responsible actor; nothing to fail in implementation.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single act (revoke EO 9301); nothing bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Revocation ends an ongoing rule rather than creating any compliance obligation; no enforcement relationship exists to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule is created by a pure revocation; there is no incentive structure to evaluate.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing revocation grants no continuing authority, so there is nothing to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Removes no pre-existing external oversight mechanism; it simply lifts a wartime workweek requirement (EO 9301 had preserved the FLSA, which continues).

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement or compliance chain is established by the revocation, so third-party incentive structure does not apply.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Narrow deregulatory revocation at war's end with no identifiable precedent or authority claim extending beyond removing the 48-hour workweek.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's mandate is redirected; it merely retires authority formerly held by the War Manpower Commission Chairman.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population or exemption structure exists once the underlying requirement is revoked.

note: Revokes EO 9301 (predecessor available). Pure one-time revocation: compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11) and Flag 6 are NOT_APPLICABLE per the concrete test; decision-structure flags score ABSENT.

title: Providing for the Restoration of Law and Order in the State of Illinois

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: All operative discretion — use of armed forces, calling up the National Guard 'for an indefinite period,' and delegation — is vested in the Secretary of Defense to act 'as he may deem necessary/appropriate' (Secs. 1-4), with no external check on that judgment.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of Defense is the single named actor responsible for restoring order and reporting up the chain of command to the President; responsibility is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose tied to Proclamation 3841 (disperse violence, restore order); no distinct remedies or frames bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The operative standard is 'all appropriate steps' and 'such of the Armed Forces as he may deem necessary' (Secs. 1-2), leaving the trigger and scope of coercive action almost entirely to the Secretary's discretion.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A short-fuse civil-disorder deployment; no design feature rewards prolonging the disorder or gaming the order's purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is emergency/crisis authority whose natural end is restoration of order, yet Sec. 3 authorizes call-up 'for an indefinite period and until relieved by appropriate orders' with no automatic expiration tied to the emergency's resolution.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Invokes existing statutory authority (10 U.S.C. Ch. 15; 3 U.S.C. 301) without removing any pre-existing external oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is a direct executive-deployment order, not an ongoing regulatory-compliance regime over regulated parties; no third-party enforcement chain applies.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Federalizing the National Guard and deploying regular forces for domestic disorder establishes an exercisable precedent for indefinite military presence in civil law-enforcement contexts beyond the immediate Illinois disturbance.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Sec. 3 calls Army and Air National Guard units into federal active service, overriding the states' (Illinois's) command of its Guard for the duration — a redirection of authority another sovereign/body ordinarily holds, though under statutory authorization.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order imposes no coverage rule on a regulated population from which exemptions could be carved; it directs military action.

note: No numbered EO predecessor (tied to Proclamation 3841 and statutory authorities). Compliance-regime Flags 8 and 11 NOT_APPLICABLE as this is a direct military-deployment order, not an ongoing regulatory relationship.

title: Transferring Jurisdiction Over Blocked Assets to the Attorney General

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Para. 1 vests in the Attorney General all powers under TWEA 3(a) and 5(b) over remaining blocked assets, to 'take such action as he may deem necessary,' concentrating broad discretionary control over foreign property in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Para. 3's no-challenge clause bars any affected person from contesting the validity of the AG's or Treasury's actions on jurisdictional grounds, leaving no actor able to correct a misallocation of authority once made.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose (consolidate residual blocked-asset jurisdiction in the AG alongside the alien-property program); nothing distinct bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The AG may 'take such action as he may deem necessary' over blocked property with no defined standard, so continued blocking, unblocking, or seizure decisions rest on open-ended discretion two officials could apply differently.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A jurisdictional consolidation of an existing program; no design feature rewards an actor for undermining its purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Post-hostilities blocked-asset authority has a natural wind-down end-state (the preamble notes 'successful termination of hostilities' and gradual release), yet the transfer continues the blocking regime indefinitely with no expiration or reauthorization trigger.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Para. 3 provides that no affected person 'may challenge the validity' of the AG's or Treasury's actions on the ground of which official had jurisdiction — an explicit removal of a route to review, insulating the transferred authority from challenge.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Administration of blocked assets proceeds through agency action and the regulated parties; no external third party is given standing/incentive to surface improper blocking or release determinations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The no-jurisdictional-challenge clause establishes a precedent that inter-agency reassignment of coercive property authority can be made immune from validity challenge, usable to shield future authority transfers from review.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order transfers control over blocked assets from the Secretary of the Treasury to the Attorney General (para. 1), reassigning operational authority between two agencies, while para. 3 disclaims disturbing other statutory powers — the reassignment itself creates the lateral shift.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Continued blocking versus unblocking of any asset rests on discretionary license/action by the AG (para. 1-2) with no defined criteria, effectively a single-actor exemption power over which assets remain covered.

note: Transfers jurisdiction under EO 8389/9095 (8389 available; 9095 cited but not read in full — noted). The para. 3 no-challenge clause drives the CRITICAL Flag 7 finding.

title: Transfer of the Federal Fire Council to the Department of Commerce

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Council is advisory and multi-member (Sec. 3 governing body of many agency reps); it develops standards and reports recommendations (Secs. 4-5) but holds no binding decision authority pooled in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Council is responsible to the Secretary of Commerce (Sec. 1), who chairs it; responsibility for the advisory function is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose (transfer/reconstitute the advisory Fire Council in Commerce); nothing distinct bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Council only surveys, studies, and recommends (Secs. 4-6); it imposes no compliance obligation on any regulated party, so there is no enforcement relationship to assess for vagueness.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: An advisory-body relocation creates no ongoing behavioral rule for regulated actors, so no incentive structure applies.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A standing intra-government advisory structure revised through ordinary supersession — Sec. 8 supersedes predecessors EO 7397 and 10257 — evidences the permanence carve-out for procedural frameworks maintained by amendment, not frozen authority.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Removes no pre-existing external oversight; Sec. 7 adds a consultation requirement with the Federal Advisory Council on Occupational Safety and Health, i.e., new coordination machinery.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement or compliance chain exists for the advisory Council, so third-party incentive structure does not apply.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow reorganization of an advisory body with no identifiable precedent or authority claim beyond fire-safety coordination.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Transfers the Council from GSA to Commerce and coordinates member agencies; no agency's statutory operational mandate is countermanded — GSA's records-salvage role is preserved (Sec. 6). Clean coordination, so ABSENT rather than NOT_APPLICABLE.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage rule over a regulated population exists, so there is no exemption architecture to assess.

note: Supersedes EO 7397 and 10257 (7397 available; predecessor was a materially similar clean advisory body). Advisory-only structure: compliance-regime flags NOT_APPLICABLE; clean design elsewhere yields ABSENT.

title: Revocation of Executive Order 9103 of March 18, 1942, Providing Uniform Control Over the Publication and Use of Federal Statistical Information Which Would Give Aid and Comfort to the Enemy

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A one-sentence revocation exercises no ongoing authority; it removes the Budget Director's wartime surveillance power that predecessor EO 9103 had concentrated, concentrating nothing itself.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Self-executing revocation with the President as sole responsible actor; nothing to fail in implementation.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single act (revoke EO 9103); nothing bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Revocation ends an ongoing control rather than creating any compliance obligation; no enforcement relationship to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A pure revocation creates no ongoing behavioral rule and thus no incentive structure.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing revocation grants no continuing authority, so there is nothing to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Removes no pre-existing external oversight; it lifts a wartime publication-control regime, if anything restoring normal statistical publication.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement/compliance chain is established by the revocation, so third-party incentive structure does not apply.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Narrow end-of-war revocation of a statistics-censorship control with no identifiable precedent or downstream authority claim.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's mandate is redirected; it retires authority formerly held by the Budget Director.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population or exemption structure exists once the underlying control is revoked.

note: Revokes EO 9103 (predecessor available). Pure one-time revocation: compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11) and Flag 6 NOT_APPLICABLE; decision-structure flags ABSENT.

title: Inspection by Renegotiation Board of Income Tax Returns Made Under the Internal Revenue Code of 1954

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Merely opens tax returns to inspection by the Renegotiation Board under statutory 26 U.S.C. 6103(a) and Treasury-decision rules approved by the President; no broad discretionary authority is pooled in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Inspection is governed by identified Treasury regulations and the President's approval; the responsible actors (Treasury, Renegotiation Board) are named and constrained by rule.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single narrow purpose (authorize Board inspection of returns); nothing bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Inspection must be 'in accordance and upon compliance with the rules and regulations prescribed by the Acting Secretary of the Treasury' in a specified Treasury decision, giving a defined standard rather than open discretion.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A defined access authorization tied to renegotiation of contracts; no design feature rewards undermining the purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A standing administrative access rule under a permanent statutory scheme (6103(a)) that is maintained by ordinary Treasury-decision revision; no crisis/temporary authority needing reauthorization pressure, so the procedural-framework carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Opening confidential tax returns to a new agency inspector reduces the pre-existing confidentiality protection around those returns for the covered class, a limitation of an existing safeguard rather than merely added machinery.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The ongoing relationship is bounded access under specified Treasury regulations that govern who may inspect and how; the regulating regime supplies the check, not mere self-reporting by the inspected.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Extending taxpayer-return access to another agency establishes precedent for widening 6103 disclosure to additional bodies, incrementally eroding return confidentiality beyond the renegotiation context.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: It grants the Renegotiation Board access to IRS-held returns under statutory authority without countermanding Treasury's mandate; the two agencies' roles are coordinated, not in conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Coverage is defined (returns under the 1954 Code, inspectable by the Board per Treasury rules); no discretionary carve-out structure that swallows the rule is created.

note: No numbered EO predecessor (relies on statute and a same-date Treasury decision, which was not provided). Creates an ongoing access/inspection relationship, so compliance-regime flags are scored on the merits rather than NOT_APPLICABLE.

title: Withdrawal of Public Lands for the Use of the War Department

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A self-executing land withdrawal under a specific statute (act of July 9, 1918); no discretionary decision authority is pooled in any official — the reservation operates by the order's own terms.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: One-time reservation with clearly identified parcels and beneficiary (War Department); nothing to fail in ongoing implementation.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose (withdraw described lands as a bombing/gunnery range); nothing distinct bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A land-status withdrawal creates no ongoing compliance rule for any regulated party, so there is no enforcement relationship to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule is created by a one-time reservation, so no incentive structure applies.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing land withdrawal grants no continuing operational authority to sunset; it also carries a built-in conditional termination on reclamation notice.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Removes no pre-existing external oversight; it expressly preserves prior withdrawals 'subject to valid existing rights' and does not rescind Public Water Reserve No. 61 or the CAA reservation.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement/compliance chain is created, so third-party incentive structure does not apply.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow, parcel-specific land reservation with no systemic or precedential implication beyond the described acreage.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Interior's reclamation and CAA interests are expressly protected and the order yields to reclamation needs on notice; no agency mandate is countermanded, so ABSENT rather than NOT_APPLICABLE.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No regulated coverage population exists, so there is no exemption architecture to assess.

note: No numbered EO predecessor (references earlier Interior/withdrawal orders by date, not EO number). One-time self-executing land withdrawal: compliance-regime flags plus Flags 6 and 9 NOT_APPLICABLE per the concrete test; decision-structure flags ABSENT.

title: Adjusting Rates of Pay for Certain Statutory Pay Systems

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order self-executes fixed statutory pay schedules under 5 U.S.C. ch. 53; no discretionary decision authority is pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: A pure rate-setting order with numeric schedules; there is no implementation failure chain to assign responsibility for.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Multiple pay schedules (GS, VA, Foreign Service) are all facets of a single pay-adjustment purpose; nothing unrelated is bundled in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A fixed pay-rate adjustment creates no ongoing compliance rule for any actor to enforce.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A one-time salary schedule creates no behavioral incentive structure to distort.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The order is a self-executing rate adjustment with a fixed effective date; there is no continuing grant of authority to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is limited, bypassed, or removed; Sec. 4 in fact restates the statutory Executive Schedule V salary cap.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no regulated conduct requiring third-party surveillance; the order simply sets numeric pay rates.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A routine statutory pay adjustment carries no identifiable precedential or systemic second-order consequence.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No agency's operational mandate is redirected or countermanded; the order only adjusts pay figures.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: There is no coverage population or exemption structure; the order sets pay rates uniformly by grade.

note: Narrow pay-adjustment order; compliance-regime and systemic flags (4,5,6,8,9,10,11) genuinely N/A per the concrete test, while decision-structure flags (1,2,7) are answerable and clean.

title: Designating the Intergovernmental Maritime Consultative Organization as a Public International Organization Entitled To Enjoy Certain Privileges, Exemptions, and Immunities

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A one-time designation under the International Organizations Immunities Act; it confers a fixed statutory status and concentrates no discretionary authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The designation is self-executing under the IOIA; there is no implementation chain with diffuse responsibility.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single provision addressing a single subject (IMCO's status); nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order creates no ongoing compliance obligation to be enforced; it simply designates immunity status.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule exists for any actor to game.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A one-time status designation contains no continuing grant of authority to reauthorize or sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight is removed; the text expressly preserves any privileges the organization holds by treaty or congressional action.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no regulated-party compliance chain requiring third-party enforcement standing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A single statutorily-anchored designation carries no identifiable precedential spillover beyond its stated subject.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's operational mandate is implicated or redirected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The immunities conferred are the statutory grant itself, not carve-outs from a coverage rule; there is no exemptions structure that swallows a rule.

note: One-time IOIA designation; despite the word 'exemptions' in the title, the immunities are the conferred status, not carve-outs from coverage, so Flag 11 is N/A.

title: Authorizing the Secretary of War to Take Possession of and Operate the Plants and Facilities of the Farrell Cheek Steel Company, Located at Sandusky, Ohio

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: All seizure and operating authority is pooled in the Secretary of War, empowered to 'do any other thing that he may deem necessary' and 'take any other steps that he deems necessary,' with no external check on the plenary grant.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary both operates the plant and determines when productive efficiency is restored (Sec. 4); the only reporting is to the President on wage funding, leaving no external actor to correct operational failure.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order addresses a single remedy—possession and operation of one plant to end a labor interruption; no distinct problems are bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The operative standard is entirely discretionary ('to the extent that he may deem necessary,' 'in any manner that he deems necessary'), so two officials could reach very different enforcement decisions in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The termination trigger is tied to restoring pre-interruption efficiency and wage increases are capped to net operating income, giving no actor an incentive to prolong the seizure.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Sec. 4 requires termination within 60 days of the Secretary's determination that pre-interruption efficiency is restored—an explicit end-condition tied to the emergency's resolution.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order directs operation under the War Labor Disputes Act and honors existing War Labor Board directive orders; it removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
