flags.justification: Compliance rests entirely with the Secretary's self-assessment (including his own determination of restored efficiency); no external party has standing or access to surface a failure to comply with the directive orders.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order exercises the Selective Training and Service Act / War Labor Disputes Act seizure power over private property, entrenching a broad plant-seizure precedent usable against other struck facilities.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Authority is vested in the Secretary of War operating under War Labor Board directives; no other agency's statutory operational mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: A single-facility seizure has no population-of-coverage against which exemptions could be mapped; there is no exemption structure.

note: Wartime single-plant seizure with plenary discretionary authority (Flag 1 CRITICAL) but an explicit efficiency-based termination trigger clearing Flag 6; invokes an active wartime statute, so the Zombie Trap does not fire.

title: Alaska Railroad Rates

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Rate authority is split: the Secretary of Transportation sets rates but is expressly 'subject to the authority of the Interstate Commerce Commission' (Secs. 1, 3), so authority is checked rather than pooled.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is clearly allocated—Transportation operates and files rates, ICC reviews them—with an identifiable actor at each step.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order addresses a single subject, Alaska Railroad rate-setting procedure; nothing distinct is bundled together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The standard is defined by reference to how comparable ICC-regulated carriers are treated under specified title 49 chapters, giving a concrete, bounded rate-review standard.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Filing rates 'to the same extent' as comparable carriers and opening contract rates to other similarly-situated shippers (Sec. 2(b)) aligns incentives with ordinary common-carrier practice.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural framework for a permanent administrative function (railroad rate-setting) that is maintained through supersession—it expressly supersedes EO 11107 (Sec. 4(a))—not a discretionary emergency grant.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Relative to superseded EO 11107, this order preserves ICC review and, if anything, broadens the ICC provisions applied; no pre-existing oversight is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Rates are filed publicly with the ICC and comparable-shipper access is guaranteed (Sec. 2(b)), giving competing shippers and the ICC standing and information to surface unjust rates.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order conforms Alaska Railroad rate-making to standard ICC practice and deems compliant rates in accord with the Alaska Railroad Act; no adverse precedent or systemic spillover is identifiable.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Transportation and ICC roles are complementary and hierarchically ordered (Transportation files, ICC reviews); no mandate conflict is created.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The only carve-out is a narrow, defined exclusion of agricultural/food goods context via title 49 references; there is no broad discretionary exemption that swallows the rate framework.

note: Diff-aware vs. superseded EO 11107 (text available): rate authority moves from Interior to Transportation but ICC review is preserved and expanded; cleanly architected, all flags applicable and ABSENT.

title: Establishing the President's Advisory Commission on Narcotic and Drug Abuse

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Commission is advisory—it develops recommendations and reports to the President (Sec. 2); it holds no eligibility, enforcement, or interpretive authority to concentrate.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: An advisory body whose sole output is reports and recommendations creates no operational failure chain requiring a responsible corrector.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order addresses a single subject—studying narcotic and drug abuse and recommending legislation; nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Commission enforces nothing; it studies and recommends, so there is no enforcement standard to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A time-limited advisory study creates no ongoing behavioral rule for any actor to game.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Sec. 6 requires a final report by November 1, 1963 and termination not later than December 31, 1963—an explicit sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds an advisory review layer and removes no pre-existing oversight of agency drug programs.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no regulated-party compliance regime requiring third-party enforcement standing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A short-lived advisory commission issuing recommendations establishes no institutional precedent or authority claim usable in unrelated contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Named departments merely designate liaisons and furnish information (Sec. 3); no agency's statutory operational mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: There is no regulated population or coverage rule from which exemptions could be carved.

note: Time-limited advisory commission with an explicit termination date; compliance-regime and exemptions flags are genuinely N/A because it enforces nothing.

title: Establishing the Federal Committee On Highway Safety

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Committee only promotes and coordinates highway-safety activities among agencies (Sec. 2); it holds no eligibility, enforcement, or interpretive authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: A coordinating body that 'requests' agency cooperation creates no binding obligation whose failure requires a named corrector; its coordination role is clearly assigned to the Committee.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single subject—coordinating federal highway-safety activity; nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Committee enforces nothing; it coordinates and encourages cooperation, so no enforcement standard exists to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A voluntary interagency coordinating body creates no ongoing compliance rule for actors to game.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is a standing interagency coordination structure for a permanent administrative function with no natural end-state; the permanence carve-out applies and no discretionary authority is granted that should face reauthorization pressure.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds a coordinating layer and removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no regulated-party compliance chain requiring third-party enforcement standing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A coordinating committee with only 'to the extent permitted by law' powers establishes no authority claim or precedent usable in unrelated contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Committee explicitly coordinates 'to the extent permitted by law' and only requests agency cooperation; it does not redirect or countermand any agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: There is no coverage population or exemption structure in a coordination body.

note: Interagency highway-safety coordinating committee with only coordinating/requesting powers; Flag 6 clears under the standing-procedural-framework carve-out since no discretionary authority is granted.

title: Advisory Committee on Small and Minority Business Ownership

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Committee 'assists in monitoring,' studies, and reports (Sec. 1-201); it holds no operative authority over eligibility, enforcement, or interpretation to concentrate.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Committee reports to the President through the SBA Administrator and annually to Congress (Secs. 1-202, 1-203), giving clear reporting responsibility for an advisory body.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single subject—implementing Sec. 7(j)(3)(A) of the Small Business Act via an advisory committee; nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Committee enforces nothing; it monitors, studies, and advises, so there is no enforcement standard to evaluate.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A time-limited advisory committee creates no ongoing behavioral rule for actors to game.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Sec. 1-402 sets an explicit termination date of December 31, 1980.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds an advisory body plus annual congressional reporting and removes no pre-existing oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no regulated-party compliance regime requiring third-party enforcement standing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A statutorily-mandated, time-limited advisory committee establishes no authority claim or precedent usable in unrelated contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Committee may only 'request' information from agencies (Sec. 1-301) and draws support from the SBA; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: There is no regulated coverage population; 'eligible small businesses' defines the study focus, not a rule with exemptions.

note: Statutorily-directed advisory committee with an explicit sunset and dual reporting to President and Congress; compliance-regime flags genuinely N/A.

title: Implementing the United States-Canada Free-Trade Implementation Act

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order distributes functions to APA-covered agencies and a Commerce Secretariat and implements statutory provisions; the President's Sec. 3 acceptance of panel decisions is a statutorily-conditioned ministerial act, not a concentration of discretionary authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Duties are assigned to identifiable actors (each APA agency, the ITA Secretariat, binational panels) with clear roles; no diffuse or circular responsibility is created.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: All provisions (notice periods, Secretariat, panel acceptance) implement the single FTA Implementation Act framework; nothing unrelated is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The notice standard is precise—no less than 75 days before comment due date—with a narrow, defined 'urgent circumstances'/'legitimate domestic objective' exception (Sec. 1); implementing officials could not reasonably diverge.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Requiring earlier notice of proposed standards and accepting binational panel review align incentives toward transparency and neutral dispute resolution, not toward gaming.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is a standing implementing framework tied to the FTA's own entry into force and duration; the procedural rules track a permanent statutory implementation function rather than a discretionary emergency grant.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order lengthens notice-and-comment lead time and adds binational panel review; it removes no pre-existing oversight. Sec. 4's no-private-right clause is a standard non-justiciability recital, not a removal of existing judicial review.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Extended public notice-and-comment and binational panels give affected parties and Canada standing and information to surface non-compliance with the standards-notice regime.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Sec. 3's advance acceptance 'as a whole' of all binational panel and extraordinary-challenge-committee decisions cedes case-by-case review of trade determinations to a binational body, a precedent expanding non-domestic adjudication of U.S. trade law.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order layers a notice requirement onto existing APA agency processes and locates the Secretariat within Commerce's ITA per statute; no agency's operational mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Sec. 1 exempts an entire category—agricultural, food, beverage, and related goods (Chapter Seven)—from the 75-day notice rule, and adds a discretionary 'urgent circumstances' escape, carving out a substantial share of standards-related measures.

note: Statutory FTA implementation order; largely clean, but Sec. 3's blanket advance acceptance of binational panel decisions (Flag 9) and the agriculture/urgent-circumstances carve-out from the notice rule (Flag 11) fire.

title: Authorizing Certain Employees of the General Accounting Office To Acquire a Classified Civil-Service Status

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Status is conferred by defined criteria upon Comptroller General recommendation and Civil Service Commission testing; authority is shared and bounded, not pooled in one unchecked official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is clear—the Comptroller General recommends, the Civil Service Commission administers the fitness tests—so there is no diffuse-blame gap.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single subject—civil-service status for a defined set of pre-July-1935 GAO appointees; nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Eligibility turns on four specific, verifiable conditions (continuous satisfactory service since July 1, 1935; noncompetitive fitness tests; citizenship; no statutory disqualification), leaving little room for divergent good-faith application.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A one-time status conversion for a closed, historically-defined group creates no ongoing behavioral rule to distort.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The order is a one-time, self-executing status conferral ('as of this date') with no continuing grant of authority to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order routes conversions through Civil Service Commission testing and requires future vacancies to be filled competitively; it adds rather than removes civil-service oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is a closed one-time conversion, not an ongoing compliance regime needing third-party enforcement.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Converting a fixed, closed set of legacy appointees to classified status, with vacancies thereafter filled competitively, establishes no broader authority claim or precedent.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Comptroller General and Civil Service Commission act within their normal roles; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Coverage is a narrowly and objectively defined class with the fourth proviso incorporating existing statutory disqualifications; there is no broad discretionary carve-out swallowing the rule.

note: One-time civil-service status conversion with clear objective criteria; cites EO 8137 as the basis for prior continuance (background, not amended), so no diff-aware comparison is required. Because it does create an eligibility determination for a class, Flag 4/11 are answerable (ABSENT) while purely-behavioral Flags 5/8 and one-time Flag 6 are N/A.

title: Classified National Security Information

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Original classification authority rests with agency heads and delegated officials who determine what is classified, and special-access-program creation is limited to a handful of officials (Sec. 4.4); the discretion to classify is broad, though bounded by standards and a new appeals structure.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement of over-classification depends largely on agencies self-inspecting and on the ISOO Director reporting to the same agency head for corrective action (Secs. 5.6, 5.7); the classifying agency substantially polices itself.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: All parts implement a single coherent subject—a uniform classification/declassification system; the components are integral to that one framework, not distinct bundled policies.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Core standards turn on whether disclosure 'reasonably could be expected to result in damage to the national security' (Sec. 1.2), an inherently elastic judgment two officials could apply very differently, and sanctions are discretionary ('if appropriate').

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Classifiers face little cost for over-classifying but bear risk for under-classifying, incentivizing excessive secrecy; the order counters this with a 'significant doubt—do not classify' rule (Sec. 1.2(b)) but the asymmetry remains.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural framework maintained through supersession—it revokes EO 12356 (Sec. 6.1(d))—and it builds in expiration pressure via 10-year default declassification and 25-year automatic declassification (Secs. 1.6, 3.4).

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Diff-aware vs. revoked EO 12356 (text available): this order adds oversight—a classification-challenge process (Sec. 1.9), automatic declassification (Sec. 3.4), and an Interagency Security Classification Appeals Panel (Sec. 5.4); it removes no pre-existing check, so Flag 7 does not fire on newly-added, imperfect machinery.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Surfacing improper classification depends on authorized holders challenging classification and on internal self-inspection (Secs. 1.9, 5.6); the public and requesters have limited access, and no external actor with independent standing routinely audits classification decisions.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order legitimizes an expansive, self-administered classification regime whose 'compilation' and reclassification-after-FOIA-request provisions (Secs. 1.8(d)-(e)) enable withholding beyond individually-classified items, entrenching secrecy precedents reusable across domains.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order establishes a hierarchical, coordinated structure (ISOO within OMB, the Appeals Panel, originating-agency control) with defined referral rules; it does not redirect or countermand any agency's separate statutory operational mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Agency heads may exempt broad categories from the 10-year and 25-year automatic-declassification rules under nine open-ended exemption grounds controlled largely by the exempting agency (Secs. 1.6(d), 3.4(b)-(c)), a carve-out structure capable of swallowing the declassification default.

note: Major standing classification framework; diff-aware vs. revoked EO 12356 shows this order ADDS oversight (Appeals Panel, classification challenges, automatic declassification), so Flag 7 is ABSENT, but self-administered classification discretion and broad declassification exemptions drive Flags 1,2,4,5,8,9,11 to PRESENT.

title: Providing for the Performance by the Director of Defense Mobilization of Certain Functions Relating to Telecommunications

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Coordination of executive-branch telecom policy plus discretionary radio-frequency assignment authority (Sec. 1(d)) is pooled in the Director of Defense Mobilization with no external check; the President may add functions but no independent oversight is provided.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Director is a single, named, presidentially-supervised official answerable to the President; responsibility for failure is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose—consolidating pre-existing telecom coordination functions under one officer; no distinct remedies or problem frames bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Functions are advisory/coordinative with defined statutory frequency-assignment authority under 47 U.S.C. 305; no ambiguous compliance standard imposed on outside parties.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Internal coordination role creates no incentive for any actor to undermine the stated purpose; authority rests on standing statutes, not a lapsed one.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Standing procedural framework for permanent executive telecom administration that is maintained by ordinary supersession—this order itself revokes and replaces EO 10297 (Sec. 6), evidencing routine amendment rather than frozen indefinite authority.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 4 expressly preserves all existing FCC authority and jurisdiction and directs cooperation; no pre-existing oversight is removed or bypassed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Governs intra-executive coordination, not a regulated party's compliance, so no third-party enforcement chain is required; the FCC retains its independent role over external actors.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Order merely reassigns existing coordination functions from one officer to another; no new authority, definition, or precedent with cross-domain reach is created.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Directs cooperation with the FCC and use of interagency committees; the FCC's statutory mandate is expressly protected (Sec. 4), so no lateral mandate conflict is created.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No exemption, waiver, or carve-out structure exists; the order defines coordination functions rather than a covered population with escape hatches.

note: Diff-aware: revokes EO 10297 (predecessor read); functions are near-identical, merely moved from the Telecommunications Advisor to the Director of Defense Mobilization, and the FCC savings clause carries over, so the amendment neither strengthens nor weakens oversight.

title: Establishing a National Commission on the Observance of International Women's Year, 1975

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Commission is advisory (promotes observance, disseminates information, reports) with no decision authority over anyone; members are appointed and the presiding officer designated by the President, but no operative power is pooled.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: A presidentially-appointed Commission reporting to the President with a State-chaired interdepartmental task force provides clear lines of responsibility for a purely promotional body.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose—domestic observance of International Women's Year—with sub-tasks all serving that frame; no distinct unrelated remedies packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Commission has no enforcement power; it promotes, informs, and reports, so there is no compliance standard that could be applied inconsistently.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A time-limited advisory body creates no incentive for any actor to undermine its promotional purpose; no lapsed statute is involved.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Sec. 2(h) requires the Commission to conclude its work by the end of 1975, report within thirty days, and then terminate—a clean built-in sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed, limited, or bypassed; the order only adds a new advisory structure.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No regulated party or compliance obligation exists, so there is no enforcement chain requiring third-party standing to surface violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A one-year promotional commission with a fixed termination creates no lasting authority, definitional import, or precedent usable in other domains.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The interdepartmental task force coordinates observance activities and requests voluntary assistance; it does not redirect or countermand any agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population and no exemption or waiver structure; the Commission imposes nothing to be exempted from.

note: Cites no numbered predecessor EO; references Proclamation 4262 only. Clean advisory order with an explicit termination date, scoring ABSENT across all flags.

title: Committee on Selection of Federal Judicial Officers

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Committee only conducts inquiries and recommends up to five names when requested (Secs. 2-3); final selection authority remains with the President, and criteria are set by the President or Attorney General, so no operative authority is pooled in the Committee.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: A named Chairman and Committee report to the President and Attorney General with the Attorney General providing staff and financing (Sec. 6); responsibility is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose—identifying and vetting candidates for non-Circuit/non-District judicial officer vacancies; no unrelated remedies or frames bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Committee applies criteria established by the President or Attorney General and follows procedures they set (Sec. 3(b)-(c)); it has an advisory, not enforcement, function with defined process.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Sec. 4 bars any member from being nominated to a position the Committee vetted, foreclosing the obvious self-dealing incentive; no lapsed statute is involved.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Sec. 8 sets an explicit termination date of December 31, 1978 unless extended—a built-in sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight is removed; Sec. 7 reassigns FACA administrative functions to the Attorney General but expressly preserves the annual reporting-to-Congress requirement.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is a candidate-vetting advisory body, not a regulatory regime; no ongoing compliance violations exist that a third party would need standing to surface.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A time-limited, request-only advisory committee with member-ineligibility safeguards creates no authority claim or precedent usable beyond its narrow vetting purpose.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Committee may request information from agencies which furnish it as permitted by law (Sec. 5); it does not redirect or countermand any agency's statutory mandate, and Sec. 7's reassignment of FACA functions to the AG is a clean allocation, not a conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No covered population subject to a rule and no exemption or waiver structure; the order establishes a vetting process, not coverage with carve-outs.

note: No numbered predecessor EO is amended or revoked (Sec. 7 references FACA generally). Clean advisory order with explicit sunset (Sec. 8) and a member-ineligibility safeguard (Sec. 4); ABSENT across all flags.

title: Setting Aside for the Use of the United States Certain Public Lands and Other Public Property Located at the Kapalama Military Reservation, Hawaii

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A self-executing set-aside of described lands for U.S. use in fee simple; no decision authority is exercised or pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The action is complete on signing with nothing to administer or fail; no responsibility chain is left ambiguous.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: A single land set-aside under one statutory authority; nothing unrelated is packaged with it.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time land set-aside creates no ongoing compliance rule for anyone, so there is no enforcement mechanism to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral relationship is created; a self-executing transfer offers no actor an incentive to game.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A completed, self-executing set-aside grants no continuing authority that could sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The set-aside is expressly subject to valid existing rights; no pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed or bypassed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No regulated party or compliance obligation exists, so no third-party enforcement interest is required.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow, parcel-specific land set-aside with no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency is implicated; the order merely sets aside described land for U.S. use.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population and no exemption structure; the metes-and-bounds exclusions are boundary descriptions, not discretionary carve-outs.

note: One-time, self-executing land set-aside: compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11), Flag 6, Flag 9, and Flag 10 are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE per the concrete test; decision-structure flags (1,2,7) are answerable and score ABSENT.

title: Amendment of Subdivision IX, Schedule B, Civil Service Rules

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Promotions of unclassified laborers to the listed classified custodial/supply positions are made subject to the approval of the Civil Service Commission; approval authority rests in the standing Commission, not a single unchecked official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Civil Service Commission is the named body responsible for approving these promotions; responsibility is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: A single narrow amendment adding one paragraph to Schedule B; no distinct remedies or frames are packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The eligibility path is defined—promotion of unclassified laborers to specified positions on non-competitive examination subject to CSC approval—leaving no undefined enforcement standard.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order narrows a prior practice (unclassified positions henceforth filled from classified registers) and does not grant classified status or transfer rights, so it creates no incentive to game the classification system; no lapsed statute is involved.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A standing procedural civil-service rule maintained through ordinary amendment (this order itself amends Schedule B); such personnel rules are evergreen administrative frameworks revised by supersession, not authority needing periodic reauthorization.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The amendment preserves CSC approval and existing Rule II/Rule III processes; no pre-existing oversight is removed—if anything the CSC's role is retained.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The Civil Service Commission independently approves each qualifying promotion, so an external body with standing and access checks eligibility determinations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order expressly limits its reach—no classified status conferred and no transfer eligibility to other branches—foreclosing spillover into the broader classified service.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Civil Service Commission and the Fourth Assistant Postmaster General act cooperatively within their own mandates; no mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order defines a narrow eligibility path rather than exempting a covered population; the express limits (no classified status, no transfer rights) bound rather than swallow coverage.

note: Amends Schedule B of the Civil Service Rules, not a numbered predecessor EO. Creates an ongoing eligibility relationship (laborer promotion subject to CSC approval), so compliance-regime flags apply and score ABSENT on the merits; clean across all eleven.

title: Withdrawal of Public Land for Classification

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A self-executing temporary withdrawal of described Alaskan lands from settlement/entry under the 1910 Act; no ongoing discretionary authority is pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The withdrawal operates by its own terms and is reversible by the President or Congress; there is no administration whose failure would leave responsibility unclear.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: A single land withdrawal under one statutory authority; nothing unrelated is bundled with it.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A land withdrawal establishes no ongoing compliance rule for any actor, so there is no enforcement mechanism to evaluate.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral relationship is created by removing land from entry; no actor is given an incentive to game.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The withdrawal is expressly 'temporary' and 'in aid of legislation' yet Sec. states it 'shall continue in force until revoked by the President or by act of Congress'—a temporary, classification-pending measure with a natural end-state but no reauthorization trigger, so it persists by default until affirmatively revoked.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order is made subject to all valid existing rights and existing withdrawals/reservations; no pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed or bypassed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No regulated party or compliance obligation exists, so no third-party enforcement interest is required.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A 'temporary' classification withdrawal covering approximately 2,500,000 acres that continues indefinitely until affirmatively revoked makes a large land status effectively durable, foreclosing settlement and entry well beyond any bounded classification period and shifting the default toward permanence.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency is implicated; the withdrawal simply removes described land from disposition.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population and no discretionary exemption structure; the acreage carve-out is a boundary description, not a waiver mechanism.

note: Largely self-executing land withdrawal, so compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11) and Flag 10 are NOT_APPLICABLE; but Flag 6 fires because the order labels itself 'temporary'/'in aid of legislation' yet grants no natural end (persists until revoked), and the resulting indefinite lock on ~2.5M acres supports Flag 9.

title: Directing the Director of the Office of Defense Transportation To Take Control of the Properties of the American Railroad Company of Porto Rico

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The ODT Director is directed to take immediate possession of all property, franchises, and assets and to operate the railroad 'in such manner as he deems necessary,' set employment terms, and make discretionary advances (Secs. 1-2, 4-5), with termination resting on presidential determination alone (Sec. 6)—sweeping, unchecked operational control concentrated in one official, the core of the order's design.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: One named official (the ODT Director) is responsible for the seizure and operation, with the President as the terminating authority; responsibility is clearly located even though the authority is broad.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose—wartime possession and operation of one railroad to keep supplies moving during a labor dispute; the labor-dispute resolution (NWLB) and seizure are integral to that one aim, not distinct bundled remedies.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Director may operate and manage 'in such manner as he deems necessary' and 'under such terms and conditions of employment as he deems advisable' (Secs. 1-2), and make advances 'in his discretion'—standards elastic enough that two officials could reach very different operating decisions in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The NWLB determination is made retroactive to the date of federal possession (Sec. 3), removing incentive to delay resolution; termination is tied to war-prosecution needs. Authority rests on active wartime statutes, not a lapsed one, so the Zombie Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Sec. 6 ties termination to the President's determination that possession is no longer required for successful prosecution of the war—an emergency-resolution end condition, satisfying the natural-end-state test for this exceptional-powers seizure.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 4 bars attachment, garnishment, or execution against the seized property absent the Director's prior written consent, disabling ordinary judicial process that creditors and the company would otherwise have against the assets.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The NWLB independently investigates and determines the labor dispute after hearing (Sec. 3), and insular/federal health, safety, and employment-standard laws remain applicable (Sec. 2), providing external actors with standing over the operation.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order establishes precedent for presidential seizure and discretionary operation of a private carrier—including insulating the seized assets from creditor process (Sec. 4)—an authority claim readily transposable to other industries and disputes beyond this one railroad.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The ODT Director consults the Secretary of the Interior and Puerto Rico's government (Sec. 8) and may request War Department protection (Sec. 7), and the NWLB keeps its labor role; the order allocates cooperating roles rather than countermanding any agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order imposes a one-time seizure on a single named company rather than a coverage regime across a population, so there is no exemption/waiver architecture to assess.

note: Wartime seizure of one named railroad under active war-powers statutes; the Zombie Trap does not fire (no acknowledged statutory lapse). Flag 11 is NOT_APPLICABLE (single-target seizure, no coverage population); Power Concentration is CRITICAL given the sweeping unchecked operational control that is the order's core design.

title: Amendment of Executive Order No. 10590, Establishing the President's Committee on Government Employment Policy

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The amendment only revises Committee membership (five to seven members, adding a Defense representative and a third public member) and quorum rules; it broadens a multi-member advisory body rather than pooling authority in any single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The amended Sec. 1 leaves a defined, presidentially-chaired Committee with specified members serving at the President's pleasure; responsibility remains clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: A single-provision membership amendment; nothing distinct is bundled with it.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The amended provision specifies exactly how each member is designated and the quorum required; the equal-employment complaint-review process it feeds (from EO 10590) continues under its own defined terms.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Broadening committee membership creates no incentive for any actor to undermine the non-discrimination policy; no lapsed statute is involved.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The underlying anti-discrimination employment policy is evergreen by nature (a rights mandate with no natural end-state), and this order is itself evidence the framework is maintained through ordinary amendment; the amended provision needs no reauthorization trigger.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The amendment adds members and does not remove or disable any oversight; the EO 10590 complaint-referral, Employment Policy Officer, and Committee-review mechanisms are untouched.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Assessed against the amended provision only: broadening membership does not alter the underlying scheme, under which complainants may request Committee review of their own cases (EO 10590 Sec. 3(a))—an external actor with standing and incentive.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Adding two members to an advisory committee produces no authority claim, definitional import, or precedent extending beyond the committee's composition.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The added Defense and Office of Defense Mobilization representatives are designated by their own agency heads to sit on a coordinating committee; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The membership amendment creates no exemption or waiver structure and does not carve anyone out of the equal-employment coverage established by EO 10590.

note: Partial amendment (diff-aware): predecessor EO 10590 was read; this order modifies only Sec. 1 membership/quorum, so only that provision is assessed against its baseline—the change enlarges an advisory body and touches none of EO 10590's oversight or complaint-review machinery, scoring ABSENT throughout.

title: Providing for the Most Effective Mobilization and Utilization of the National Manpower and Transferring the Selective Service System to the War Manpower Commission

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Selective Service System, its Director's functions, personnel, and funds are transferred to the WMC Chairman, who gains sweeping authority to issue binding rules, control all hiring/recruitment in designated establishments, bar employers from retaining needed workers (Secs. 2,5,7,8), and exercise these powers through anyone he determines—vast discretionary manpower authority pooled in one official with only self-initiated appeal machinery, the order's central design.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: While the Chairman is named, appeals from local/regional actions run back through the WMC itself (Sec. 7(c)) and inter-agency disputes are appealable 'to the President or to such agent or agency as the President may designate' (Sec. 9), so the body determining whether its own actions were unfair is largely the implementing authority—a diffuse, self-referential correction chain.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The distinct actions—reconstituting the WMC, absorbing Selective Service, and controlling civilian labor allocation—all serve the single stated frame of wartime manpower mobilization; they are integrated components, not unrelated remedies forced into one package.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Chairman designates which 'establishment, plant, facility, occupation, or area' is subject to hiring controls and which workers are 'more urgently needed' (Sec. 5) using undefined criteria, and takes 'all lawful and appropriate steps'—standards under which two officials could reach materially different enforcement decisions in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The design does not reward undermining mobilization; Sec. 7(b) directs the Chairman to relieve gross inequities and Sec. 7(c) provides hearings. Authority is expressly tied to the active, unexpired First War Powers Act (Sec. 13), not a lapsed statute, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Sec. 13 provides the order 'shall continue in force and effect until the termination of Title I of the First War Powers Act, 1941'—an explicit expiration tied to the underlying emergency statute, satisfying the natural-end-state test.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 12 amends all prior conflicting Executive Orders 'accordingly' and subordinates the Selective Service System and its boards to the Chairman's supervision and direction, folding the previously distinct Director of Selective Service's authority under the Chairman and thereby displacing the prior independent line of authority over that system.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement of the hiring/retention controls (Sec. 5) runs through the WMC and USES with no external actor given standing or access to surface an employer's or the agency's non-compliance; the built-in hearing (Sec. 7(c)) responds only to complaints from affected individuals, not to a third party positioned to police the system generally.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Consolidating Selective Service and civilian labor allocation under one chairman with power to freeze workers into jobs and channel all hiring through USES establishes a precedent for comprehensive federal control over the labor market that reaches well beyond immediate war-supply needs and would be hard to unwind.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order transfers the entire Selective Service System and its Director's functions to the WMC Chairman and directs every executive department to utilize its facilities and personnel as the Chairman determines (Sec. 9), redirecting agencies' statutory operations under a superior coordinator and countermanding the independent Selective Service line established by statute.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Coverage of the hiring/retention controls turns entirely on the Chairman's discretionary designation of which establishments, occupations, and areas are 'subject to the provisions of this section' (Sec. 5), so a single official controls, with undefined criteria, who is covered at all versus effectively exempt.

note: Major wartime manpower consolidation citing EO 9139 (predecessor read) and active First War Powers Act; the Zombie Trap does not fire because Sec. 13 ties authority to the statute's still-active term, not a lapse. Broad discretionary designation power drives Power Concentration to CRITICAL and fires multiple compliance-regime, cannibalization, and exemption flags.

title: Regulations Relating to the Safeguarding of Vessels, Harbors, Ports, and Waterfront Facilities of the United States

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Captain of the Port and Commandant hold sweeping discretionary power to prevent any person/article from boarding vessels (6.04-5), search and take possession of vessels (6.04-7,-8), and deny or revoke employment documents and Port Security Cards whenever 'satisfied' a person's 'character and habits of life' are 'inimical to the security of the United States' (6.10-1,-3,-5,-7)—an open-ended security standard whose determination rests with a single officer, the core mechanism of the order.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Exclusion and document-denial decisions turn on the same Coast Guard officer being 'satisfied,' and while an appeal exists (6.10-9), the appeal board is appointed by the Commandant and only recommends final action back to the Commandant—the authority that made the original determination also controls its review.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: All provisions—access control, document validation, dangerous-cargo handling, sabotage reporting—serve the single stated frame of safeguarding ports against sabotage/subversion; they are components of one coherent security scheme, not unrelated remedies packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The operative standard—whether a person's 'character and habits of life' make their presence 'inimical to the security of the United States' (6.10-1,-7)—is entirely subjective, so two officers could in good faith reach opposite exclusion decisions on the same person; this ill-defined trigger governs who may work on the waterfront and defeats consistent application at the order's core.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The scheme does not reward undermining port security; regulated parties gain nothing from non-compliance given seizure/forfeiture penalties (6.18-1). Authority rests on the active, unexpired 1917 Act as amended in 1950 and a current security finding, not a lapsed statute, so the Zombie Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The regulations rest on a presidential finding that 'the security of the United States is endangered by reason of subversive activity'—an emergency/exceptional-security condition with a natural resolution point—yet the order provides no expiration or reauthorization trigger, persisting until affirmatively revoked.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order establishes a new regulatory regime and adds an appeal mechanism (6.10-9) rather than removing any pre-existing external check; no prior oversight over waterfront security is disabled by its text.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Detection of sabotage/subversion depends on self-reporting: the master, owner, or operator must report and take precautions (6.16-1,-3), and enforcement flows through the Coast Guard; no external actor is given standing and access to independently surface non-compliance or wrongful exclusion beyond the affected individual's own appeal.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Excluding maritime workers on an open-ended 'character and habits of life'/'inimical to security' standard establishes a precedent for security-screening that bars individuals from an entire trade based on subjective loyalty judgments, an authority framework readily extendable to other workforces and durable well beyond any specific subversion threat.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Commandant consults the Secretary of Labor on credentials and appeal-board nominees (6.10-7,-9) and sabotage is reported to the FBI (6.16-1); agencies act in cooperating roles within their mandates rather than having any statutory mandate redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The Commandant may unilaterally 'designate categories of merchant vessels to which the foregoing shall not apply' and define which vessels/areas require credentials (6.10-1,-5), placing coverage carve-outs in a single officer's discretion with no external check or defined criteria on who is exempted.

note: Port-security regulations under an active 1917/1950 statute and a current subversion finding; no acknowledged statutory lapse, so the Zombie Trap does not fire. The subjective 'inimical to security'/'character and habits of life' standard drives Flags 1 and 4 to CRITICAL, with discretionary vessel-category exemptions firing Flag 11.

title: Abolishing Customs Collection District Number 44 (Iowa); Extending Limits of Customs Collection District Number 39 (Chicago) To Include the State of Iowa; and Revoking the Designations of Des Moines, Iowa, Oklahoma City, Oklahoma, and Tulsa, Oklahoma, as Customs Ports of Entry

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Self-executing reorganization under 19 U.S.C. sec. 2; no discretionary authority is pooled in any official, districts are merely consolidated and port designations revoked.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The order is a fixed field-organization change with no implementation chain that could fail; responsibility for customs administration is unchanged and clear.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: All four provisions serve one coherent purpose (consolidating customs field organization); nothing unrelated is packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time district/port reorganization creates no ongoing compliance rule for any actor to enforce.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral relationship is created; no actor is incentivized in any direction by a boundary change.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A self-executing structural change grants no ongoing authority that could be subject to reauthorization pressure.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is limited, bypassed, or removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing enforcement regime exists in which a third party would have standing to surface violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow customs-district reorganization with no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the Customs Service's own field organization is affected; no second agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population or exemption structure exists in a boundary/port-designation change.

note: Narrow, self-executing customs field-organization reorganization; compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11) and Flags 6,9 are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE per the concrete test, while decision-structure flags (1,2,7) and Flag 10 are answerable and clean (ABSENT).

title: Further Providing for the Administration of the Defense Production Act of 1950, as Amended

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Transfers all DPA references, records, and functions of the Defense Production Administrator/Administration to the Director of Defense Mobilization (revoking EO 10200 Sec. 1 which had created a Senate-confirmed Administrator), pooling defense-production authority under a single non-confirmed director without a commensurate external check.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is clearly and singularly vested in the Director of Defense Mobilization; the failure chain leads to a named actor rather than a diffuse committee.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: All provisions coherently reassign DPA administrative machinery to one office under a single stated purpose; nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order redesignates administrative authority; the underlying DPA priorities/allocations regime and its standards continue unchanged, so no new enforcement ambiguity is introduced.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Reassigning authority to the Director of Defense Mobilization creates no incentive for any actor to undermine the DPA's stated mobilization purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Continues a mobilization/defense-production authority tied to the Defense Production Act with no review, expiration, or reauthorization trigger; this emergency-adjacent authority has a natural end-state (mobilization needs) yet persists until revoked.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No existing oversight mechanism (judicial, IG, congressional reporting) is removed; the order only relocates administrative authority between executive officers.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The order is an internal reallocation of administrative functions; it does not itself establish a self-reporting enforcement regime lacking third-party standing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: By eliminating the separate Senate-confirmed Administrator and folding DPA authority into the Director of Defense Mobilization, the order sets a precedent for consolidating statutorily distinct positions into a single ex-officio office.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Redirects records, property, personnel, and funds of the Defense Production Administration to the Office of Defense Mobilization and makes the Director ex-officio Economic Stabilization Administrator (amending EO 10161 Sec. 401(a)), overriding the operational allocation among previously distinct agencies.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No carve-outs, waivers, or exemptions from coverage are created; the order does not exempt any actor from the DPA regime.

note: Diff-aware amendment: revokes EO 10200 (present in predecessors) which had created a Senate-confirmed Defense Production Administrator, consolidating that authority into the Director of Defense Mobilization; also amends EO 10161 Sec. 401(a). Partial-amendment/reorganization scoring applied to the specific mechanisms touched.

title: Designating Public International Organizations Entitled to Enjoy Certain Privileges Exemptions, and Immunities

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The President exercises a specific statutory designation power under the International Organizations Immunities Act; authority is exercised and exhausted in the act of designation, not pooled in an unchecked official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The designation is a completed act with no implementation chain that could fail; responsibility is clear.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Three organizations are designated under one identical statutory basis; this is parallel application, not bundling of distinct policy questions.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order confers statutory immunities on named organizations; it creates no ongoing compliance rule that an official must enforce against anyone.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A one-time immunities designation creates no ongoing behavioral incentive for any actor.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A self-executing designation grants no ongoing discretionary authority that could be subject to reauthorization pressure.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed; the order expressly preserves immunities acquired by treaty or Congressional action.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing enforcement regime is created in which a third party would surface violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A routine designation supplementing prior orders under a fixed statute, with no systemic or precedential implications beyond the named organizations.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No agency's statutory operational mandate is redirected or countermanded; the order only extends statutory immunities to organizations.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The immunities themselves are the statutory subject; the order creates no exemption structure carving actors out of a coverage rule of its own.

note: Supplements EO 9698 (present), 9751, and 9823 (9751/9823 not in referenced-predecessors, but this is an additive designation supplement, not a framework rewrite); 9698 confirms the same immunities-designation framework. Compliance-regime and Flags 6,9 NOT_APPLICABLE for a one-time designation; decision-structure flags clean.

title: Assigning Emergency Preparedness Functions to Federal Departments and Agencies

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Continuity-of-government activities are made subject to guidance and evaluation by the Director of the Office of Emergency Preparedness (Sec. 102(b)), and Sec. 3013 lets the OEP Director assign/transfer emergency functions among agencies, concentrating coordinating authority in one director; however Sec. 105/3011 bar actual effectuation absent separate statutory/presidential authority, limiting the concentration to planning.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Duties are distributed across 28 departments/agencies with heavy 'assist,' 'cooperate,' and 'coordinate' language and overlapping resource responsibilities; if preparedness planning fails, responsibility is diffuse across many actors with the OEP Director evaluating the same plans it guides.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order consolidates 21 Executive orders and 2 Defense Mobilization orders (Sec. 101, 3015) into one omnibus instrument spanning every cabinet department and independent agency; a reviewer cannot accept or reject any single agency's assignment without the whole package.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Agencies are directed to 'develop such plans and take such actions... as may be necessary' and attain 'an appropriate state of readiness' (Sec. 102) with no metrics, deadlines, or defined standard, so two officials could in good faith reach very different views of compliance.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The planning-only structure and the Sec. 105/3011 bar on effectuation limit any incentive to over-claim authority; no design feature rewards undermining preparedness. Invokes active national-emergency/preparedness statutes but acknowledges no statutory lapse, so the Zombie Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This emergency/national-survival preparedness framework grants standing planning authority indefinitely with only a self-guided readiness posture and no reauthorization or expiration trigger; emergency-powers subject matter is exactly the category the permanence carve-out does not cover.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 3014 expressly preserves existing statutory function assignments and Sec. 105/3011 preserve Congress's role in authorizing effectuation; no pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is inter-governmental planning coordination, not a regulatory regime over external parties; no self-reporting-dependent enforcement chain against a regulated party is created that would require third-party standing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Building standing, redelegable predelegation-of-emergency-authority and continuity-of-government machinery across the entire executive branch (Sec. 102, 3012) creates institutional precedent and infrastructure usable well beyond the stated nuclear-attack scenario.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order layers OEP-coordinated emergency functions across agencies with overlapping resource claims (e.g., Commerce/Interior/Agriculture production splits) and allows the OEP Director to transfer functions between agencies (Sec. 3013), producing parallel authority structures and jurisdictional friction, though Sec. 3014's savings clause softens outright mandate conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No waiver or carve-out structure lets covered agencies exempt themselves from their assignments; coverage is comprehensive rather than exemption-riddled.

note: Omnibus consolidation revoking ~23 prior orders (Sec. 3015) and assigning emergency-preparedness functions across 28 agencies; scored as an independent framework. The Sec. 105/3011 no-effectuation bar and Sec. 3014 savings clause materially moderate several flags (kept PRESENT not CRITICAL).

title: Protection and Enhancement of Environmental Quality

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Council on Environmental Quality is empowered to issue binding guidelines and 'such other instructions to agencies... as may be required' (Sec. 3(h),(i)) and to seek resolution of significant environmental issues, concentrating interpretive/directive authority over NEPA implementation in one council reporting only to the President.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each agency head self-monitors and self-assesses its own environmental performance (Sec. 2(a)) while CEQ advises; if agencies fail to meet national environmental goals, no actor with both obligation and correction capacity is clearly identified beyond the agency grading itself.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: All provisions coherently implement NEPA's stated purpose; the EO 11472 amendments in Sec. 4 are conforming redesignations tied to the same environmental-quality frame, not unrelated matter bundled for accept/reject leverage.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Agencies must act 'to the fullest practicable' extent and take measures 'needed to direct their policies' with no defined standard or trigger; two agency heads could in good faith reach very different compliance conclusions.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Because agencies self-assess, incentives could favor lax self-grading, but the order pairs this with public-disclosure and CEQ-review checks; no design feature affirmatively rewards undermining environmental goals to a degree that fires the flag.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This implements a permanent, evergreen statutory mandate (NEPA/environmental protection) with no natural end-state; per the permanence carve-out for evergreen policy mandates, absence of a sunset is not a design gap here.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds oversight machinery (public hearings, public information procedures, CEQ review, guidelines) rather than removing any pre-existing check; the EO 11472 amendments reassign advisory-body roles without stripping an external check. Direction is additive, so Flag 7 does not fire.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Sec. 2(b) mandates public information, hearings, and disclosure of alternatives, and Sec. 3(d) authorizes public hearings, giving interested outside parties access and standing-in-fact to surface agency non-compliance.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Empowering CEQ to issue government-wide NEPA guidelines (Sec. 3(h)) established durable environmental-review infrastructure whose precedential reach extends across essentially all federal action affecting the environment, well beyond any single program.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: CEQ coordinates and advises rather than redirecting other agencies' statutory operational mandates; the EO 11472 amendments reshuffle an advisory council's own membership/naming, not a competing operational mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No carve-out or waiver structure lets agencies exempt themselves from NEPA compliance; coverage applies to all federal agencies without an exemption swallowing the rule.

note: Implements NEPA and partially amends EO 11472 (present in predecessors) via conforming redesignations (Council -> Cabinet Committee on the Environment, etc.); the amendments are assessed against the specific provisions touched, not inherited wholesale. Flag 6 cleared under the evergreen-mandate carve-out; Flag 7 cleared because oversight is added, not removed.

title: Amending Executive Order 11157 as It Relates to a Basic Allowance for Quarters While on Sea Duty

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The amendment defines 'sea duty' by a bright-line three-month rule and strikes subsection (f) of Sec. 401 of EO 11157; it narrows rather than concentrates discretion, and no decision authority is pooled in an unchecked official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility for administering the quarters-allowance rule remains with the Secretaries concerned under the unchanged EO 11157 framework; the definitional change introduces no diffuse-blame structure.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order addresses a single definitional question (what counts as sea duty for the quarters allowance); nothing unrelated is packaged with it.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The specific mechanism amended is made more precise: duty under three months, and temporary duty with return to the same permanent station, are expressly excluded, reducing rather than creating enforcement ambiguity.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The three-month threshold closes a potential gaming avenue (claiming short assignments as sea duty); it does not create an incentive to undermine the allowance's purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This amends a standing military-pay administrative framework (EO 11157) maintained through ordinary supersession/amendment; per the standing-procedural-framework carve-out, absence of a sunset is not a design gap, and the amendment itself evidences periodic revision.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed; the order only edits a definition within an existing pay-regulation framework.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The ongoing eligibility determination for the quarters allowance is administered by the services; the narrow definitional edit does not remove any third-party check and no new self-reporting-only regime is created.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow military-pay definitional amendment with no systemic or precedential implications beyond its own allowance.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the military departments' own pay administration is affected; no second agency's statutory mandate is redirected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The three-month exclusions define coverage boundaries precisely rather than creating a discretionary waiver structure that swallows the rule.

note: Partial amendment of EO 11157 (present in predecessors): adds a definitional sentence to Sec. 401 Part IV and strikes subsection (f). Because an ongoing quarters-allowance eligibility relationship continues, compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11) are assessed on the merits (all clean), not waved off; only Flag 9 is NOT_APPLICABLE.

title: Establishing the Office of Defense Health and Welfare Services in the Executive Office of the President and Defining its Functions and Duties

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Creates the Office of Defense Health and Welfare Services headed by a single Director who discharges duties under presidential direction, may organize the office and appoint advisory committees, with authority concentrated in one director whose only check is the President himself (Secs. 2, 4, 7).

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: A single named Director is responsible and reports directly to the President (Sec. 3(d)); the failure chain leads to an identifiable actor rather than a diffuse committee.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order coherently establishes one coordinating office for defense health and welfare services; no unrelated policy questions are packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The office coordinates and advises rather than enforcing rules against regulated parties; its coordinating functions (Sec. 3) do not turn on an ambiguous enforcement standard applied to outside actors.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The coordinating/advisory design creates no incentive for any actor to undermine the provision of health and welfare services. Invokes the active May 27, 1941 national-emergency declaration but acknowledges no statutory lapse, so the Zombie Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The office is tied expressly to 'the period of the emergency' (Sec. 3(c)) yet contains no expiration or reauthorization trigger; an emergency-response body is exactly the category whose absent sunset the permanence carve-out does not excuse.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing external oversight mechanism is removed; the order stands up a new coordinating office and directs it to collaborate with existing agencies (Sec. 5).

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The office coordinates governmental and private health/welfare services rather than running a self-reporting compliance regime, so no third-party enforcement standing is definitionally foreclosed.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Establishing a new coordinating office inside the Executive Office of the President under the Office for Emergency Management extends the wartime pattern of building durable presidential-office machinery justified by an emergency, infrastructure that outlasts the immediate coordinating task.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The office is charged with coordinating health/welfare services 'made available by the departments and agencies of the Federal Government' and working through the Federal Security Agency and Office of Civilian Defense (Secs. 3, 5), layering a new central coordinator over agencies with existing mandates and creating potential jurisdictional friction, though its collaborative framing limits outright countermanding.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No waiver or carve-out structure is created; the order defines an office's functions, not a coverage rule with exemptions.

note: Standalone WWII-era order creating an emergency coordinating office in the EOP; no numbered predecessor amended. Emergency-tied permanence drives Flag 6 PRESENT; Flags 1, 9, 10 fire on the single-director/EOP-coordination architecture.

title: Exemption of Robert W. Shumate from Compulsory Retirement for Age

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The President exercises a specific, statute-bounded exemption power (5 U.S.C. sec. 715a) for one named individual; the authority is exercised and exhausted, not pooled in an unchecked official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: A one-time individual exemption with a fixed end date (January 1, 1938) has no implementation chain that could fail; responsibility is clear.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order addresses a single individual's retirement exemption; nothing is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time personal exemption creates no ongoing compliance rule for any actor to enforce.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral relationship is created by a single individual exemption.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The exemption is self-limiting with an explicit expiration (continued in service only until January 1, 1938), so the absence-of-sunset failure mode does not fire; there is in fact a built-in end date.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed; the President acts within the statute's own exemption proviso.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing enforcement regime exists in which a third party would surface violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A single-person administrative exemption with no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's statutory mandate is redirected; the order concerns one employee of the Public Health Service.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Although titled an 'exemption,' this is a single statutorily-authorized individual carve-out, not an exemption structure over a population of coverage; no ongoing coverage rule exists for exemptions to swallow.

note: Narrow one-time individual retirement exemption under an explicit statutory proviso with a built-in expiration date. Compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11) and Flag 9 are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE; decision-structure flags and Flag 6 (self-limiting) are answerable and clean.

title: Amending Executive Order No. 10422, as Amended, Prescribing Procedures for Making Available to the Secretary General of the United Nations Certain Information Concerning United States Citizens Employed or Being Considered for Employment on the Secretariat of the United Nations

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The amendment distributes investigative steps between the Secretary of State and the Civil Service Commission with defined National Agency Check references (revised Paras. 1-3); no unchecked concentration of authority is created, and prior EO 10422's Loyalty Board hearing structure is not enlarged into unchecked power.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is clearly assigned to named actors (Secretary of State, Civil Service Commission) at each investigative step; the amended procedure does not diffuse responsibility.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
