flags.justification: The order amends specific paragraphs of one investigation framework and refines one standard clause; no unrelated matters are packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The amended Part II(f) standard turns on 'knowing membership with the specific intent of furthering the aims of, or adherence to and active participation in' organizations that unlawfully advocate force or violence; despite tightening prior language, this associational/loyalty standard remains open to substantially divergent good-faith application by different investigators.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The procedure screens UN Secretariat applicants; it creates no incentive structure rewarding actors to undermine its purpose, and the Privacy Act conditioning tends to discipline over-collection.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This amends a standing administrative screening framework (EO 10422) maintained through ordinary supersession/amendment ('as amended'); per the standing-procedural-framework carve-out, absence of a sunset is not a design gap here.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The amendment adds Privacy Act (5 U.S.C. 552a) and 'other applicable law' conditioning to the investigations (revised Paras. 1-2) rather than removing any existing check; direction is additive, so Flag 7 does not fire.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The subject of an adverse determination retains hearing and appeal rights carried over from EO 10422's Loyalty Board structure, giving the affected individual standing and access to contest derogatory information; the amendment does not remove that check.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The changes are conforming refinements (Privacy Act compliance, updated NAC waiver conditions, tightened associational standard) to an existing screening procedure with no identifiable spillover precedent beyond this narrow UN-employment context.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order allocates defined roles between State and the Civil Service Commission consistent with their functions; no agency's statutory operational mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The revised Para. 3(b) NAC-waiver conditions are narrow, specific, and criteria-bounded (defined prior-employment/service categories), not a broad discretionary carve-out that swallows the screening requirement.

note: Partial amendment of EO 10422 (present in predecessors): revises investigation Paras. 1-3 (adding Privacy Act conditioning and NAC-waiver conditions) and tightens the Part II(f) associational standard. Diff-aware read shows the amendment adds oversight/precision; only the residual associational-standard vagueness (Flag 4) fires.

title: Delegation of Certain Functions of the President to the Secretary of Defense

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Delegates numerous presidential statutory authorities to the Secretary of Defense (and, by his designation, subordinate service secretaries) 'without the approval, ratification, or other action of the President' (Secs. 1-2), pooling and further redelegating discretionary authority in the Secretary with the President's own review affirmatively waived.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The delegated functions rest with the named Secretary of Defense and identified designees; responsibility for each exercised authority is traceable to a specific officer.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order packages many delegations, but all serve one coherent purpose (delegating routine presidential military-personnel functions under 3 U.S.C. 301); each is independently justified by that stated purpose, so this is enumeration, not accountability-obscuring bundling.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Each delegated function is tightly tied to a specific enumerated statute defining its scope (e.g., retirement approvals, appointment numbers); the standards governing exercise come from those statutes, not from an undefined EO standard.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Delegating administrative approvals to the Secretary of Defense creates no incentive for any actor to undermine the underlying personnel statutes' purposes.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The delegation of discretionary presidential authority is granted indefinitely with no review or reauthorization trigger and persists until revoked (Sec. 3 keeps prior actions in force until amended/revoked under this order); this ongoing discretionary grant has no built-in expiration pressure.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order expressly removes the President's own approval/ratification step for each delegated function ('without the approval, ratification, or other action of the President'), eliminating a pre-existing layer of presidential review over these determinations.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: These are internal executive personnel-approval functions, not a self-reporting compliance regime over regulated parties; no third-party enforcement standing is definitionally foreclosed.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: By authorizing the Secretary to act with presidential authority and to further designate subordinate secretaries, the order establishes and normalizes a redelegation precedent for exercising presidential power without presidential action across a broad set of military-personnel statutes.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Authority flows from the President to the Secretary of Defense within a single department; no other agency's existing statutory operational mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No waiver or carve-out structure over a population of coverage is created; the order delegates approval authorities, it does not exempt anyone from a coverage rule.

note: Standalone delegation order under 3 U.S.C. 301 (no numbered predecessor amended). The express waiver of presidential approval drives Flags 1, 7, and the redelegation precedent drives Flag 9; permanence of the discretionary delegation drives Flag 6.

title: Establishing the Office of Production Management in the Executive Office of the President and Defining Its Functions and Duties

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Broad defense-production authority (formulate/execute all measures, set priorities, mobilize facilities under Sec. 9 Selective Training Act) pools in OPM and its Director General; checked only by the President, who created and directs it (Sec. 2, 3). Not CRITICAL because War/Navy Secretaries sit on the body and a Priorities Board advises.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: OPM 'shall formulate and execute all measures needful' but no named actor is responsible if production coordination fails; the Priorities Board is advisory only and OPM reports upward only to the President who supervises it (Sec. 2a, 3, 5).

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent frame (increase defense production) with functionally related provisions; no distinct unrelated remedies forced together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: OPM determines 'when, to what extent, and in what manner priorities shall be accorded' and takes 'all lawful steps necessary,' with no defined standard governing how priority determinations or mandatory orders are applied to producers (Sec. 2d, 2g).

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No structural feature rewards behavior contrary to the stated purpose of expanding defense production; the Priorities Board is directed to weigh civilian impact (Sec. 5). Authority rests on an active declared national emergency and unexpired priorities statute, not a lapsed one.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Grants emergency defense-mobilization authority tied to the September 1939 national emergency, a condition with a natural end-state, yet contains no expiration or reauthorization trigger; continues until revoked.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Order adds a coordinating body and revokes a prior priorities delegation (EO 8572/8612) but does not remove any pre-existing external oversight of defense procurement; departments are 'utilized to the maximum extent' rather than stripped.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Compliance with priority/allocation orders depends on OPM's own directives and agency cooperation; no external party has standing and incentive to surface non-compliance with production priorities beyond the implementing structure itself (Sec. 2, 5).

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Vesting sweeping mobilization and priority-setting authority in a new EOP office establishes precedent for a centralized wartime production apparatus that reallocates industrial capacity and displaces civilian production well beyond any single program.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: OPM is directed to 'insure effective coordination' of and to 'advise/coordinate placement of' War, Navy, and other departments' defense purchasing and contracting, inserting a superordinate coordinator over agencies that hold their own procurement mandates, creating jurisdictional friction (Sec. 2a, 2c, 2i).

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No waiver or carve-out structure that would exempt actors from the order's coverage; priorities apply per the June 28, 1940 Act to all covered deliveries.

note: Revokes EO 8572 (as amended by 8612), both available in referenced-predecessors; the revoked orders were a narrow priorities-board delegation, so the revocation is cleanup and Flag 7 does not fire. Scored on this order's own substantial architecture (creation of OPM).

title: Prescribing Additional Regulations Governing Persons and Vessels in Defensive Sea Areas

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Rule is self-executing (a flat prohibition on cameras/film in defensive sea areas) with no discretionary decision authority pooled in any official; enforcement runs through ordinary criminal prosecution under section 44 of the Criminal Code (Sec. 4).

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is clearly assigned: the master/officer in charge of a vessel must take custody of prohibited devices (Sec. 2), and violators face prosecution (Sec. 4).

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single subject (photographic devices in defensive sea areas); no distinct unrelated provisions bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Trigger (possession of a camera/film-capable device in a defensive sea area), enforcer (prosecution under sec. 44), and consequence (criminal liability) are all specified with exceptions for official duty and authorized persons (Sec. 1, 4).

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No design feature rewards behavior contrary to the security purpose; masters are affirmatively obligated to safeguard devices. Authority rests on an active criminal statute (sec. 44) and wartime conditions, not a lapsed statute.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Wartime security restriction on civilian conduct tied to defensive sea areas has a natural end-state (the war/emergency) yet includes no expiration or review trigger; persists until revoked.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Adds a prohibition and custody duty but removes no pre-existing external oversight; enforcement remains in the ordinary criminal courts.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement does not depend on self-reporting; vessel masters have a mandatory custodial duty and violations are surfaced through criminal prosecution and the required posted notice (Sec. 2, 3, 4).

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow contraband-possession rule confined to designated defensive sea areas; no identifiable precedent or authority claim extending meaningfully beyond its stated purpose.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Duties fall on vessel masters and prosecutors; no second agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Exceptions are narrow and specific (official duty in national defense; authorization under the June 25, 1942 Act), not broad or discretionary enough to swallow the prohibition (Sec. 1).

note: Cleanly-drafted, self-executing wartime prohibition with a clear enforcement chain; only the absence of any sunset on emergency-linked authority fires a flag. Cited the June 25, 1942 Act (Public Law 627) but that is a statute, not an EO predecessor.

title: Designation of the Secretary of Transportation to Approve and Certificate Containers and Vehicles For Use in International Transport

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: All authority to approve and certificate containers/vehicles and to 'issue regulations' under the TIR and Container Conventions is designated to a single official, the Secretary of Transportation, with discretion over 'all actions he considers appropriate' and no external check named.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is clearly and singly assigned to the Secretary of Transportation; if certification fails, the responsible actor is identifiable.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single subject (certification of containers/vehicles under two related customs conventions); nothing distinct bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order applies 'the procedures and technical conditions set forth in the Annexes' but delegates open-ended authority to take 'all actions he considers appropriate' to carry out approval/certification, leaving the certification standard and its administration largely to the Secretary's discretion.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No design feature rewards conduct contrary to the purpose of certifying containers for sealed international transport; incorporates convention technical conditions.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Standing administrative certification function tied to ongoing treaty obligations (conventions 'as modified, amended, or otherwise supplemented from time to time') is evergreen by nature with no natural end-state; permanence carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Delegates a treaty-implementation function to a Secretary; removes no pre-existing external oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Certification of containers relies on the Secretary's approval process with no external actor having standing and incentive to surface improperly certified containers; the order further authorizes delegation of certification functions to 'nonprofit firms or associations,' compounding reliance on the certifying parties themselves.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Narrow treaty-implementation designation for container certification; no identifiable precedent or authority claim extending meaningfully beyond the stated customs-transport purpose.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the Secretary of Transportation is implicated; no second agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No waiver or carve-out structure exempting actors from certification coverage; fee schedule and delegation authority are administrative, not exemptions.

note: Cites two customs conventions (treaties), not EO predecessors, so no diff comparison applies. Ongoing certification regulatory relationship exists (containers must be approved/certified), so Flags 4, 5, 8, 11 are applicable and scored on the merits.

title: Establishing the President's Committee on Manpower

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Creates an advisory committee that only assesses, evaluates, and recommends (Sec. 2); no decision, eligibility, or enforcement authority is pooled in any body, and it is chaired by the Secretary of Labor within a multi-agency membership.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Committee's role is purely advisory to the Secretary of Labor and other officials; statutory responsibility for manpower appraisal remains with the Secretary under the MDTA, so no responsibility is diffused.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (assess and coordinate national manpower assessment); recitals and functions all serve that one frame.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Committee has no enforcement function to be vague about; it makes recommendations only, so there is no compliance standard applied to any regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: An advisory coordination body creates no incentive rewarding conduct contrary to its purpose; membership is by office. Authority rests on the active MDTA, no lapsed statute.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Establishes a standing advisory committee exercising a delegated coordination function with no expiration, review, or reauthorization trigger; it continues until revoked and lacks the explicit termination mechanism that would clear this flag.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Adds an advisory body; Sec. 4 expressly preserves all functions vested by law in any agency, removing no pre-existing oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The Committee imposes no compliance obligation requiring external enforcement; it advises, so there are no violations for a third party to surface.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A recommendation-only coordinating committee produces no identifiable authority-claim precedent or definitional import extending beyond its manpower-assessment purpose.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Membership spans many agencies but Sec. 4 states nothing shall abrogate, modify, or restrict any function vested by law in any agency; the Committee coordinates rather than redirecting or countermanding statutory mandates.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage rule with exemptions; the order creates an advisory body, not a regime from which actors are carved out.

note: Advisory-committee order with an explicit non-abrogation clause (Sec. 4); nearly all flags clear ABSENT, only the absence of any sunset/termination on a standing delegated committee fires. No EO predecessors cited (only statutes).

title: Abolishing Certain Committees on Government Organization and Management Improvement

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Order abolishes two advisory committees and transfers only their orderly-termination functions to the Director of the Bureau of the Budget (Sec. 1-2); no ongoing decision or enforcement authority is pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Termination responsibility is clearly assigned to the Director of the Bureau of the Budget, who takes 'all necessary steps' for winding up affairs (Sec. 2).

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent action (abolishing two related government-organization advisory committees and revoking/amending their establishing orders); no unrelated remedies bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time abolition and wind-up of advisory committees creates no ongoing behavioral rule for any actor to comply with, so vague-enforcement has nothing to attach to.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: One-time dissolution creates no ongoing regulatory relationship or incentive for any actor class going forward.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Self-executing, one-time abolition grants no continuing authority that could be subject to sunset or reauthorization.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The abolished bodies were advisory committees, not oversight mechanisms; removing them does not eliminate any external check on implementation (judicial, IG, congressional). Diff against EO 10432 and Sec. 3 of EO 10072 (both available) confirms they only advised on organization/management, exercising no oversight function.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance obligation is created, so there is no enforcement chain in which a third party would have standing to surface violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Abolishing two internal advisory committees produces no identifiable precedent or authority claim extending beyond the housekeeping act itself.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only wind-up functions move to the Bureau of the Budget, which already housed related management-improvement functions (EO 10072); no agency's operative statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage regime exists from which actors could be exempted; the order only dissolves advisory bodies.

note: One-time abolition/wind-up of two advisory committees; per the compliance-regime rule, Flags 4, 5, 8, 11 and Flag 6 are NOT_APPLICABLE (no ongoing regulatory relationship or continuing authority). Predecessors EO 10432 (revoked) and EO 10072 (Sec. 3 deleted) were read from referenced-predecessors and confirm the abolished bodies were advisory only, so Flag 7 clears ABSENT.

title: Establishing the Surplus War Property Administration

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A single Surplus War Property Administrator, appointed by the Director of War Mobilization, has 'general supervision and direction' over the handling/disposition of all surplus war property government-wide and may prescribe binding regulations (Sec. 1, 2, 4); the only external check is the Director who appoints him. Not CRITICAL because a multi-agency Policy Board assists and disposition follows assigned channels.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Administrator directs and other agencies must comply, but the Policy Board is only assistive and the Administrator answers only to the Director of War Mobilization who appointed him; if surplus disposition goes wrong, no external actor has both obligation and capacity to correct it (Sec. 1, 2, 4).

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent frame (centralized disposition of surplus war property); provisions all serve that purpose.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Administrator may 'prescribe regulations and issue directions necessary to effectuate the purposes' and no agency may act 'in contravention thereof,' with disposition assigned 'so far as it is deemed feasible by the Administration' — an open, discretion-laden standard governing what compliance requires (Sec. 2c, 4).

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No design feature structurally rewards conduct contrary to orderly surplus disposition. Authority rests on the active First War Powers Act, 1941 and wartime commander-in-chief powers, not a lapsed statute.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: War-mobilization authority over surplus war property has a natural end-state (the war/demobilization) yet the order sets no expiration, review, or reauthorization trigger; it persists until revoked.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 3 provides that disposition powers 'heretofore conferred by law on any Government agency' may be exercised by the Administration, and no agency may dispose of property in contravention of the Administrator's regulations (Sec. 4), overriding agencies' own statutory control over their property.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Compliance depends on agencies submitting reports 'as the Administrator shall direct' and on the Administration's own directives; no external actor has standing and incentive to surface improper surplus disposition (Sec. 4).

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Concentrating override authority to exercise disposition powers 'heretofore conferred by law on any Government agency' (Sec. 3) establishes precedent for a central body superseding statutory property authority across the entire government, reaching well beyond surplus war goods.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Sec. 3 lets the Administration exercise disposition/transfer powers 'conferred by law on any Government agency' and Sec. 4 forbids agencies from disposing of property contrary to its regulations, directly overriding and redirecting the statutory property mandates of numerous named agencies (Treasury, RFC, Maritime Commission, War Food Admin, etc.) — a core lateral reassignment of authority that defines the order.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The Administrator may act 'unless otherwise directed by the Director of War Mobilization' and disposition proceeds only 'so far as it is deemed feasible by the Administration,' giving concentrated discretion to exempt property or channels from the default disposition scheme with no external check (Sec. 2c).

note: Amends 'all prior Executive Orders insofar as in conflict' (Sec. 7) generically rather than naming a specific predecessor, so no single diff comparison applies. Flag 10 scored CRITICAL because assuming disposition powers 'conferred by law on any Government agency' is the order's central mechanism and directly overrides multiple agencies' statutory mandates.

title: Blocking Iraqi Government Property and Prohibiting Transactions with Iraq

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Sec. 4 authorizes the Secretary of the Treasury to 'take such actions, including the promulgation of rules and regulations, as may be necessary,' to define prohibited transactions and redelegate at will; broad IEEPA sanctions authority is concentrated in one official with the President as the only check. Not CRITICAL because it operates within the IEEPA/NEA statutory framework requiring State consultation.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is clearly assigned to the Secretary of the Treasury in consultation with the Secretary of State (Sec. 4); the accountable actor is identifiable.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent frame (national-emergency sanctions response to Iraqi threat); the enumerated prohibitions all serve the one blocking purpose.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Prohibitions turn on undefined breadth — e.g., any transaction 'which evades or avoids, or has the purpose of evading or avoiding' the order (Sec. 2h) and 'controlled entities' directly or indirectly owned by Iraq — leaving substantial discretion over what conduct is covered pending regulations that 'may hereafter be issued' (Sec. 2, 4).

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No design feature rewards conduct contrary to the sanctions purpose. Zombie trap does not fire: the order declares a fresh, active national emergency under IEEPA/NEA — an unexpired declaration, not authority continued past a statutory lapse.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Emergency sanctions authority has a natural end-state (resolution of the Iraqi threat), and while the NEA imposes an external annual-renewal regime, the order's own text sets no expiration or review trigger and is 'effective immediately' with no termination condition (Sec. 3).

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order directs transmission to Congress and Federal Register publication (per NEA) and removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; it adds sanctions rather than stripping checks.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement of the transaction prohibitions depends on U.S. persons' compliance and Treasury/OFAC detection; no external party with independent standing and incentive is created to surface evasive transactions, which are numerous and easily concealed (Sec. 2).

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The sweeping IEEPA blocking and the 'evades or avoids' catch-all (Sec. 2h) establish a broad sanctions template and precedent for blocking foreign-government assets and criminalizing indirect transactions, applicable well beyond the immediate Iraq context.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Directs all agencies to 'take all appropriate measures within their authority' and centralizes implementation in Treasury with State consultation; it coordinates rather than redirecting or countermanding any agency's conflicting statutory mandate (Sec. 4).

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Prohibitions apply 'except to the extent provided in regulations which may hereafter be issued' (Sec. 2), giving Treasury open-ended discretion to license/exempt transactions from the blocking regime with criteria left to future regulation, concentrated in a single actor.

note: IEEPA/NEA sanctions order declaring a fresh, active national emergency; Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire because no statutory lapse is acknowledged. Cites statutes only, no EO predecessor to diff against.

title: Consultations on the Admission of Refugees

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Delegates consultation and reporting functions across multiple officials (Secretary of State, Attorney General, U.S. Coordinator for Refugee Affairs) while expressly reserving the substantive determinations — specifying refugee circumstances and fixing admission numbers — to the President (Sec. 1-103); authority is distributed, not pooled.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each delegated function is assigned to a named official and coordinated with specified others (Sec. 1-101, 1-102); responsibility for congressional consultation and reporting is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (allocating refugee-consultation functions under the Refugee Act/INA); no distinct unrelated remedies bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The delegated functions are precisely tied to specific INA sections (e.g., 207(a),(b),(d),(e)); this is an internal allocation of statutory consultation duties, not a compliance standard applied ambiguously to regulated parties.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No design feature rewards conduct contrary to the consultation purpose; requires consultation with named committees and officials. Rests on the active Refugee Act of 1980 and INA, no lapsed statute.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A standing delegation implementing permanent statutory consultation duties under the Refugee Act/INA is evergreen by nature (an ongoing administrative function), with no natural end-state; permanence carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order strengthens congressional oversight by mandating consultation with and reporting to the Judiciary Committees (Sec. 1-101, 1-102); it adds checks rather than removing any pre-existing oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The congressional Judiciary Committees are given a defined consultation and information-receipt role, providing an external actor with standing and incentive in the refugee-admission process (Sec. 1-102); no self-reporting-only gap.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: An internal allocation of refugee-consultation functions with reserved presidential determinations produces no identifiable precedent or authority claim extending meaningfully beyond the Refugee Act framework.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Functions are allocated to State, Justice, HEW, and the Refugee Coordinator by delegation within statutory bounds and require consultation among them (Sec. 1-102); no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded into conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage rule with exemptions; the order allocates consultation duties and reserves determinations to the President, carving no actor out of a regime.

note: Clean delegation order that distributes functions, reserves key determinations to the President, and mandates congressional consultation/reporting — scores ABSENT across all eleven flags. Cites statutes only, no EO predecessor to diff against.

title: Delegating Authority of the President Under Section 4102(a)(2)(B) of Title 5, United States Code, to Designate U.S. Marshals and U.S. Attorneys for Training

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Delegates a narrow, specific statutory function (designating presidentially-appointed U.S. Attorneys and Marshals for training under Chapter 41) to the Attorney General; a bounded administrative delegation, not a pooling of discretionary authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is clearly and singly assigned to the Attorney General; the accountable actor is identifiable.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single, narrow subject (training designation); nothing bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-line delegation of a training-designation function creates no ongoing behavioral rule or enforcement standard applied to any regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory or compliance relationship is created; the delegation simply lets the AG designate officials for training, with no incentive structure to assess.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Delegates a standing statutory administrative function (training designation) with no natural end-state; this is an evergreen procedural delegation, not emergency or temporary authority, so the permanence carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: A bare delegation of a training-designation power; removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance obligation is created, so there is no enforcement chain in which a third party would have standing to surface violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow training-designation delegation carries no identifiable precedent or authority claim beyond its stated administrative purpose.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the Attorney General is implicated; no second agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage regime exists from which actors could be exempted; the order designates officials for training only.

note: Minimal single-function delegation to the Attorney General; per the compliance-regime rule, Flags 4, 5, 8, 11 are NOT_APPLICABLE (no ongoing regulatory relationship), and the remaining decision-structure/procedural flags all clear ABSENT. No EO predecessor cited (Title 5 / Title 3 statutes only).

title: Delegation of Certain Functions of the President to the Housing and Home Finance Administrator

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Housing and Home Finance Administrator is empowered to perform the President's functions under section 611 of the 1940 defense-housing act 'without the approval, ratification, or other action by the President,' vesting an unreviewed presidential function in a single official with no external check named.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is clearly and singly assigned to the Housing and Home Finance Administrator; the accountable actor is identifiable.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single, narrow subject (delegating one statutory function); nothing bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order delegates a specific statutory function (section 611) but creates no ongoing behavioral rule or enforcement standard applied to regulated parties; its content is a bare transfer of a presidential function.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory or compliance relationship is created by the delegation itself; there is no incentive structure to assess in the order's text.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Delegates a standing statutory administrative function under the housing act with no natural end-state; an evergreen procedural delegation, so the permanence carve-out applies rather than firing the absence-of-sunset flag.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The delegation authorizes the Administrator to act 'without the approval, ratification, or other action by the President,' removing the presidential-approval check that would otherwise attend exercise of this presidential function.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The delegation creates no compliance obligation, so there is no enforcement chain in which a third party would have standing to surface violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow delegation of one housing-statute function carries no identifiable precedent or authority claim extending beyond its stated purpose.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the Housing and Home Finance Administrator is implicated; no second agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage regime exists from which actors could be exempted; the order delegates a single presidential function.

note: Bare single-function delegation; the 'without approval, ratification, or other action by the President' clause fires Flag 1 (unreviewed authority in one official) and Flag 7 (removal of the presidential-approval check). Compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11) are NOT_APPLICABLE. No EO predecessor cited (statutes/Title 3 only).

title: Termination and Liquidation of the Electric Home and Farm Authority

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A self-executing dissolution and transfer of assets/personnel to the RFC per statute; no discretionary decision authority is pooled in any single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The action is mechanical (cease to exist, transfer assets, pay surplus to Treasury) with the RFC named as the transferee; no failure chain with diffuse responsibility arises.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (terminate one agency and liquidate it); nothing unrelated is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time liquidation creates no ongoing compliance rule for any party to enforce.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral regime is established, so no actor-incentive structure exists to assess.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing termination has no ongoing grant of authority to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is excluded or bypassed; dissolution proceeds under D.C. law.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing enforcement relationship exists over which a third party would need standing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A routine agency wind-down under existing statutory authority establishes no precedent or definitional framework usable in unrelated contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The RFC absorbs the terminated agency's residual functions cleanly by statute; no conflicting mandate or jurisdictional friction is created.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population or exemption structure exists in a pure liquidation order.

note: One-time agency termination/liquidation with no ongoing regulatory relationship; compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11) and Flag 6 are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE per the concrete-test rule, decision-structure flags scored ABSENT.

title: Defining Noncombatant Service and Noncombatant Training

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order prescribes fixed definitions for Selective Service Act sec. 6(j); it vests no discretionary authority in any single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Definitional rule with no implementation-failure chain; responsibility for classification remains within the existing Selective Service apparatus.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Two closely related definitions serving a single statutory purpose (defining noncombatant categories); nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The catch-all in 1(c) — 'any other assignment the primary function of which does not require the use of arms in combat' — is open-textured enough that two officials could classify the same assignment differently in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Definitions of noncombatant status create no reward for undermining the statute's purpose; the 'acceptable to the individual' proviso protects rather than games the framework.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: These are standing statutory-definition rules for an evergreen conscription-administration function, maintained by ordinary supersession rather than reauthorization; the permanence carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds definitional content under sec. 6(j); it removes no pre-existing judicial, administrative, or reporting check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Classification runs through the Selective Service system where boards and registrants both surface disputes; enforcement does not depend solely on regulated-party self-report.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow statutory definition bounded to sec. 6(j) conscientious-objector service; no precedent or definitional import usable in unrelated domains is identifiable.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the armed forces/Selective Service framework is implicated; no second agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The definitions set who qualifies for noncombatant status under clear categories; no broad discretionary waiver structure swallows the rule.

note: Definitional order establishing an ongoing classification standard, so compliance-regime flags apply; only the open-ended 1(c) catch-all fires Flag 4.

title: Authorizing the Secretary of War to Take Possession of and Operate the Plants and Facilities of the United States Rubber Company, Located in Or Around Detroit, Michigan

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: All operating authority over the seized plants is pooled in the Secretary of War, who may 'do any other thing that he may deem necessary' and 'take any other steps that he deems necessary,' with no external check on his discretion — a core concentration defining the order's operative machinery.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary both operates the plants and determines when the terminating condition (restored productive efficiency) is met, so the actor exercising the power also judges its cessation.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose — seize and operate one company's plants to prevent a wartime production interruption; nothing unrelated is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The operative standard ('any manner that he deems necessary,' 'necessary or desirable') is defined by the Secretary's own judgment, so what compliance/operation requires is left almost wholly to his discretion.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Sec. 2 preserves existing terms of employment and Sec. 3 keeps existing management, and the 60-day termination trigger ties continuation to restored efficiency, so the design does not reward prolonging the seizure; authority rests on an active, unexpired wartime statute, not a lapsed one.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Sec. 5 supplies an explicit termination mechanism — possession must end within 60 days after productive efficiency is determined restored — tying this exceptional-powers grant to a resolution condition.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order directs cooperation of other agencies but removes no pre-existing external check; it operates subject to Section 5 of the War Labor Disputes Act.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: A government-run seizure has no regulated-party self-reporting problem; the operating agency and cooperating agencies (including FBI investigation) directly surface any operational issue.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The seizure asserts sweeping executive authority to take and run private industry over a labor dispute, a precedent extensible to other plants and disputes beyond this one company.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Secretary of War is directed to operate an industrial facility and to command cooperation from the War Manpower Commission, Selective Service, and DOJ/FBI, redirecting those agencies' effort toward a function outside the War Department's ordinary mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order covers one named company's plants with no waiver or carve-out structure that could swallow its coverage.

note: Wartime plant seizure under an active (unexpired) Selective Training and Service Act as amended by the War Labor Disputes Act; broad 'deems necessary' discretion drives Flags 1/2/4 but the explicit 60-day termination trigger clears Flag 6 and no statutory lapse means the zombie trap does not fire.

title: Transferring Certain Lands From the Secretary of Agriculture to the Secretary of the Interior and Reserving Them as a Part of the Necedah National Wildlife Refuge

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A self-executing one-acre transfer between two named secretaries under statute; no discretionary decision authority is pooled anywhere.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The transfer is complete on its own terms with no implementation-failure chain to trace.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single action — transfer one described parcel and add it to an existing refuge; nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time land transfer creates no ongoing compliance rule to enforce.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral regime is created, so there is no actor-incentive structure to assess.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing transfer/reservation has no ongoing grant of authority to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is excluded or limited; the transfer is 'subject to valid rights.'

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing enforcement relationship exists over which a third party would need standing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A one-acre boundary addition to an existing refuge under settled statutory authority (Bankhead-Jones sec. 32) produces no identifiable precedent or effect beyond its subject.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Interior receives the parcel on Agriculture's recommendation; the predecessors (EO 7908/8065) confirm this is an ordinary, agreed transfer, not a countermanding of either mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population or exemption structure exists in a land-transfer order.

note: One-time inter-secretary land transfer; predecessors EO 7908 (transfer to Agriculture) and EO 8065 (establishing the refuge) confirm a clean, agreed reallocation, so Flag 10 is ABSENT and compliance-regime flags are NOT_APPLICABLE.

title: Extension of Trust Periods on Indian Lands Expiring During the Calendar Year 1951

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A self-executing, across-the-board 25-year extension of trust periods; it vests no discretionary decision authority in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The extension operates automatically by its terms with no implementation-failure chain requiring a responsible actor.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose — extend expiring 1951 trust periods; nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A blanket status extension creates no ongoing compliance obligation for any party to enforce.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule is established, so no actor-incentive structure exists to assess.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The extension is itself time-bounded — a fixed 25-year term from each patent's otherwise-expiration date — so it does not create open-ended permanent authority.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order expressly preserves cases where Congress reserved extension authority to itself, removing no pre-existing check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing enforcement relationship is created over which a third party would need standing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A routine annual trust-period extension under longstanding statutory authority produces no identifiable precedent or effect beyond preserving existing restrictions.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order expressly carves out congressional reservations and implicates only Interior's ordinary trust administration; no second agency's mandate is redirected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The congressional-reservation carve-out defines statutory scope, not a discretionary waiver structure; no ongoing coverage regime exists to be swallowed.

note: Self-executing, fixed-term status extension with a clean statutory carve-out; compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11) NOT_APPLICABLE as no ongoing conduct rule is created, and Flag 6 is ABSENT because the extension is itself time-bounded.

title: Suspension of the Eight-Hour Law as to Laborers and Mechanics Employed by the Department of Defense on Public Work Essential to the National Defense

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The President himself suspends the eight-hour limit by the order's terms; no open-ended discretionary authority is delegated to a single subordinate official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The suspension operates by its own terms with a wage/overtime proviso; there is no implementation-failure chain lacking a responsible actor.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose — suspend the eight-hour daily limit for DoD defense public work during the emergency; nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The scope turns on work 'essential to the national defense,' an undefined standard leaving which projects qualify to varying good-faith reading; note that predecessor EO 9898 by contrast required a specific Secretary finding tied to enumerated categories, so this order loosens the qualifying trigger.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The mandatory time-and-a-half overtime proviso removes any incentive to over-schedule labor for cost savings; authority rests on an active, unexpired national emergency (Proc. 2914), so the zombie sub-flag does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The suspension runs 'for the duration of the national emergency' — an emergency-tied exceptional-powers grant that should have a resolution condition, but unlike superseded EO 9898 (fixed July 1, 1948 date) it swaps a hard sunset for open-ended duration lasting until the emergency is terminated.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order supersedes an earlier eight-hour suspension order but removes no external oversight mechanism; it operates within the statute's own extraordinary-emergency exception.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The overtime-pay proviso is enforceable by the affected employees themselves through ordinary wage channels, so enforcement does not rest solely on agency self-report.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Tying a labor-hour suspension to the open-ended 1950 national emergency normalizes emergency-duration suspension of protective labor limits, a framework extensible as long as the emergency persists.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the Department of Defense's own labor is affected; no other agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order is itself a suspension of a statutory limit rather than a coverage rule with carve-outs; no exemption structure swallows any rule.

note: Supersedes EO 9898 (and its extension chain 9926/9974/10064/10135); diff-aware comparison shows this order replaces 9898's hard July-1-1948 sunset and specific-finding trigger with an open-ended 'duration of the emergency' suspension and looser 'essential to national defense' scope, driving Flags 4/6. Emergency is active/unexpired, so no zombie trap.

title: Authorizing the Petroleum Administrator to Take the Possession of and to the Cities Service Refining Corporation, Located in and Around Lake Charles, Louisiana

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: All operating authority over the seized refinery is pooled in the Petroleum Administrator, who may 'do any other things that he may deem necessary' and 'take any other steps that he deems necessary,' with delegation to a Deputy but no external check — a core concentration of unreviewable discretion.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Administrator both operates the plant and determines when possession 'is no longer required for the successful prosecution of the war,' so the actor wielding the power also judges its cessation.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose — seize and operate one refinery to end a wartime production interruption; nothing unrelated is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The operating standard is whatever the Administrator 'may deem necessary,' leaving what the seizure requires almost entirely to his discretion.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Sec. 4 preserves existing management and Sec. 5 caps possession at 60 days after restored efficiency, so the design does not reward prolonging control; authority rests on an active, unexpired wartime statute, not a lapsed one.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Sec. 5 supplies an explicit termination trigger — possession ends when no longer required and in no event more than 60 days after restored productive efficiency — tying this exceptional grant to a resolution condition.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order directs other agencies to cooperate but removes no pre-existing external check; it requires operation 'in accordance with applicable laws.'

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: A government-run seizure has no regulated-party self-reporting gap; the operating agency and cooperating agencies directly surface any operational issue.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The seizure asserts broad executive power to take and run private industry over a labor dispute, a precedent extensible to other facilities and disputes beyond this one refinery.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Petroleum Administrator is directed to operate an industrial facility and to command cooperation from the National War Labor Board, War Manpower Commission, Selective Service, DOJ, and the Secretary of War, redirecting their effort toward a function outside the Administrator's ordinary mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order covers one named refinery with no waiver or carve-out structure that could swallow its coverage.

note: Wartime refinery seizure paralleling order-273; broad 'deems necessary' discretion plus Sec. 6 delegation to a Deputy drive Flags 1/2/4, while the explicit 60-day termination trigger clears Flag 6 and the active (unexpired) wartime statute means no zombie trap.

title: Providing for the Stabilizing of the National Economy

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Economic Stabilization Director is empowered to 'formulate and develop a comprehensive national economic policy' over prices, rents, wages, salaries, profits, rationing, and subsidies and to 'issue directives on policy' binding all federal departments, with a Board only to 'advise and consult' — sweeping economy-wide authority pooled in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Title VI(5) makes the Director's decisions on delegation and manner of exercise 'final,' and the advisory Board has no corrective power, so no external actor can hold the Director responsible for stabilization failures.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The single 'stabilize the economy' frame bundles substantively distinct regimes — wage/salary control, agricultural price control, profit limits, subsidies, and a $25,000 net-salary cap — into one order where the distinct policy questions cannot be separated for independent evaluation.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Standards like approving wage increases to 'correct maladjustments or inequalities,' 'gross inequities,' or to 'aid in the effective prosecution of the war' are open-textured enough that officials could reach materially different determinations in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The design channels wage/price decisions through review boards with defined baselines (Sept. 15, 1942 levels); no actor is rewarded for undermining stabilization, and authority rests on an active statute (Act of Oct. 2, 1942), not a lapsed one.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This sweeping economy-wide control regime is emergency/wartime authority with a natural end-state (the war and inflationary emergency) yet contains no expiration or reauthorization mechanism, continuing until revoked.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order suspends inconsistent provisions of the Emergency Price Control Act (Title II(9); Title IV(4)) and makes the Director's delegation decisions 'final,' displacing statutory checks that otherwise applied to these price/wage functions.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement runs through the War Labor Board's approval process and the disregard of noncompliant wages for tax/contract purposes (Title III(4)), giving employees, competitors, and revenue officials standing and incentive to surface violations beyond self-report.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Concentrating directive authority over the entire civilian economy in a single presidential appointee establishes a precedent for economy-wide executive control extensible well beyond the immediate anti-inflation purpose.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Director may issue binding 'directives on policy' overriding the ordinary authority of the Treasury, Agriculture, Commerce, the Price Administrator, RFC, and others, subordinating their statutory functions to his central direction and creating parallel-authority friction.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The War Labor Board may 'by general regulation make such exemptions' from the wage-control title as it deems necessary (Title II(4)), and the Director controls exemptions from the salary cap — broad discretionary carve-out authority concentrated in the implementing bodies.

note: Sweeping economy-wide stabilization order; the Director's binding directive power plus 'final' delegation drive CRITICAL Flag 1, and the breadth triggers nine of eleven flags. References active EO 9017 (War Labor Board, read from predecessors) which continues to operate, so this is partial extension of that board's role, not a lapsed-statute case.

title: Establishing Naval Defensive Sea Areas Around and Naval Airspace Reservations Over the Islands of Rose, Tutuila, and Guam

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Navy holds sole discretion to authorize entry into the defensive sea areas and airspace and to 'prescribe such regulations as may be necessary,' with no external check on those access determinations.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Navy is expressly named as the enforcing actor ('shall be enforced by the Secretary of the Navy'), so responsibility for implementation is clearly assigned.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: A single coherent purpose — reserve defensive sea areas and overlying airspace around three islands for national defense; the parallel sea/air provisions serve one frame.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The rule is a clear bright line — no non-public vessel or aircraft may enter without Navy authorization — and consequences are the specified statutory criminal penalties, leaving little room for divergent good-faith enforcement.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A flat entry prohibition backed by criminal penalties creates no reward for undermining the order's protective purpose; authority rests on active statutes, not a lapsed one.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The reservations are national-defense/exceptional-security measures established with no expiration or reauthorization mechanism, continuing until revoked despite subject matter (defensive reservations) that would ordinarily have a resolution condition.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order establishes new access controls under existing statutory authority; it removes no pre-existing judicial, administrative, or reporting check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement does not rely on regulated-party self-report — the Navy plus 'local law enforcement officers' actively police the areas, and criminal penalties give prosecutors standing to surface violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Reserving territorial waters and airspace as exclusion zones enforced by criminal penalty asserts a template for expansive defensive-zone controls over civilian navigation extensible to other coastal areas.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order directs cooperation of local federal law-enforcement officers with the Navy but redirects no agency's statutory mandate into conflict; roles are complementary.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Entry is barred to all but public U.S. vessels/aircraft 'unless authorized by the Secretary of the Navy' — an exemption power vested entirely in a single official with no defined criteria bounding when authorization is granted.

note: Establishes an ongoing access-control regime with criminal penalties, so compliance-regime flags apply; single-official discretionary authorization drives Flags 1 and 11, and the permanent defensive reservation with no end-condition fires Flag 6.

title: Preservation of Endangered Species

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Interior is designated the sole Management Authority for CITES and chairs and staffs the Scientific Authority, concentrating both the treaty-communication and administrative-support roles in one department, though tempered by the multi-agency Scientific Authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Roles are specifically assigned — Interior as Management Authority, a named inter-agency body as Scientific Authority — with clear designation of who is responsible for each treaty function.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose — implement CITES and the Western Hemisphere Convention by designating authorities and a permit system; nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order directs a system 'to standardize and simplify the requirements, procedures and other activities related to the issuance of permits' but leaves the substantive permit standards undefined, so the operative issuance criteria are not specified in the text.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Designating scientific and management authorities to gatekeep trade permits creates no reward for undermining species protection; the Scientific Authority is directed to draw on broad expert and conservation input.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is a standing administrative framework implementing permanent treaty obligations (CITES, the Endangered Species Act, the 1942 Convention) — an evergreen mandate maintained by ordinary amendment, so the permanence carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds implementing machinery and directs consultation and coordination with numerous agencies; it removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The Scientific Authority is directed to ascertain the views of and utilize non-governmental scientific, conservation, humane, and commercial communities, giving external actors standing and access to surface concerns about permit decisions.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order implements defined treaty obligations through inter-agency designation and coordination; no precedent or definitional framework extensible to unrelated domains is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order overlays a new Scientific Authority drawing representatives from Interior, Agriculture, Commerce, HEW, NSF, and CEQ and directs Interior to develop a permit system 'in coordination with' Treasury and others, creating parallel authority over species-trade functions that several agencies already touch and inviting jurisdictional friction.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order sets up a permitting/authority structure without any broad discretionary waiver or carve-out that would exempt actors from CITES coverage.

note: CITES/ESA implementation order; no numbered predecessor EO is amended (it derives from a treaty and statute), so no diff-aware comparison was needed. Interior's dual role drives Flag 1 and the multi-agency Scientific Authority plus coordination mandate drives Flag 10; undefined permit standards fire Flag 4.

title: Establishing Black Coulee Migratory Waterfowl Refuge, Montana

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A self-executing land reservation setting apart 1,160 acres for the Department of Agriculture; no discretionary decision authority is pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The reservation names Agriculture as steward; there is no ongoing implementation that could fail with diffuse blame.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single-purpose land reservation under the Migratory Bird Conservation Act; nothing distinct is bundled with it.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time land-status reservation creates no ongoing compliance rule for any actor to enforce.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral regime exists that could create incentives for any actor class.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A permanent wildlife refuge is evergreen by nature (protected land has no natural end-state requiring reauthorization), so permanence is appropriate.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Reservation is 'subject to valid existing rights'; no pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed or bypassed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory relationship exists over which a third party would have standing to surface violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Narrow land reservation with no systemic or precedential implications beyond the described tract.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only Agriculture is implicated; no other agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage regime exists from which parties could be carved out; the proviso merely folds in private lands once acquired.

note: One-time self-executing land reservation; compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11) and Flag 9 are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE per the concrete test, while decision-structure flags score ABSENT.

title: Establishing the Division of Defense Aid Reports in the Office for Emergency Management of the Executive Office of the President

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: An Executive Officer appointed by and reporting to the President runs the Division 'subject to such policies and directions as the President may from time to time prescribe,' pooling coordination authority under the President with no external check.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Executive Officer is a named actor responsible for the Division's clearing-house functions and reporting; responsibility is clear.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose: establishing a records/reporting clearing house for Lend-Lease aid under the March 11, 1941 Act; no distinct matters bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: An internal coordination/reporting body imposes no compliance rule on outside parties to enforce.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No regulated party or beneficiary regime exists to create gameable incentives; this is an internal accounting/reporting unit.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The Division is created to administer aid under a wartime/emergency Act tied to the declared national emergency of September 8, 1939, yet has no termination trigger tied to that emergency's resolution.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Records/summary accounts require Bureau of the Budget approval, but the Division becomes the 'central channel' clearing all Lend-Lease transactions, routing them through a presidential unit rather than existing departmental review channels.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No external compliance relationship exists; the unit reports internally to the President and serves participating agencies.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Centralizing Lend-Lead aid clearance in a presidential EOP division establishes precedent and machinery for the President to direct war-materiel flows through an office insulated from departmental structures.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Division is made the 'central channel' coordinating requests for aid across 'agencies participating in the program,' inserting a presidential unit into flows that departments (War, Navy, Treasury) would otherwise process under their own mandates.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage-and-exemption structure; the order sets up an internal clearing house, not a rule applying to a covered population.

note: Internal EOP coordination body for Lend-Lease aid; compliance-regime flags NOT_APPLICABLE, but power concentration, lateral coordination friction, wartime-authority permanence, and downstream precedent all fire.

title: Conforming the Central Intelligence Agency and Civil Service Retirement and Disability Systems With Respect to Cost of Living Adjustments

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Director of Central Intelligence merely computes a percent change in a published price index by a fixed formula; the adjustment is mechanical, not discretionary.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The DCI is the named responsible actor for the ministerial determination; a mis-computation is traceable and correctable.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose: conforming CIA retirement COLAs to the Civil Service system's formula; nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The trigger (a rise in the specified price index) and the consequence (annuity increased by that percent, rounded to nearest 1/10 percent) are precisely defined; two officials would compute the same result.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A formula-driven COLA tied to a published index gives no actor a way to game the outcome to undermine the purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A permanent annuity-adjustment formula is an evergreen standing procedural mechanism with a natural end only if the retirement statute changes; permanence is appropriate.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds a computation duty under statutory authority; it removes no pre-existing review or oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The adjustment is automatic and index-driven; annuitants themselves would surface any under-payment, and no self-reporting compliance chain is involved.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Conforming one retirement system's COLA formula to another has no identifiable precedential or systemic spillover beyond the annuitants covered.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: It aligns CIA retirement adjustments with the Civil Service system by statute without countermanding either system's mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Every qualifying annuity with a commencing date not later than the effective date is covered by the same formula; no carve-outs exist.

note: Ongoing benefit-adjustment relationship exists (compliance-regime flags apply and score ABSENT), but the formula is fully mechanical and index-driven, so every flag clears cleanly.

title: Removing Wage and Salary Controls Adopted Pursuant to the Stabilization Act of 1942

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A self-executing termination of existing wage/salary controls; it concentrates no authority, it removes a control regime.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing implementation follows; the order simply lifts controls with a savings clause preserving pending suits, leaving no failure chain.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single action: terminating all wage/salary stabilization controls under the Stabilization Act; nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Deregulatory termination creates no forward compliance obligation to enforce; the savings clause only preserves already-accrued liabilities.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Lifting controls establishes no ongoing behavioral regime that could reward the wrong conduct.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A one-time termination grants no continuing authority that could require a sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes a substantive control program, not an oversight mechanism; it expressly preserves suits and prosecutions for prior offenses.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No forward compliance relationship remains over which any third party would surface violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A wind-down of a wartime control program back to a peacetime baseline carries no identifiable systemic or precedential spillover from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's mandate is redirected or countermanded; controls are simply terminated.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage regime remains from which parties could be exempted; the exception clause only preserves pre-existing accrued rights/liabilities.

note: Deregulatory one-time termination of wage controls; compliance-regime flags and forward-looking flags NOT_APPLICABLE because it dismantles rather than creates an ongoing regime, decision-structure flags ABSENT.

title: Federal Compliance With Pollution Control Standards

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Exemption authority is pooled in the President, and unresolved compliance conflicts ultimately go to the OMB Director (Sec. 1-603) rather than a neutral adjudicator; the EPA Administrator's role is largely advisory/technical.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each agency head is 'responsible for compliance' with standards applying to its own facilities, and violations are resolved through OMB rather than binding external enforcement, so the regulated agency effectively participates in judging its own compliance (Sec. 1-101, 1-601 to 1-603).

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single coherent frame: making federal facilities comply with existing pollution-control statutes; the enumerated statutes are all instances of one purpose, not distinct bundled remedies.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Compliance rests on the Administrator 'notifying' an agency, which then submits a plan for the notifier's approval 'as soon as practicable' (Sec. 1-601); the undefined timeline and reliance on negotiated plans leave enforcement discretion loosely bounded.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
