flags.justification: Because a violating agency negotiates its own compliance plan and conflicts route to OMB (which controls the agency's budget), agencies face weak pressure and can slow-walk compliance behind resource claims; the conflict-resolution structure rewards internal bargaining over abatement.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A standing federal-facility compliance framework maintained through ordinary supersession (it revokes and replaces EO 11752/11507 line) is an evergreen procedural structure; permanence is appropriate.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 1-604 preserves other enforcement procedures, but Sec. 1-603 routes unresolved interagency compliance conflicts to the OMB Director for resolution, and Sec. 1-701 confines exemptions to presidential determination, channeling disputes into internal executive processes rather than external review; relative to predecessor EO 11752 the internal-conflict pathway is comparable, so this fires only mildly.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Detection depends on the Administrator or a state/local agency notifying the violator (Sec. 1-601); no private party or independent actor is given standing or an incentive to surface federal-facility violations, and enforcement leans on inter-agency self-reporting via annual plans.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Routing federal-facility environmental compliance disputes through OMB establishes precedent for budget-office arbitration of statutory compliance, and the presidential-exemption architecture (Sec. 1-7) creates a reusable national-security/paramount-interest override applicable across environmental domains.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: EPA's statutory enforcement role against federal facilities is partly displaced: the Administrator can only notify, mediate, and refer, with final conflict resolution vested in the OMB Director (Sec. 1-602, 1-603), creating parallel authority and mandate friction between EPA and OMB.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Sec. 1-7 lets the President exempt any facility from standards under 1-102(a)-(f) on a broad 'national security' or 'paramount interest of the United States' determination, a discretionary override concentrated in one actor that can carve major facilities out of coverage.

note: Revokes EO 11752 (predecessor text reviewed); the compliance-through-notification-and-negotiated-plan structure plus presidential exemption power and OMB conflict resolution drive multiple PRESENT flags, but none rises to core collapse (agencies remain substantively subject to the same standards as private persons).

title: Amending Executive Order No. 10450, as Amended, Relating to Security Requirements for Government Employment, and for Other Purposes

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: This amendment narrows and removes authority (abolishing the Attorney General's organization-designation list and the subversive-list machinery); relative to EO 10450's baseline it deconcentrates rather than pools authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The amendment abolishes a designation list and revises a membership criterion; it introduces no new implementation whose failure would lack a responsible actor.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The order bundles distinct actions under one instrument: abolishing the Attorney General's list (Sec. 2), narrowing the 'membership' security criterion to require specific intent (Sec. 3), and separately revoking EO 11605 on the Subversive Activities Control Board (Sec. 4) — related but severable matters combined for binary adoption.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The revised Sec. 8(a)(5) criterion turns on 'knowing membership with the specific intent of furthering the aims of' organizations that 'unlawfully advocate' force/violence — an improvement over the prior sweeping language, but 'adherence to and active participation in' still leaves adjudicators meaningful room for divergent good-faith determinations.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Tightening the membership standard and removing the list reduces, rather than creates, incentives to over-designate; no actor is rewarded for undermining the order's purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A standing government-employment security criterion is an evergreen procedural framework maintained through amendment (this order itself amends EO 10450); permanence is appropriate and this order in fact contracts it.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes designation machinery (a list and SACB functions) but does not remove any external check on implementation; if anything it eliminates a process that operated without adjudicative safeguards.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Security adjudications under EO 10450 remain internal to employing agencies with no external actor holding standing/incentive to surface erroneous determinations; this amendment does not add one.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Abolishing the Attorney General's list of designated organizations and requiring specific intent establishes a meaningful precedent constraining loyalty-security programs, foreclosing future reliance on status-based organizational designation.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Directing that no agency (AG, SACB, or other) designate or publish the list removes an activity rather than creating conflicting parallel mandates; agencies' core adjudicative roles under EO 10450 continue unchanged.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order narrows a coverage criterion; it creates no waiver or carve-out structure that could swallow the rule.

note: Diff-aware amendment of EO 10450 (predecessor reviewed); it deconcentrates and tightens the security-employment regime, so most flags clear, with residual bundling, still-imperfect adjudicative criterion, and precedential effects firing PRESENT. Revoked EO 11605 and EO 9835 texts not separately reviewed but their removal is deregulatory.

title: Adopting and Ratifying the Capture of the German Steamship "Milwaukee"

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A one-time ratification of a prize capture already made; no ongoing decision authority is concentrated in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: A self-executing ratification with no implementation to fail; nothing requires a responsible actor going forward.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single discrete act ratifying the capture of one named vessel; nothing is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time prize ratification creates no ongoing compliance rule for anyone to enforce.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral regime exists that could reward the wrong conduct.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A completed, self-executing ratification grants no continuing authority that could require a sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; it merely adopts a capture under existing prize statutes.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance relationship exists over which a third party would surface violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow ratification of a single wartime prize under existing statutes carries no identifiable systemic or precedential spillover from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the War Shipping Administration's completed capture is ratified; no other agency's mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage regime exists from which parties could be exempted.

note: One-time self-executing prize ratification; compliance-regime and forward-looking flags NOT_APPLICABLE, decision-structure flags ABSENT.

title: Executive Office of the President (Office of Administration)

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Primary responsibility for all administrative support functions of EOP units is 'transferred and reassigned' to a single Office of Administration whose Director reports directly to the President (Sec. 2, 5), pooling cross-cutting administrative authority in one presidential office.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Sec. 4(b) expressly preserves each unit head's responsibility for its own program and management, and the Director is named chief administrative officer, so responsibility lines are clear.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single purpose: effectuating the establishment of the Office of Administration under Reorganization Plan No. 1 of 1977; nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: An internal reorganization providing common administrative services imposes no compliance rule on any regulated party to enforce.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No external regulated or beneficiary class exists whose incentives the design could distort; this is internal service centralization.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A permanent administrative-support office created under a reorganization plan is an evergreen standing administrative structure with its own accountability (Sec. 4(b)); permanence is appropriate.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order reassigns internal support functions but expressly excepts functions vested by law in unit heads (Sec. 5); it removes no external oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No external compliance relationship exists; the Office serves EOP units internally.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Centralizing EOP administrative support (personnel, data processing, procurement, records) in one presidential office establishes lasting institutional infrastructure and precedent for consolidating cross-unit functions under direct presidential control.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Sec. 5-6 transfer records, property, personnel, and appropriation balances relating to administrative functions from individual EOP units to the Office of Administration, redirecting resources across units, though authorized by Reorganization Plan No. 1 of 1977.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The carve-outs (functions vested by law in unit heads; White House Office direct-support services) are defined and bounded exceptions, not a discretionary structure broad enough to swallow the consolidation.

note: Internal EOP reorganization implementing Reorganization Plan No. 1 of 1977; compliance-regime flags NOT_APPLICABLE, with vertical consolidation, lateral resource transfer, and institutional-precedent effects firing PRESENT.

title: Organization for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A one-sentence designation of the OPCW as a public international organization under the International Organizations Immunities Act; no discretionary authority is pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The designation is self-executing under statutory findings; there is no ongoing implementation whose failure would lack a responsible actor.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single discrete act designating one organization; nothing is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A status designation conferring statutory immunities creates no ongoing compliance rule for any actor to enforce.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral or compliance regime exists that could reward the wrong conduct.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A one-time self-executing designation grants no continuing operational authority that could require a sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes no pre-existing oversight; it expressly states it does not abridge immunities acquired by agreement or congressional action.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory relationship exists over which a third party would surface violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Conferring IOIA privileges, exemptions, and immunities on the OPCW has downstream legal consequences (e.g., immunity from suit and certain taxes for the organization and its property), an effect extending beyond the bare designation, though bounded by the statute.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No domestic agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded by the designation.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order confers statutory immunities on one organization rather than creating a coverage-and-exemption structure over a regulated population.

note: Self-executing IOIA designation of the OPCW; compliance-regime flags NOT_APPLICABLE, decision-structure flags ABSENT, with only the downstream immunities effect firing Flag 9.

title: Amending Regulations Relating to the Safeguarding of Vessels, Harbors, Ports, and Waterfront Facilities of the United States

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The amendments concentrate broad discretionary security authority in the Coast Guard Commandant, who alone decides whether a person's 'character and habits of life' warrant denying employment documents or Port Security Cards, and may 'prescribe such conditions and restrictions... as he finds to be necessary' (revised 6.10-1, 6.10-7, new 6.14-1).

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Commandant both sets the standard and adjudicates satisfaction of it under an open-ended 'inimical to the security of the United States' test, with no named external actor obligated to correct erroneous denials; relative to EO 10173 the appeals board (6.10-9) is unchanged but sits within the same authority.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: All five amendments serve one coherent purpose (waterfront/port security under the 1917 Act as amended); the enforcement, credential, and facility-safety provisions are instances of that single frame, not distinct bundled matters.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The operative standard — that presence 'would not be inimical to the security of the United States' judged by 'character and habits of life' (6.10-1, 6.10-7) — is undefined, so two officials could reach different good-faith denial decisions; new 6.14-1 lets the Commandant impose whatever restrictions he 'finds to be necessary.'

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The amendments impose discretionary screening but create no incentive rewarding actors for undermining port security; the regulated parties (workers, facility owners) gain nothing from non-compliance.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The underlying EO 10173 rests on a Presidential finding that 'the security of the United States is endangered by reason of subversive activity' — an emergency/exceptional-security predicate that should carry a natural end tied to that condition, yet neither the base order nor this amendment provides any expiration or reauthorization trigger.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The amendment expands the Commandant's authority but removes no pre-existing external check; the EO 10173 appeals-board mechanism (6.10-9) remains available to persons refused documents.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Security screening turns on the Commandant's satisfaction and self-reporting of sabotage (retained 6.16-1); affected individuals may appeal, but no external actor has standing and incentive to surface wrongful denials or lax screening, and enforcement rests within the Coast Guard chain.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Extending Commandant/District Commander authority (new 6.04-1) and the discretionary 'character and habits of life' credential-denial standard establishes precedent for open-ended security exclusion of maritime workers on undefined grounds, applicable across the waterfront workforce.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Authority is redistributed within the Coast Guard hierarchy (captain of the port up to District Commander and Commandant) and Port Security Card conditions are set 'after consultation with the Secretary of Labor'; no second agency's statutory mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Revised 6.10-1 lets the Commandant unilaterally 'designate categories of merchant vessels to which the foregoing shall not apply,' and 6.10-7 lets him recognize substitute credentials — discretionary carve-outs concentrated in one actor with no defined criteria or external check.

note: Partial amendment of EO 10173 (predecessor reviewed); the diff-aware read shows the amendment preserves and extends broad discretionary Commandant authority over a coverage/screening regime, firing power concentration, vague standards, exemption discretion, and related flags.

title: Blocking Libyan Government Property in the United States or Held by U.S. Persons

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: All IEEPA powers to 'carry out the provisions of this Order' are vested in the Secretary of the Treasury (in consultation with State) with no external check on the blocking/licensing determinations.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Treasury Secretary both administers the blocking and determines its scope; no named actor is responsible for correcting erroneous designations of blocked property.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: Single remedy (asset blocking) under a single frame (Libya threat previously declared in EO 12543); nothing distinct is packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order blocks 'all property and interests in property' of Libya and its 'controlled entities' but leaves the standard for what constitutes a controlled entity and the licensing/exemption criteria entirely to Treasury's later regulations.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A property freeze creates no evident incentive for regulated parties or the agency to undermine the stated purpose; the underlying EO 12543 emergency was active and unexpired, so the Zombie Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Emergency-derived blocking authority is exactly the kind that should track the emergency's resolution, yet the order is 'effective immediately' with no termination or reauthorization mechanism tied to the Libya emergency.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order is transmitted to Congress and published in the Federal Register per NEA/IEEPA; it removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement of the freeze depends on U.S. persons holding blocked property self-identifying and reporting it to Treasury; no external actor has both standing and incentive to surface undisclosed Libyan interests.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Blocking a sovereign's central bank and 'controlled entities' establishes precedent and machinery for asset freezes reusable against other states, extending IEEPA blocking authority well beyond the immediate Libya context.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Treasury acts in consultation with State within their normal IEEPA roles; no agency's existing statutory mandate is countermanded or redirected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Exemptions are deferred entirely to 'regulations which may hereafter be issued,' concentrating unbounded discretion to carve property out of the freeze in the Treasury Secretary with no stated criteria.

note: Extends/adds to EO 12543 (available in referenced-predecessors), which declared an active, unexpired Libya national emergency; the diff here is an additional blocking remedy, not a lapse-based continuation, so the Zombie Trap does not fire.

title: Termination of the Office of Strategic Services and Disposition of Its Functions

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Director of the Bureau of the Budget is given unilateral discretion to determine which personnel, property, funds, and 'further measures' relate to each transfer and to direct their redistribution, concentrating reorganization authority in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretaries of State and War may 'curtail' or 'discontinue' transferred activities at their own discretion with no named actor accountable if intelligence functions lapse in the handoff.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: A single reorganization action (winding up OSS and distributing its functions) under one frame; the several transfers all serve the one stated disposition purpose.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time reorganization/transfer of functions creates no ongoing behavioral rule to be enforced against any regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order imposes no ongoing compliance regime creating incentives for any actor class; it is a fixed transfer of functions. No lapsed statute is continued, so the Zombie Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The Interim Research and Intelligence Service is expressly abolished as of the close of business December 31, 1945 (Sec. 2), giving the temporary structure a built-in termination.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes no pre-existing external oversight mechanism; it redistributes operational functions among departments.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing enforcement chain exists for a self-executing internal reorganization; there is nothing for a third party to surface.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Dissolving OSS and lodging its research/analysis functions in State and residual functions in War set the institutional groundwork for the later reconstitution of a permanent centralized intelligence apparatus.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Functions of the Director of Strategic Services and the Joint Chiefs of Staff are transferred to the Secretaries of State and War, reassigning authority a wartime military body already held (Sec. 1, 3); Sec. 5 amends all conflicting prior orders.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order defines no covered population subject to a rule from which exemptions could be carved; it is an internal transfer of functions.

note: A one-time executive reorganization: the four compliance-regime flags (4, 5, 8, 11) are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE since no ongoing regulatory relationship is created. Sec. 5 amends unnamed 'prior orders in conflict,' with no specific numbered predecessor to compare against.

title: Administration of Scientific Research by Agencies of the Federal Government

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The NSF's role is to 'recommend,' 'study,' and 'consult'; substantive authority over research programs remains distributed among the responsible agency heads (Sec. 4, 6), so no decision authority pools in one body.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each agency head is made responsible for effective practices in its own programs (Sec. 6) and reports to the President (Sec. 9); responsibility for implementation failure is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: A single coordinated frame (coherent administration of federal basic research) with mutually reinforcing provisions; nothing distinct is packaged to foreclose targeted correction.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order sets coordinating and advisory duties rather than an enforceable standard; the obligations (consult NSF, review programs, share reports) are stated with sufficient specificity that officials would not reach divergent enforcement outcomes.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The design channels agencies toward coordination, avoidance of duplicative support, and dissemination; no rational actor is rewarded for undermining the stated purpose. No lapsed statute is continued.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural/coordination framework for a permanent administrative function (federal research administration) with no natural end-state, falling within the standing-procedural-framework carve-out rather than firing as an emergency/temporary grant.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds coordination and reporting obligations and removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; agencies retain their own authorities 'as now or hereafter authorized by law' (Sec. 4).

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Compliance is surfaced through cross-agency consultation, NSF review of agency programs, and reporting to the President (Sec. 3, 6, 9), giving actors beyond the acting agency visibility into performance.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order institutionalizes NSF's coordinating role but this is contained to research administration; no identifiable authority claim or definition imports coverage into unrelated domains.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: NSF coordinates with, rather than countermands, other agencies; the order expressly preserves other agencies' mission-related basic research and their statutory authorities (Sec. 4), avoiding mandate conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No exemption or waiver structure is created; the security/law caveats on dissemination (Sec. 6(d), 7) are ordinary and do not gut coverage of the coordination scheme.

note: A structurally clean coordination framework: authority stays distributed among agency heads, NSF's role is advisory, and accountability runs to the President. Flag 6 clears under the standing-procedural-framework carve-out for a permanent administrative function.

title: Establishing the Tybee Migratory Bird Refuge

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A self-executing reservation of specified lands for a refuge; no discretionary decision authority is pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Administration is assigned to the Department of Agriculture for wildlife conservation with clear, bounded terms; responsibility for the reservation's purpose is located.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: A single action (establishing one named refuge) under one frame; nothing unrelated is packaged with it.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time land reservation creates no ongoing behavioral rule for any regulated party to comply with or enforce.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance regime is established, so there is no incentive structure for any actor class to game. No lapsed statute is involved.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A self-executing land-status reservation grants no ongoing operational authority that could be subject to sunset or reauthorization.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; it in fact preserves Commerce and War Department uses and regulations without interference (Sec. 2).

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no ongoing enforcement chain for a self-executing land reservation, so no third-party surfacing role exists.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow, site-specific land reservation with no systemic or precedential implications beyond the named tract.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Though Commerce and War Department jurisdictions are implicated, Sec. 2 expressly subordinates the refuge use to their existing and future lighthouse and river/harbor uses, avoiding any mandate conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No covered population subject to a rule exists, so there is no exemption structure to assess.

note: A one-time, self-executing land reservation under the Migratory Bird Conservation Act; the compliance-regime flags (4, 5, 8, 11) plus Flags 6 and 9 are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE, while decision-structure Flags 1, 2, 7, Flag 3, and the multi-agency Flag 10 score cleanly ABSENT.

title: Continuing the Regulation of Exports

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Full authority to administer export controls and to amend or revoke the governing regulations is vested in the Secretary of Commerce (Sec. 1-2), continuing the delegation under EO 11533 without an external check on that discretion.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of Commerce administers the controls and unilaterally amends or revokes the regulations under which he operates, with no named actor accountable for correcting overbroad or under-enforced export restrictions.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: The stated purpose bundles three distinct problem frames under one continuation — national-security export vigilance, foreign-policy/international-responsibility ends, and protecting the domestic economy from scarce-material drain and inflation — obscuring accountability for each.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order continues in force the entire body of Commerce export regulations (15 CFR pts. 368-399) 'until amended or revoked by the Secretary,' leaving the operative standards to be set and changed unilaterally by the enforcing official.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Sec. 1 continues export-control authority 'Notwithstanding the expiration of the Export Administration Act of 1969,' re-anchoring it in the 1917 Trading with the Enemy Act; this textually-acknowledged statutory lapse with continued exercise of authority is the Zombie Emergency Trap — the agency keeps operating without the framework being reauthorized on its own terms.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Authority rests on the 1950 and 1971 national-emergency proclamations and continues indefinitely 'in full force and effect' with no reauthorization trigger — emergency-derived authority that should track the emergency's resolution but does not.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: By continuing export controls under Section 5(b) of the 1917 Act after the Export Administration Act lapsed, the order routes around the congressional reauthorization process that would have supplied legislative oversight of the export-control regime.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Export-control compliance depends on exporters' own licensing submissions and disclosures to Commerce; no external actor has both standing and access to information to surface unlicensed or evasive exports.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Sustaining a lapsed statutory scheme on 1917 emergency authority establishes precedent that expired regulatory frameworks can be indefinitely continued via emergency powers, a template applicable well beyond export controls.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Sec. 3 expressly preserves other agencies' and officials' authority under Section 5(b) and the parallel Rhodesia-sanctions regulations under EOs 11322 and 11419, avoiding conflict with those existing mandates.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The Secretary of Commerce holds unbounded discretion to amend, revoke, or license out of the entire continued export-control regime 'until amended or revoked,' concentrating exemption/licensing power in one actor with no defined criteria or external check.

note: Textbook Zombie Emergency Trap: Sec. 1 explicitly continues authority 'Notwithstanding the expiration of the Export Administration Act of 1969' by re-anchoring in the 1917 Trading with the Enemy Act. Predecessors EO 11533, 11683, 11322, 11419 were all available in referenced-predecessors and confirm this is a wholesale continuation of the lapsed EAA administration framework, not a clean partial amendment.

title: Critical Infrastructure Protection

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authority is layered across a Commission, a Steering Committee, a Principals Committee of cabinet officials, and an Advisory Committee (Sec. 1-5); the Commission only studies and recommends, so no single official holds discretionary decision authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Commission reports to the President through the Principals Committee, which reviews recommendations before submission (Sec. 2); responsibility for the work product is clearly located in named bodies.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: A single coherent frame (protecting critical infrastructures from physical and cyber threats); the study, IPTF coordination, and advisory provisions all serve that one purpose.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order creates no enforceable regulatory standard — it directs a study and interim coordination; the bodies' tasks and reporting duties are specified with adequate clarity (Sec. 4, 7).

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The design rewards analysis, consultation, and coordination toward the stated protective purpose; no actor is incentivized to undermine it, and the IPTF 'will not supplant any existing programs' (Sec. 7(b)). No lapsed statute is continued.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: The Commission and all committees 'shall terminate 1 year from the date of this order, unless extended' (Sec. 6(f)) and the IPTF terminates no later than 180 days after the Commission (Sec. 7(h)) — explicit sunsets requiring affirmative extension.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds advisory and coordinating machinery and complies with the Federal Advisory Committee Act (Sec. 5(c)); it removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The order imposes no compliance obligation requiring third-party surfacing; it mandates cross-agency information sharing with the IPTF and consultation with private owners/operators and Congress (Sec. 4(b), 7(g)), providing external visibility.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: By defining 'critical infrastructures' broadly (telecom, finance, transportation, continuity of government) and creating an FBI-chaired interagency task force with threat-warning and information-sharing functions, the order lays durable institutional groundwork for expanded government-private surveillance and coordination beyond the one-year study.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The IPTF expressly 'will not supplant any existing programs or organizations' (Sec. 7(b)) and the order 'is not intended to change any existing statutes or Executive orders' (Sec. 8(a)), avoiding conflict with existing agency mandates.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage rule with exemptions is created; the order establishes study and coordination bodies rather than a regulated population from which carve-outs could be granted.

note: Structurally clean study/advisory order with explicit one-year and 180-day sunsets, FACA compliance, layered committee review, and an express non-supplant clause; only Flag 9 fires for the durable interagency infrastructure-protection precedent it establishes.

title: Withdrawal of Public Land for Use of the War Department

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A self-executing withdrawal of one 14.24-acre tract for War Department military use; no discretionary decision authority is pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The land is reserved for a single, defined purpose (War Department military use) with no implementation discretion that could produce diffuse blame.

flags.name: Bundling (remedy / frame)
flags.justification: A single action (withdrawing one described tract) under one frame; nothing unrelated is packaged with it.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time land withdrawal creates no ongoing behavioral rule for any regulated party to comply with or enforce.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance regime is established, so no incentive structure exists for any actor class to game. No lapsed statute is involved.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: A self-executing land-status withdrawal grants no ongoing operational authority subject to sunset; it continues as a land status 'until revoked by the President or by act of Congress,' which is an ordinary revocability clause, not a discretionary authority grant.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; it is expressly 'subject to valid existing rights.'

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no ongoing enforcement chain for a self-executing land withdrawal, so no third-party surfacing role exists.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow, site-specific temporary land withdrawal with no systemic or precedential implications beyond the named tract.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the War Department is implicated as beneficiary of the reservation; no second agency's mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No covered population subject to a rule exists, so there is no exemption structure to assess.

note: A one-time, self-executing temporary land withdrawal under the 1910/1912 withdrawal acts; compliance-regime flags (4, 5, 8, 11) plus Flags 6, 9, and 10 are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE, while decision-structure Flags 1, 2, 7 and Flag 3 score cleanly ABSENT.
